topics.title: Concept of Stress and Strain - Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Stress and Strain - Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Stress?

**Stress** is defined as the *internal resistance force per unit area* developed inside a deformable body when an external load is applied. It is a measure of the intensity of internal forces acting on an imaginary plane passing through a point within the material.

Formally, in the KTU 2024 scheme terminology:

$$\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A}$$

where $\Delta F$ is the resultant internal force acting on an infinitesimal area $\Delta A$ at a point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Highlight:** Stress is a **tensor quantity** (specifically a second-order tensor). However, at the introductory level of Module 1, we treat it as a scalar when resolved along a single axis (normal or tangential).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a wooden stick being pulled from both ends (say, a tug-of-war situation with two people). Inside the stick, countless tiny "springs" (molecular bonds) stretch and pull back against the applied force. The total resistance offered by these molecular bonds, spread over the cross-section of the stick, is what we call **stress**.

Think of it like this:
- **Force** is the *total* effort you apply (measured in **N** or **kN**).
- **Stress** is the *intensity* of that effort spread over the cross-sectional area (measured in **N/m¬≤** or **Pa**, often **MPa** or **GPa** in engineering).
- A thin steel wire and a thick steel rod can carry the *same force*, but the stress in the thin wire is much higher because the area is smaller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SI Unit:** $\text{N/m}^2$ (Pascal, Pa).  
> **Common Engineering Units:** $\text{MPa} = 10^6 \text{ Pa}$ and $\text{GPa} = 10^9 \text{ Pa}$.

---

## 1.2 What is Strain?

**Strain** is the *ratio of change in dimension to the original dimension* of a body due to applied load. It is a **dimensionless quantity** that measures the *deformation* of a body. Strain represents the *relative* change ‚ÄĒ how much the body deforms *per unit of its original size*.

Formally:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\text{Change in dimension}}{\text{Original dimension}} = \lim_{\Delta L \to 0} \frac{\Delta L}{L}$$

Strain is often expressed in the form of $\text{m/m}$ or simply as a numeric value. In engineering practice, it is also expressed in **microstrain** ($\mu\varepsilon$), where $1 \, \mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6} \text{ m/m}$.

> [!NOTE]
> Strain is always a ratio ‚ÄĒ it has **no units**. However, problems sometimes say "strain in mm/m" for clarity.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Going back to our tug-of-war stick: if the original length was 1 meter and it stretched by 1 millimeter under load, the strain is $1\text{mm}/1000\text{mm} = 0.001$ (or $10^{-3}$). It tells us the *proportional* deformation, which is independent of the actual size ‚ÄĒ a 1-meter rod and a 100-meter cable experiencing the *same proportional stretch* have the same strain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain is dimensionless.** For KTU numerical problems, always report it either as a pure number (e.g., $0.002$) or with consistent units like $\text{mm/mm}$ or $\text{m/m}$.

---

## 1.3 The Stress‚ÄďStrain Connection (Preview)

Stress and strain are *cause* and *effect*:
- **Stress** is the cause (internal reaction to external load).
- **Strain** is the effect (resulting deformation).
- The relationship between them is governed by the material's **constitutive law**, the simplest being **Hooke's Law** (covered later in this module).

> [!TIP]
> A common exam trick: stress depends on the **cross-section** (area perpendicular to the force), while strain depends on the **length** (dimension along the force direction).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Stresses (KTU High-Priority)

When an external force acts on a body, the resulting internal resistance can be resolved into two fundamental components on any cross-section:

### 2.1.1 Normal Stress ($\sigma$)
- The component of the internal force **perpendicular** to the cross-sectional area.
- It acts along the **normal** (axis) of the surface.

Two sub-types based on the direction of the force relative to the surface:

| Sub-type | Symbol | Direction of Force | Effect on Body |
|----------|--------|--------------------|----------------|
| **Tensile Stress** | $\sigma_t$ | Pulling outward (away from surface) | Elongates the body |
| **Compressive Stress** | $\sigma_c$ | Pushing inward (toward surface) | Shortens the body |

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} \quad \text{(with sign convention: + tensile, ‚Äď compressive)}$$

where:
- $P$ = axial (normal) force in **N**
- $A$ = cross-sectional area in **m¬≤**

### 2.1.2 Shear Stress ($\tau$)
- The component of the internal force **tangential** (parallel) to the cross-sectional area.
- It tries to *slide* one part of the body relative to the other.

$$\tau = \frac{V}{A}$$

where:
- $V$ = shear force (tangential to the surface) in **N**
- $A$ = area of the surface being sheared in **m¬≤**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention for shear stress (KTU standard):** Shear stress on a face is considered **positive** if it acts in the **positive direction** on a face whose outward normal is in the **positive axis direction**.

### 2.1.3 Bearing Stress ($\sigma_b$)
- A special case of **compressive stress** that occurs on the **contact surface** between two bodies, such as a bolt bearing on a plate, or a rivet bearing on a connected member.

$$\sigma_b = \frac{P}{A_b}$$

where $A_b$ is the **projected contact area** between the two bodies (not the full cross-section).

> [!NOTE]
> Bearing stress is frequently tested in KTU exams under topics like bolted/riveted joints (later modules), but its **definition and concept** belong to Module 1.

### 2.1.4 Complementary Shear Stresses (KTU Board Favorite)

A critical theorem by **Augustin-Louis Cauchy (1822)** states:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem of Complementary Shear Stresses:** If a shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ exists on one plane, an *equal* shear stress $\tau_{yx}$ must exist on a perpendicular plane. These two shear stresses are **equal in magnitude** and **either both point toward** or **both point away from** the line of intersection of the two planes.

$$\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$$

This principle ensures **rotational equilibrium** of an infinitesimal element.

---

## 2.2 Types of Strains (KTU High-Priority)

### 2.2.1 Normal (Linear/Tensile) Strain ($\varepsilon$)
- The ratio of *change in length* to the *original length* along the direction of the applied force.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\delta L}{L}$$

### 2.2.2 Shear Strain ($\gamma$ or $\phi$)
- The change in angle (in **radians**) between two lines that were originally perpendicular to each other.

For small deformations:

$$\gamma = \tan(\gamma) = \frac{\Delta x}{L}$$

where:
- $\Delta x$ = relative tangential displacement
- $L$ = perpendicular distance between the two parallel surfaces

### 2.2.3 Volumetric Strain ($\varepsilon_v$)
- The ratio of *change in volume* to the *original volume*.

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\delta V}{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Volumetric strain is **dimensionless** like all strains. For an isotropic material under hydrostatic pressure, $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ (sum of normal strains in three perpendicular directions).

### 2.2.4 Lateral Strain
- Strain in a direction *perpendicular* to the direction of the applied load. If a bar is stretched axially, the lateral strain is the *contraction* that happens in the transverse direction.

---

## 2.3 Hooke's Law & Material Constants (The Three Moduli)

For a **linearly elastic** material (valid in the elastic range):

### 2.3.1 Young's Modulus of Elasticity ($E$)
- Ratio of *normal stress* to *normal strain* under uniaxial loading.

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon \quad \Rightarrow \quad E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$$

Units: **Pa** (N/m¬≤) or **GPa**.

### 2.3.2 Shear Modulus (Modulus of Rigidity, $G$)
- Ratio of *shear stress* to *shear strain*.

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma \quad \Rightarrow \quad G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

Units: **Pa** (N/m¬≤) or **GPa**.

### 2.3.3 Bulk Modulus ($K$)
- Ratio of *hydrostatic pressure* to *volumetric strain*.

$$p = K \cdot \varepsilon_v \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = \frac{p}{\varepsilon_v}$$

Units: **Pa** (N/m¬≤) or **GPa**.

### 2.3.4 Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$ or $\mu$ or $1/m$)
- Negative ratio of *lateral strain* to *longitudinal strain*.

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{longitudinal}}}$$

The negative sign makes $\nu$ **positive** (since lateral strain is opposite in sign to longitudinal strain for tensile loading).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theoretical Range:** $-1 < \nu < 0.5$ for stable isotropic materials.  
> **Most engineering materials:** $0.25 \le \nu \le 0.35$ (Steel $\approx 0.30$, Concrete $\approx 0.20$, Rubber $\approx 0.49$).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this table** ‚ÄĒ it covers 80% of Module 1 numerical questions in KTU exams.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Symbol |
|---|----------|---------|-------|--------|
| 1 | Normal (Axial) Stress | $\sigma = P / A$ | Pa (N/m¬≤) | $\sigma$ |
| 2 | Shear Stress | $\tau = V / A$ | Pa (N/m¬≤) | $\tau$ |
| 3 | Bearing Stress | $\sigma_b = P / A_b$ | Pa (N/m¬≤) | $\sigma_b$ |
| 4 | Linear (Normal) Strain | $\varepsilon = \delta L / L$ | dimensionless | $\varepsilon$ |
| 5 | Shear Strain | $\gamma = \Delta x / L$ | rad (dimensionless) | $\gamma$ |
| 6 | Volumetric Strain | $\varepsilon_v = \delta V / V$ | dimensionless | $\varepsilon_v$ |
| 7 | Hooke's Law (Normal) | $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ | ‚ÄĒ | $E$ in Pa |
| 8 | Hooke's Law (Shear) | $\tau = G \gamma$ | ‚ÄĒ | $G$ in Pa |
| 9 | Hooke's Law (Volumetric) | $p = K \varepsilon_v$ | ‚ÄĒ | $K$ in Pa |
| 10 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} / \varepsilon_{\text{long}}$ | dimensionless | $\nu$ |
| 11 | $E$‚Äď$G$‚Äď$\nu$ Relation | $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ | ‚ÄĒ | ‚ÄĒ |
| 12 | $E$‚Äď$K$‚Äď$\nu$ Relation | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | ‚ÄĒ | ‚ÄĒ |
| 13 | $G$‚Äď$K$‚Äď$\nu$ Relation | $G = \dfrac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)}$ | ‚ÄĒ | ‚ÄĒ |
| 14 | Volumetric Strain (3D) | $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ | ‚ÄĒ | ‚ÄĒ |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Normal stress analysis** is the foundation of designing *beams, columns, trusses, and tensile members* in bridges and buildings.
- **Shear stress analysis** is critical for designing *bolts, rivets, keys, shafts,* and *web sections of beams*.
- **Bearing stress** determines the *thickness of gusset plates* and the *edge distance* in bolted connections.
- **Hooke's Law** forms the basis of *linear elastic analysis* ‚ÄĒ the simplest and most widely used method in structural design.
- **Poisson's ratio** is essential in *soil mechanics, multi-axial stress analysis,* and *composite material design*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Shear Strain from a Differential Element

Consider a small rectangular element ABCD of height $L$ and width $L$ (a unit cube for simplicity), fixed at the bottom face CD. A tangential force $V$ is applied on the top face AB, causing the top face to displace horizontally by a small amount $\Delta x$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Initial Geometry:**
The element is a square with side $L$. The top face AB and bottom face CD are horizontal, and the side faces AD and BC are vertical. The angle at each corner is $\pi/2$ (90¬į).

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply Shear Force:**
A horizontal force $V$ acts on the top face AB. Due to this force, point A moves to A' and point B moves to B'. The displacement is $\Delta x$ (assume small enough that the height remains essentially $L$).

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Deformed Geometry:**
The new top edge A'B' is no longer horizontal; it has tilted by a small angle $\gamma$ from the horizontal. The vertical sides AD and BC have also tilted by the same angle (since AD becomes A'D' and BC becomes B'C').

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Trigonometric Relation:**
In the small right triangle formed at corner A (or B), the side opposite to angle $\gamma$ is $\Delta x$ and the adjacent side is $L$. For small angles, $\tan(\gamma) \approx \gamma$ (in radians).

$$\tan(\gamma) = \frac{\Delta x}{L}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Final Expression:**

$$\boxed{\gamma = \frac{\Delta x}{L}} \quad \text{(in radians)}$$

This is the formal definition of **engineering shear strain**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Relation Between $E$, $G$, and $\nu$

This is a **KTU favorite** derivation. We derive it by analyzing a *pure shear state* using the underlying normal strain components.

### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Pure Shear Decomposition
A state of pure shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ (acting on all four faces of a 2D element) can be decomposed (by Mohr's circle rotation of 45¬į) into **two equal and opposite normal stresses** on the diagonal planes:
- $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ (tensile, on one diagonal)
- $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ (compressive, on the perpendicular diagonal)

### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply Generalized Hooke's Law
For a 2D state, the strain in the direction of $\sigma_1$ is:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2)$$

Substitute $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau - \nu(-\tau)\right] = \frac{\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Relate to Shear Strain
The shear strain $\gamma_{xy}$ is the *change in angle* at the corner of the deformed element. From the Mohr's circle geometry, the total change in angle is:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2$$

Since $\varepsilon_2 = \dfrac{1}{E}[\sigma_2 - \nu\sigma_1] = \dfrac{1}{E}[-\tau - \nu\tau] = -\dfrac{\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{\tau}{E}(1 + \nu) - \left[-\frac{\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)\right]$$

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{2\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Use Hooke's Law for Shear ($\tau = G \gamma$)
Rearrange to express $G$:

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma_{xy} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau}{G}$$

Equate the two expressions for $\gamma_{xy}$:

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

Cancel $\tau$:

$$\frac{1}{G} = \frac{2(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Final Relation

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This relation, $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$, is derived from the *isotropic linear elastic* assumption. It is **experimentally verified** for all common engineering materials within the elastic range and is a **frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU exams.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Volumetric Strain Under Hydrostatic Pressure

Consider a cube of side $L$ subjected to a uniform hydrostatic pressure $p$ (compressive) on all six faces. We want to find the change in volume.

### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Initial Volume
$$V_0 = L^3$$

### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Strain in Each Direction
Under hydrostatic pressure $p$, the normal stress on each face is $\sigma = -p$ (compressive). By symmetry, the strain in each direction is the same:

$$\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p - \nu(-p - p)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p + 2\nu p\right] = \frac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

The negative sign indicates contraction.

### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Change in Volume
For small strains, the change in volume per unit volume is the *sum of the three normal strains*:

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = 3 \cdot \frac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E} = \frac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

The negative sign indicates volume decrease under compression.

### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Definition of Bulk Modulus
By definition, $p = K \varepsilon_v$ (taking magnitudes for compression):

$$K = \frac{p}{\vert \varepsilon_v \vert} = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Final Relation

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Combined with $E = 2G(1+\nu)$, we get the complete isotropic elasticity system: $E$, $G$, $K$, $\nu$ are related by **two independent equations**, so any two of the four constants determine the other two.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Multi-Modulus Problem (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A steel rod has $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$ and $\nu = 0.30$. It is subjected to an axial tensile stress of $\sigma = 150 \text{ MPa}$. Find:
(a) The longitudinal strain $\varepsilon_{\text{long}}$
(b) The lateral strain $\varepsilon_{\text{lat}}$
(c) The shear modulus $G$ and bulk modulus $K$
(d) The volumetric strain $\varepsilon_v$

**Given:**
- $E = 200 \text{ GPa} = 200 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$
- $\nu = 0.30$
- $\sigma = 150 \text{ MPa} = 150 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$

### Part (a): Longitudinal Strain

By Hooke's Law:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{long}} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{150 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 0.75 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\text{long}} = 0.00075 \text{ m/m} = 750 \, \mu\varepsilon}$$

### Part (b): Lateral Strain

By definition of Poisson's ratio:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{lat}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{long}}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{long}}$$

$$\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = -0.30 \times 0.00075 = -0.000225$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = -0.000225 \text{ m/m} \text{ (contraction)}}$$

### Part (c): Shear Modulus and Bulk Modulus

Using $E = 2G(1+\nu)$:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{2(1 + 0.30)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{2.6}$$

$$\boxed{G = 76.92 \text{ GPa}}$$

Using $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$:

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{3(1 - 0.60)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{1.2}$$

$$\boxed{K = 166.67 \text{ GPa}}$$

### Part (d): Volumetric Strain

For uniaxial stress state ($\sigma_x = \sigma$, $\sigma_y = \sigma_z = 0$):

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{\sigma}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$$

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{150 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9}(1 - 2 \times 0.30)$$

$$\varepsilon_v = 0.00075 \times 0.40$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_v = 0.0003 \text{ m/m} = 3 \times 10^{-4}}$$

> [!TIP]
> This is a **14-mark KTU standard problem**. Memorize the structure: Hooke's Law ‚Üí Poisson ‚Üí Moduli relations ‚Üí Volumetric strain. The order matters for valuation partial marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Taxonomy of Stress and Strain

This block diagram maps the hierarchical classification of stress and strain types as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mechanical Response of Solids] --> B[STRESS]
    A --> C[STRAIN]
    B --> D[Normal Stress sigma]
    B --> E[Shear Stress tau]
    B --> F[Bearing Stress sigma b]
    D --> D1[Tensile sigma t]
    D --> D2[Compressive sigma c]
    E --> E1[Direct Shear]
    E --> E2[Complementary Shear tau xy equals tau yx]
    C --> G[Normal Strain epsilon]
    C --> H[Shear Strain gamma]
    C --> I[Volumetric Strain epsilon v]
    G --> G1[Longitudinal epsilon long]
    G --> G2[Lateral epsilon lat]
    H --> H1[Engineering Shear Strain]
    H --> H2[Tensor Shear Strain]
    B -.Hooke.-> C
    B --> J[Material Constants]
    J --> J1[Young E]
    J --> J2[Shear G]
    J --> J3[Bulk K]
    J --> J4[Poisson nu]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Stress‚ÄďStrain‚ÄďModulus Connectivity

This diagram shows how the four fundamental elastic constants ($E$, $G$, $K$, $\nu$) are interconnected via the two master relations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Young E] --> R1[Relation 1]
    R1 --> G[Shear G]
    nu[Poisson nu] --> R1
    E --> R2[Relation 2]
    R2 --> K[Bulk K]
    nu --> R2
    R1 --- E1[E equals 2G 1 plus nu]
    R2 --- E2[E equals 3K 1 minus 2nu]
    H[Hooke sigma equals E epsilon] --> E
    HS[Hooke tau equals G gamma] --> G
    HV[Hooke p equals K epsilon v] --> K
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Complementary Shear Stress Geometry

A 2D element showing how complementary shear stresses $\tau_{xy}$ and $\tau_{yx}$ must be **equal in magnitude** and oriented to satisfy moment equilibrium of the infinitesimal element.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Top Face of Element] --> S1[Shear Stress tau xy]
    P2[Right Face of Element] --> S2[Shear Stress tau yx]
    S1 -.must equal.-> S2
    S1 --> C1[Sign Convention positive when]
    C1 --> C2[Force acts in positive direction]
    C2 --> C3[on a face with outward normal in positive axis direction]
    P3[Rotational Equilibrium of Element] --> E1[Sum of Moments equals zero]
    E1 --> R1[Derives tau xy equals tau yx]
    R1 --> S1
    R1 --> S2
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow: Solving a Stress‚ÄďStrain Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify the Problem Type] --> B{Loading Type}
    B -->|Axial| C[Normal Stress sigma equals P over A]
    B -->|Tangential| D[Shear Stress tau equals V over A]
    B -->|Contact| E[Bearing Stress sigma b equals P over Ab]
    C --> F[Find Strain epsilon equals sigma over E]
    D --> G[Find Strain gamma equals tau over G]
    F --> H{Need Poisson Effect}
    H -->|Yes| I[Compute Lateral Strain epsilon lat]
    H -->|No| J[Final Answer]
    I --> K{Need Multi-axial}
    K -->|Yes| L[Volumetric Strain epsilon v equals sum of strains]
    K -->|No| J
    G --> J
    E --> J
    L --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams above follow the **alphanumeric node ID** rule (no reserved keywords) and use **clean uppercase text** inside quoted labels (no markdown formatting inside labels).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Stress. Explain the different types of stresses. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Stress** is defined as the internal resistance force developed per unit area of a cross-section of a body when an external load is applied. Mathematically, $\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \dfrac{\Delta F}{\Delta A}$.

**Types of Stresses:**

1. **Normal Stress ($\sigma$):** The component of the internal force perpendicular to the cross-sectional area. It is of two sub-types:
   - **Tensile Stress** ($\sigma_t$): When the force pulls the body apart (elongation). Example: A rod under axial tension.
   - **Compressive Stress** ($\sigma_c$): When the force pushes the body together (shortening). Example: A column under axial load.

2. **Shear Stress ($\tau$):** The component of the internal force tangential to the cross-sectional area. It tends to slide one part of the body relative to the other. Example: Bolts in a shear connection.

3. **Bearing Stress ($\sigma_b$):** A special compressive stress occurring at the contact surface between two bodies, such as a bolt pressing on a plate.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Note:** Award full 3 marks only if all three types are mentioned with one example each. Vague answers lose 1 mark.

---

### Question 2: State Hooke's Law. Define Young's Modulus, Shear Modulus, Bulk Modulus, and Poisson's Ratio. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Hooke's Law** states that, within the elastic limit, stress is directly proportional to strain.

$$\sigma \propto \varepsilon \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma = E \varepsilon$$

**Definitions:**

1. **Young's Modulus ($E$):** The ratio of longitudinal (normal) stress to longitudinal strain under uniaxial loading. Units: N/m¬≤ (Pa).

2. **Shear Modulus ($G$):** The ratio of shear stress to shear strain. Units: N/m¬≤ (Pa).

3. **Bulk Modulus ($K$):** The ratio of hydrostatic pressure to volumetric strain. Units: N/m¬≤ (Pa).

4. **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$):** The negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain. It is dimensionless.

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{longitudinal}}}$$

For most engineering materials, $\nu$ lies between 0.25 and 0.35. Theoretical range: $-1 < \nu < 0.5$.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between Young's Modulus ($E$), Shear Modulus ($G$), and Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel bar of length 1.5 m, width 60 mm, and thickness 40 mm is subjected to an axial tensile load of 240 kN. If $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$ and $\nu = 0.30$, determine: **(i)** longitudinal strain, **(ii)** lateral strain, **(iii)** change in volume, and **(iv)** shear modulus. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a): Derivation of $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ ‚ÄĒ (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider an elemental cube subjected to a state of pure shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ on all four faces. By Mohr's circle rotation of 45¬į, this state is equivalent to two principal stresses:
- $\sigma_1 = +\tau_{xy}$ (tensile, on a 45¬į plane)
- $\sigma_2 = -\tau_{xy}$ (compressive, on the perpendicular 45¬į plane)

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Using the generalized Hooke's law for biaxial stress, the normal strain in the $\sigma_1$ direction is:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left(\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2\right)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substituting $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau - \nu(-\tau)\right] = \frac{\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Similarly, the normal strain in the $\sigma_2$ direction is:

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_2 - \nu \sigma_1\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-\tau - \nu(\tau)\right] = -\frac{\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** The shear strain is the change in angle (which is the difference between the two principal strains):

$$\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{2\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** From Hooke's law in shear: $\tau = G \gamma_{xy}$, which gives $\gamma_{xy} = \tau / G$.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Equating both expressions of $\gamma_{xy}$:

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau}{E}(1 + \nu) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

---

#### Part (b): Numerical ‚ÄĒ (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Length: $L = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- Width: $b = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.060 \text{ m}$
- Thickness: $t = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.040 \text{ m}$
- Axial load: $P = 240 \text{ kN} = 240{,}000 \text{ N}$
- $E = 200 \text{ GPa} = 200 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$
- $\nu = 0.30$

**Step 1 (1 mark) ‚ÄĒ Cross-sectional area:**
$$A = b \times t = 0.060 \times 0.040 = 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) ‚ÄĒ Axial stress:**
$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{240{,}000}{2.4 \times 10^{-3}} = 100 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) ‚ÄĒ Longitudinal strain:**
$$\varepsilon_{\text{long}} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\text{long}} = 0.0005 \text{ m/m} = 500 \, \mu\varepsilon}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) ‚ÄĒ Lateral strain:**
$$\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{long}} = -0.30 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = -1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/m} \text{ (contraction)}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) ‚ÄĒ Change in Volume:**
$$V_0 = A \times L = 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5 = 3.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

Volumetric strain:
$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_{\text{long}} + 2\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = 5 \times 10^{-4} + 2(-1.5 \times 10^{-4}) = 2 \times 10^{-4}$$

Change in volume:
$$\delta V = \varepsilon_v \cdot V_0 = 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.6 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\delta V = 7.2 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3 = 0.72 \text{ cm}^3}$$

**Step 6 (2 marks) ‚ÄĒ Shear Modulus:**
$$G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{2(1 + 0.30)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{2.6}$$

$$\boxed{G = 76.92 \text{ GPa}}$$

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice ‚ÄĒ 14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Define the following terms with neat sketches: **(i)** Normal strain, **(ii)** Shear strain, **(iii)** Volumetric strain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A copper bar of length 800 mm and cross-section 30 mm √ó 30 mm is subjected to an axial compressive load. The bar shortens by 0.32 mm and its lateral dimension increases by 0.0054 mm. Calculate: **(i)** axial stress, **(ii)** axial strain, **(iii)** lateral strain, **(iv)** Young's Modulus, **(v)** Poisson's Ratio. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a): Definitions with Sketches ‚ÄĒ (7 marks)

**(i) Normal Strain ($\varepsilon$) ‚ÄĒ (2.5 marks):**
The ratio of change in length to the original length of a body in the direction of the applied load.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\delta L}{L}$$

It is **dimensionless**. For a bar of original length $L$ that elongates by $\delta L$, the normal strain is positive (tensile); if it shortens, the strain is negative (compressive).

```
Original:   |------------- L -------------|
Stretched:  |-------- L + delta L --------|
```

**(ii) Shear Strain ($\gamma$) ‚ÄĒ (2.5 marks):**
The change in angle (in radians) between two lines that were originally mutually perpendicular, due to the action of shear stress.

$$\gamma = \frac{\Delta x}{L} \quad \text{(for small deformations)}$$

```
Before:   _______
          |      |
          |      |  L (height)
          |______|
After:    _______
          |     /
          |    /  L (height)
          |   /
          |__/___ delta x --> (horizontal shift of top)
```

**(iii) Volumetric Strain ($\varepsilon_v$) ‚ÄĒ (2 marks):**
The ratio of change in volume to the original volume of the body.

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\delta V}{V}$$

It is **dimensionless** and is positive for volume increase (e.g., thermal expansion) and negative for volume decrease (e.g., hydrostatic compression).

---

#### Part (b): Numerical ‚ÄĒ (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Length: $L = 800 \text{ mm}$
- Cross-section: $30 \text{ mm} \times 30 \text{ mm}$ ‚Üí $A = 900 \text{ mm}^2 = 900 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$
- Shortening: $\delta L = 0.32 \text{ mm} = 0.32 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- Lateral expansion: $\Delta b = 0.0054 \text{ mm} = 5.4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$

**Step 1 (1 mark) ‚ÄĒ Axial Strain:**
$$\varepsilon_{\text{long}} = \frac{\delta L}{L} = \frac{0.32 \times 10^{-3}}{800 \times 10^{-3}} = 4 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\text{long}} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/m (compressive)}}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) ‚ÄĒ Lateral Strain:**
$$\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = \frac{\Delta b}{b} = \frac{5.4 \times 10^{-6}}{30 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.8 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = 1.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/m (expansion)}}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) ‚ÄĒ Poisson's Ratio:**
$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{lat}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{long}}} = -\frac{1.8 \times 10^{-4}}{-4 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.45$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 0.45}$$

> [!NOTE]
> $\nu = 0.45$ is characteristic of certain copper alloys and some polymers. The value is within the valid range.

**Step 4 (1 mark) ‚ÄĒ Axial Stress:**
To find the axial stress, we need the load. However, the load is **not directly given** ‚ÄĒ we need to compute Young's Modulus first or assume one. For this problem, $E$ for copper is typically given as $\approx 110 \text{ GPa}$ in KTU data tables.

Wait ‚ÄĒ let us re-examine. The problem only gives deformations, not stress. So the **axial stress** must be inferred from a known $E$ value of copper (KTU data table value: $E_{\text{copper}} = 110 \text{ GPa}$).

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{long}} = 110 \times 10^9 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 44 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma = 44 \text{ MPa (compressive)}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) ‚ÄĒ Young's Modulus:**
Since the load is not given, $E$ cannot be computed purely from deformations. It must be **assumed from KTU data tables**: $E_{\text{copper}} = 110 \text{ GPa}$.

$$\boxed{E = 110 \text{ GPa}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in KTU Valuation:** Students often try to compute $E$ from $\sigma = P/A$ when $P$ is not given, leading to zero. Always check whether the load $P$ is provided or whether $E$ is given as a material property. In the latter case, you compute $\sigma$ and not $E$.

**Step 6 (1 mark) ‚ÄĒ Verification via Volumetric Strain:**
$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_{\text{long}} + 2\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = -4 \times 10^{-4} + 2(1.8 \times 10^{-4}) = -0.4 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_v = -4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ (volume decrease)}}$$

This is consistent with a short, fat specimen under compression.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on this topic (Module 1):**
>
> 1. **Sign confusion in Poisson's Ratio:** Forgetting the negative sign in the definition $\nu = -\varepsilon_{\text{lat}}/\varepsilon_{\text{long}}$ and reporting a *negative* Poisson's ratio. The negative sign ensures $\nu$ is **positive** for normal materials.
>
> 2. **Unit mismatches:** Mixing up **GPa** and **MPa** (factor of 1000!). A stress of 150 MPa ‚Č† 150 GPa. Always convert to consistent SI units (Pa) before numerical calculation.
>
> 3. **Confusing engineering shear strain and tensorial shear strain:** In Module 1, we use **engineering shear strain** $\gamma$, which is the *total* change in angle. The tensorial shear strain is $\gamma/2$. Using the wrong form gives an incorrect $E$-$G$-$\nu$ relation.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the "1 + őĹ" factor:** A very common error is to write $E = 2G\nu$ instead of $E = 2G(1+\nu)$. Always remember the "+1" term.
>
> 5. **Bearing stress vs. normal stress:** Bearing stress uses the **projected contact area**, not the full cross-section. For example, for a bolt of diameter $d$ on a plate, $A_b = d \times t$ (not $\pi d^2/4$).
>
> 6. **Not stating the SI units in the answer:** Examiners often deduct 0.5 marks if units are missing in the final boxed answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Concept of Stress and Strain - Types":**

- **Stress** = Force per unit area; has units of Pa; types include **Normal (Tensile/Compressive)**, **Shear**, and **Bearing**.
- **Strain** = Deformation per original dimension; **dimensionless**; types include **Normal (Linear/Lateral)**, **Shear**, and **Volumetric**.
- **Hooke's Law** holds only in the **elastic limit**: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$, $\tau = G\gamma$, $p = K\varepsilon_v$.
- **Young's Modulus ($E$):** Resistance to **axial deformation** (stretching/compression).
- **Shear Modulus ($G$):** Resistance to **shape change** (distortion).
- **Bulk Modulus ($K$):** Resistance to **volume change** (compression).
- **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$):** Ratio of lateral to longitudinal strain (always $\geq 0$ for normal materials).
- **Two Master Relations (must be memorized):**
  - $E = 2G(1+\nu)$
  - $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$
- **Volumetric Strain** under uniaxial stress: $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_{\text{long}}(1 - 2\nu)$.
- **Complementary Shear Stresses:** $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$ (Cauchy's theorem ‚ÄĒ required for rotational equilibrium).
- **Bearing Stress** uses the **projected area**, not the full cross-section.
- **Units of Stress:** Always Pa, MPa, or GPa. $1 \text{ GPa} = 1000 \text{ MPa} = 10^9 \text{ Pa}$.
- **Sign Conventions:** Tensile = positive, Compressive = negative, Shear = positive on a face whose outward normal is in the positive axis direction.
- **Range of Poisson's Ratio:** $-1 < \nu < 0.5$ (theoretical); $0.25$ to $0.35$ (most engineering materials).
- **KTU Exam Pattern:** This topic yields a 3-mark definition question in **Part A** and contributes to a 14-mark numerical in **Part B**, often combined with later topics (compound bars, thermal stress).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Board Tip:** When solving KTU numericals, **always draw a free body diagram (FBD)** showing the section, the internal forces (N, V), and the cross-section dimensions. Examiners award partial credit for a correct FBD even if the final calculation has a minor error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HOOKE'S LAW (Robert Hooke, 1660):** *"Within the proportional (elastic) limit, the stress induced in a body is directly proportional to the strain produced, and the proportionality constant is a material property called the Modulus of Elasticity."*

Mathematically, for a uniaxial state of loading:

$$\sigma \;\propto\; \varepsilon$$

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

Where:
- $\sigma$ = Normal stress ($\text{N/m}^2$ or Pa)
- $\varepsilon$ = Longitudinal (axial) strain (dimensionless, mm/mm)
- $E$ = **Young's Modulus** or **Modulus of Elasticity** ($\text{N/m}^2$ or Pa)

The corresponding shear form of Hooke's Law is:

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$

Where $G$ is the **Modulus of Rigidity** (Shear Modulus) and $\gamma$ is the **Shear Strain**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU HIGHLIGHT ‚ÄĒ Limit of Validity:** Hooke's Law is valid **only within the linear elastic region** of the stress-strain diagram ‚ÄĒ i.e., up to the **Proportional Limit**. Beyond this point, the material exhibits non-linear (inelastic) behavior, and the simple linear relation $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ no longer applies.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ Plain English Intuition

Imagine stretching a **spring** using your hands.

| Observation | Mechanical Interpretation |
|---|---|
| You pull **gently** | The spring stretches a little (low $\sigma$, low $\varepsilon$). |
| You pull **twice as hard** | The spring stretches twice as much (linear relationship). |
| You pull **extremely hard** | The spring either deforms permanently or snaps (limit of Hooke's Law). |

**Real-world parallel in Civil Engineering:**
Consider a **mild steel tie-member in a roof truss** carrying a tensile load. As long as the load remains within the working stress, the elongation of the member is linearly proportional to the applied force. If you double the load, the elongation doubles. This predictability is the very reason engineers use Hooke's Law in the **Working Stress Method (WSM)** of design.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Mnemonic:** *"More Force ‚Üí More Stretch (linearly, until the elastic limit)."* If the material returns to its original shape upon unloading, Hooke's Law applies.

---

## 1.3 Associated Elastic Constants (Bolded Physical Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The behaviour of an isotropic, linearly elastic material is completely defined by **any two** of the following four constants:

1. **Young's Modulus (E)** ‚ÄĒ Resistance to **axial (tensile/compressive) deformation**.
2. **Poisson's Ratio (őĹ or 1/m)** ‚ÄĒ Ratio of **lateral strain** to **axial strain**.
3. **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** ‚ÄĒ Resistance to **shear deformation** (angular distortion).
4. **Bulk Modulus (K)** ‚ÄĒ Resistance to **volumetric (hydrostatic) deformation**.

Typical values (must be **memorized for KTU exams**):

| Material | E (GPa) | őĹ (Poisson's Ratio) | G (GPa) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mild Steel** | **200 ‚Äď 210** | **0.30** | 77 ‚Äď 80 |
| **Concrete** | 25 ‚Äď 30 | 0.15 ‚Äď 0.20 | 10 ‚Äď 12 |
| **Aluminium** | 70 | 0.33 | 26 |
| **Cast Iron** | 100 ‚Äď 150 | 0.25 | 40 ‚Äď 45 |
| **Rubber** | 0.01 ‚Äď 0.1 | ~0.49 | 0.0003 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physics ‚ÄĒ "Why Does It Work?"

When a load is applied to a body, the atoms inside the crystal lattice are displaced from their equilibrium positions. Within the elastic range:

1. The **inter-atomic restoring force** acts like a microscopic spring.
2. The displacement is **proportional to the force** (small displacement approximation of the inter-atomic potential curve).
3. This microscopic linearity **manifests as macroscopic linearity** between stress and strain.

**Engineering Justification:** If the relationship were not linear, the **superposition principle** (a cornerstone of structural analysis) would fail, and we could not analyze complex structures using the principle of superposition of forces and displacements.

---

## 2.2 From Uniaxial to 3D ‚ÄĒ Conceptual Build-Up

### Step 1: Simple Tension
$$\sigma_x = E \cdot \varepsilon_x \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}$$

### Step 2: Effect of Lateral Stresses (Poisson Effect)
If only $\sigma_y$ acts on a cube, it produces an axial strain $\sigma_y/E$ **in the y-direction** and a lateral (x-direction) strain of $-\nu \sigma_y / E$ due to Poisson contraction.

### Step 3: Superposition for 3D
By the **principle of superposition**, the total strain in any direction is the algebraic sum of strains caused by each stress acting independently.

### Step 4: Shear Behaviour
Pure shear stresses cause only **angular distortion**, no volumetric change. The relation is independent of $\nu$:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{yz} = \frac{\tau_{yz}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{zx} = \frac{\tau_{zx}}{G}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD ‚ÄĒ Memorize)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** the KTU examiner can ask from this topic. Master these thoroughly.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | Uniaxial Hooke's Law | Pa |
| 2 | $\tau = G\gamma$ | Shear Hooke's Law | Pa |
| 3 | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{axial}}}$ | Poisson's Ratio definition | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_x = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$ | Generalized strain (x) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_y = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$ | Generalized strain (y) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_z = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$ | Generalized strain (z) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\sigma_x = \lambda(\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z) + 2G\varepsilon_x$ | Generalized stress (x) | Pa |
| 8 | $\sigma_y = \lambda(\varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_z) + 2G\varepsilon_y$ | Generalized stress (y) | Pa |
| 9 | $\sigma_z = \lambda(\varepsilon_z + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y) + 2G\varepsilon_z$ | Generalized stress (z) | Pa |
| 10 | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | E‚ÄďG‚ÄďőĹ relationship | Pa |
| 11 | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | E‚ÄďK‚ÄďőĹ relationship | Pa |
| 12 | $E = \dfrac{9KG}{3K+G}$ | All-moduli relationship | Pa |
| 13 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\nu E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$ | Lam√©'s constant | Pa |
| 14 | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z}{3}$ | Hydrostatic / Mean stress | Pa |
| 15 | $e = \varepsilon_x+\varepsilon_y+\varepsilon_z$ | Volumetric strain | dimensionless |
| 16 | $K = -\dfrac{\sigma_h}{e}$ | Bulk Modulus definition | Pa |
| 17 | $p = \dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Axial stress (uniaxial) | Pa |
| 18 | $\delta L = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$ | Elongation of uniform bar | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick:** Formula (10) ‚ÄĒ $G = E/2(1+\nu)$ ‚ÄĒ is the **single most tested** derived relation in KTU exams. You must be able to derive it from the generalized 3D Hooke's Law and a pure-shear stress element.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Use of Hooke's Law |
|---|---|
| **Design of RCC beams** | Predict deflection under service load (working stress method). |
| **Tall building columns** | Estimate shortening due to axial load. |
| **Pre-stressed concrete** | Calculate pre-stress losses due to elastic shortening. |
| **Bridge cables & tie-rods** | Predict elongation for proper sag/tension setting. |
| **Machine tool frames** | Avoid excess elastic deflection that affects accuracy. |
| **Soil mechanics (small strains)** | Linear elastic models for initial settlement. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Mandate:** Derivations carry **7 marks each** in Part B. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly below ‚ÄĒ no step is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Generalized Hooke's Law (Triaxial Stress State)

### Given
A small cubic element of an isotropic, homogeneous, linearly elastic material is subjected to three mutually perpendicular normal stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\sigma_z$ (see diagram in Section 4). The shear strains are independent of the normal stresses for an isotropic material.

### To Prove
The generalized strain equations:
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

### Proof (using Principle of Superposition)

**Case (i): Only $\sigma_x$ is applied** (all other stresses are zero).

By simple uniaxial Hooke's Law:
$$\varepsilon_x' = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}$$

Due to Poisson's effect, the lateral strains in the y and z directions are:
$$\varepsilon_y' = -\nu \cdot \frac{\sigma_x}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_z' = -\nu \cdot \frac{\sigma_x}{E}$$

**Case (ii): Only $\sigma_y$ is applied.**
$$\varepsilon_x'' = -\nu \cdot \frac{\sigma_y}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_y'' = \frac{\sigma_y}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_z'' = -\nu \cdot \frac{\sigma_y}{E}$$

**Case (iii): Only $\sigma_z$ is applied.**
$$\varepsilon_x''' = -\nu \cdot \frac{\sigma_z}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_y''' = -\nu \cdot \frac{\sigma_z}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_z''' = \frac{\sigma_z}{E}$$

**Superposition (Linear Elasticity Assumption):**
Total strain in the x-direction is the algebraic sum of all three cases:

$$\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_x' + \varepsilon_x'' + \varepsilon_x'''$$

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E} + \left(-\nu \frac{\sigma_y}{E}\right) + \left(-\nu \frac{\sigma_z}{E}\right)$$

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right] \quad \blacksquare$$

By symmetry of the procedure:
$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right] \quad \blacksquare$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right] \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relation $G = E / 2(1+\nu)$

### Given
A small element is subjected to a state of **pure shear** $\tau_{xy}$ on its faces (with complementary shear $\tau_{yx} = \tau_{xy}$). All normal stresses are zero.

### To Prove
$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

### Proof

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Consider the principal stresses.**

The principal stresses for a pure shear element are obtained by Mohr's circle (or by rotating the element 45¬į):

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau_{xy}, \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau_{xy}, \quad \sigma_3 = 0$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute the principal strains using generalized Hooke's Law** (with $\sigma_3 = 0$ for plane stress):

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau_{xy} - \nu(-\tau_{xy} + 0)\right] = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_2 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left[-\tau_{xy} - \nu(\tau_{xy} + 0)\right] = -\frac{\tau_{xy}}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute the shear strain from principal strains.**

The engineering shear strain is the **change in angle** between two originally perpendicular fibers. For a pure shear state:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Substitute the principal strains.**

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{E}(1 + \nu) - \left[-\frac{\tau_{xy}}{E}(1 + \nu)\right]$$

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Compare with the shear Hooke's Law** $\tau_{xy} = G \gamma_{xy}$:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G} = \frac{2\tau_{xy}(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Equate and simplify.**

$$\frac{1}{G} = \frac{2(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Volumetric Strain under Hydrostatic Pressure

### Given
An element is subjected to equal hydrostatic pressure $p$ on all faces:
$$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$$

### To Prove
$$e = \frac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E} \quad \text{and} \quad K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$

### Proof

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Sum the three normal strains** (volumetric strain for small strains is $e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$):

$$e = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right] + \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right] + \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Group the terms.**

$$e = \frac{1}{E}\left[(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z) - 2\nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$e = \frac{1}{E}(1 - 2\nu)(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Substitute hydrostatic stresses.**

$$e = \frac{1}{E}(1 - 2\nu)(-3p) = -\frac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

The negative sign indicates volume **decreases** under pressure (compression is conventionally positive here; the magnitude is):

$$e = \frac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Use the definition of Bulk Modulus.**

By definition:
$$K = -\frac{p}{e} = -\frac{p}{\frac{-3p(1-2\nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$

$$\boxed{K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example (Uniaxial Loading) ‚ÄĒ KTU Style

> **Problem:** A mild steel rod of length 2 m and diameter 25 mm is subjected to an axial tensile load of 100 kN. Find the stress, strain, and elongation. Take $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$.

### Step 1: Cross-sectional Area

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(25 \times 10^{-3})^2$$

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2: Axial Stress

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{100 \times 10^3}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\sigma = 203.72 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 = 203.72 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 3: Axial Strain (Hooke's Law)

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{203.72 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9}$$

$$\varepsilon = 1.0186 \times 10^{-3} \;\; (\text{dimensionless})$$

### Step 4: Elongation

$$\delta L = \varepsilon \cdot L = 1.0186 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 = 2.037 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\delta L = 2.037 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mark Distribution:** Stress = 1 mark, Strain = 1 mark, Elongation = 2 marks, Diagram (if any) = 1 mark, Final boxed answer = 1 mark. Always show **units** explicitly.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example (Multiaxial + Poisson's Ratio) ‚ÄĒ KTU Style

> **Problem:** A brass cube of side 40 mm is subjected to stresses $\sigma_x = 60$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, and $\sigma_z = 20$ MPa. Find the strain in each direction and the change in volume. Take $E = 100$ GPa, $\nu = 0.32$.

### Step 1: Apply Generalized Hooke's Law

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^9}\left[60 \times 10^6 - 0.32(40 + 20) \times 10^6\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^9}\left[60 - 19.2\right] \times 10^6 = \frac{40.8 \times 10^6}{100 \times 10^9}$$

$$\varepsilon_x = 4.08 \times 10^{-4}$$

### Step 2: Similarly compute $\varepsilon_y$ and $\varepsilon_z$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^9}\left[40 - 0.32(60 + 20)\right] \times 10^6$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^9}\left[40 - 25.6\right] \times 10^6 = \frac{14.4 \times 10^6}{100 \times 10^9}$$

$$\varepsilon_y = 1.44 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^9}\left[20 - 0.32(60 + 40)\right] \times 10^6$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^9}\left[20 - 32\right] \times 10^6 = -\frac{12 \times 10^6}{100 \times 10^9}$$

$$\varepsilon_z = -1.2 \times 10^{-4}$$

### Step 3: Volumetric Strain

$$e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = (4.08 + 1.44 - 1.2) \times 10^{-4}$$

$$e = 4.32 \times 10^{-4}$$

### Step 4: Change in Volume

Original volume $V = (40 \text{ mm})^3 = 64000 \text{ mm}^3$

$$\Delta V = e \cdot V = 4.32 \times 10^{-4} \times 64000 = 27.648 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V = 27.65 \text{ mm}^3}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Generalized Hooke's Law Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA["INPUT STRESS SIGMA X"]

    inA --> proc2

    outD --> outE
```

> **Interpretation:** The diagram models the **3D Generalized Hooke's Law** as a computational block. Three normal stresses enter the **Superposition Engine**; the Poisson Coupling Matrix accounts for lateral coupling. The system outputs three normal strains, a volumetric strain, and allows derivation of secondary elastic constants $G$ and $K$.

---

```
            +Z  ŌÉ_z (downward on top face)
             |

        +-----------+
```

**Key notation:**
- Each face of the cube experiences a normal stress perpendicular to it.
- Positive sign = **tensile**; negative sign = **compressive**.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram ‚ÄĒ Elastic Constants Network (E, G, K, őĹ)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    eNode["YOUNGS MODULUS E"]
```

> **Interpretation:** This network visualizes the inter-dependency of the four classical elastic constants ($E$, $G$, $K$, $\nu$) plus Lam√©'s constant $\lambda$. For an isotropic material, **any two** of these constants (typically $E$ and $\nu$) completely determine the elastic response.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**State Hooke's Law. Define Young's Modulus and Poisson's Ratio with their SI units.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Hooke's Law:** Within the proportional limit, the stress in a body is directly proportional to the strain produced, i.e., $\sigma \propto \varepsilon$ or $\sigma = E\varepsilon$.
>
> **Young's Modulus (E):** The ratio of longitudinal stress to longitudinal strain within the elastic limit. It represents the stiffness of a material.
> $$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}, \quad \text{Units: N/m}^2 \text{ or Pa (GPa in practice)}$$
>
> **Poisson's Ratio (őĹ):** The ratio of lateral strain to axial strain (with a negative sign to account for opposite signs).
> $$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{axial}}}, \quad \text{Units: dimensionless}$$
>
> *(1 mark ‚ÄĒ Statement of Hooke's Law; 1 mark ‚ÄĒ Definition + units of E; 1 mark ‚ÄĒ Definition + units of őĹ)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**Derive the relationship $G = E / 2(1+\nu)$ between the elastic constants.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Consider a pure shear element with shear stress $\tau$ and shear strain $\gamma$.

The principal stresses are: $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$.

Using generalized Hooke's Law:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2] = \frac{\tau}{E}(1+\nu)$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_2 - \nu \sigma_1] = -\frac{\tau}{E}(1+\nu)$$

The shear strain is the change in the right angle:

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

From shear Hooke's Law, $\tau = G\gamma$, hence:

$$\frac{1}{G} = \frac{2(1+\nu)}{E} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

*(1 mark ‚ÄĒ Principal stresses; 1 mark ‚ÄĒ Strain computation; 1 mark ‚ÄĒ Final boxed result)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Select Either A or B)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, sub-divided into (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Solve **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B (full marks to either).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Derive the generalized Hooke's Law for a 3D element subjected to triaxial stresses. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement (1 mark):** For an isotropic, homogeneous, linearly elastic material subjected to triaxial stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_z$, the normal strains are obtained by applying the principle of superposition to three uniaxial stress cases.

**Step 1:** When only $\sigma_x$ acts:
$$\varepsilon_x^{(1)} = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_y^{(1)} = -\frac{\nu \sigma_x}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_z^{(1)} = -\frac{\nu \sigma_x}{E}$$

**Step 2:** When only $\sigma_y$ acts:
$$\varepsilon_x^{(2)} = -\frac{\nu \sigma_y}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_y^{(2)} = \frac{\sigma_y}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_z^{(2)} = -\frac{\nu \sigma_y}{E}$$

**Step 3:** When only $\sigma_z$ acts:
$$\varepsilon_x^{(3)} = -\frac{\nu \sigma_z}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_y^{(3)} = -\frac{\nu \sigma_z}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_z^{(3)} = \frac{\sigma_z}{E}$$

**Step 4 (Superposition ‚ÄĒ 3 marks for algebra):**
$$\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_x^{(1)} + \varepsilon_x^{(2)} + \varepsilon_x^{(3)} = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

**Step 5 (Shear ‚ÄĒ 1 mark):** The shear strains are independent of normal stresses for isotropic materials:
$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{yz} = \frac{\tau_{yz}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{zx} = \frac{\tau_{zx}}{G}$$

**Final Statement (1 mark):** The above six equations constitute the **Generalized Hooke's Law** for a 3D stress state in an isotropic elastic material.

---

#### **(b) An aluminium rod 1.5 m long and 20 mm in diameter is subjected to an axial tensile load of 30 kN. Find the stress, strain, elongation, and the lateral contraction. Take $E = 70$ GPa and $\nu = 0.33$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Cross-sectional area (1 mark):**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(20 \times 10^{-3})^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(4 \times 10^{-4}) = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Axial stress (1 mark):**
$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{30 \times 10^3}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 95.49 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 95.49 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Axial strain by Hooke's Law (1 mark):**
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{95.49 \times 10^6}{70 \times 10^9} = 1.364 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Elongation (1 mark):**
$$\delta L = \varepsilon_x \cdot L = 1.364 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5 = 2.046 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$
$$\boxed{\delta L = 2.046 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Lateral strain (Poisson's effect) (1 mark):**
$$\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_x = -0.33 \times 1.364 \times 10^{-3} = -4.502 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Lateral contraction (1 mark):**
$$\delta d = \varepsilon_{\text{lateral}} \cdot d = -4.502 \times 10^{-4} \times 20 = -9.00 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}$$
$$\boxed{\delta d = 0.009 \text{ mm (contraction)}}$$

**Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Final tabulation (1 mark):**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Stress $\sigma$ | 95.49 MPa |
| Strain $\varepsilon_x$ | $1.364 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| Elongation $\delta L$ | 2.046 mm |
| Lateral contraction $\delta d$ | 0.009 mm |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) A steel bar of 1.2 m length, 25 mm width, and 12 mm thickness is subjected to an axial pull of 80 kN in a Universal Testing Machine (UTM). The bar extends by 0.45 mm and its width decreases by $8.5 \times 10^{-3}$ mm. Determine Young's Modulus, Poisson's Ratio, and the Modulus of Rigidity. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Cross-sectional area (1 mark):**
$$A = 25 \times 12 = 300 \text{ mm}^2 = 300 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Axial stress (1 mark):**
$$\sigma_x = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{80 \times 10^3}{300 \times 10^{-6}} = 266.67 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 266.67 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Axial strain (1 mark):**
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\delta L}{L} = \frac{0.45 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2} = 3.75 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Young's Modulus (1 mark):**
$$E = \frac{\sigma_x}{\varepsilon_x} = \frac{266.67 \times 10^6}{3.75 \times 10^{-4}}$$
$$\boxed{E = 7.111 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa} = 711.11 \text{ GPa}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> *(The very high value suggests experimental error in the UTM test ‚ÄĒ for real mild steel, $E \approx 200$ GPa. The KTU examiner still awards full marks if the calculation is correct.)*

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Lateral strain (1 mark):**
$$\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}} = \frac{\delta w}{w} = \frac{8.5 \times 10^{-3}}{25} = 3.4 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Poisson's Ratio (1 mark):**
$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}}}{\varepsilon_x} = -\frac{3.4 \times 10^{-4}}{3.75 \times 10^{-4}}$$
$$\boxed{\nu = 0.907}$$

**Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Modulus of Rigidity (1 mark):**
$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{711.11 \times 10^9}{2(1 + 0.907)}$$
$$\boxed{G = 186.27 \text{ GPa}}$$

---

#### **(b) A solid circular shaft of diameter 60 mm and length 1.0 m is subjected to a torque of 5 kN¬∑m. If the modulus of rigidity is 80 GPa, find: (i) the maximum shear stress, (ii) the angle of twist, and (iii) the shear strain at the surface. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $d = 60$ mm = 0.06 m, $L = 1.0$ m, $T = 5$ kN¬∑m = $5 \times 10^3$ N¬∑m, $G = 80$ GPa = $80 \times 10^9$ Pa.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Polar moment of inertia (1 mark):**
$$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.06)^4}{32}$$
$$J = \frac{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 1.272 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Maximum shear stress at the surface (2 marks):**
$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{J}$$
$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{5 \times 10^3 \times 0.03}{1.272 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{150}{1.272 \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} = 117.92 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 117.92 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Angle of twist (2 marks):**
$$\theta = \frac{TL}{GJ} = \frac{5 \times 10^3 \times 1.0}{80 \times 10^9 \times 1.272 \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$\theta = \frac{5 \times 10^3}{1.0176 \times 10^5} = 0.04914 \text{ rad}$$
$$\boxed{\theta = 0.04914 \text{ rad} = 2.815¬į}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Shear strain at the surface (2 marks):**

From shear Hooke's Law: $\tau = G\gamma$

$$\gamma = \frac{\tau_{\max}}{G} = \frac{117.92 \times 10^6}{80 \times 10^9}$$
$$\boxed{\gamma = 1.474 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS ‚ÄĒ Stress-Strain / Hooke's Law Problems**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (‚Äď2 marks typical):** Mixing mm, cm, m, and N, kN without conversion. Always convert to **N and m** for stress in Pa.
> 2. **Sign of Poisson's Ratio (‚Äď1 mark):** Poisson's ratio is **positive by definition**; the negative sign in the formula already accounts for the contraction. Do **not** write a negative answer.
> 3. **Forgetting the Poisson coupling terms in 3D problems (‚Äď2 marks):** In a triaxial problem, the strain in x depends on $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ also. Missing the $\nu$ term is a common, expensive mistake.
> 4. **Confusing the limit of Hooke's Law (‚Äď1 mark):** Never apply $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ beyond the **proportional limit** (e.g., in a yield or plastic region). State the assumption explicitly.
> 5. **Wrong formula for shear modulus (‚Äď3 marks):** If asked to "derive $G = E/2(1+\nu)$," writing it without proof is treated as a **definition**, not a derivation. The **principal stress method** shown in Section 3.2 must be reproduced step-by-step.
> 6. **Forgetting the complementary shear (‚Äď1 mark):** $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$ in a 2D problem. If the problem says shear force, do not omit the complementary face.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision ‚ÄĒ Read this 5 times before the exam.**

### **Core Concepts**
- [ ] Hooke's Law: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ ‚ÄĒ valid only within the **proportional (elastic) limit**.
- [ ] Shear form: $\tau = G\gamma$ ‚ÄĒ valid for **pure shear** loading.
- [ ] Poisson's ratio: $\nu = -\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}} / \varepsilon_{\text{axial}}$ ‚ÄĒ dimensionless, $0 < \nu < 0.5$ for stable materials.
- [ ] **Volumetric strain** under hydrostatic pressure: $e = 3p(1-2\nu)/E$.

### **Critical Formula Triad (Most Tested)**
- [ ] $G = E / 2(1+\nu)$
- [ ] $K = E / 3(1-2\nu)$
- [ ] $\lambda = \nu E / [(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)]$

### **Generalized Hooke's Law (3D)**
- [ ] $\varepsilon_x = (1/E)[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)]$ (and cyclic permutations).
- [ ] $\sigma_x = \lambda(\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z) + 2G\varepsilon_x$ (and cyclic).

### **Problem-Solving Workflow**
- [ ] **Step 1:** Convert all units to **SI (N, m, Pa)**.
- [ ] **Step 2:** Draw the **free body diagram / stress element**.
- [ ] **Step 3:** Identify if uniaxial, biaxial (plane stress), or triaxial.
- [ ] **Step 4:** Apply the appropriate form of Hooke's Law.
- [ ] **Step 5:** Show **all** intermediate algebra (KTU awards process marks).
- [ ] **Step 6:** Box the final numerical answer with **explicit units**.

### **Numerical Values to Memorize (Standard KTU Data)**
- [ ] Mild Steel: $E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$, $G = 77$ GPa.
- [ ] Concrete: $E = 25$ GPa, $\nu = 0.20$.
- [ ] Aluminium: $E = 70$ GPa, $\nu = 0.33$.

### **Common Trap Zones**
- [ ] A material with $\nu = 0.5$ is **incompressible** (rubber-like). $K \to \infty$ at $\nu = 0.5$.
- [ ] A material with $\nu = 0$ has **no lateral strain** (cork-like).
- [ ] Always check whether the question is asking for **stress, strain, elongation**, or **lateral contraction** ‚ÄĒ they are different quantities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mantra for KTU 2024 Exam:** *"If the question has more than one normal stress, the strain in any direction is the result of ALL three stresses, not just the one in that direction ‚ÄĒ don't forget the Poisson coupling!"*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Young's Modulus of Elasticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Young's Modulus of Elasticity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Young's Modulus of Elasticity** (denoted by the symbol **E**) is defined as the ratio of longitudinal (axial) stress to longitudinal strain within the linear elastic limit of a material. It quantifies the inherent stiffness of a solid material when subjected to uniaxial tensile or compressive loading along a single axis.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$E = \frac{\text{Longitudinal Stress } (\sigma)}{\text{Longitudinal Strain } (\varepsilon)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Young's Modulus is a *material property* (not a structural property) and remains constant for a given homogeneous, isotropic material *only* within the proportional limit of its stress‚Äďstrain curve. It has the same units as stress: **N/m¬≤** or **Pascal (Pa)**.

The formal definition recognized by KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCET205) follows the framework of classical linear elasticity (Hookean behaviour), where the relationship between stress and strain is linear, reversible, and path-independent for small deformations.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The Spring Intuition

Imagine stretching a **steel spring** versus a **rubber band** using the same pulling force.

- The **rubber band** stretches a lot (it is "compliant" ‚ÄĒ low stiffness).
- The **steel spring** stretches very little (it is "stiff" ‚ÄĒ high stiffness).

Even though both undergo the same *force*, the *amount they stretch* is different. The material that resists deformation more has a **higher Young's Modulus**. Steel has E ‚Čą **200 GPa**, while rubber has E ‚Čą **0.01 GPa** ‚ÄĒ that's a 20,000-fold difference in stiffness!

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Explanation:** Young's Modulus tells you "how much a material pushes back per unit stretch." A higher E means the material is *stiffer* and deforms less under the same stress. A lower E means the material is *softer* and deforms more.

### 1.3 Key Terminology ‚ÄĒ The Foundations

Before diving deeper, three foundational quantities must be crystal clear:

| Term | Symbol | Definition | Unit |
|------|--------|------------|------|
| **Longitudinal Stress** | ŌÉ | Axial force per unit cross-sectional area: ŌÉ = P/A | N/m¬≤ (Pa) |
| **Longitudinal Strain** | őĶ | Change in length per unit original length: őĶ = őīL/L | Dimensionless |
| **Young's Modulus** | E | Ratio of longitudinal stress to longitudinal strain | N/m¬≤ (Pa) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (Examiner's Favourite Trap):** Stress has units, but **strain is dimensionless**. Therefore, Young's Modulus inherits the units of stress. Many students incorrectly write E in N/m or N/mm ‚ÄĒ avoid this!

---

### 1.4 Physical Significance of Young's Modulus

The value of E reflects the *interatomic bond stiffness* within the material. In atomic terms:

- **Covalent/Metallic bonds** ‚Üí very high E (steel, diamond, tungsten)
- **Ionic bonds** ‚Üí moderate E (ceramics, alumina)
- **Weak Van der Waals bonds** ‚Üí very low E (polymers, foams)

This is why a **carbon nanotube** (E ‚Čą 1 TPa) is so extraordinarily stiff ‚ÄĒ its carbon atoms are locked in a perfect hexagonal lattice of covalent bonds.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Principles ‚ÄĒ The Logic Chain

Young's Modulus emerges from three sequential concepts:

1. **Stress Generation (Newton's Third Law):** When an axial force *P* is applied to a bar of cross-section *A*, the internal resisting force per unit area is ŌÉ = P/A.
2. **Strain Accumulation (Geometric Effect):** The same force causes the bar to elongate by őīL, producing strain őĶ = őīL/L.
3. **Linear Material Response (Hooke's Law):** For most engineering materials up to the proportional limit, stress and strain are linearly related via a constant ‚ÄĒ that constant is **E**.

---

### 2.2 Hooke's Law ‚ÄĒ The Governing Principle

Robert Hooke (1676) stated that within the elastic limit, the force required to deform a spring is proportional to the displacement:

$$F \propto x \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = kx$$

In the context of material mechanics, this translates to:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Within the Elastic Limit"?** Beyond this limit, microstructural damage (dislocation motion in metals, microcracking in ceramics) causes permanent deformation. Hooke's Law fails, and E is no longer a meaningful constant.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet ‚ÄĒ High-Yield Equations

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Axial (longitudinal) stress | N/m¬≤ |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\delta L}{L}$ | Axial (longitudinal) strain | dimensionless |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{PL}{A\,\delta L}$ | Young's Modulus (core definition) | N/m¬≤ |
| 4 | $\delta L = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$ | Elongation of a uniform bar under axial load | m |
| 5 | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | Relation between E, Shear Modulus (G), Poisson's Ratio (őĹ) | N/m¬≤ |
| 6 | $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ | Relation between E, Bulk Modulus (K), Poisson's Ratio (őĹ) | N/m¬≤ |
| 7 | $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{yield}}}{\text{FoS}}$ | Allowable design stress (from E-based yield criterion) | N/m¬≤ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on absolute values:** When writing $\vert P \vert$ or $\vert \delta L \vert$ in any answer, use LaTeX \vert or \mid ‚ÄĒ **never** the bare pipe symbol inside markdown tables, as it breaks table syntax.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Young's Modulus governs countless civil engineering applications:

- **Design of suspension bridges:** Cable elongation under traffic load must be predicted using E.
- **High-rise buildings:** Column shortening due to cumulative axial loads (őīL = PL/AE) affects floor-to-floor alignment.
- **Pre-stressed concrete:** Tendons are pre-tensioned; the resulting elastic elongation governs prestress loss.
- **Piping systems:** Thermal expansion (modeled via E) determines expansion joint design.
- **Railway tracks:** Rail deflection under wheel loads is computed using E to ensure passenger comfort.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Insight:** Civil engineers rarely use Young's Modulus in *isolation*. It is combined with geometry (I, A, L) to form **flexural rigidity (EI)** and **axial rigidity (AE)** ‚ÄĒ the two most important stiffness parameters in structural analysis.

---

### 2.5 The Three Elastic Constants ‚ÄĒ Interconnection

A complete elastic characterization of an isotropic material requires **three independent constants**:

- **E** ‚ÄĒ Young's Modulus (uniaxial stiffness)
- **G** ‚ÄĒ Shear Modulus or Modulus of Rigidity (shape-change resistance)
- **K** ‚ÄĒ Bulk Modulus (volume-change resistance)
- **őĹ** ‚ÄĒ Poisson's Ratio (lateral contraction per unit axial extension)

For a homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic material, **only two are independent**. KTU expects students to know the two master relations:

$$E = 2G(1+\nu) \qquad \text{and} \qquad E = 3K(1-2\nu)$$
