# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024, Model]`

**Q:** Define *axial stress* and *axial strain*. State the relationship between them for a linearly elastic material. What are the SI units of each?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Axial Stress ($\sigma$):** The intensity of internal resisting force per unit area acting normal to a cross-section, when the bar is subjected to an axial load. It is given by $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$, where $P$ is the internal axial force (N) and $A$ is the cross-sectional area (m¬≤). **SI unit: N/m¬≤ or Pascal (Pa).** Commonly used sub-units: **kPa**, **MPa**, **GPa**.
- **Axial Strain ($\varepsilon$):** The ratio of change in length (deformation $\delta$) to the original length $L$, i.e., $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\delta}{L}$. **SI unit: dimensionless (no unit).** Often expressed in micro-strain ($\mu\varepsilon$).
- **Relationship (Hooke's Law, valid in the linear elastic range):** $\sigma = E\varepsilon$, where $E$ is the **Young's Modulus** (modulus of elasticity), in **N/m¬≤ (Pa)**. Hence, $\varepsilon = \dfrac{P}{AE}$ for a uniform bar.

**Mark Split (Board Pattern):** Definition of stress ‚ÄĒ 1 mark; Definition of strain ‚ÄĒ 1 mark; Hooke's law with unit of E ‚ÄĒ 1 mark.

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Q:** Differentiate between a bar of *uniform cross-section* and a bar of *uniformly varying cross-section* with respect to (i) the variation of stress along the length, and (ii) the formula for total elongation under an axial load $P$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Uniform Cross-Section Bar | Uniformly Varying Cross-Section Bar |
|--------|---------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Area variation | $A$ = constant along $L$ | $A(x) = A_1 + (A_2 - A_1)\dfrac{x}{L}$ |
| Stress variation | $\sigma = P/A$ ‚ÄĒ **constant** | $\sigma(x) = P/A(x)$ ‚ÄĒ **varies** along $L$ |
| Strain variation | $\varepsilon = P/AE$ ‚ÄĒ constant | $\varepsilon(x) = P/[A(x)E]$ ‚ÄĒ varies |
| Total elongation | $\delta = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$ | $\delta = \dfrac{PL}{E(A_2 - A_1)}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$ |
| Stress concentration | None ‚ÄĒ uniform | Maximum at smaller end ($A_1$) |

**Mark Split:** Identifying variation of stress in each ‚ÄĒ 1.5 marks; Writing the correct elongation formula in each ‚ÄĒ 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚ÄĒ Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B Module-Consolidated questions carry **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. Each 7-mark sub-question should ideally be a *full problem with sub-parts* and require a 2‚Äď3 page solution. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are designed as independent alternative choices (Module-1 internal choice pattern).

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď July 2024, Model]`

**Q(A)(a)** A stepped steel bar is composed of three segments connected in series. Segment 1: length $300\text{ mm}$, diameter $30\text{ mm}$. Segment 2: length $400\text{ mm}$, diameter $20\text{ mm}$. Segment 3: length $300\text{ mm}$, diameter $40\text{ mm}$. An axial tensile load of $60\text{ kN}$ is applied at the free end. Take $E = 200\text{ GPa}$ throughout. Determine: (i) the stress in each segment, (ii) the strain in each segment, (iii) the total elongation of the bar. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $P = 60\text{ kN} = 60 \times 10^3\text{ N}$ (constant through all segments; no intermediate loads)
- $E = 200\text{ GPa} = 2 \times 10^5\text{ N/mm}^2$
- Segment 1: $L_1 = 300\text{ mm}$, $d_1 = 30\text{ mm}$
- Segment 2: $L_2 = 400\text{ mm}$, $d_2 = 20\text{ mm}$
- Segment 3: $L_3 = 300\text{ mm}$, $d_3 = 40\text{ mm}$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Compute cross-sectional areas:**

$$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}d_1^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(30)^2 = 225\pi \approx 706.86\text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}d_2^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 = 100\pi \approx 314.16\text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_3 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}d_3^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(40)^2 = 400\pi \approx 1256.64\text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Stress in each segment ($\sigma_i = P/A_i$):**

$$\sigma_1 = \dfrac{60 \times 10^3}{706.86} \approx 84.88\text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \dfrac{60 \times 10^3}{314.16} \approx 190.99\text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_3 = \dfrac{60 \times 10^3}{1256.64} \approx 47.75\text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Strain in each segment ($\varepsilon_i = \sigma_i / E$):**

$$\varepsilon_1 = \dfrac{84.88}{2 \times 10^5} \approx 4.244 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \dfrac{190.99}{2 \times 10^5} \approx 9.549 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\varepsilon_3 = \dfrac{47.75}{2 \times 10^5} \approx 2.387 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Elongation of each segment ($\delta_i = P L_i / (A_i E)$):**

$$\delta_1 = \dfrac{(60 \times 10^3)(300)}{(706.86)(2 \times 10^5)} = \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^7}{1.4137 \times 10^8} \approx 0.1273\text{ mm}$$

$$\delta_2 = \dfrac{(60 \times 10^3)(400)}{(314.16)(2 \times 10^5)} = \dfrac{2.4 \times 10^7}{6.283 \times 10^7} \approx 0.3820\text{ mm}$$

$$\delta_3 = \dfrac{(60 \times 10^3)(300)}{(1256.64)(2 \times 10^5)} = \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^7}{2.513 \times 10^8} \approx 0.0716\text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Total elongation (compatibility):**

$$\delta_{\text{total}} = \delta_1 + \delta_2 + \delta_3 = 0.1273 + 0.3820 + 0.0716 \approx 0.5809\text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\;\delta_{\text{total}} \approx 0.581\text{ mm}\;}$$

**Critical Observation:** Segment 2 has the **smallest area** and therefore the **highest stress** ($\sigma_2 \approx 191\text{ MPa}$). If the allowable stress for steel is, say, $165\text{ MPa}$ (as per IS 800 for mild steel), this segment would **fail** in service ‚ÄĒ a key design takeaway.

---

**Q(A)(b)** A vertical steel rod of length $1.5\text{ m}$, uniform cross-section $A = 600\text{ mm}^2$, and $E = 200\text{ GPa}$, hangs from a rigid support and carries a $12\text{ kN}$ weight at its lower end. Find: (i) the stress in the rod, (ii) the total elongation, (iii) the strain energy stored in the rod. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $L = 1.5\text{ m} = 1500\text{ mm}$, $A = 600\text{ mm}^2$, $E = 2 \times 10^5\text{ N/mm}^2$, $P = 12\text{ kN} = 12 \times 10^3\text{ N}$.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Stress in the rod (uniform):**

$$\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{12 \times 10^3}{600} = 20\text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Strain (Hooke's Law):**

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E} = \dfrac{20}{2 \times 10^5} = 1 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Elongation:**

$$\delta = \dfrac{PL}{AE} = \dfrac{(12 \times 10^3)(1500)}{(600)(2 \times 10^5)} = \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^7}{1.2 \times 10^8} = 0.15\text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Strain energy stored:**

$$U = \dfrac{1}{2} P\,\delta = \dfrac{1}{2}(12 \times 10^3)(0.15) = 900\text{ N¬∑mm} = 0.9\text{ J}$$

**Cross-verification using $U = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2E}\cdot V$:**

Volume $V = A \cdot L = 600 \times 1500 = 9 \times 10^5\text{ mm}^3$

$$U = \dfrac{(20)^2}{2 \times 2 \times 10^5} \times 9 \times 10^5 = \dfrac{400}{4 \times 10^5} \times 9 \times 10^5 = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 9 \times 10^5 = 900\text{ N¬∑mm} = 0.9\text{ J} \;\checkmark$$

$$\boxed{\;\sigma = 20\text{ MPa},\quad \delta = 0.15\text{ mm},\quad U = 0.9\text{ J}\;}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, Model]`

**Q(B)(a)** A bar of length $L = 500\text{ mm}$ has a circular cross-section whose **diameter varies linearly** from $d_1 = 20\text{ mm}$ at the top end to $d_2 = 40\text{ mm}$ at the bottom end. The bar is subjected to an axial tensile load of $P = 30\text{ kN}$. Take $E = 200\text{ GPa}$. Determine: (i) the elongation of the bar, (ii) the maximum and minimum stresses in the bar, and (iii) the average stress. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $L = 500\text{ mm}$, $d_1 = 20\text{ mm}$, $d_2 = 40\text{ mm}$, $P = 30 \times 10^3\text{ N}$, $E = 2 \times 10^5\text{ N/mm}^2$.

**Important Note:** Since the *diameter* is linear in $x$, the *area* is **quadratic** in $x$. Therefore, we must use the *diameter-linear* derivation, not the area-linear formula.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Cross-sectional areas at the two ends:**

$$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(d_1)^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 = 100\pi \approx 314.16\text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(d_2)^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(40)^2 = 400\pi \approx 1256.64\text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Elongation (using the diameter-linear formula from Section 3.2):**

$$\delta = \dfrac{4PL}{\pi E\, d_1 d_2} = \dfrac{4 \times (30 \times 10^3) \times 500}{\pi \times (2 \times 10^5) \times 20 \times 40}$$

Numerator: $4 \times 30{,}000 \times 500 = 6 \times 10^7$.
Denominator: $\pi \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 800 = \pi \times 1.6 \times 10^8 \approx 5.0265 \times 10^8$.

$$\delta = \dfrac{6 \times 10^7}{5.0265 \times 10^8} \approx 0.1194\text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\;\delta \approx 0.1194\text{ mm}\;}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Maximum stress (at smaller end, $A_1$):**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{P}{A_1} = \dfrac{30 \times 10^3}{314.16} \approx 95.49\text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Minimum stress (at larger end, $A_2$):**

$$\sigma_{\min} = \dfrac{P}{A_2} = \dfrac{30 \times 10^3}{1256.64} \approx 23.87\text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Average stress (for cross-check, NOT a simple average of areas ‚ÄĒ it is the *volume-weighted* average for energy equivalence):**

The simple linear-area-based average: $\sigma_{\text{avg,simple}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\max} + \sigma_{\min}}{2} = \dfrac{95.49 + 23.87}{2} \approx 59.68\text{ MPa}$.

A more accurate *strain-energy-equivalent* average stress:

$$\sigma_{\text{avg,U}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2EU}{V}}$$

where $U = \dfrac{P\delta}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2}(30{,}000)(0.1194) = 1791\text{ N¬∑mm}$ and $V = \dfrac{\pi L}{12}(d_1^2 + d_1 d_2 + d_2^2) = \dfrac{\pi \times 500}{12}(400 + 800 + 1600) = \dfrac{\pi \times 500 \times 2800}{12} \approx 366{,}519\text{ mm}^3$.

$$\sigma_{\text{avg,U}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 1791}{366{,}519}} = \sqrt{1954.5} \approx 44.21\text{ MPa}$$

**For KTU answer, present the simple arithmetic mean:**

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_{\text{avg}} \approx 59.68\text{ MPa}\;}$$

---

**Q(B)(b)** A composite axial member consists of a steel rod of $20\text{ mm}$ diameter encased concentrically inside a brass tube of outer diameter $40\text{ mm}$. Both steel and brass are bonded at rigid end-plates. The total length of the member is $250\text{ mm}$. If the assembly is subjected to an axial compressive load of $50\text{ kN}$, find the stresses in steel and brass, and the shortening of the assembly. Given: $E_s = 200\text{ GPa}$, $E_b = 100\text{ GPa}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Areas:**

$$A_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 = 100\pi \approx 314.16\text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_b = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(40^2 - 20^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(1600 - 400) = 300\pi \approx 942.48\text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Equilibrium:** Total load shared between the two materials (parallel mechanism):

$$P_s + P_b = P = 50\text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compatibility (key principle for composite/parallel members):**

The steel rod and brass tube must shorten by the **same amount** (since they are bonded to rigid end plates):

$$\delta_s = \delta_b = \delta$$

$$\dfrac{P_s L}{A_s E_s} = \dfrac{P_b L}{A_b E_b}$$

$$\dfrac{P_s}{A_s E_s} = \dfrac{P_b}{A_b E_b} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad P_b = P_s \cdot \dfrac{A_b E_b}{A_s E_s}$$

Compute the stiffness ratio:

$$\dfrac{A_b E_b}{A_s E_s} = \dfrac{(942.48)(100 \times 10^3)}{(314.16)(200 \times 10^3)} = \dfrac{942.48}{628.32} = 1.5$$

So $P_b = 1.5 P_s$.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Solve for $P_s$ and $P_b$:**

$$P_s + 1.5 P_s = 50\text{ kN} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 2.5 P_s = 50 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad P_s = 20\text{ kN}, \quad P_b = 30\text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Stresses:**

$$\sigma_s = \dfrac{P_s}{A_s} = \dfrac{20 \times 10^3}{314.16} \approx 63.66\text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{P_b}{A_b} = \dfrac{30 \times 10^3}{942.48} \approx 31.83\text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Shortening of the assembly:**

$$\delta = \dfrac{P_s L}{A_s E_s} = \dfrac{(20 \times 10^3)(250)}{(314.16)(2 \times 10^5)} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^6}{6.283 \times 10^7} \approx 0.0796\text{ mm}$$

Cross-verify using brass:

$$\delta = \dfrac{P_b L}{A_b E_b} = \dfrac{(30 \times 10^3)(250)}{(942.48)(1 \times 10^5)} = \dfrac{7.5 \times 10^6}{9.425 \times 10^7} \approx 0.0796\text{ mm} \;\checkmark$$

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_s \approx 63.66\text{ MPa},\quad \sigma_b \approx 31.83\text{ MPa},\quad \delta \approx 0.0796\text{ mm}\;}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls ‚ÄĒ Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing diameter-linear and area-linear tapered formulas.** If the problem says *diameter* varies linearly, the area is *quadratic* in $x$ ‚ÄĒ use $\delta = \dfrac{4PL}{\pi E d_1 d_2}$. If the problem says *area* (or *side of square section*) varies linearly, use $\delta = \dfrac{PL}{E(A_2 - A_1)}\ln(A_2/A_1)$. Confusing these two costs **3‚Äď4 marks outright**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units.** $E$ in GPa, $A$ in mm¬≤, $L$ in mm, $P$ in N gives $\delta$ in mm. Mixing $\text{m}$ and $\text{mm}$ is a *very* common blunder.
> 3. **Ignoring intermediate loads** when computing the internal force in stepped bars. Always draw a free body and identify the internal axial force in each segment ‚ÄĒ it may not equal the externally applied load $P$.
> 4. **Sign convention for strain energy.** Use $U = \dfrac{1}{2}P\delta$ for a *gradually applied* load (full work done by external load is $\dfrac{1}{2}P\delta$, not $P\delta$).
> 5. **Composite (parallel) bars** require the **compatibility equation** $\delta_s = \delta_b$, not the *series* formula. KTU frequently tests this distinction.
> 6. **Maximum stress location in a tapered bar** ‚ÄĒ at the *smaller* end (smaller area), not the middle. Stating the wrong location costs 1 mark.
> 7. **Neglecting the L'H√īpital / limiting check** ‚ÄĒ examiners love the verification that the tapered formula reduces to the uniform formula when $A_2 \to A_1$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ KTU Module 1: Axially Loaded Bars**

- **Three pillars of analysis:** Equilibrium $\Rightarrow$ Compatibility $\Rightarrow$ Hooke's Law. Memorize this order; it solves 90% of Mechanics of Solids problems.
- **Uniform prismatic bar elongation:** $\delta = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$. (Memorize; it is the basis of *all* axial-deformation problems.)
- **Stepped bar (series segments):** $\delta = \sum \dfrac{P_i L_i}{A_i E_i}$. Each segment's internal force must be found first.
- **Linearly tapered bar (area-linear):** $\delta = \dfrac{PL}{E(A_2 - A_1)}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$. Note the *natural logarithm* of the area ratio.
- **Linearly tapered bar (diameter-linear):** $\delta = \dfrac{4PL}{\pi E d_1 d_2}$. This is the result for circular cross-sections with *linear diameter* taper.
- **Hooke's Law (linear elastic):** $\sigma = E\varepsilon$. Always valid *only* within the proportional limit (i.e., $\sigma < \sigma_{\text{proportional\ limit}}$).
- **Stress at any section:** $\sigma(x) = \dfrac{N(x)}{A(x)}$, where $N(x)$ is the internal axial force from a free body diagram.
- **Strain energy in axial member:** $U = \dfrac{1}{2}P\delta = \dfrac{P^2 L}{2AE} = \dfrac{\sigma^2 V}{2E}$. Important for Module 2 (Castigliano's theorem).
- **Maximum stress in a tapered bar:** At the **smaller-area end** (i.e., the end with the smaller $A$). This is where failure is most likely.
- **Composite / parallel bars:** Stiffness additivity ‚ÄĒ $k_{\text{eff}} = k_1 + k_2 + \cdots + k_n$ when members share the same elongation (parallel springs). Use $\delta$ compatibility, not series.
- **Limiting case (sanity check):** As $A_2 \to A_1$ in the tapered formula, the logarithmic expression $\to 1$ (by L'H√īpital), recovering $\delta = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$.
- **Units mnemonic:** $1 \text{ GPa} = 10^3 \text{ MPa} = 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$. Always express $E$ in **N/mm¬≤** when $A$ is in **mm¬≤** and $L$ in **mm**.
- **Stress units:** Pa = N/m¬≤; kPa = 10¬≥ Pa; MPa = 10‚Ā∂ Pa; GPa = 10‚ĀĻ Pa.
- **Strain is dimensionless** but conventionally reported in $\mu\varepsilon$ (10‚ĀĽ‚Ā∂) in experimental work and in plain decimals in textbook problems.
- **Real-world applications to mention in answers:** Suspension bridge cables, pre-stressed tendons, rock anchors, tower guy wires, RCC rebar, tie-rods in roof trusses, hydraulic piston rods.
- **Common exam traps to avoid:** Wrong formula (diameter vs. area linear), unit mismatch, ignoring intermediate loads, wrong sign for strain energy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Temperature Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Temperature Effects** in the context of Mechanics of Solids refer to the internal stresses, strains, and dimensional changes induced in a structural member when it is subjected to a change in temperature ($\Delta T$). When a body is free to expand or contract, the dimensional change is purely thermal; however, when the thermal deformation is **restrained** (fully or partially) by external supports or by continuity with other members of different materials, **thermal stresses** (also called temperature stresses) develop within the body.

Mathematically, the free thermal strain in a homogeneous isotropic material is expressed as:

$$\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = **Coefficient of Linear Thermal Expansion** (per ¬įC or per K), a material property
- $\Delta T$ = Change in temperature (Final Temperature ‚ąí Initial Temperature)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 ‚Äď Module 1):** The topic covers thermal stresses in simple bars, composite (compound) bars, and the effect of temperature on statically indeterminate structures. Students must master the interplay between *mechanical strain* and *thermal strain* in deriving the total deformation compatibility equation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a metal rod placed between two **immovable concrete walls** and then heated with a blowtorch:

- **Scenario 1 ‚ÄĒ Free Expansion:** If the walls were removed, the rod would simply grow longer. No stress would develop ‚ÄĒ only a free elongation ($\delta L = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$). The atoms vibrate more vigorously and occupy a slightly larger mean inter-atomic spacing.
- **Scenario 2 ‚ÄĒ Fully Restrained Expansion:** With the walls locked in place, the rod "wants" to grow but cannot. The walls push back with a compressive force, producing an internal **compressive thermal stress**. Equivalently, think of it as a spring being compressed by a rigid boundary.
- **Scenario 3 ‚ÄĒ Partial Restraint (Composite Bar):** Two different metals (e.g., brass and steel) are bonded together and heated. Each "wants" a different free expansion. The bond forces them to share a common elongation, creating internal stresses even without any external walls.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ‚ÄĒ Railway Tracks:** Railway tracks have small gaps (expansion joints) deliberately left between consecutive rails. If the rails were continuous, summer heating would cause them to buckle sideways ("sun kinks") ‚ÄĒ a classic failure driven by restrained thermal expansion. Bridges, pipelines, and concrete slabs use **expansion joints** for exactly the same reason.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

| Material | Coefficient of Thermal Expansion $\alpha$ (per ¬įC) |
|---|---|
| Steel | $12 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Concrete | $10 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Brass | $19 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Copper | $17 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Aluminium | $23 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Cast Iron | $11 \times 10^{-6}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The values of $\alpha$ above are **standard reference values** used universally in KTU textbook numericals. For exam problems, the value of $\alpha$ is **always provided in the question**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logic Steps ‚ÄĒ How Temperature Effects Operate

The analysis of temperature effects is a **two-stage logical process**:

**Stage 1 ‚ÄĒ Hypothetical Free Expansion:**
Mentally "release" all restraints on the member and compute the unrestrained thermal elongation:

$$\delta_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$$

This is what *would* have happened if nothing held the bar back.

**Stage 2 ‚ÄĒ Restoration of Restraint (Mechanical Counter-Deformation):**
Apply an imaginary external force (or pair of forces) that pushes the member back to its original (restrained) length. The force needed to do this is the **thermal force**, and the corresponding stress is the **thermal stress**.

This force is governed by the same Hooke's Law framework as any mechanical problem:

$$\delta_{mech} = \frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E}$$

Setting $|\delta_{mech}| = |\delta_{th}|$ (compatibility condition) gives:

$$\frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$$

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{P}{A} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The thermal stress $\sigma_{th} = E \alpha \Delta T$ is the **master formula** for a fully restrained homogeneous bar. It is *independent of length and cross-sectional area* ‚ÄĒ a fact that surprises many students.

### 2.2 The Superposition Principle (Mechanical + Thermal)

When a bar is **simultaneously** subjected to an axial mechanical load $P$ **and** a temperature change $\Delta T$, the total stress and total elongation are obtained by **superposition**:

$$\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{mech} + \sigma_{th} = \frac{P}{A} \pm E \alpha \Delta T$$

$$\delta_{total} = \delta_{mech} \pm \delta_{th} = \frac{PL}{AE} \pm \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$$

The **sign convention** for thermal effects:
- **Rise in temperature** ($\Delta T > 0$) ‚Üí Expansion tendency ‚Üí produces **compressive** stress when restrained
- **Drop in temperature** ($\Delta T < 0$) ‚Üí Contraction tendency ‚Üí produces **tensile** stress when restrained

### 2.3 Composite (Compound) Bars Under Temperature

A composite bar consists of **two different materials** bonded together (e.g., a brass tube sleeved over a steel rod). When such a system is heated:

1. Each material *wants* a different free expansion (because $\alpha_1 \neq \alpha_2$).
2. Because they are bonded, they must share a **common final length**.
3. Internal forces develop at the interface ‚ÄĒ one material is pushed beyond its free length, the other is held back from its free length.

The equilibrium condition requires the internal forces to be equal and opposite:

$$P_1 = P_2 = P$$

The compatibility condition states that the total elongations of the two materials must be equal:

$$\delta_1^{total} = \delta_2^{total}$$

$$\frac{P \cdot L}{A_1 \cdot E_1} + \alpha_1 \cdot \Delta T \cdot L = \frac{P \cdot L}{A_2 \cdot E_2} + \alpha_2 \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$$

Solving for $P$ gives the internal thermal force in the compound bar.

### 2.4 Statically Indeterminate Systems with Temperature

For a bar fixed between two rigid walls and subjected to a temperature rise $\Delta T$:

- **Free thermal elongation:** $\delta_{th} = \alpha \Delta T L$
- **Wall reaction force:** $P$ (compressive, pushing inward)
- **Mechanical shortening due to P:** $\delta_P = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$
- **Compatibility:** $\delta_P = \delta_{th}$

This yields the wall reaction $P = A E \alpha \Delta T$, and the corresponding thermal stress is $\sigma = E \alpha \Delta T$.

For **two-bar systems** (e.g., a bar supported by two parallel members of different materials), the problem becomes a 2√ó2 system of equations:
1. **Equilibrium equation** (forces sum to zero)
2. **Compatibility equation** (geometric constraint)

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \Delta T$ | Free thermal strain | All temperature problems |
| 2 | $\delta_{th} = \alpha \Delta T L$ | Free thermal elongation | All temperature problems |
| 3 | $\sigma_{th} = E \alpha \Delta T$ | Fully restrained thermal stress | Single bar, both ends fixed |
| 4 | $P_{th} = A E \alpha \Delta T$ | Fully restrained thermal force | Single bar, both ends fixed |
| 5 | $\sigma_{total} = \dfrac{P}{A} + E \alpha \Delta T$ | Combined stress (P + temperature) | Bar with load + heating |
| 6 | $\delta_{total} = \dfrac{PL}{AE} + \alpha \Delta T L$ | Combined elongation | Bar with load + heating |
| 7 | $P\left(\dfrac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{A_2 E_2}\right) = (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T \cdot L$ | Two-material composite force | Compound bars |
| 8 | $\sigma_1 = \dfrac{P}{A_1} \quad ; \quad \sigma_2 = \dfrac{P}{A_2}$ | Stresses in each material | Compound bars |
| 9 | $\delta_1 = \delta_2$ (compatibility) | Equal total elongations | Compound bars |
| 10 | $P_1 = P_2$ (equilibrium) | Internal forces balance | Compound bars |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder:** $\alpha$ has units of **per ¬įC** (or **per K**, numerically identical for temperature *differences*). $E$ has units of **N/mm¬≤** (MPa) or **GPa**. Always keep $L$ in **mm** and $A$ in **mm¬≤** when $E$ is in N/mm¬≤, or use a fully consistent SI set (m, Pa).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Temperature effects govern the design philosophy of:
- **Bridges:** Bearings and expansion joints accommodate thermal growth (a 100 m steel bridge can grow ~12 mm for a 10 ¬įC rise).
- **Railway tracks:** Continuous welded rails (CWR) are pre-tensioned to a "stress-free temperature" so that neither summer heat nor winter cold pushes the rail past its safe stress range.
- **Pipelines:** Loop expansion joints absorb thermal growth in long oil/gas pipelines.
- **Concrete pavements:** Expansion joints every 4‚Äď5 m prevent summer buckling.
- **Tall buildings:** A 200 m reinforced concrete chimney can grow ~20 mm in summer sun; the design must allow for this without cracking the lining.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Thermal Stress in a Fully Restrained Homogeneous Bar

**Problem Setup:** A uniform steel bar of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, Young's modulus $E$, and coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha$ is fixed rigidly between two immovable walls. The temperature of the bar is raised by $\Delta T$. Find the thermal stress and the wall reaction.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Mentally release the right wall. The bar is now free to expand. The free thermal elongation is:

$$\delta_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$$

**Step 2:** The free expansion leaves a gap of $\delta_{th}$ between the right end of the bar and the right wall. To close this gap, we must apply a compressive axial force $P$ to the right end of the bar (this is the wall reaction).

**Step 3:** The mechanical shortening produced by this compressive force, according to Hooke's Law, is:

$$\delta_{mech} = \frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E}$$

**Step 4:** **Compatibility Condition:** The mechanical shortening must exactly equal the free thermal elongation (so the bar just fits back between the walls):

$$\delta_{mech} = \delta_{th}$$

$$\frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $L$ from both sides and solve for $P$:

$$P = A \cdot E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 6:** The corresponding thermal stress is:

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{P}{A} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The thermal stress depends *only* on $E$, $\alpha$, and $\Delta T$. It is **independent of the bar's length and cross-sectional area**. Doubling the length does not change the stress; it only doubles the wall force.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Composite Bar with Two Materials Subjected to Temperature Change

**Problem Setup:** A composite bar consists of a steel core (area $A_1$, modulus $E_1$, expansion coefficient $\alpha_1$) and a brass sleeve (area $A_2$, modulus $E_2$, expansion coefficient $\alpha_2$), perfectly bonded over a common length $L$. The system is heated uniformly by $\Delta T$. Find the stresses developed in each material.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Identify the unknown internal force $P$ at the bonded interface. By equilibrium (no external axial load on the composite), the force in the steel and the force in the brass are equal in magnitude and opposite in sense (the brass is in compression, the steel is in tension, or vice versa, depending on which $\alpha$ is larger).

**Step 2:** Write the **total elongation** of each material, using superposition:

For Material 1 (mechanical strain from $P$ + thermal strain from $\Delta T$):

$$\delta_1 = \frac{P \cdot L}{A_1 \cdot E_1} + \alpha_1 \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$$

For Material 2 (mechanical strain from $P$ + thermal strain from $\Delta T$):

$$\delta_2 = \frac{P \cdot L}{A_2 \cdot E_2} + \alpha_2 \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$$

> [!NOTE]
> The mechanical terms for both materials carry the **same sign** for $P$ (the internal force at the interface is the same in magnitude, and we treat it as a tensile pull on each). The signs of the thermal terms depend on whether $\Delta T$ is positive (rise) or negative (drop).

**Step 3:** **Compatibility Condition:** Because the materials are bonded, they must elongate by the *same* amount:

$$\delta_1 = \delta_2$$

$$\frac{P \cdot L}{A_1 \cdot E_1} + \alpha_1 \Delta T L = \frac{P \cdot L}{A_2 \cdot E_2} + \alpha_2 \Delta T L$$

**Step 4:** Divide throughout by $L$ and group the $P$ terms on one side and the $\Delta T$ terms on the other:

$$P \left( \frac{1}{A_1 E_1} - \frac{1}{A_2 E_2} \right) = (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $P$:

$$P = \frac{(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{A_1 E_1} - \dfrac{1}{A_2 E_2}}$$

**Step 6:** Compute the stresses in each material:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P}{A_1} \quad \text{(in material 1)}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{P}{A_2} \quad \text{(in material 2)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **higher-$\alpha$ material ends up in compression** (its free expansion was restrained, so it was pushed back), while the **lower-$\alpha$ material ends up in tension** (its free expansion was less, so it was stretched to match the higher-$\alpha$ material).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Bar with Combined Axial Load and Temperature Change

**Problem Setup:** A bar of length $L$, area $A$, modulus $E$, and coefficient $\alpha$ carries an axial tensile load $P$ and is simultaneously heated by $\Delta T$. Find the total elongation.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Compute the mechanical elongation due to the axial load alone:

$$\delta_{mech} = \frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the free thermal elongation:

$$\delta_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$$

**Step 3:** Apply the principle of superposition (valid because both effects are linear and within the elastic range):

$$\delta_{total} = \delta_{mech} + \delta_{th}$$

$$\delta_{total} = \frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E} + \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $L$ for compactness:

$$\delta_{total} = L \left( \frac{P}{A E} + \alpha \Delta T \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the bar is **fully restrained** (ends fixed) **and** carries an axial load $P$, the total stress in the bar is:
> $$\sigma_{total} = \frac{P}{A} + E \alpha \Delta T$$
> This is the **superposition of mechanical stress and thermal stress** ‚ÄĒ a frequently tested KTU concept.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Common Problem Patterns

| Pattern | Setup | Key Equation | Solve For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single bar, free end | One end fixed, other end free | $\delta = \alpha \Delta T L$ | Free elongation |
| Single bar, both ends fixed | Walls at both ends | $P = AE\alpha\Delta T$ | Wall reaction |
| Single bar, load + temperature | Axial load + $\Delta T$ | $\delta = PL/(AE) + \alpha\Delta T L$ | Total elongation |
| Composite bar (no external load) | Bonded materials, heated | $P(1/A_1E_1 - 1/A_2E_2) = (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)\Delta T$ | Interface force |
| Two-bar truss (statically indeterminate) | Two bars in parallel supporting a rigid bar | Solve equilibrium + compatibility | Bar forces |
| Bar with gap between walls | Initial gap of $\delta_0$ exists | Compatibility adjusts for gap | Wall force (smaller) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Free vs Restrained Thermal Expansion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bar subjected to temperature change dT] --> B{Is the bar restrained?}
    B -- NO: Free end --> C[Free thermal elongation]
    C --> C1[delta_th = alpha * dT * L]
    C1 --> C2[Thermal stress = 0]
    C2 --> C3[No internal force]

    B -- YES: Both ends fixed --> D[Thermal strain is prevented]
    D --> D1[Mechanical counter-strain must equal thermal strain]
    D1 --> D2[sigma_th = E * alpha * dT]
    D2 --> D3[Wall reaction P = A * E * alpha * dT]
    D3 --> D4[Compressive for heating, tensile for cooling]

    B -- PARTIAL: Composite bonded --> E[Two materials, different alphas]
    E --> E1[Equilibrium: P1 = P2 = P]
    E1 --> E2[Compatibility: delta1_total = delta2_total]
    E2 --> E3[Solve 2x2 system for P]
    E3 --> E4[Stresses: sigma1 = P/A1, sigma2 = P/A2]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart ‚ÄĒ Solution Algorithm for Composite Bar Temperature Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Composite bar problem] --> S1[Identify Material 1: A1, E1, alpha1]
    S1 --> S2[Identify Material 2: A2, E2, alpha2]
    S2 --> S3[Note common length L and temperature change dT]
    S3 --> S4[Write equilibrium: P1 = P2 = P]
    S4 --> S5[Write compatibility: delta1 = delta2]
    S5 --> S6[Expand: PL over A1E1 plus alpha1*dT*L = PL over A2E2 plus alpha2*dT*L]
    S6 --> S7[Solve linear equation for P]
    S7 --> S8[Compute sigma1 = P / A1]
    S8 --> S9[Compute sigma2 = P / A2]
    S9 --> S10[Verify: sum of forces = 0, and check sign convention]
    S10 --> S11[End: Report forces and stresses in each material]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Real-World Application Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Restrained thermal expansion] --> B[Bridge bearings]
    A --> C[Railway CWR tracks]
    A --> D[Pipeline expansion loops]
    A --> E[Concrete pavement joints]
    A --> F[Chimney and tower design]
    A --> G[Pressure vessel thermal stress]
    B --> B1[Allows longitudinal sliding]
    C --> C1[Pre-tensioned to stress-free temperature]
    D --> D1[Loop absorbs growth]
    E --> E1[Prevents summer buckling]
    F --> F1[Allows vertical growth up to 50 mm]
    G --> G1[Critical in power plant boilers]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024, Model]**
Define the *Coefficient of Linear Thermal Expansion*. A steel bar 2 m long is heated from 20 ¬įC to 80 ¬įC. If $\alpha_{steel} = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ per ¬įC, find the free expansion of the bar. Will any thermal stress develop in this case?

**Model Answer:**

The **Coefficient of Linear Thermal Expansion ($\alpha$)** is defined as the increase in length per unit original length per unit rise in temperature. Its SI unit is **per ¬įC** (or per K).

$$\alpha = \frac{\delta L}{L \cdot \Delta T}$$

**Calculation:**

$$\Delta T = 80 - 20 = 60 \, ¬įC$$

$$L = 2 \, m = 2000 \, mm$$

$$\delta L = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L = (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 60 \times 2000$$

$$\delta L = 1.44 \, mm$$

**Stress:** Since the bar is free to expand (no restraint mentioned), the elongation is *free*, and **no thermal stress will develop** in the bar. The atoms simply move apart to a new equilibrium spacing corresponding to the higher temperature.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, Model]**
A brass tube of length 1.5 m is placed over a steel rod of the same length. The composite bar is placed between two rigid walls and heated through 50 ¬įC. Given: $E_{brass} = 100 \, GPa$, $\alpha_{brass} = 19 \times 10^{-6} /¬įC$, $E_{steel} = 200 \, GPa$, $\alpha_{steel} = 12 \times 10^{-6} /¬įC$, and the cross-sectional areas are equal. State, with reason, which material will be in tension and which in compression.

**Model Answer:**

Since $\alpha_{brass} (19 \times 10^{-6}) > \alpha_{steel} (12 \times 10^{-6})$, the brass *wants* to expand more than the steel. Because they are bonded and constrained by the walls, they must share a common final length.

- The **brass is restrained from expanding fully** ‚Üí it is pushed back ‚Üí **brass is in compression**.
- The **steel is stretched beyond its free length** to match the brass ‚Üí **steel is in tension**.

Mathematically, the common compatibility equation gives a positive $P$ in the direction that pulls the steel outward, confirming steel is in tension and brass is in compression.

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ ESE Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **commonly lose 2‚Äď3 marks** by forgetting the **sign convention** in composite bars. Always state explicitly: "$\alpha$ larger ‚Üí compression; $\alpha$ smaller ‚Üí tension."
> - Another frequent error is **mixing units** (e.g., $L$ in metres with $E$ in N/mm¬≤). Convert everything to a single consistent unit system **before** substituting.
> - For statically indeterminate problems, **always** write *both* the equilibrium equation and the compatibility equation. Marks are split between them ‚ÄĒ losing either costs you roughly 4‚Äď5 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024, Model]**

**(a)** A steel bar of length 1.2 m, cross-sectional area 600 mm¬≤, is held between two rigid supports. The bar is initially at 30 ¬įC. Find the temperature to which the bar must be heated so that a compressive stress of 90 MPa develops in it. Take $E = 200 \, GPa$ and $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /¬įC$.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A composite bar consists of a copper core (area = 400 mm¬≤, $E = 100 \, GPa$, $\alpha = 17 \times 10^{-6} /¬įC$) surrounded by a steel tube (area = 600 mm¬≤, $E = 200 \, GPa$, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /¬įC$). If the composite bar, initially at 25 ¬įC, is heated uniformly to 95 ¬įC, determine the stresses developed in the copper and the steel. The bar is free to expand at both ends.

**(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Q.A(a):**

**Given Data:**
- $L = 1.2 \, m = 1200 \, mm$
- $A = 600 \, mm^2$
- $E = 200 \, GPa = 200 \times 10^3 \, N/mm^2$
- $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /¬įC$
- $\sigma_{th} = 90 \, MPa = 90 \, N/mm^2$ (compressive)
- $T_1 = 30 \, ¬įC$

**Formula:** For a fully restrained bar:

$$\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

**Solving for $\Delta T$:**

$$\Delta T = \frac{\sigma_{th}}{E \cdot \alpha} = \frac{90}{(200 \times 10^3) \times (12 \times 10^{-6})}$$

**Step-by-step calculation:**

$$\Delta T = \frac{90}{200 \times 10^3 \times 12 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{90}{2.4}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T = 37.5 \, ¬įC}$$

**Final temperature:**

$$T_2 = T_1 + \Delta T = 30 + 37.5 = \boxed{67.5 \, ¬įC}$$

---

##### **Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Q.A(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Copper: $A_1 = 400 \, mm^2$, $E_1 = 100 \, GPa = 100 \times 10^3 \, N/mm^2$, $\alpha_1 = 17 \times 10^{-6} /¬įC$
- Steel: $A_2 = 600 \, mm^2$, $E_2 = 200 \times 10^3 \, N/mm^2$, $\alpha_2 = 12 \times 10^{-6} /¬įC$
- $\Delta T = 95 - 25 = 70 \, ¬įC$
- The bar is **free to expand at both ends** ‚ÄĒ this means the *overall* length is unrestrained, but the *two materials* still must share a common elongation because they are bonded.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Compatibility Equation:** Total elongations of copper and steel must be equal:

$$\delta_1 = \delta_2$$

$$\frac{P L}{A_1 E_1} + \alpha_1 \Delta T L = \frac{P L}{A_2 E_2} + \alpha_2 \Delta T L$$

(Here, $P$ is the internal tensile force in the steel and the equal-magnitude compressive force in the copper ‚ÄĒ consistent with $\alpha_1 > \alpha_2$.)

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Cancel $L$ and solve for $P$:**

$$P \left( \frac{1}{A_1 E_1} - \frac{1}{A_2 E_2} \right) = (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T$$

Compute the coefficients:

$$\frac{1}{A_1 E_1} = \frac{1}{400 \times 100 \times 10^3} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^7} = 2.5 \times 10^{-8} \, /N$$

$$\frac{1}{A_2 E_2} = \frac{1}{600 \times 200 \times 10^3} = \frac{1}{12 \times 10^7} = 0.833 \times 10^{-8} \, /N$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Substitute into the equation:**

$$P \times (2.5 \times 10^{-8} - 0.833 \times 10^{-8}) = (12 - 17) \times 10^{-6} \times 70$$

$$P \times 1.667 \times 10^{-8} = -5 \times 10^{-6} \times 70$$

$$P \times 1.667 \times 10^{-8} = -350 \times 10^{-6} = -3.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$P = \frac{-3.5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.667 \times 10^{-8}} \approx -20{,}998 \, N \approx -21{,}000 \, N$$

The **negative sign** indicates that the assumed direction of $P$ was wrong. The copper is in **tension** and the steel is in **compression** in our assumed sense ‚ÄĒ wait, let us re-interpret:

- $\alpha_{copper} > \alpha_{steel}$ ‚Üí copper *wants* to expand more.
- If the bar is free at the ends, the common final elongation is determined by force balance.
- The **higher-$\alpha$ material (copper) ends up in tension** because it is being pulled along by the steel (which is doing the opposite).
- Actually, re-checking: in this case, with the bar free to expand, the system is *internally* indeterminate only by virtue of the bond. The interface force $P$ acts to pull the steel back and stretch the copper beyond its free length.

**Magnitude of $P$:** $|P| = 21{,}000 \, N$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Compute the stresses:**

$$\sigma_{copper} = \frac{P}{A_1} = \frac{21{,}000}{400} = 52.5 \, N/mm^2 = 52.5 \, MPa \, (Tension)$$

$$\sigma_{steel} = \frac{P}{A_2} = \frac{21{,}000}{600} = 35.0 \, N/mm^2 = 35.0 \, MPa \, (Compression)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** The internal forces in copper (tension) and steel (compression) must balance each other at the interface: $52.5 \times 400 = 21{,}000 \, N$ (copper pulls outward), and $35.0 \times 600 = 21{,}000 \, N$ (steel pushes inward). ‚úď

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, Model] (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the thermal stress developed in a homogeneous bar of uniform cross-section when it is fully restrained between two rigid walls and subjected to a uniform temperature rise of $\Delta T$. State the key observation from the final expression.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A steel rod of 20 mm diameter passes centrally through a brass tube of 30 mm outer diameter and 25 mm inner diameter. The assembly is placed between two rigid supports with a small clearance of 0.05 mm. Calculate the stresses in the steel and brass when the temperature is raised by 60 ¬įC. Take $E_s = 200 \, GPa$, $E_b = 100 \, GPa$, $\alpha_s = 12 \times 10^{-6} /¬įC$, $\alpha_b = 19 \times 10^{-6} /¬įC$. Assume the bar is 1 m long.

**(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Q.B(a):**

[This is the standard derivation ‚ÄĒ see Derivation 1 in Section 3 above for the full step-by-step working.]

**Result:**

$$\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

**Key Observation:** The thermal stress in a fully restrained homogeneous bar is **independent of the length and cross-sectional area** of the bar. It depends *only* on the material properties ($E$, $\alpha$) and the temperature change ($\Delta T$). Doubling the length of the bar does not change the stress ‚ÄĒ it only doubles the wall reaction force.

**Engineering Implication:** Long slender members and short stubby members made of the same material, when fully restrained, experience the *same* thermal stress for the same $\Delta T$. This is why thermal stress problems are often more critical for **thick** members (where the cross-section's strength matters) than for thin ones ‚ÄĒ although in both cases, the stress is identical, the force is proportional to area.

---

##### **Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Q.B(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Steel rod: diameter = 20 mm ‚Üí $A_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 = 314.16 \, mm^2$
- Brass tube: OD = 30 mm, ID = 25 mm ‚Üí $A_b = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (30^2 - 25^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (900 - 625) = 216.77 \, mm^2$
- Length: $L = 1 \, m = 1000 \, mm$
- Initial clearance: $0.05 \, mm$
- $\Delta T = 60 \, ¬įC$
- $E_s = 200 \times 10^3 \, N/mm^2$, $E_b = 100 \times 10^3 \, N/mm^2$
- $\alpha_s = 12 \times 10^{-6} /¬įC$, $\alpha_b = 19 \times 10^{-6} /¬įC$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Check whether the free thermal expansion exceeds the clearance:**

The free expansion of the composite (if both could expand independently) is governed by the brass (higher $\alpha$):

$$\delta_{th,brass} = \alpha_b \cdot \Delta T \cdot L = 19 \times 10^{-6} \times 60 \times 1000 = 1.14 \, mm$$

This is **much larger** than the 0.05 mm clearance, so the walls are engaged and thermal stresses **will develop**.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compatibility Equation (with clearance):**

The brass wants to expand by 1.14 mm; the clearance is 0.05 mm; so the brass is restrained by 1.14 ‚ąí 0.05 = 1.09 mm. This restraint is provided by the wall reaction $P$ acting through both materials.

For the brass: free expansion (1.14 mm) ‚ąí mechanical shortening from $P$ = final elongation = clearance (0.05 mm)
$$\alpha_b \Delta T L - \frac{P L}{A_b E_b} = 0.05$$
$$1.14 - \frac{P \times 1000}{216.77 \times 100 \times 10^3} = 0.05$$

For the steel: free expansion + mechanical elongation from $P$ = final elongation = clearance (0.05 mm)
$$\alpha_s \Delta T L + \frac{P L}{A_s E_s} = 0.05$$
$$(12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 60 \times 1000 + \frac{P \times 1000}{314.16 \times 200 \times 10^3} = 0.05$$
$$0.72 + \frac{P \times 1000}{6.2832 \times 10^7} = 0.05$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Solve the system:**

From the brass equation:

$$\frac{P \times 1000}{216.77 \times 100 \times 10^3} = 1.14 - 0.05 = 1.09$$

$$P = \frac{1.09 \times 216.77 \times 100 \times 10^3}{1000} = \frac{1.09 \times 2.1677 \times 10^7}{1000}$$

$$P_{brass} = 23{,}628 \, N$$

From the steel equation (substituting the same $P$ value to check consistency):

$$\frac{23{,}628 \times 1000}{6.2832 \times 10^7} = \frac{2.3628 \times 10^7}{6.2832 \times 10^7} = 0.376 \, mm$$

$$0.72 + 0.376 = 1.096 \, mm$$

This is **not equal to 0.05 mm** ‚ÄĒ there's an inconsistency because we used a *single* force $P$ but the equations are not simultaneously satisfied with a common $P$ unless the compatibility is set up correctly. Let us redo with the **correct compatibility condition** for a parallel-bar system with clearance:

**Correct Approach (using a unified compatibility):**

For both materials, the **final elongation = clearance**:

$$\delta_{steel} = \delta_{brass} = 0.05 \, mm$$

The brass is in **compression** (its free expansion is too large; the wall pushes it back):

$$\delta_{brass} = \alpha_b \Delta T L - \frac{P L}{A_b E_b} = 0.05$$
$$1.14 - \frac{P \times 1000}{2.1677 \times 10^7} = 0.05$$
$$P = \frac{(1.14 - 0.05) \times 2.1677 \times 10^7}{1000} = 23{,}628 \, N$$

The steel is in **tension** (its free expansion is too small; the wall pulls it out):

$$\delta_{steel} = \alpha_s \Delta T L + \frac{P L}{A_s E_s} = 0.05$$
$$0.72 + \frac{P \times 1000}{6.2832 \times 10^7} = 0.05$$

Substituting $P = 23{,}628 \, N$:

$$0.72 + \frac{23{,}628 \times 1000}{6.2832 \times 10^7} = 0.72 + 0.376 = 1.096 \, mm$$

This does **not** equal 0.05 mm. The issue is that the brass and steel are at the same end-points, so the elongations *must* match, but my equations are not consistent with a single $P$.

**The correct formulation:** The walls apply a single force $P$ that pulls the steel outward (tension) and compresses the brass inward. Both materials must have the *same* final length. Let the original length be $L_0$ and the final length be $L_0 + \delta$ where $\delta = 0.05 \, mm$ (the closure of the gap, since the system has to close the 0.05 mm gap to engage the walls).

Wait ‚ÄĒ re-reading the problem: "placed between two rigid supports **with a small clearance of 0.05 mm**" means the gap between the bar and the wall is 0.05 mm initially. Heating expands the bar; once the bar grows by 0.05 mm, it contacts the walls; thereafter, the walls restrain further expansion. So the **free expansion must first close the 0.05 mm gap** before any stress develops.

Free thermal expansion of the composite (if it were one material with effective $\alpha$) is the *average* governed by the bond. Let us use a clean approach: treat it as a two-material parallel system with the wall reaction $P$ acting after the gap closes.

**Step-by-step (cleaner version):**

**Free expansion of the composite (no stress, no walls):**
- Both materials would like to expand freely, but they are bonded.
- The common free expansion is determined by the equilibrium of internal forces (no external load): $P = 0$ at this stage.
- Therefore both materials expand freely by their own $\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$.
- The brass expands by 1.14 mm; the steel expands by 0.72 mm. **But they are bonded!** This is a contradiction ‚ÄĒ they cannot expand by different amounts if bonded.

**Resolution:** The correct approach is to recognize that as soon as the temperature starts rising, internal forces build up at the bond. The "free expansion" concept applies only if the materials are unbonded. With bonding, internal stresses develop from the very start.

**The true compatibility condition** (with the wall clearance of 0.05 mm and bonded materials) is:

The bar as a whole must elongate by exactly **0.05 mm** (the gap closure) ‚ÄĒ but the two materials must share this common elongation.

$$\delta_{steel} = \delta_{brass} = 0.05 \, mm$$

For the **brass** (higher $\alpha$): free expansion tendency is 1.14 mm, but it is compressed back by the wall to a final elongation of 0.05 mm. The wall force $P$ is compressive on the brass:

$$\delta_{brass} = \alpha_b \Delta T L - \frac{P L}{A_b E_b}$$

For the **steel** (lower $\alpha$): free expansion tendency is 0.72 mm, but it is stretched by the wall to a final elongation of 0.05 mm. Wait ‚ÄĒ 0.72 mm is already more than 0.05 mm. So the steel is *also* being compressed back? Let us think again.

Actually, both materials would expand freely by amounts *larger* than 0.05 mm (brass: 1.14 mm, steel: 0.72 mm). Both are restrained to a final elongation of 0.05 mm. So both are in **compression**.

The wall reaction compresses the entire composite bar. This compression is shared between the two materials in proportion to their axial stiffness $AE/L$.

**Total compressive force on the composite:**
The composite's "free expansion" (if unconstrained) is somewhere between 0.72 and 1.14 mm. The effective free expansion of a bonded composite, when unrestrained by external walls, is determined by the requirement that internal forces balance (i.e., $P = 0$ for the *unrestrained* case, but the materials must share a common length ‚ÄĒ this is the case where the bar is *not* between walls, and the elongation is determined by the internal force balance giving $P = 0$).

If $P = 0$ (unrestrained composite), then both materials share a common length such that the sum of forces on each cross-section is zero. This is exactly the case we analyzed in Q.A(b) above, which gave a finite $P$ in the bonded case. 

**The cleanest approach for this problem:**

Let me re-derive carefully. The composite bar (brass + steel bonded) is placed between two walls with a gap of 0.05 mm. As temperature rises, the system needs to:

1. Close the 0.05 mm gap (no stress during this phase).
2. Engage the walls and develop thermal stresses.

The "effective free expansion" of the composite (unrestrained by walls, but internally bonded) is what we calculated in Q.A(b). Let me calculate that:

From the bonded composite equilibrium (no external walls, $P_{wall} = 0$):
$$P_{internal} = \frac{(\alpha_b - \alpha_s) \Delta T L}{\frac{L}{A_b E_b} - \frac{L}{A_s E_s}} \cdot \frac{1}{L}$$

Wait, we computed this in Q.A(b) and got $|P| = 21{,}000 \, N$. The common elongation of the bonded composite (without external walls) is:

$$\delta_{common} = \frac{P L}{A_s E_s} + \alpha_s \Delta T L$$

With $P = 21{,}000 \, N$ (steel in tension as we found ‚ÄĒ actually let me recompute the sign):

In Q.A(b), we set $P$ as the force in steel in tension. The result was $\sigma_{steel} = -35.0 \, MPa$ (compression) and $\sigma_{copper} = 52.5 \, MPa$ (tension), with the steel in compression because $\alpha_{copper} > \alpha_{steel}$. Here, with brass instead of copper, the same logic applies: $\alpha_{brass} > \alpha_{steel}$, so **brass is in tension** and **steel is in compression** in the bonded-but-unrestrained case.

The free elongation of the bonded composite (without external walls) is:

Using the steel equation (steel is in compression, so $-P$ acts on it; the elongation is *less* than free):

$$\delta_{common} = \alpha_s \Delta T L - \frac{P L}{A_s E_s}$$

$$= 0.72 - \frac{21{,}000 \times 1000}{314.16 \times 200 \times 10^3} = 0.72 - 0.334 = 0.386 \, mm$$

Hmm, but this used $P$ from Q.A(b) which had different areas. Let me redo with the current problem's areas.

**For Q.B(b) ‚ÄĒ clean re-derivation:**

A bonded brass-steel composite, unrestrained by external walls, has internal forces determined by:

$$P \left( \frac{1}{A_s E_s} - \frac{1}{A_b E_b} \right) = (\alpha_b - \alpha_s) \Delta T$$

$$\frac{1}{A_s E_s} = \frac{1}{314.16 \times 200 \times 10^3} = 1.5915 \times 10^{-8} \, /N$$

$$\frac{1}{A_b E_b} = \frac{1}{216.77 \times 100 \times 10^3} = 4.6132 \times 10^{-8} \, /N$$

$$P = \frac{(19 - 12) \times 10^{-6} \times 60}{1.5915 \times 10^{-8} - 4.6132 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$P = \frac{420 \times 10^{-6}}{-3.0217 \times 10^{-8}} = -13{,}898 \, N$$

The negative sign tells us the assumed direction was wrong. Convention: $P$ is the force in the steel. The negative sign means the **steel is in compression** and the **brass is in tension** (consistent with $\alpha_{brass} > \alpha_{steel}$).

$$|P| = 13{,}898 \, N$$

The **free elongation of the bonded composite** (no walls) is the common elongation of either material:

$$\delta_{free} = \alpha_s \Delta T L - \frac{|P| L}{A_s E_s}$$

$$= 0.72 - \frac{13{,}898 \times 1000}{314.16 \times 200 \times 10^3} = 0.72 - 0.2212 = 0.499 \, mm$$

Now, the walls are 0.05 mm away. Since $\delta_{free} = 0.499 \, mm > 0.05 \, mm$, the bar grows past the gap and engages the walls. The walls then apply an additional compressive force $P_{wall}$ to the entire composite, further compressing both materials by:

$$\delta_{wall} = \frac{P_{wall} L}{A_s E_s + A_b E_b}$$

The final elongation = $\delta_{free} - \delta_{wall} = 0.05 \, mm$:

$$\delta_{wall} = 0.499 - 0.05 = 0.449 \, mm$$

$$P_{wall} = \frac{\delta_{wall} (A_s E_s + A_b E_b)}{L} = \frac{0.449 \times (6.2832 \times 10^7 + 2.1677 \times 10^7)}{1000}$$

$$P_{wall} = \frac{0.449 \times 8.4509 \times 10^7}{1000} = \frac{3.7945 \times 10^7}{1000} = 37{,}945 \, N$$

**Total force in each material** = internal bonded force + wall force share:

The wall force $P_{wall}$ compresses both materials (since the bar is being squeezed between the walls). The force in the steel becomes:

$$P_{steel,total} = -|P_{internal}| - P_{wall} \times \frac{A_s E_s}{A_s E_s + A_b E_b}$$

Wait ‚ÄĒ let me re-approach this with a cleaner sign convention.

**Cleaner Approach (recommended for the exam):**

Let the wall reaction be $R$ (compressive on the entire composite). The total force in the steel is $P_s$ and in the brass is $P_b$, with $P_s + P_b = R$ (since the wall pushes the composite inward, and the internal forces inside the composite sum to the wall reaction).

The compatibility condition (both materials elongate by 0.05 mm):

For steel: $\alpha_s \Delta T L - \dfrac{P_s L}{A_s E_s} = 0.05$

For brass: $\alpha_b \Delta T L - \dfrac{P_b L}{A_b E_b} = 0.05$

Wait ‚ÄĒ this assumes both are in compression, which would be true if the free expansion of both exceeds 0.05 mm (which it does). The "$P$" in each equation is the **compressive** force in that material, reducing its elongation from the free value.

**Steel equation:**

$$0.72 - \frac{P_s \times 1000}{314.16 \times 200 \times 10^3} = 0.05$$

$$0.72 - \frac{P_s}{6.2832 \times 10^4} = 0.05$$

$$\frac{P_s}{6.2832 \times 10^4} = 0.67$$

$$P_s = 42{,}097 \, N \quad (\text{compression in steel})$$

**Brass equation:**

$$1.14 - \frac{P_b \times 1000}{216.77 \times 100 \times 10^3} = 0.05$$

$$1.14 - \frac{P_b}{2.1677 \times 10^4} = 0.05$$

$$\frac{P_b}{2.1677 \times 10^4} = 1.09$$

$$P_b = 23{,}628 \, N \quad (\text{compression in brass})$$

**Stresses:**

$$\sigma_s = \frac{P_s}{A_s} = \frac{42{,}097}{314.16} = 134.0 \, N/mm^2 = 134.0 \, MPa \quad (\text{compressive})$$

$$\sigma_b = \frac{P_b}{A_b} = \frac{23{,}628}{216.77} = 109.0 \, N/mm^2 = 109.0 \, MPa \quad (\text{compressive})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of Equilibrium:** $P_s + P_b = 42{,}097 + 23{,}628 = 65{,}725 \, N$ is the total wall reaction. The internal stress distribution depends on the stiffness $AE$ of each material, with the stiffer steel carrying a larger share of the load ‚ÄĒ exactly as expected.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake in This Type of Problem:** Many students forget that the **bonded composite has internal stresses even without external walls** (the case we analyzed in Q.A(b)). They then try to use the simple formula $\sigma = E \alpha \Delta T$ on each material separately, which gives a wildly incorrect answer. Always derive the internal force $P$ from the *bonded compatibility condition* first, *then* add the wall reaction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Temperature Effects (Module 1)**

- [ ] **Free thermal strain:** $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \Delta T$ ‚ÄĒ universal for all materials.
- [ ] **Free thermal elongation:** $\delta_{th} = \alpha \Delta T L$ ‚ÄĒ applies only when unrestrained.
- [ ] **Fully restrained thermal stress:** $\sigma_{th} = E \alpha \Delta T$ ‚ÄĒ independent of $L$ and $A$.
- [ ] **Sign rule:** Temperature *rise* (positive $\Delta T$) ‚Üí **compressive** stress when restrained; temperature *drop* ‚Üí **tensile** stress.
- [ ] **Superposition principle:** $\delta_{total} = \dfrac{PL}{AE} \pm \alpha \Delta T L$ ‚ÄĒ valid only in the linear elastic range.
- [ ] **Composite bar master equation:** $P \left( \dfrac{1}{A_1 E_1} - \dfrac{1}{A_2 E_2} \right) = (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T$ ‚ÄĒ assumes $P_1 = P_2$ (equal internal forces) and $\delta_1 = \delta_2$ (equal elongations).
- [ ] **Composite bar stress sign rule:** Higher-$\alpha$ material ‚Üí **compression**; Lower-$\alpha$ material ‚Üí **tension** (when heated together, no external walls).
- [ ] **Statically indeterminate temperature problems** require *two equations*: one equilibrium + one compatibility.
- [ ] **Clearance problems** (gap between bar and wall): compute free expansion, subtract the gap to get the *effective* free expansion that produces stress.
- [ ] **Units consistency:** If $E$ is in N/mm¬≤, then $L$ in mm and $A$ in mm¬≤.
- [ ] **Real-world analogues:** Railway tracks (sun kinks), bridge bearings, pipeline loops, pavement joints ‚ÄĒ all are engineered to *avoid* the consequences of restrained thermal expansion.
- [ ] **Thermal stress is independent of bar geometry** for a fully restrained homogeneous bar ‚ÄĒ a unique and counter-intuitive property worth remembering for viva voce questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature Stress in Composite Bars
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Temperature Stress in Composite Bars

# Temperature Stress in Composite Bars

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

A **composite bar** (also termed a *compound bar* or *bi-metallic strip*) is a structural member composed of two or more distinct engineering materials rigidly bonded along their longitudinal interface, such that they act as a single, monolithic unit when subjected to external mechanical or thermal loading.

**Temperature stress** (or **thermal stress**) refers to the internal, self-equilibrating stresses that develop within a composite bar when it is subjected to a uniform change in temperature ($\Delta T$), and whose ends are **constrained against free expansion or contraction**. Because the constituent materials possess different **coefficients of thermal expansion** ($\alpha$), each component would naturally deform by a different magnitude if left unrestrained. The bonded interface, however, enforces **geometric compatibility**, and the resulting constraint generates internal axial forces and corresponding stresses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> *"When a composite bar, made of two or more materials of different coefficients of thermal expansion, is subjected to a change in temperature with its ends fixed, internal stresses are induced due to the mutual restraint offered by the materials upon one another. Such stresses are called temperature stresses or thermal stresses."*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two friends of **vastly different heights** ‚ÄĒ say, Riya (tall) and Arun (short) ‚ÄĒ holding hands and being asked to walk together at the **same stride length**. If the ground is uneven and forces them to take identical steps, both will feel uncomfortable: Riya will be pulled back (compressed) and Arun will be stretched forward (pulled). Neither is happy, but they must move together.

In a **composite bar under temperature change**:
- The **material with the higher $\alpha$** (say, brass) "wants" to expand more, but is held back by its neighbour ‚Üí it experiences **compressive stress**.
- The **material with the lower $\alpha$** (say, steel) "wants" to expand less, but is dragged along by its neighbour ‚Üí it experiences **tensile stress**.
- The **bonded interface** acts like the linked hands ‚ÄĒ enforcing equal final lengths.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - Coefficient of thermal expansion ($\alpha$): units are **per ¬įC** or **/¬įC** (or equivalently **/K**, since temperature *differences* are identical on both scales).
> - Typical values: Steel $\alpha \approx 12 \times 10^{-6} /¬įC$, Brass $\alpha \approx 19 \times 10^{-6} /¬įC$, Copper $\alpha \approx 17 \times 10^{-6} /¬įC$, Concrete $\alpha \approx 10 \times 10^{-6} /¬įC$.
> - Young's modulus ($E$): units are **N/mm¬≤** or **MPa** (commonly $E_{steel} = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm¬≤).

### When Does the Problem Become Trivial?

It is important to recognise a special case:
- If both materials have the **same $\alpha$**, both expand equally when free ‚Üí **no relative deformation is restrained** ‚Üí **no thermal stress** develops, even though the bar elongates.
- If the bar is **free to expand** (ends not constrained), no thermal stress arises regardless of the materials' $\alpha$ values.
- Thermal stress arises **only** when: (a) $\alpha_1 \neq \alpha_2$, AND (b) the bar is **axially constrained** at its ends.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Logic ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Reasoning

Consider a composite bar consisting of **two materials (1) and (2)**:
- Material 1: Area $A_1$, Young's modulus $E_1$, coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha_1$
- Material 2: Area $A_2$, Young's modulus $E_2$, coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha_2$
- Common length: $L$
- Temperature rise: $\Delta T$ (assumed positive for heating)
- Both ends are **rigidly fixed** (no overall change in length is permitted)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Free Thermal Expansion (Imaginary, Unrestrained Case):**
If the two materials were *not* bonded and could expand independently, the free elongations would be:

$$\delta L_{1,free} = \alpha_1 \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\delta L_{2,free} = \alpha_2 \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$$

Without loss of generality, assume $\alpha_1 > \alpha_2$. Then $\delta L_{1,free} > \delta L_{2,free}$ ‚ÄĒ material 1 wants to grow more.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Identify the Need for a Restraining Force:**
The bonded interface demands that both materials reach the **same final length**. This is the **compatibility condition**. To enforce it, an internal axial force $P$ must develop at the interface such that:
- Material 1 is **compressed** by an amount $P L / (A_1 E_1)$
- Material 2 is **stretched** by an amount $P L / (A_2 E_2)$

The sum of these two "correction" deformations must exactly compensate the difference in free expansions.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compatibility Equation:**

$$\frac{P \cdot L}{A_1 \cdot E_1} + \frac{P \cdot L}{A_2 \cdot E_2} = (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the sum?** Because material 1 expands *more* and must be pushed back, while material 2 expands *less* and must be pulled forward. Both deformations of correction go in the *same* direction (towards each other), so they **add up** to fill the gap.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Equilibrium Consideration:**
Since both ends are externally fixed, the bar is in static equilibrium. The internal force $P$ in material 1 (compressive) and the equal-magnitude force $P$ in material 2 (tensile) form a self-equilibrating pair. **No external reaction is needed** beyond the end constraints.

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Solve for $P$:**

$$P \left[ \frac{1}{A_1 E_1} + \frac{1}{A_2 E_2} \right] = (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T$$

$$P = \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{1}{A_2 E_2}}$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Compute Individual Stresses:**

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P}{A_1} \quad \text{(compressive in material 1)}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{P}{A_2} \quad \text{(tensile in material 2)}$$

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Free expansion of material $i$ | $\delta L_i = \alpha_i L \Delta T$ | mm, m | Independent deformation |
| 2 | Compatibility equation | $\dfrac{PL}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{PL}{A_2 E_2} = (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) L \Delta T$ | mm | Both corrections add |
| 3 | Restoring force $P$ | $P = \dfrac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\,\Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{1}{A_2 E_2}}$ | N | Same magnitude, opposite sense in the two materials |
| 4 | Alternative form of $P$ | $P = \dfrac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\,\Delta T \cdot A_1 E_1 A_2 E_2}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$ | N | Rationalised denominator |
| 5 | Stress in material 1 | $\sigma_1 = P / A_1$ | N/mm¬≤ (MPa) | Compressive if $\alpha_1 > \alpha_2$ |
| 6 | Stress in material 2 | $\sigma_2 = P / A_2$ | N/mm¬≤ (MPa) | Tensile if $\alpha_1 > \alpha_2$ |
| 7 | Resultant stress (combined) | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A_1 + A_2}$ | N/mm¬≤ | If bar treated as a single unit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Used at KTU Board Exams:**
> - $\Delta T > 0$ (temperature **rise**) and $\alpha_1 > \alpha_2$ ‚Üí Material 1 is in **compression**, Material 2 is in **tension**.
> - $\Delta T < 0$ (temperature **fall/cooling**) ‚Üí Stresses **reverse** sign ‚ÄĒ Material 1 goes into tension, Material 2 into compression.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Temperature stresses in composite bars are of immense practical concern in:

1. **Bimetallic thermostats** ‚ÄĒ Deliberately engineered to exploit differential expansion; used in circuit breakers, fire alarms, and temperature gauges.
2. **Reinforced concrete** ‚ÄĒ Steel rebar ($\alpha \approx 12 \times 10^{-6}/¬įC$) embedded in concrete ($\alpha \approx 10 \times 10^{-6}/¬įC$) generates pre-compression in concrete when heated ‚ÄĒ beneficial for crack control.
3. **Composite railway tracks** ‚ÄĒ Steel rails on concrete sleepers experience differential expansion; design must account for induced stresses.
4. **Bridges and pipelines** ‚ÄĒ Expansion joints are precisely *designed* to relieve such stresses; their omission in poorly designed structures leads to buckling.
5. **Aerospace and turbine blades** ‚ÄĒ Multi-material blades must be analysed for thermal stress to prevent fatigue failure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 ‚ÄĒ General Two-Material Composite Bar (Most Common KTU Form)

**Given:**
- Materials 1 and 2, areas $A_1, A_2$, moduli $E_1, E_2$, expansion coefficients $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$, common length $L$, temperature rise $\Delta T$, ends fixed.

**Step 1:** Imagine removing the bond. The free thermal elongations are:

$$\delta L_{1,free} = \alpha_1 L \Delta T$$

$$\delta L_{2,free} = \alpha_2 L \Delta T$$

**Step 2:** If $\alpha_1 > \alpha_2$, then $\delta L_{1,free} > \delta L_{2,free}$. To equalise the lengths, an internal force $P$ is generated:
- $P$ acts **compressive** on material 1 (shortens it)
- $P$ acts **tensile** on material 2 (elongates it)

**Step 3:** Mechanical shortening of material 1 due to $P$:

$$\delta L_{1,mech} = \frac{P L}{A_1 E_1}$$

**Step 4:** Mechanical elongation of material 2 due to $P$:

$$\delta L_{2,mech} = \frac{P L}{A_2 E_2}$$

**Step 5:** The compatibility condition ‚ÄĒ final length of material 1 must equal final length of material 2:

$$\delta L_{1,free} - \delta L_{1,mech} = \delta L_{2,free} + \delta L_{2,mech}$$

**Step 6:** Rearranging (all "correction" terms on one side):

$$\frac{P L}{A_1 E_1} + \frac{P L}{A_2 E_2} = \delta L_{1,free} - \delta L_{2,free}$$

**Step 7:** Substituting:

$$\frac{P L}{A_1 E_1} + \frac{P L}{A_2 E_2} = \alpha_1 L \Delta T - \alpha_2 L \Delta T$$

**Step 8:** Factor $L$ on both sides and cancel:

$$P \left( \frac{1}{A_1 E_1} + \frac{1}{A_2 E_2} \right) = (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T$$

**Step 9:** Solve explicitly for $P$:

$$P = \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{1}{A_2 E_2}}$$

**Step 10:** Rationalising the denominator:

$$P = \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T \cdot A_1 E_1 \cdot A_2 E_2}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

**Step 11:** Stresses:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P}{A_1} \quad , \quad \sigma_2 = \frac{P}{A_2}$$

### Derivation 2 ‚ÄĒ Special Case: Equal Areas ($A_1 = A_2 = A$)

If both materials have the same cross-sectional area, the formula simplifies beautifully.

**Step 1:** Substitute $A_1 = A_2 = A$ into the general $P$ expression:

$$P = \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{A E_1} + \dfrac{1}{A E_2}} = \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T \cdot A}{ \dfrac{1}{E_1} + \dfrac{1}{E_2} }$$

**Step 2:** Simplify using $\dfrac{1}{E_1} + \dfrac{1}{E_2} = \dfrac{E_1 + E_2}{E_1 E_2}$:

$$P = \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T \cdot A \cdot E_1 E_2}{E_1 + E_2}$$

**Step 3:** Stress in each material (same area, so):

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T \cdot E_1 E_2}{E_1 + E_2} \quad \text{(compressive)}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T \cdot E_1 E_2}{E_1 + E_2} \quad \text{(tensile)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** When $A_1 = A_2$, the two stresses are **numerically equal** in magnitude. This is a frequently-tested KTU scenario.

### Derivation 3 ‚ÄĒ Three-Material Composite Bar (Extension)

For three materials in parallel, bonded together with a common length $L$ and subjected to $\Delta T$:

**Step 1:** Free expansions: $\delta L_i^{free} = \alpha_i L \Delta T$ for $i = 1, 2, 3$.

**Step 2:** Let $\alpha_1$ be the largest. Each material carries an internal force $P_i$ such that the *mechanical* deformation of the $i$-th material equals the difference between its free expansion and that of a reference material (say material 1).

**Step 3:** Compatibility: total mechanical deformation in each material = difference from material 1's free expansion.

$$\frac{P_1 L}{A_1 E_1} = 0 \quad \text{(reference, no correction needed)}$$

$$\frac{P_2 L}{A_2 E_2} = (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) L \Delta T$$

$$\frac{P_3 L}{A_3 E_3} = (\alpha_1 - \alpha_3) L \Delta T$$

**Step 4:** Equilibrium: External ends provide reaction $R$. The internal forces must sum to zero (no net external force on the bar), so:

$$P_1 + P_2 + P_3 = R$$

But since ends are fixed and there is no external axial load other than the constraint reactions, the **sum of internal forces in the materials = end reaction** $R$. With materials beyond the reference, this can be solved systematically.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module Coverage:** The two-material case is the **primary focus** of Module 1. Three-material problems are occasionally given as 14-mark questions but typically as a sub-part.

### Numerical Worked Example (Model KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A composite bar consists of a steel rod (Area = 600 mm¬≤, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm¬≤, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$/¬įC) bonded to a brass tube (Area = 1200 mm¬≤, $E = 1 \times 10^5$ N/mm¬≤, $\alpha = 18 \times 10^{-6}$/¬įC). The length is 500 mm. If the temperature rises by 60¬įC, find the stresses developed. Assume ends are fixed.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify:**
- $\alpha_1 = 18 \times 10^{-6}$/¬įC (brass ‚ÄĒ higher, will be in compression)
- $\alpha_2 = 12 \times 10^{-6}$/¬įC (steel ‚ÄĒ lower, will be in tension)
- $A_1 = 1200$ mm¬≤, $E_1 = 1 \times 10^5$ N/mm¬≤ (brass)
- $A_2 = 600$ mm¬≤, $E_2 = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm¬≤ (steel)
- $\Delta T = 60$¬įC, $L = 500$ mm

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute $A_1 E_1$ and $A_2 E_2$:**

$$A_1 E_1 = 1200 \times 1 \times 10^5 = 12 \times 10^7 \text{ N}$$

$$A_2 E_2 = 600 \times 2 \times 10^5 = 12 \times 10^7 \text{ N}$$

(Symmetric case!)

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute $P$:**

$$P = \frac{(18 - 12) \times 10^{-6} \times 60 \times 12 \times 10^7 \times 12 \times 10^7}{12 \times 10^7 + 12 \times 10^7}$$

$$P = \frac{6 \times 10^{-6} \times 60 \times 144 \times 10^{14}}{24 \times 10^7}$$

$$P = \frac{6 \times 60 \times 144 \times 10^8}{24 \times 10^7} = \frac{51840 \times 10^8}{24 \times 10^7}$$

$$P = 21600 \text{ N} = 21.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Compute stresses:**

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P}{A_1} = \frac{21600}{1200} = 18 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compressive in brass)}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{P}{A_2} = \frac{21600}{600} = 36 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (tensile in steel)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of Equilibrium:** The total internal force on the fixed ends must be zero net. Since material 1 is in compression and material 2 in tension with the same magnitude $P$, the end reactions are indeed self-equilibrating. ‚úď

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 ‚ÄĒ Physical Setup of Composite Bar

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Fixed Support] --> B[Material 1 - Higher alpha - Brass]
    B --> C[Material 2 - Lower alpha - Steel]
    C --> D[Fixed Support]
    E[Temperature Change őĒT] -.-> B
    E -.-> C
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style C fill:#64748b,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
```

### Diagram 2 ‚ÄĒ Force Flow and Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Input[Input: Temperature Change őĒT] --> Free1[Free Expansion of Material 1: alpha1 √ó L √ó őĒT]
    Input --> Free2[Free Expansion of Material 2: alpha2 √ó L √ó őĒT]
    Free1 --> Compare{Is alpha1 ‚Č† alpha2?}
    Free2 --> Compare
    Compare -->|Yes| Comp[Compatibility Enforced by Bonded Interface]
    Compare -->|No| NoStress[No Thermal Stress - Both Expand Equally]
    Comp --> Mech1[Mechanical Shortening in Material 1: P√óL / A1√óE1]
    Comp --> Mech2[Mechanical Elongation in Material 2: P√óL / A2√óE2]
    Mech1 --> EqForce[Equilibrium: Force P = Self-Equilibrating]
    Mech2 --> EqForce
    EqForce --> SolveP[Solve: P = alpha1 minus alpha2 √ó őĒT √ó A1E1A2E2 / A1E1 plus A2E2]
    SolveP --> Stress1[Stress in Material 1: sigma1 = P / A1 - Compressive]
    SolveP --> Stress2[Stress in Material 2: sigma2 = P / A2 - Tensile]
    Stress1 --> Output[Output: Magnitudes of Thermal Stresses]
    Stress2 --> Output
    style Input fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff
    style NoStress fill:#10b981,color:#ffffff
    style Output fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style SolveP fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000
```

### Diagram 3 ‚ÄĒ Stress Distribution in the Two Materials

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph "Material 1 (alpha1 > alpha2)"
        M1A[Free Expansion: alpha1 L őĒT] --> M1B[Compression due to P: P L / A1 E1]
        M1B --> M1C[Net Deformation: alpha2 L őĒT]
    end
    subgraph "Material 2 (alpha2)"
        M2A[Free Expansion: alpha2 L őĒT] --> M2B[Tension due to P: P L / A2 E2]
        M2B --> M2C[Net Deformation: alpha2 L őĒT]
    end
    M1C -.Equals.-> M2C
    style M1A fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000
    style M1B fill:#ef4444,color:#ffffff
    style M1C fill:#22c55e,color:#ffffff
    style M2A fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000
    style M2B fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff
    style M2C fill:#22c55e,color:#ffffff
```

### Diagram 4 ‚ÄĒ Problem-Solving Flowchart (KTU Exam Strategy)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Read the Problem] --> Identify[Identify: A1, A2, E1, E2, alpha1, alpha2, L, őĒT]
    Identify --> Check{End Conditions}
    Check -->|Both Ends Fixed| Constrained[Constrained Case - Use Thermal Stress Formula]
    Check -->|One End Free| Free[Free Expansion Case - No Thermal Stress]
    Constrained --> CompareAlpha[Determine which alpha is larger]
    CompareAlpha --> ApplyFormula[Apply: P = alpha1 - alpha2 √ó őĒT √ó A1E1A2E2 / A1E1 plus A2E2]
    ApplyFormula --> ComputeP[Compute numerical value of P]
    ComputeP --> ComputeSigma[Compute sigma1 = P / A1 and sigma2 = P / A2]
    ComputeSigma --> Sign[Assign signs: compression for higher alpha, tension for lower alpha]
    Sign --> Verify[Verify equilibrium and units]
    Verify --> Final[Final Answer with Units: N/mm¬≤ or MPa]
    style Start fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    style Final fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style ApplyFormula fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style Verify fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff
```
