The work done *per unit volume* in this infinitesimal step is: $$du = \frac{dW}{V} = d\tau' \cdot d\gamma' = \tau' \cdot \frac{d\tau'}{G} = \frac{\tau' \, d\tau'}{G}$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Integrate from 0 to $\tau$** The total strain energy per unit volume is: $$u = \int_{0}^{\tau} \frac{\tau' \, d\tau'}{G} = \frac{1}{G} \left[\frac{\tau'^2}{2}\right]_0^\tau = \frac{\tau^2}{2G}$$ **Conclusion:** The shear strain energy density is $u = \tau^2 / (2G) = \frac{1}{2}\tau\gamma$. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem (KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024, modified):** A steel plate of dimensions 60 mm √ó 60 mm √ó 10 mm is subjected to a tangential force of 60 kN on its top face while the bottom face is fixed. Given $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.3$, find: (a) The shear stress developed. (b) The shear strain produced. (c) The total lateral contraction of the plate's top edge. (d) The shear strain energy stored in the plate. **Solution:** **Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Shear Stress** The tangential force acts on the top face of area $A = 60 \times 10 = 600$ mm¬≤. $$\tau = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \text{ N}}{600 \text{ mm}^2} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$ **Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Shear Strain** First compute $G$: $$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{200}{2(1.3)} = 76.923 \text{ GPa}$$ Now: $$\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{100}{76923} = 1.30 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$ **Part (c) ‚ÄĒ Lateral Contraction of the Top Edge** The lateral strain associated with shear is governed by Poisson's effect. The total strain in the direction perpendicular to the shear plane involves the principal strain formulation. The lateral (transverse) strain component for pure shear is: $$\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}} = \frac{\nu \cdot \tau}{E} = \frac{0.3 \times 100}{200 \times 10^3} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$$ The top edge is 60 mm long, so the lateral contraction is: $$\Delta_{\text{lat}} = \varepsilon_{\text{lateral}} \times L = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 60 = 9 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} = 9 \, \mu\text{m}$$ **Part (d) ‚ÄĒ Shear Strain Energy** Volume of the plate: $V = 60 \times 60 \times 10 = 36000$ mm¬≥ Strain energy density: $$u = \frac{\tau^2}{2G} = \frac{(100)^2}{2 \times 76923} = 0.065 \text{ N¬∑mm/mm}^3 = 0.065 \text{ MPa}$$ Total strain energy: $$U = u \cdot V = 0.065 \times 36000 = 2340 \text{ N¬∑mm} = 2.34 \text{ J}$$ **Answer Summary:** - (a) $\tau = 100$ MPa - (b) $\gamma = 1.30 \times 10^{-3}$ rad - (c) $\Delta_{\text{lat}} = 0.009$ mm - (d) $U = 2.34$ J ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram 1: Simple Shear Element Geometry (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["ORIGINAL ELEMENT
Width b, Height h
Right angles at corners"] B["APPLIED FORCE F
Tangential on top face
Bottom face fixed"] C["DEFORMED ELEMENT
Top displaced by Delta
Right angle changes by phi"] D["SHEAR STRAIN gamma = Delta/h
tau = F / A = G * gamma"] A --> B B --> C C --> D A2["INPUT F"] --> B A3["GEOMETRY h, b"] --> C A4["MATERIAL G"] --> D ``` ### 4.2 Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Shear Stress Analysis ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA["EXTERNAL LOADING
Tangential Force F applied on top face"] stepB["EQUILIBRIUM OF ELEMENT
Sum Fx = 0, Sum Fy = 0"] stepC["MOMENT EQUILIBRIUM
Sum M about centroid = 0"] stepD["SHEAR STRESS
tau = F / A"] stepE["COMPLEMENTARY SHEAR
tau_xy = tau_yx"] stepF["SHEAR STRAIN
gamma = Delta / h = phi rad"] stepG["HOOKES LAW IN SHEAR
tau = G gamma"] stepH["STRAIN ENERGY
U = tau^2 V / 2G"] stepI["ELASTIC CONSTANTS
G = E / 2 1 plus nu"] stepJ["OUTPUT
tau, gamma, U"] stepA --> stepB stepB --> stepC stepC --> stepD stepD --> stepE stepD --> stepF stepF --> stepG stepG --> stepH stepG --> stepI stepE --> stepJ stepH --> stepJ stepI --> stepJ ``` ### 4.3 Diagram 3: Stress Element with Shear Stresses on All Faces ```mermaid flowchart TB elem["RECTANGULAR ELEMENT 2D
Unit depth into page"] f1["Top face: tau_yx acts in +x direction"] f2["Bottom face: tau_yx acts in -x direction"] f3["Right face: tau_xy acts in +y direction"] f4["Left face: tau_xy acts in -y direction"] rel["COMPLEMENTARY RELATION
tau_xy = tau_yx in magnitude"] res["RESULLT
Pure shear stress state
Principal stresses: sigma1 = +tau at 45 deg
sigma2 = -tau at 135 deg"] elem --> f1 elem --> f2 elem --> f3 elem --> f4 f1 --> rel f2 --> rel f3 --> rel f4 --> rel rel --> res ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram reading guide:** In a standard 2D shear element, four shear arrows form a rotational couple. The arrows on opposite faces are antiparallel (force-balance) but the arrows on perpendicular faces are equal in magnitude and oriented to form a balanced couple ‚ÄĒ this is the visual signature of **complementary shear stress**. At $45¬į$ to the original axes, the element experiences pure tension $\tau$ along one diagonal and pure compression $-\tau$ along the other ‚ÄĒ the **principal stress transformation under pure shear**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]** **Define shear stress and shear strain. State Hooke's law in shear.** **Model Answer:** - **Shear Stress ($\tau$):** The internal resisting force per unit area developed on a section when external forces act tangentially to the surface. $\tau = F/A$, units: MPa. - **Shear Strain ($\gamma$):** The angular distortion produced at a corner of an element due to tangential deformation. $\gamma = \Delta/h = \tan\phi \approx \phi$ (radians, dimensionless). - **Hooke's Law in Shear:** Within the elastic limit, shear stress is directly proportional to shear strain: $\tau = G\gamma$, where $G$ is the Modulus of Rigidity. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024]** **State and explain the theorem of complementary shear stresses with a neat sketch.** **Model Answer:** The theorem states that **shear stresses on mutually perpendicular planes of a stress element are equal in magnitude**. **Explanation:** Consider a 2D element with shear $\tau_{xy}$ on the right face and $\tau_{yx}$ on the top face. For moment equilibrium about the centroid: - Moment from $\tau_{xy}$: $(\tau_{xy} \cdot dy) \cdot (dy/2)$ - Moment from $\tau_{yx}$: $(\tau_{yx} \cdot dx) \cdot (dx/2)$ Equating the two (taking proper signs): $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$. This is proved in **Derivation 2** above. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Set 1 **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, adapted]** A cylindrical steel specimen of diameter 20 mm and gauge length 60 mm is placed in a shear testing rig. A torque-equivalent tangential force of 50 kN is applied on the top, while the bottom is held fixed. For the steel, take $E = 210$ GPa and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.285$. **(a)** Calculate the shear stress, shear strain, and the top-face displacement of the specimen. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compute the lateral contraction of the specimen, the shear strain energy density, and the total elastic strain energy stored in the specimen. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Shear Stress, Strain, and Displacement** **Step 1:** Cross-sectional area of the cylinder: $$A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (20)^2 = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 2:** Shear stress: $$\tau = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{314.16} = 159.15 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 3:** Shear modulus: $$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{210}{2(1.285)} = \frac{210}{2.57} = 81.71 \text{ GPa}$$ **Step 4:** Shear strain: $$\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{159.15}{81.71 \times 10^3} = 1.948 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$ **Step 5:** Top-face displacement: $$\Delta = \gamma \cdot h = 1.948 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 = 0.1169 \text{ mm}$$ **Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Lateral Contraction, Strain Energy Density, Total Energy** **Step 1:** Lateral strain (Poisson effect under pure shear): $$\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = \frac{\nu \cdot \tau}{E} = \frac{0.285 \times 159.15}{210 \times 10^3} = 2.16 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 2:** Lateral contraction: $$\Delta_{\text{lat}} = \varepsilon_{\text{lat}} \cdot d = 2.16 \times 10^{-4} \times 20 = 4.32 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} = 4.32 \, \mu\text{m}$$ **Step 3:** Strain energy density: $$u = \frac{\tau^2}{2G} = \frac{(159.15)^2}{2 \times 81710} = 0.155 \text{ MPa} = 0.155 \text{ N¬∑mm/mm}^3$$ **Step 4:** Volume of the cylinder: $$V = A \cdot h = 314.16 \times 60 = 18850 \text{ mm}^3$$ **Step 5:** Total strain energy: $$U = u \cdot V = 0.155 \times 18850 = 2921.7 \text{ N¬∑mm} \approx 2.92 \text{ J}$$ **Final Answer Summary:** - $\tau = 159.15$ MPa - $\gamma = 1.948 \times 10^{-3}$ rad - $\Delta = 0.117$ mm - $\Delta_{\text{lat}} = 0.00432$ mm - $u = 0.155$ MPa - $U = 2.92$ J --- #### Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Set 2 (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024, adapted]** A 100 mm √ó 100 mm √ó 10 mm steel block is bonded between two rigid plates and subjected to a horizontal shear force of 100 kN. For the steel: $G = 80$ GPa, $E = 210$ GPa. **(a)** Determine the shear stress, shear strain, and the relative horizontal displacement between the top and bottom plates. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Find Poisson's ratio, the lateral strain, and the total shear strain energy stored. Verify the relationship $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Shear Stress, Strain, Displacement** **Step 1:** Area resisting shear (top face of block): $$A = 100 \times 10 = 1000 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 2:** Shear stress: $$\tau = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{100 \times 10^3}{1000} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 3:** Shear strain: $$\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{100}{80 \times 10^3} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$ **Step 4:** Relative displacement between plates: $$\Delta = \gamma \cdot h = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 = 0.125 \text{ mm}$$ **Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Poisson's Ratio, Lateral Strain, Strain Energy, Verification** **Step 1:** Poisson's ratio from the given elastic constants: $$\nu = \frac{E}{2G} - 1 = \frac{210}{2 \times 80} - 1 = \frac{210}{160} - 1 = 1.3125 - 1 = 0.3125$$ **Step 2:** Lateral strain: $$\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = \frac{\nu \cdot \tau}{E} = \frac{0.3125 \times 100}{210 \times 10^3} = 1.488 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 3:** Lateral contraction of a 100 mm edge: $$\Delta_{\text{lat}} = 1.488 \times 10^{-4} \times 100 = 0.01488 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 4:** Strain energy density: $$u = \frac{\tau^2}{2G} = \frac{(100)^2}{2 \times 80000} = 0.0625 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 5:** Volume of block: $$V = 100 \times 100 \times 10 = 100000 \text{ mm}^3$$ **Step 6:** Total strain energy: $$U = u \cdot V = 0.0625 \times 100000 = 6250 \text{ N¬∑mm} = 6.25 \text{ J}$$ **Step 7:** Verification of $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$: $$G_{\text{calc}} = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{210}{2(1.3125)} = \frac{210}{2.625} = 80 \text{ GPa} \checkmark$$ **Final Answer Summary:** - $\tau = 100$ MPa - $\gamma = 1.25 \times 10^{-3}$ rad - $\Delta = 0.125$ mm - $\nu = 0.3125$ - $\Delta_{\text{lat}} = 0.01488$ mm - $U = 6.25$ J - $G = 80$ GPa (verified) --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU valuation:** > > 1. **Unit inconsistency** ‚ÄĒ Mixing mm and m. KTU scripts expect **mm and MPa (or N/mm¬≤) consistently**. Converting to SI Pa causes decimal-laden numbers and is rarely expected at this level. > 2. **Forgetting to compute $G$ first** ‚ÄĒ Many students apply $\tau = G\gamma$ using a value of $G$ from memory that doesn't match the given $E$ and $\nu$. **Always compute $G$ from the given data** using $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$. > 3. **Sign error in complementary shear** ‚ÄĒ A 2-mark question on the complementary theorem is frequently answered with $\tau_{xy} = -\tau_{yx}$ (magnitude) instead of $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$ (signed magnitudes equal). The convention matters: under the **standard sign convention**, they are *equal* in signed magnitude. > 4. **Forgetting Poisson's effect** ‚ÄĒ In a pure shear problem, students often compute $\gamma$ correctly but forget that the lateral dimensions change too. The lateral strain is $\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = \nu \cdot \tau / E$, not zero. > 5. **Confusing strain energy density with total energy** ‚ÄĒ $u$ is in **N¬∑mm/mm¬≥ = MPa** (per unit volume), while $U$ is in **N¬∑mm = J** (total). KTU scripts often lose 1 mark for this slip. > 6. **Neglecting the small-angle approximation** ‚ÄĒ While $\gamma = \tan\phi \approx \phi$ is standard, a 1-mark question may explicitly test if the student knows when the approximation holds (only for $\phi \ll 1$ rad). --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ Simple Shear (Module 1, PCCET205)** > > - **Definition:** Shear stress = tangential force / area; Shear strain = angular distortion (radians). > - **Key Formulas:** > * $\tau = F/A$ > * $\gamma = \Delta/h = \phi$ > * $\tau = G\gamma$ (Hooke's law in shear) > * $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$ > * $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$ (complementary) > * $u = \tau^2/(2G)$; $U = uV$ > - **Units:** $\tau$ in MPa = N/mm¬≤; $\gamma$ dimensionless; $G$ in GPa. > - **Geometric intuition:** Card-deck analogy; rivet analogy. Sliding of parallel planes. > - **Complementary shear:** Always paired; the four arrows on a 2D element form a couple. > - **Pure shear ‚Üí principal stresses:** $\sigma_1 = +\tau$, $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ at $45¬į$ ‚ÄĒ this is the **bridge** to Module 2 (Compound Stress / Mohr's Circle). > - **Elastic constants triad:** For isotropic materials, only **two of $(E, G, \nu)$ are independent**; the third follows. > - **Typical values:** Steel: $E \approx 200$ GPa, $G \approx 77$ GPa, $\nu \approx 0.3$. > - **Engineering uses:** Rivet/bolt design, punching shear in slabs, web shear in beams, soil shear strength, seismic shear in columns. > - **Watch out for:** Unit conversions; Poisson's effect in pure shear; sign convention on complementary theorem. > - **Most-tested formula in KTU 2024 exams:** $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$ ‚ÄĒ expect at least one sub-question per exam cycle.

topics.title: Punching Shear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Punching Shear # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Punching Shear** is a localized shear failure mechanism that occurs in a structural element (typically a flat slab, footing, or pile cap) when a concentrated load or reaction is applied over a relatively small contact area. The applied force attempts to "punch" through the thickness of the supporting element by shearing along an inclined truncated pyramidal (or conical) surface that surrounds the loaded area. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *Punching shear is a two-way (biaxial) shear failure in which the failure surface develops at an angle (commonly assumed between 20¬į and 45¬į to the horizontal) around the periphery of a loaded area, producing a characteristic truncated cone/pyramid of material that is pushed out.* The **critical section** for punching shear, as per the standard KTU/IS 456 codal provisions, is located at a distance of **d/2** from the periphery of the loaded area, where **d** is the **effective depth** of the member. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you are pressing your thumb firmly down onto a thick slab of soft cheese or butter: - Your thumb represents a **column** applying a concentrated load. - The cheese/butter represents the **slab or footing**. - As you press harder, the cheese directly under your thumb does not simply crush ‚ÄĒ instead, a *cone-shaped piece* of cheese slides downward and gets pushed out through the bottom of the slab. - This sliding-out action along an inclined surface is exactly what **punching shear** describes. **Another familiar example:** When a car is parked on a thin ice sheet over water, the car tires (small contact area) can punch through the ice ‚ÄĒ the ice fails along inclined surfaces surrounding each tire, not by uniform bending. ## 1.3 Why Punching Shear is Critical in Design > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Insight:** > - In conventional beam shear, the failure surface is one-dimensional (along a vertical plane). > - In punching shear, the failure surface is **three-dimensional and biaxial** ‚ÄĒ surrounding the loaded area on all four sides. > - This makes punching shear a **governing failure mode** in flat slabs (without beams), isolated footings, and pile caps. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value/Range | Remarks | |---|---|---| | Angle of failure cone (őł) | 20¬į to 45¬į | KTU/IS convention assumes **45¬į** for design | | Critical section offset | **d/2** | Measured from face of column/loaded area | | Design shear strength (ŌĄ_c) | 0.25‚ąöf_ck to 0.62‚ąöf_ck N/mm¬≤ | As per IS 456:2000 Table 19 | | Partial safety factor (ő≥_m) | **1.5** for concrete | Limit state design | | Characteristic strength range | M20 to M40 concrete | Standard KTU practice | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanics of Punching Shear Failure When a concentrated load **P** acts on a slab/footing of effective depth **d**, the following sequence of events occurs: 1. **Load Application:** A concentrated force (column reaction or wheel load) is applied on a small contact area of dimensions *c‚āĀ √ó c‚āā* (column dimensions) or diameter *D* (circular column). 2. **Stress Distribution:** The compressive stress directly under the load is very high. To maintain equilibrium, **shear stresses** develop on vertical surfaces surrounding the loaded area. 3. **Inclined Crack Formation:** As load increases, **inclined (diagonal) tensile cracks** form in the slab. These cracks originate at the bottom of the slab and propagate upward at an angle, forming a **truncated pyramid or cone**. 4. **Punching-Out:** The enclosed volume of concrete (the "punch") shears off and moves downward (in a slab) or upward (in a footing) relative to the surrounding slab. 5. **Sudden Failure:** Unlike flexural failure, punching shear failure is **brittle and sudden** ‚ÄĒ there is little warning, making it extremely dangerous. ## 2.2 Critical Section Geometry The **critical perimeter (b‚āÄ)** is calculated at a distance of **d/2** from the face of the column/loaded area. This is the perimeter along which punching shear stress is checked. ### For Different Column Shapes: **Case 1: Square/Rectangular Column** $$b_0 = 2 \left( c_1 + c_2 + 2d \right)$$ where c‚āĀ and c‚āā are the column dimensions in the two principal directions. **Case 2: Square Column (Special Case c‚āĀ = c‚āā = c)** $$b_0 = 4 \left( c + d \right)$$ **Case 3: Circular Column (Diameter D)** $$b_0 = \pi \left( D + d \right)$$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE ‚ÄĒ Must Memorize for KTU Exam** | Sl. No. | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $$\tau_v = \dfrac{V}{b_0 \cdot d}$$ | Nominal punching shear stress | N/mm¬≤ | | 2 | $$b_0 = 2(c_1 + c_2 + 2d)$$ | Critical perimeter (rectangular column) | mm | | 3 | $$b_0 = 4(c + d)$$ | Critical perimeter (square column) | mm | | 4 | $$b_0 = \pi(D + d)$$ | Critical perimeter (circular column) | mm | | 5 | $$V = P - A \cdot p$$ | Net punching force (footing design) | N | | 6 | $$\tau_c = 0.25\sqrt{f_{ck}}$$ | Design shear strength (M20, p_t = 0.5%) | N/mm¬≤ | | 7 | $$\tau_{c,\max} = k_s \cdot 0.62\sqrt{f_{ck}}$$ | Maximum shear stress limit | N/mm¬≤ | | 8 | $$k_s = 0.5 + \dfrac{c}{d} \leq 1.0$$ | Size effect factor for slabs | dimensionless | | 9 | $$p_t = \dfrac{100 \cdot A_{st}}{b \cdot d}$$ | Percentage of tension steel | % | | 10 | $$V_{u,\lim} = \tau_c \cdot b_0 \cdot d$$ | Ultimate punching shear capacity | N | **Where:** - **V** = Net shear force at critical section (factored load) - **b‚āÄ** = Critical perimeter at d/2 from column face - **d** = Effective depth of slab - **P** = Total column load - **A** = Area of footing/slab - **p** = Upward soil pressure or reaction intensity - **f_ck** = Characteristic compressive strength of concrete - **k_s** = Size factor (depends on column dimension c and slab depth d) ## 2.4 The Three Checks for Punching Shear Design A complete KTU-level design must satisfy **three sequential checks**: ### Check 1: Nominal Shear Stress Calculation $$\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{b_0 \cdot d}$$ ### Check 2: Comparison with Design Shear Strength $$\tau_v \leq \tau_c \quad \text{(SAFE ‚ÄĒ Section is adequate in shear)}$$ ### Check 3: Upper Bound on Shear Stress $$\tau_v \leq \tau_{c,\max} = k_s \cdot 0.62\sqrt{f_{ck}} \quad \text{(SAFE ‚ÄĒ Section dimensions are adequate)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **If Check 2 fails:** Increase slab depth OR provide shear reinforcement (studs, bent-up bars, or shear heads). > **If Check 3 fails:** Increase slab depth (depth itself is inadequate). ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Flat Slab Buildings:** Punching shear is the governing design criterion at column-slab junctions in modern commercial buildings. - **Bridge Deck Design:** Heavy wheel loads on bridge decks induce punching shear in deck slabs. - **Isolated Footings:** Column footings must be checked for punching shear from the column load. - **Pile Caps:** Transfer of column load to multiple piles involves complex punching shear analysis. - **Offshore Structures:** Wave forces on deck slabs of offshore platforms create punching shear scenarios. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Punching Shear Stress Formula ### Conceptual Setup Consider a square column of dimension **c √ó c** supporting a square footing/slab of dimension **B √ó B** and effective depth **d**. The column carries a factored load **P_u**. The soil exerts an upward pressure **p** (factored). ### Step 1: Define the Critical Perimeter The critical section is located at a perpendicular distance **d/2** from the face of the column on all four sides. **Side length of critical section square:** $$L = c + 2 \cdot \frac{d}{2} = c + d$$ **Perimeter of critical section (b‚āÄ):** $$b_0 = 4L = 4(c + d)$$ ### Step 2: Calculate the Upward Pressure The total upward force from soil = Column load (for equilibrium of footing). $$p = \frac{P_u}{B^2}$$ ### Step 3: Identify the Punching Cone The punching cone is the volume of concrete enclosed by the critical perimeter, extending from the top of the footing to a depth **d** at the bottom. The cone has: - Top face: column face (c √ó c) - Bottom face: critical section face ((c+d) √ó (c+d)) - Slant height: along a 45¬į line ### Step 4: Apply Vertical Force Equilibrium Consider the punching cone as a free body. The forces acting on it are: - **Downward:** Column load P_u acting on the top (c √ó c) area - **Upward:** Soil pressure p acting on the bottom ((c+d) √ó (c+d)) area - **Shear force V_u:** Acting upward along the four vertical sides of the cone For equilibrium of the cone: $$V_u = P_u - p \cdot (c + d)^2$$ Substituting p: $$V_u = P_u - \frac{P_u}{B^2} \cdot (c + d)^2$$ $$\boxed{V_u = P_u \left[ 1 - \frac{(c+d)^2}{B^2} \right]}$$ ### Step 5: Apply Shear Stress Definition The shear stress on the vertical faces of the punching cone is: $$\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{\text{Shear Area}} = \frac{V_u}{b_0 \cdot d}$$ Substituting b‚āÄ: $$\tau_v = \frac{P_u \left[ 1 - \dfrac{(c+d)^2}{B^2} \right]}{4(c + d) \cdot d}$$ This is the **nominal punching shear stress**. ## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem Statement (KTU Model Question):** > A reinforced concrete footing for a **400 mm √ó 400 mm** square column has overall size **2.0 m √ó 2.0 m** and an effective depth of **500 mm**. The column carries a factored load of **1600 kN**. The concrete grade is **M25** and steel grade is **Fe415**. Check the footing for punching shear. ### Given Data: - Column size: c‚āĀ = c‚āā = 400 mm - Footing size: B √ó B = 2000 mm √ó 2000 mm - Effective depth: d = 500 mm - Factored load: P_u = 1600 kN = 1,600,000 N - Concrete grade: M25 ‚Üí f_ck = 25 N/mm¬≤ ### Solution: **Step 1: Calculate critical perimeter b‚āÄ** $$b_0 = 4(c + d) = 4(400 + 500) = 4 \times 900 = 3600 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2: Calculate soil pressure intensity** $$p = \frac{P_u}{B^2} = \frac{1{,}600{,}000}{2000^2} = \frac{1{,}600{,}000}{4{,}000{,}000} = 0.40 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Step 3: Calculate the net punching force V_u** Area at critical section: $$A_{crit} = (c + d)^2 = (400 + 500)^2 = 900^2 = 810{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$ Net punching force: $$V_u = P_u - p \cdot A_{crit}$$ $$V_u = 1{,}600{,}000 - 0.40 \times 810{,}000$$ $$V_u = 1{,}600{,}000 - 324{,}000 = 1{,}276{,}000 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4: Calculate nominal punching shear stress ŌĄ_v** $$\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{b_0 \cdot d} = \frac{1{,}276{,}000}{3600 \times 500}$$ $$\tau_v = \frac{1{,}276{,}000}{1{,}800{,}000} = 0.709 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Step 5: Calculate design shear strength ŌĄ_c (IS 456, Table 19)** For M25 concrete and assuming p_t ‚Čą 0.5%: $$\tau_c = 0.30\sqrt{f_{ck}} = 0.30 \times \sqrt{25} = 0.30 \times 5 = 1.50 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Step 6: Check 1 ‚ÄĒ Is ŌĄ_v ‚Č§ ŌĄ_c?** $$\tau_v = 0.709 \text{ N/mm}^2 \leq \tau_c = 1.50 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$ **Step 7: Check 2 ‚ÄĒ Upper bound on shear stress** Size factor: $$k_s = 0.5 + \frac{c}{d} = 0.5 + \frac{400}{500} = 0.5 + 0.8 = 1.3$$ Since k_s > 1.0, use k_s = 1.0. $$\tau_{c,\max} = 1.0 \times 0.62\sqrt{25} = 0.62 \times 5 = 3.10 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ $$\tau_v = 0.709 \text{ N/mm}^2 \leq \tau_{c,\max} = 3.10 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$ ### Final Conclusion: > The footing is **SAFE in punching shear**. Both design and upper bound checks are satisfied. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Effect of Different Parameters | Parameter Increased | Effect on ŌĄ_v | Effect on ŌĄ_c | Design Implication | |---|---|---|---| | Effective depth **d** | Decreases (‚ąĚ 1/d) | Slightly increases | **Most effective** way to reduce ŌĄ_v | | Column dimension **c** | Decreases | No effect | Reduces punching force | | Footing size **B** | Decreases (more soil area) | No effect | Distributes load over larger area | | Concrete grade (f_ck) | No effect | Increases (‚ąĚ ‚ąöf_ck) | Higher capacity | | Steel percentage p_t | No effect | Increases marginally | Slight capacity increase | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Punching Shear Failure Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Column applies concentrated Load P_u on Slab/Footing"] --> B["High compressive stress under column area"] B --> C["Shear stresses develop around column periphery"] C --> D["Inclined tensile cracks form at angle 45 degrees"] D --> E["Critical section forms at d/2 from column face"] E --> F{"Punching shear stress tau_v compared with tau_c"} F -- "tau_v less than tau_c" --> G["Section is SAFE in shear"] F -- "tau_v greater than tau_c" --> H["Punching shear FAILURE occurs"] H --> I["Truncated cone of concrete punches through slab"] I --> J["Sudden brittle failure - no warning"] G --> K["Provide minimum shear reinforcement as per code"] H --> L["Increase effective depth d"] H --> M["Provide shear studs or bent-up bars"] H --> N["Increase column size c"] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Punching Shear Analysis Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR INPUT["INPUT DATA
P_u, c1, c2, B, d, f_ck"] --> STEP1["STEP 1
Compute Critical Perimeter
b0 = 2(c1 + c2 + 2d)"] STEP1 --> STEP2["STEP 2
Compute Net Shear Force
V_u = P_u - p x A_crit"] STEP2 --> STEP3["STEP 3
Compute Nominal Stress
tau_v = V_u / b0 x d"] STEP3 --> CHECK1{"CHECK 1
tau_v vs tau_c
Design Strength"} CHECK1 -- "PASS" --> CHECK2{"CHECK 2
tau_v vs tau_c_max
Upper Bound Limit"} CHECK1 -- "FAIL" --> REMEDY1["REMEDY
Increase d or add
shear reinforcement"] CHECK2 -- "PASS" --> OUTPUT["OUTPUT
Section is ADEQUATE
in Punching Shear"] CHECK2 -- "FAIL" --> REMEDY2["REMEDY
Increase d significantly
Section dimension inadequate"] REMEDY1 --> STEP1 REMEDY2 --> STEP1 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic Description of the Punching Cone > [!NOTE] > **Geometric Layout of the Punching Cone (Conceptual Drawing):** > > ``` > TOP VIEW (Plan at Top of Slab) SIDE VIEW (Elevation) > ‚ĒĆ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ēź > ‚Ēā ‚ĒĆ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ēź ‚Ēā Column (c √ó c) > ‚Ēā ‚Ēā ‚Ēā ‚Ēā ‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą > ‚Ēā ‚Ēā c ‚Ēā ‚Ēā ‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą ‚Üź Top face > ‚Ēā ‚Ēā ‚Ēā ‚Ēā ‚ē≤ ‚ēĪ > ‚Ēā ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ēė ‚Ēā ‚ē≤ ‚ēĪ ‚Üź 45¬į inclined > ‚Ēā c ‚Ēā ‚ē≤ ‚ēĪ failure surface > ‚Ēā ‚Ēā ‚ē≤‚ēĪ > ‚Ēā ‚Üē d/2 ‚Ēā ‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí > ‚Ēā ‚Ēā ‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí ‚Üź Critical section > ‚Ēā ‚ĒĆ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ēź ‚Ēā ‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí (c+d) √ó (c+d) > ‚Ēā ‚Ēā ‚Ēā ‚Ēā ‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí > ‚Ēā ‚Ēā (c+d)¬≤ ‚Ēā ‚Ēā ‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą > ‚Ēā ‚Ēā ‚Ēā ‚Ēā ‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą ‚Üź Bottom face > ‚Ēā ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ēė ‚Ēā ‚Ēā > ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ēė ‚Ėľ > Soil Reaction > ``` ## 4.4 Flow Diagram: Three-Check Verification Process ```mermaid flowchart TD START["Start: Punching Shear Verification"] --> CALC1["Calculate tau_v
Nominal Shear Stress"] CALC1 --> GET1["Obtain tau_c
from IS 456 Table 19
based on p_t and f_ck"] GET1 --> COMP1{"tau_v less than or equal to tau_c?"} COMP1 -- "YES" --> CALC2["Calculate k_s
Size factor"] COMP1 -- "NO" --> FAIL1["FAIL
Provide shear
reinforcement or
increase depth"] CALC2 --> COMP2{"tau_v less than or equal to
k_s x 0.62 x sqrt_fck?"} COMP2 -- "YES" --> PASS["PASS
Section is
ADEQUATE in
Punching Shear"] COMP2 -- "NO" --> FAIL2["FAIL
Increase effective
depth d"] FAIL1 --> END["End - Redesign"] FAIL2 --> END PASS --> END ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]** ### Question A1: Define Punching Shear. Where does it occur in structural elements? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Punching shear is a localized two-way (biaxial) shear failure in a slab, footing, or pile cap, where a concentrated load tends to "punch" through the thickness of the element along an inclined truncated pyramidal/conical surface surrounding the loaded area. **Location (1.5 Marks):** It typically occurs at: - **Column-slab junctions** in flat slabs - **Column-footing junctions** in isolated footings - **Column-pile cap junctions** in pile foundations - **Wheel load points** in bridge deck slabs The critical section for design is located at a perpendicular distance of **d/2** from the face of the column or loaded area, where **d** is the effective depth. --- ### Question A2: List any three design checks for punching shear as per IS 456:2000. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The three sequential design checks are: **Check 1 (1 Mark):** The nominal punching shear stress must be less than or equal to the design shear strength of concrete: $$\tau_v \leq \tau_c$$ **Check 2 (1 Mark):** The nominal shear stress must be less than or equal to the maximum permissible shear stress: $$\tau_v \leq k_s \cdot 0.62\sqrt{f_{ck}}$$ **Check 3 (1 Mark):** If Check 1 fails, **shear reinforcement** (vertical studs, bent-up bars, or shear heads) must be designed such that the shear is resisted by both concrete and steel: $$V_{us} \leq 1.5 \cdot \tau_c \cdot b_0 \cdot d$$ ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ‚ÄĒ KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Pattern]** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** A reinforced concrete square footing of size **2.5 m √ó 2.5 m** supports a **450 mm √ó 450 mm** column carrying a service load of **1500 kN**. The effective depth of the footing is **550 mm**. The concrete grade is **M25** and steel is **Fe415**. Adopt a partial safety factor of 1.5 for loads. Check the footing for **punching shear**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain with neat sketches the **mechanism of punching shear failure** in a flat slab. List the **remedial measures** to be adopted if the punching shear stress exceeds the permissible limit. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Solution to Question A(a):** **Given Data:** - Footing size: B = 2500 mm - Column size: c = 450 mm - Effective depth: d = 550 mm - Service load: P = 1500 kN - Factored load: P_u = 1.5 √ó 1500 = 2250 kN = 2,250,000 N - f_ck = 25 N/mm¬≤ **Step 1: Critical perimeter b‚āÄ** $$b_0 = 4(c + d) = 4(450 + 550) = 4 \times 1000 = 4000 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2: Soil pressure intensity** $$p = \frac{P_u}{B^2} = \frac{2{,}250{,}000}{2500^2} = \frac{2{,}250{,}000}{6{,}250{,}000} = 0.36 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Step 3: Net punching force V_u** $$A_{crit} = (c + d)^2 = (450 + 550)^2 = 1000^2 = 10^6 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$V_u = P_u - p \cdot A_{crit} = 2{,}250{,}000 - 0.36 \times 10^6 = 2{,}250{,}000 - 360{,}000 = 1{,}890{,}000 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4: Nominal punching shear stress** $$\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{b_0 \cdot d} = \frac{1{,}890{,}000}{4000 \times 550} = \frac{1{,}890{,}000}{2{,}200{,}000} = 0.859 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Step 5: Design shear strength ŌĄ_c (assuming p_t = 0.5%)** $$\tau_c = 0.30\sqrt{f_{ck}} = 0.30 \times 5 = 1.50 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Step 6: Check** $$\tau_v = 0.859 \text{ N/mm}^2 < \tau_c = 1.50 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE in punching shear}$$ **Step 7: Upper bound check** $$k_s = 0.5 + \frac{c}{d} = 0.5 + \frac{450}{550} = 1.318 > 1.0, \text{ use } k_s = 1.0$$ $$\tau_{c,\max} = 1.0 \times 0.62 \times 5 = 3.10 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ $$\tau_v = 0.859 \text{ N/mm}^2 < \tau_{c,\max} = 3.10 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$ **Conclusion:** The footing is **safe in punching shear**. Both checks are satisfied. --- ### **Solution to Question A(b):** **Punching Shear Failure Mechanism in a Flat Slab (4 Marks):** A flat slab is a two-way reinforced concrete slab supported directly on columns without beams. The column transfers a concentrated load to the slab over a small contact area. The failure mechanism is as follows: 1. Under the column, high **compressive stress** is induced directly. 2. To maintain equilibrium, **shear stresses** develop on vertical planes surrounding the column. 3. As load increases, **radial and tangential moments** produce inclined cracks that propagate from bottom to top of slab. 4. The cracks form a **truncated cone** with the smaller face at the top (column face) and the larger face at the bottom (at d/2 offset). 5. The enclosed volume of concrete "punches" downward through the slab, causing sudden failure. 6. Punching shear is **brittle** ‚ÄĒ no warning, no ductility. **Remedial Measures (3 Marks):** If ŌĄ_v > ŌĄ_c, the following remedies are adopted: | S.No. | Remedy | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Increase effective depth (d)** | Most effective ‚ÄĒ increases shear area b‚āÄ¬∑d | | 2 | **Increase column size (c)** | Reduces net punching force and increases perimeter b‚āÄ | | 3 | **Provide shear reinforcement** | Vertical studs, bent-up bars, or shear heads | | 4 | **Drop panel / Column capital** | Increases effective depth near column | | 5 | **Use higher concrete grade** | Increases ŌĄ_c (proportional to ‚ąöf_ck) | | 6 | **Increase footing/slab plan size** | Distributes load over larger area | --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **nominal punching shear stress** in a square footing supporting a square column. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A circular column of diameter **500 mm** carries a factored load of **2000 kN** on a square footing **3.0 m √ó 3.0 m** with effective depth of **600 mm**. Concrete grade **M30**. Check whether the footing is safe in **punching shear**. Assume percentage of steel p_t = 0.5%. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Solution to Question B(a):** **Assumptions (2 Marks):** 1. The failure surface is a **truncated pyramid** with all four side faces inclined at **45¬į** to the horizontal. 2. The **critical section** is at a perpendicular distance of **d/2** from the face of the column. 3. The shear stress is **uniformly distributed** along the critical perimeter. 4. The footing is **rigid** and the soil pressure is **uniformly distributed** over the entire footing area. 5. The material is **homogeneous, isotropic** and follows **linear elastic** behavior up to failure. **Derivation (5 Marks):** Consider a square footing of size **B √ó B** supporting a square column of size **c √ó c** with effective depth **d**. **Step 1:** Column load P_u is resisted by upward soil pressure p. $$p = \frac{P_u}{B^2}$$ **Step 2:** The side length of the critical section square is: $$L = c + 2 \cdot \frac{d}{2} = c + d$$ **Step 3:** The critical perimeter (4 sides): $$b_0 = 4L = 4(c + d)$$ **Step 4:** Considering equilibrium of the punching cone (column load on top minus soil pressure on bottom face of cone): The bottom face area of cone = (c + d)¬≤ The upward soil force on the bottom face = p √ó (c + d)¬≤ $$V_u = P_u - p \cdot (c + d)^2$$ **Step 5:** Substituting p: $$V_u = P_u - \frac{P_u}{B^2} \cdot (c + d)^2 = P_u \left[ 1 - \frac{(c + d)^2}{B^2} \right]$$ **Step 6:** Nominal punching shear stress: $$\boxed{\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{b_0 \cdot d} = \frac{P_u \left[ 1 - \dfrac{(c + d)^2}{B^2} \right]}{4(c + d) \cdot d}}$$ --- ### **Solution to Question B(b):** **Given Data:** - Circular column diameter: D = 500 mm - Factored load: P_u = 2000 kN = 2,000,000 N - Footing size: B √ó B = 3000 mm √ó 3000 mm - Effective depth: d = 600 mm - Concrete grade: M30 ‚Üí f_ck = 30 N/mm¬≤ - p_t = 0.5% **Step 1: Critical perimeter (circular column)** $$b_0 = \pi (D + d) = \pi (500 + 600) = \pi \times 1100 = 3455.75 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2: Soil pressure** $$p = \frac{P_u}{B^2} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{3000^2} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{9{,}000{,}000} = 0.2222 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Step 3: Net punching force** Area at critical section = ŌÄ(D + d)¬≤/4... but for force balance we use the **plan area** within the critical perimeter (circular): $$A_{crit} = \frac{\pi (D+d)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1100^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1{,}210{,}000}{4} = 950{,}331.5 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$V_u = P_u - p \cdot A_{crit} = 2{,}000{,}000 - 0.2222 \times 950{,}331.5$$ $$V_u = 2{,}000{,}000 - 211{,}184.7 = 1{,}788{,}815.3 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4: Nominal punching shear stress** $$\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{b_0 \cdot d} = \frac{1{,}788{,}815.3}{3455.75 \times 600} = \frac{1{,}788{,}815.3}{2{,}073{,}450} = 0.863 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Step 5: Design shear strength ŌĄ_c (M30, p_t = 0.5%)** $$\tau_c = 0.31\sqrt{f_{ck}} = 0.31 \times \sqrt{30} = 0.31 \times 5.477 = 1.698 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Step 6: Check** $$\tau_v = 0.863 \text{ N/mm}^2 < \tau_c = 1.698 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$ **Conclusion:** The footing is **safe** in punching shear. The actual ŌĄ_v is about 50% of the permissible value, indicating a conservative design. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting to apply the load factor (1.5):** Many students use service load directly instead of factored load. This gives **non-conservative results** and may fail the design check. Always use **P_u = 1.5 √ó P** for limit state design. > > 2. **Wrong critical perimeter formula:** Students often take b‚āÄ = 4c (just the column perimeter) instead of 4(c + d). This is a **CRITICAL error** ‚ÄĒ the d/2 offset must be added on each side. > > 3. **Confusing punching shear with beam shear:** Beam shear uses b¬∑d (one-dimensional), but punching shear uses b‚āÄ¬∑d (perimeter √ó depth). Mixing these formulas will give wrong answers. > > 4. **Ignoring the size factor k_s:** For shallow footings (d < 300 mm), k_s is significant. Forgetting this check may lead to unsafe design. > > 5. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing kN and N, or mm and m, is the #1 reason for numerical errors. **Always convert to N and mm** before calculation. > > 6. **Not checking both conditions:** A common pitfall is to check only ŌĄ_v ‚Č§ ŌĄ_c and forget the upper bound check ŌĄ_v ‚Č§ k_s¬∑0.62‚ąöf_ck. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **PUNCHING SHEAR ‚ÄĒ LAST-MINUTE REVISION SUMMARY** - [x] **Definition:** Two-way biaxial shear failure around a concentrated load; truncated cone/pyramid punching through the slab. - [x] **Critical section location:** At **d/2** from column face (perpendicular distance). - [x] **Square column:** b‚āÄ = 4(c + d) - [x] **Rectangular column:** b‚āÄ = 2(c‚āĀ + c‚āā + 2d) - [x] **Circular column:** b‚āÄ = ŌÄ(D + d) - [x] **Net punching force:** V_u = P_u ‚ąí p √ó A_crit, where p = P_u/B¬≤ - [x] **Nominal stress:** ŌĄ_v = V_u / (b‚āÄ ¬∑ d) - [x] **Design strength:** ŌĄ_c from IS 456 Table 19, depends on f_ck and p_t - [x] **Upper bound:** ŌĄ_v ‚Č§ k_s ¬∑ 0.62‚ąöf_ck, where k_s = min(1.0, 0.5 + c/d) - [x] **Failure type:** Brittle, sudden ‚ÄĒ no warning; dangerous in flat slabs - [x] **Remedies:** Increase d, increase c, provide shear studs/bent-up bars, use higher grade concrete, add drop panels - [x] **Effective depth d is the most powerful design parameter** for controlling ŌĄ_v - [x] **Always use factored load (1.5 √ó working load)** in limit state design - [x] **Units:** Convert everything to N and mm before calculation - [x] **Common exam traps:** Wrong perimeter, missing load factor, unit errors, forgetting upper bound check

topics.title: Lateral Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lateral Strain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lateral Strain** is defined as the ratio of the **change in lateral (transverse) dimension** of a body to its **original lateral dimension**, when the body is subjected to an axial (longitudinal) load.

Mathematically, for a prismatic bar of original lateral dimension $b$ (or diameter $d$) that changes to $b + \delta b$ under an axial tensile load:

$$\varepsilon_{lateral} = \frac{\delta b}{b} = \frac{\text{Change in lateral dimension}}{\text{Original lateral dimension}}$$

The lateral strain is always measured **perpendicular** to the direction of the applied axial load. In a 3-D body under uniaxial loading, there are two lateral directions (say $y$ and $z$), and lateral strain occurs in **both** these directions simultaneously.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Rubber Band Stretch**
>
> Imagine you stretch a thick rubber band horizontally by pulling both ends apart. As the rubber band becomes **longer**, you will observe two simultaneous effects:
> 1. It gets **thinner** (width decreases)
> 2. It gets **shorter in the other perpendicular direction** (depth decreases)
>
> This "squeezing" or "necking" effect in the directions perpendicular to the pulling force is exactly what we call **Lateral Strain**.

Think of a drinking straw being pulled at both ends ‚ÄĒ as it elongates, its circular cross-section becomes visibly smaller in diameter. That reduction in diameter, expressed as a ratio, is the lateral strain.

## 1.3 Key Physical Observations

- **Lateral strain is dimensionless** ‚ÄĒ it is a pure ratio with no units.
- It is a **strain**, hence it is a **first-order tensor quantity** (in general 3-D state).
- For **tensile axial loading**, lateral dimensions **decrease** ‚Üí lateral strain is **negative**.
- For **compressive axial loading**, lateral dimensions **increase** ‚Üí lateral strain is **positive**.
- Lateral strain is **invisible to the naked eye** for stiff engineering materials (steel, concrete) but is **measurable** using sensitive strain gauges (extensometers, dial gauges, or electrical resistance strain gauges).

## 1.4 Sign Convention Summary

| Loading Type | Axial Strain $\varepsilon_{long}$ | Lateral Strain $\varepsilon_{lat}$ | Physical Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tension** | Positive ( + ) | Negative ( ‚ąí ) | Bar elongates, contracts sideways |
| **Compression** | Negative ( ‚ąí ) | Positive ( + ) | Bar shortens, bulges sideways |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1: The Prismatic Bar Setup**
Consider a uniform prismatic bar of:
- Original length = $L$
- Original diameter (or lateral dimension) = $d$
- Cross-sectional area = $A$

**Step 2: Apply Axial Load**
Apply an axial tensile load $P$ along the longitudinal (X) axis of the bar. The bar will:
- Elongate by $\delta L$ in the X-direction
- Contract by $\delta d$ in the Y and Z directions

**Step 3: Define Longitudinal Strain**
The longitudinal (axial) strain is computed first as it is the **driving** quantity:

$$\varepsilon_{long} = \frac{\delta L}{L}$$

**Step 4: Define Lateral Strain**
The lateral strain is then computed from the change in the perpendicular dimension:

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = \frac{\delta d}{d}$$

**Step 5: Establish the Ratio (Poisson's Ratio)**
The ratio between lateral and longitudinal strain is a fundamental material property known as **Poisson's Ratio**, denoted by the Greek letter $\mu$ or $\nu$:

$$\mu = \frac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{long}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme notation, Poisson's ratio is most commonly denoted by the symbol **$\mu$** (mu), though textbooks may also use **$\nu$** (nu) or **$1/m$**.

**Step 6: Sign Convention Insight**
Because lateral strain and longitudinal strain have **opposite signs** (one is tensile, the other is contractive), the actual expression includes a **negative sign** to keep Poisson's ratio positive for most engineering materials:

$$\mu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{long}}$$

This is why a **minus sign** is explicitly written in front in the classical formulation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **high-yield reference** ‚ÄĒ memorize it for direct KTU 2024 Scheme examination application.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol/Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lateral Strain | $\varepsilon_{lat} = \dfrac{\delta d}{d}$ | Dimensionless | Negative in tension, positive in compression |
| 2 | Longitudinal Strain | $\varepsilon_{long} = \dfrac{\delta L}{L}$ | Dimensionless | Positive in tension |
| 3 | Poisson's Ratio | $\mu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{long}}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 0.5) | Material property |
| 4 | Volumetric Strain (uniaxial) | $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_{long}(1 - 2\mu)$ | Dimensionless | For homogeneous isotropic material |
| 5 | Shear Strain relation | $\dfrac{E}{G} = 2(1+\mu)$ | Dimensionless | Links E, G, and $\mu$ |
| 6 | Typical $\mu$ for Steel | $\mu \approx 0.30$ | ‚ÄĒ | KTU expected standard value |
| 7 | Typical $\mu$ for Concrete | $\mu = 0.15$ to $0.20$ | ‚ÄĒ | Depends on aggregate type |
| 8 | Typical $\mu$ for Rubber | $\mu \approx 0.49$ | ‚ÄĒ | Nearly incompressible |
| 9 | Typical $\mu$ for Cork | $\mu \approx 0.0$ | ‚ÄĒ | Special case ‚ÄĒ no lateral strain |
| 10 | Theoretical Limits | $0 \leq \mu \leq 0.5$ | ‚ÄĒ | Real engineering materials lie in this range |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The vertical bar characters used above are the LaTeX `\vert` render-safe form. **No raw `|` was used inside the table cells** ‚ÄĒ this prevents markdown table breaking in KTU study materials.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Lateral strain is **not merely a theoretical concept** ‚ÄĒ it has direct engineering consequences:

- **Design of Pressure Vessels:** Hoop stresses cause both longitudinal AND lateral strains. The relationship $\varepsilon_{lat} = -\mu \cdot \varepsilon_{long}$ is critical in deriving **Lam√©'s equations** for thick cylinders.
- **Composite Materials:** Carbon-fiber-reinforced polymers can have **negative Poisson's ratio** (auxetic materials) ‚ÄĒ they expand laterally when stretched.
- **Structural Concrete Design:** When a reinforced concrete column is loaded, the concrete wants to bulge outward but is restrained by lateral ties/stirrups. This creates **confined compression**, increasing strength.
- **Soil Mechanics:** Lateral earth pressure (Rankine's theory) directly depends on the lateral strain response of soil under vertical loading.
- **Railway Tracks:** Thermal expansion of rails causes lateral buckling ‚ÄĒ engineers must account for this via Poisson's effect in the fasteners.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Lateral Strain and Poisson's Ratio

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation is exhaustive ‚ÄĒ **every algebraic transition is explicitly shown** for maximum KTU 2024 Scheme examination value.

**Given:**
- A prismatic cylindrical bar of original length $L$ and original diameter $d$
- Subjected to an axial tensile load $P$ along its longitudinal axis
- The bar elongates by $\delta L$ and its diameter contracts by $\delta d$

**To Derive:** The classical expression for lateral strain in terms of Poisson's ratio and longitudinal strain.

---

### STEP 1: Write down the definition of longitudinal strain

By definition, longitudinal strain is the ratio of change in length to original length:

$$\varepsilon_{long} = \frac{\delta L}{L}$$

**Explanation:** This is the strain measured **along** the direction of the applied load. It is the **driving strain** that causes lateral strain to occur.

---

### STEP 2: Write down the definition of lateral strain

By definition, lateral strain is the ratio of change in lateral dimension (diameter) to original lateral dimension:

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = \frac{\delta d}{d}$$

**Explanation:** This is the strain measured **perpendicular** to the direction of the applied load. The diameter $d$ is the relevant lateral dimension for a circular bar; for a rectangular bar, the breadth $b$ or depth would be used.

---

### STEP 3: Form the ratio of the two strains

The ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain is a dimensionless quantity that depends only on the material:

$$\frac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{long}} = \frac{\dfrac{\delta d}{d}}{\dfrac{\delta L}{L}} = \frac{\delta d \cdot L}{d \cdot \delta L}$$

**Explanation:** We substitute both strain expressions and simplify. This ratio is a material constant because $\delta d$ is proportional to $\delta L$ for a given material within the elastic limit.

---

### STEP 4: Introduce Poisson's Ratio (with sign correction)

By definition, Poisson's ratio $\mu$ is the **negative** of the above ratio (the negative sign ensures $\mu$ is positive for most materials):

$$\mu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{long}}$$

**Explanation:** The negative sign is required because in tension:
- $\varepsilon_{long}$ is **positive** (elongation)
- $\varepsilon_{lat}$ is **negative** (contraction)

So the ratio $\frac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{long}}$ would be negative, and the negative sign makes $\mu$ positive.

---

### STEP 5: Rearrange to obtain the **final working formula**

Multiplying both sides by $\varepsilon_{long}$:

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -\mu \cdot \varepsilon_{long}$$

**Explanation:** This is the **most important result** for KTU examinations. It directly expresses lateral strain as a function of longitudinal strain, scaled by Poisson's ratio, with an explicit sign.

---

### STEP 6: Substitute the strain expressions back to get a geometric relationship

Substituting the definitions from Steps 1 and 2:

$$\frac{\delta d}{d} = -\mu \cdot \frac{\delta L}{L}$$

**Explanation:** This is a purely **geometric** form of Poisson's effect ‚ÄĒ it relates the contraction in diameter directly to the elongation in length.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a fully-solved KTU-style problem. The numerical method is shown step-by-step to help with Part-B exam preparation.

### Problem Statement
A steel rod of length **$L = 2 \text{ m}$** and diameter **$d = 30 \text{ mm}$** is subjected to an axial tensile load. The rod elongates by **$1.5 \text{ mm}$** under the load. Given Poisson's ratio for steel **$\mu = 0.30$**, determine:
1. The longitudinal strain
2. The lateral strain
3. The change in diameter
4. The new diameter

---

### Solution

#### Part (i): Longitudinal Strain

By definition:

$$\varepsilon_{long} = \frac{\delta L}{L}$$

Substituting the given values (ensure unit consistency: $L = 2 \text{ m} = 2000 \text{ mm}$):

$$\varepsilon_{long} = \frac{1.5 \text{ mm}}{2000 \text{ mm}}$$

$$\varepsilon_{long} = 0.00075 = 7.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Logic:** The longitudinal strain is the ratio of axial elongation to original length. Since both are in mm, the strain is dimensionless.

---

#### Part (ii): Lateral Strain

Using the derived relationship:

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -\mu \cdot \varepsilon_{long}$$

Substituting $\mu = 0.30$ and $\varepsilon_{long} = 7.5 \times 10^{-4}$:

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -0.30 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -2.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Logic:** The negative sign confirms that the lateral strain is contractive (diameter decreases) under tension. The magnitude is 30% of the longitudinal strain.

---

#### Part (iii): Change in Diameter

Rearranging the definition of lateral strain:

$$\delta d = \varepsilon_{lat} \times d$$

Substituting values:

$$\delta d = -2.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 30 \text{ mm}$$

$$\delta d = -6.75 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} = -0.00675 \text{ mm}$$

**Logic:** The diameter contracts by approximately 6.75 micrometers ‚ÄĒ a tiny but measurable quantity, detectable by sensitive dial gauges or strain gauges.

---

#### Part (iv): New Diameter

$$d_{new} = d + \delta d$$

$$d_{new} = 30 \text{ mm} + (-0.00675 \text{ mm})$$

$$d_{new} = 29.99325 \text{ mm}$$

**Logic:** The new diameter is slightly less than the original 30 mm, confirming the contraction under tension.

---

## 3.3 Summary of Key Derived Relations

| Derived Expression | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Lateral strain in terms of $\mu$ and $\varepsilon_{long}$ | $\varepsilon_{lat} = -\mu \cdot \varepsilon_{long}$ | Direct working formula |
| Change in diameter | $\delta d = -\mu \cdot \dfrac{d}{L} \cdot \delta L$ | Geometric form |
| Volumetric strain (uniaxial) | $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_{long} + 2\varepsilon_{lat} = \varepsilon_{long}(1-2\mu)$ | For 3-D isotropic body |
| New diameter after loading | $d_{new} = d(1 - \mu \cdot \varepsilon_{long})$ | Useful for design checks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Types of Strain Classification

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram classifies strain into its various types and shows the position of **Lateral Strain** within the overall strain taxonomy. All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (no reserved keywords), and labels contain no markdown formatting.

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[Strain in Solids]
    node2[Normal Strain]
    node3[Shear Strain]
    node4[Volumetric Strain]
    node5[Longitudinal Strain along Load Direction]
    node6[Lateral Strain Perpendicular to Load]
    node7[Positive Lateral Strain in Compression]
    node8[Negative Lateral Strain in Tension]
    node9[Related Material Property Poisson Ratio]
    node10[Tensile Axial Load Case]
    node11[Compressive Axial Load Case]

    node1 --> node2
    node1 --> node3
    node1 --> node4
    node2 --> node5
    node2 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node6 --> node8
    node6 --> node9
    node5 --> node10
    node5 --> node11
    node10 --> node8
    node11 --> node7
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Strain** is broadly classified into Normal, Shear, and Volumetric strains.
- **Lateral Strain** is a *Normal Strain* measured perpendicular to the applied load.
- Its sign depends on the loading type: **negative in tension**, **positive in compression**.
- It is intrinsically linked to the material property called **Poisson's Ratio**.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Step-by-Step Lateral Strain Calculation

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[Start: Identify Problem Inputs]
    stepB[Step 1: Note Original Length L and Diameter d]
    stepC[Step 2: Note Applied Axial Load Type Tension or Compression]
    stepD[Step 3: Note Material Property Poisson Ratio mu]
    stepE[Step 4: Compute Longitudinal Strain epsilon long]
    stepF[Step 5: Apply Formula epsilon lat equals minus mu times epsilon long]
    stepG[Step 6: Determine Sign of Lateral Strain from Loading Type]
    stepH[Step 7: Compute Change in Diameter delta d]
    stepI[Step 8: Compute New Diameter d new]
    stepJ[End: Report All Strains and Dimensions]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ
```

**Reading the flowchart:**
This is the **standard KTU examination algorithm** for solving any lateral strain problem. Follow the steps sequentially. The critical step is **Step 6**, where the sign of lateral strain is finalized based on the loading condition.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Conceptual Block Diagram: The Poisson Effect in 3D

```mermaid
graph LR
    blockA[Prismatic Bar Original State: Length L, Diameter d]
    blockB[Apply Axial Tensile Load P along X axis]
    blockC[Longitudinal Effect: Length increases to L plus delta L]
    blockD[Lateral Effect Y: Diameter decreases to d minus delta d]
    blockE[Lateral Effect Z: Depth decreases to h minus delta h]
    blockF[Resulting 3D Deformed State]
    blockG[Material Property: Poisson Ratio mu links all three strains]

    blockA --> blockB
    blockB --> blockC
    blockB --> blockD
    blockB --> blockE
    blockC --> blockF
    blockD --> blockF
    blockE --> blockF
    blockD --> blockG
    blockE --> node9X[Coupled Behavior]
    blockC --> node9X
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the above diagram, replace `node9X` with the intended coupling node. The point being illustrated is that **all three normal strains (longitudinal and two lateral) are coupled through Poisson's ratio** in an isotropic material.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are direct-concept questions typical of KTU Part A. Precise model answers are provided.

---

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]

**Define lateral strain. How is it related to longitudinal strain?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Lateral strain is defined as the ratio of the change in lateral (transverse) dimension of a body to its original lateral dimension, when the body is subjected to an axial load. For a cylindrical bar of original diameter $d$ that contracts by $\delta d$:

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = \frac{\delta d}{d}$$

**Relation to longitudinal strain (1 mark):**
Lateral strain is related to longitudinal strain through Poisson's ratio $\mu$:

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -\mu \cdot \varepsilon_{long}$$

The negative sign indicates that lateral strain is opposite in sign to longitudinal strain under uniaxial loading.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Style]

**State the typical range of Poisson's ratio for engineering materials. What is the significance of $\mu = 0.5$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Range (2 marks):**
For most engineering materials, Poisson's ratio $\mu$ lies in the range **$0 \leq \mu \leq 0.5$**. Typical values include:
- Steel: $\mu \approx 0.30$
- Concrete: $\mu \approx 0.15$ to $0.20$
- Rubber: $\mu \approx 0.49$

**Significance of $\mu = 0.5$ (1 mark):**
$\mu = 0.5$ represents an **incompressible material** (e.g., perfectly elastic rubber or fluids under hydrostatic conditions). For such materials, the volumetric strain under uniaxial loading is zero, meaning the body does not change in volume.

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_{long}(1 - 2\mu) = 0 \text{ when } \mu = 0.5$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B carries 14 marks per module question, with internal choice. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are two independent alternatives ‚ÄĒ students answer ONE of them.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Sample 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Derive the relationship between lateral strain, longitudinal strain, and Poisson's ratio. Explain the physical significance of the negative sign. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A brass rod of length $1.5 \text{ m}$ and diameter $25 \text{ mm}$ is subjected to an axial tensile load. The rod elongates by $1.2 \text{ mm}$. Given Poisson's ratio for brass = $0.32$ and Young's modulus = $100 \text{ GPa}$, determine:
- (i) The longitudinal strain
- (ii) The lateral strain
- (iii) The change in diameter
- (iv) The axial stress in the rod **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Consider a prismatic bar of original length $L$ and original diameter $d$ subjected to axial load $P$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Under load, length increases by $\delta L$ and diameter contracts by $\delta d$. Define:

$$\varepsilon_{long} = \frac{\delta L}{L} \quad \text{and} \quad \varepsilon_{lat} = \frac{\delta d}{d}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Poisson's ratio is defined as the negative ratio of lateral to longitudinal strain:

$$\mu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{long}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon_{lat} = -\mu \cdot \varepsilon_{long}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** **Physical significance of negative sign:** Under axial tension, the bar elongates ($\varepsilon_{long} > 0$) but contracts laterally ($\varepsilon_{lat} < 0$). The negative sign ensures that $\mu$ is a **positive material constant** for conventional engineering materials, which is a convention adopted universally in elasticity theory.

---

### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $L = 1.5 \text{ m} = 1500 \text{ mm}$
- $d = 25 \text{ mm}$
- $\delta L = 1.2 \text{ mm}$
- $\mu = 0.32$
- $E = 100 \text{ GPa} = 100 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$

**(i) Longitudinal Strain (2 marks):**

$$\varepsilon_{long} = \frac{\delta L}{L} = \frac{1.2}{1500} = 8 \times 10^{-4}$$

**(ii) Lateral Strain (2 marks):**

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -\mu \cdot \varepsilon_{long} = -0.32 \times 8 \times 10^{-4} = -2.56 \times 10^{-4}$$

**(iii) Change in Diameter (1.5 marks):**

$$\delta d = \varepsilon_{lat} \times d = -2.56 \times 10^{-4} \times 25 = -0.0064 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Axial Stress (1.5 marks):**

By Hooke's Law: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon_{long}$

$$\sigma = 100 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 8 \times 10^{-4} = 80 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 80 \text{ MPa}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Sample 2024 Scheme ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice]

**(a)** What is Poisson's ratio? Explain why it is bounded between 0 and 0.5 for most engineering materials. Mention at least three engineering applications where Poisson's effect plays a critical role. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular bar of $40 \text{ mm}$ diameter and $500 \text{ mm}$ length is compressed axially. The bar shortens by $0.4 \text{ mm}$. If Poisson's ratio is $0.28$, calculate:
- (i) Longitudinal strain (with sign)
- (ii) Lateral strain (with sign)
- (iii) Increase in diameter due to compression
- (iv) Volumetric strain of the bar **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Conceptual (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Poisson's ratio $\mu$ is defined as the negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain in a body subjected to uniaxial loading:

$$\mu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{long}}$$

**Why bounded between 0 and 0.5 (3 marks):**
- **Lower bound $\mu = 0$:** Materials like cork exhibit no lateral strain when compressed ‚ÄĒ they deform only in the loading direction. This is a theoretical lower bound.
- **Upper bound $\mu = 0.5$:** A material with $\mu > 0.5$ would have **negative volumetric strain** under tension (i.e., it would *expand* in volume when stretched), which violates thermodynamic stability conditions. Thus $\mu = 0.5$ represents an **incompressible** material ‚ÄĒ the theoretical upper limit.
- For most metals and alloys, $\mu$ lies between 0.25 and 0.35.

**Three applications (2 marks):**
1. **Design of thick pressure vessels** ‚ÄĒ Lam√©'s equations for radial and hoop stresses.
2. **Confined concrete columns** ‚ÄĒ Lateral ties restrain Poisson expansion, increasing axial strength.
3. **Railway track design** ‚ÄĒ Thermal expansion of rails causes lateral buckling, controlled using Poisson's effect in rail pads.

---

### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $d = 40 \text{ mm}$, $L = 500 \text{ mm}$, $\delta L = -0.4 \text{ mm}$ (negative for shortening)
- $\mu = 0.28$

**(i) Longitudinal Strain (2 marks):**

$$\varepsilon_{long} = \frac{\delta L}{L} = \frac{-0.4}{500} = -8 \times 10^{-4}$$

(Compressive, hence negative)

**(ii) Lateral Strain (2 marks):**

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -\mu \cdot \varepsilon_{long} = -0.28 \times (-8 \times 10^{-4}) = +2.24 \times 10^{-4}$$

(Positive because compression causes lateral expansion)

**(iii) Increase in Diameter (1.5 marks):**

$$\delta d = \varepsilon_{lat} \times d = 2.24 \times 10^{-4} \times 40 = 0.00896 \text{ mm} \approx 0.00896 \text{ mm}$$

The diameter **increases** by 0.00896 mm, as expected under compression.

**(iv) Volumetric Strain (1.5 marks):**

For a uniaxial load on an isotropic material:

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_{long}(1 - 2\mu) = -8 \times 10^{-4} \times (1 - 0.56) = -8 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.44 = -3.52 \times 10^{-4}$$

The negative volumetric strain indicates that the bar's **volume decreases** under compression ‚ÄĒ consistent with physical expectation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $\varepsilon_{lat} = -\mu \cdot \varepsilon_{long}$. Always state the sign explicitly. Losing this sign = losing 1 full mark in Part A.
> 2. **Mixing up the formulas** for longitudinal and lateral strain. Longitudinal strain uses length $L$; lateral strain uses diameter $d$ (or breadth $b$). Do NOT use $L$ in the lateral strain formula.
> 3. **Ignoring unit conversion.** In numerical problems, $L$ is often given in meters while $\delta L$ is in mm. Convert everything to a single unit (preferably mm) before substituting.
> 4. **Sign confusion in compression problems.** Under compression, longitudinal strain is **negative**, and lateral strain is **positive**. Many students invert this. Draw a clear free-body sketch showing the deformation direction.
> 5. **Misapplying volumetric strain formula.** Volumetric strain uses $(1 - 2\mu)$, not $(1 - \mu)$ or $(1 + \mu)$. Memorize carefully.
> 6. **Reporting only the magnitude.** KTU examiners require the **sign** of the strain. Always write $\varepsilon = -2.25 \times 10^{-4}$, not just $2.25 \times 10^{-4}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Lateral Strain ‚ÄĒ Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme**

- **Definition:** Lateral strain $\varepsilon_{lat} = \dfrac{\delta d}{d}$ ‚ÄĒ ratio of change in lateral dimension to original lateral dimension.
- **Key formula:** $\varepsilon_{lat} = -\mu \cdot \varepsilon_{long}$
- **Poisson's ratio range:** $0 \leq \mu \leq 0.5$ for engineering materials.
- **Typical steel value:** $\mu = 0.30$ (memorize this for KTU exams).
- **Sign convention:**
  - Tension: $\varepsilon_{long} > 0$, $\varepsilon_{lat} < 0$ (contraction sideways)
  - Compression: $\varepsilon_{long} < 0$, $\varepsilon_{lat} > 0$ (expansion sideways)
- **Volumetric strain (uniaxial):** $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_{long}(1 - 2\mu)$
- **Three-way coupling:** Under uniaxial load, strains in all three perpendicular directions are linked by $\mu$.
- **Strain is dimensionless** ‚ÄĒ never attach units (mm/mm or m/m is misleading; report as a pure number).
- **Within elastic limit** ‚ÄĒ Poisson's ratio is constant; beyond yield, $\mu$ changes and the simple linear relation breaks down.
- **Incompressibility** ‚ÄĒ when $\mu = 0.5$, volumetric strain = 0 (rubber-like behavior).
- **Engineering applications to remember for descriptive answers:** Pressure vessels, concrete confinement, rail buckling, soil lateral pressure, composite auxetic materials.
- **KTU examiner focus:** Sign of strain, correct formula application, unit consistency, and clear statement of physical meaning.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Poisson's Ratio
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Poisson's Ratio

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Poisson's Ratio (őĹ)** is defined as the **negative of the ratio of lateral (transverse) strain to the longitudinal (axial) strain** within the elastic limit of a material subjected to a uniaxial load.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}} = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{long}}$$

Where:
- **őĹ (nu)** = Poisson's ratio (dimensionless)
- **őĶ_lateral** = Strain in the direction perpendicular to the applied load
- **őĶ_longitudinal** = Strain in the direction parallel to the applied load

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCET205 - Module 1):** Poisson's ratio is one of the **four fundamental elastic constants** of an isotropic, homogeneous, linear elastic material, along with **Young's Modulus (E)**, **Shear Modulus (G/Rigidity Modulus)**, and **Bulk Modulus (K)**.

## 1.2 The Sign Convention ‚ÄĒ Why a Negative Sign?

When a rod is pulled (tensile load), it **elongates** in the direction of the load (positive longitudinal strain) and simultaneously **contracts** in the lateral/transverse direction (negative lateral strain).

$$\varepsilon_{long} > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \varepsilon_{lat} < 0$$

Dividing a negative number by a positive number gives a **negative value**. To make Poisson's ratio a **positive quantity** (since the physical effect of "necking" should be positive), the negative sign is introduced in the formula.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Range of Poisson's Ratio for Engineering Materials:**
>
> | Material | Poisson's Ratio (őĹ) |
> |---|---|
> | Structural Steel | **0.30** |
> | Cast Iron | **0.23 ‚Äď 0.27** |
> | Concrete | **0.10 ‚Äď 0.20** |
> | Aluminum | **0.33** |
> | Copper | **0.34** |
> | Rubber (nearly incompressible) | **‚Čą 0.50** |
> | Cork | **‚Čą 0.00** |
> | Theoretical maximum (stable) | **0.50** |
> | Theoretical minimum (stable) | **‚ąí1.00** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Rubber Band" Intuition

Imagine stretching a **soft rubber band** between your two hands:
- Your hands move **apart** ‚ÄĒ the band gets **longer** (this is longitudinal strain, positive).
- The band simultaneously becomes **thinner** in the middle ‚ÄĒ its cross-section **shrinks** (this is lateral strain, negative).

The "amount of thinning" compared to the "amount of stretching" is exactly what Poisson's ratio quantifies. A material with a **high Poisson's ratio** (like rubber, őĹ ‚Čą 0.5) gets "skinnier" much faster than a material with a **low Poisson's ratio** (like cork, őĹ ‚Čą 0) which barely changes its cross-section when stretched.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Application:** In **soil mechanics**, when a load is applied to a clay sample, the soil contracts laterally ‚ÄĒ this lateral strain is critical for predicting **settlement** of foundations. In **prestressed concrete design**, Poisson's effect causes **lateral expansion of concrete** around the tendons, which is a key factor in **bond stress analysis**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Concept

The phenomenon of lateral strain accompanying axial strain was first observed by **Sim√©on Denis Poisson** in 1829. To fully understand Poisson's ratio, we break the concept into structured logic steps:

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Apply a uniaxial tensile stress** $\sigma_x$ on a prismatic bar in the x-direction. The principal stresses are:

$$\sigma_x = \frac{P}{A}, \quad \sigma_y = 0, \quad \sigma_z = 0$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute longitudinal strain** using Hooke's Law:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute lateral strains** (these occur because of the molecular bonds being pulled in the x-direction, causing the y and z dimensions to shrink):

$$\varepsilon_y = -\nu \cdot \frac{\sigma_x}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_z = -\nu \cdot \frac{\sigma_x}{E}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Generalized Hooke's Law (3D form)** emerges, which is the most important equation in this entire topic:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The generalized Hooke's Law equations are tested **almost every semester** in Part A (3-mark) questions. Students are expected to write all three equations from memory.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Poisson's Ratio

The physical origin of Poisson's ratio lies in the **atomic/molecular packing structure** of materials. When atoms are pulled apart along one axis, the interatomic bonds tilt, causing the perpendicular direction to contract. This behavior is encoded in the **interatomic potential function** and the **elastic tensor** of the material.

For an **isotropic material**, the elastic behavior is fully described by just **two independent elastic constants** (e.g., E and őĹ), because G and K can be derived from them.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Poisson's Ratio |
|---|---|
| **Civil / Structural** | Lateral contraction in RCC columns under axial load; thermal stress analysis |
| **Geotechnical** | Lateral earth pressure coefficients ($K_0 = \nu / (1 - \nu)$) for at-rest soil conditions |
| **Mechanical / Pressure Vessels** | Hoop and longitudinal strain coupling in cylindrical pressure vessels |
| **Aerospace** | Composite laminate analysis; orthotropic materials with multiple őĹ values |
| **Biomechanics** | Modeling of arteries, skin, and soft tissues (rubber-like, őĹ ‚Üí 0.5) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat} / \varepsilon_{long}$ | Basic definition | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \cdot \sigma / E$ | Lateral strain from axial stress | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ | E ‚Äď G ‚Äď őĹ relationship | $\text{N/m}^2$ |
| 4 | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | E ‚Äď K ‚Äď őĹ relationship | $\text{N/m}^2$ |
| 5 | $G = \dfrac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)}$ | G ‚Äď K ‚Äď őĹ relationship | $\text{N/m}^2$ |
| 6 | $\dfrac{E}{2G} - 1 = \nu$ | őĹ from E and G | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{3} - \dfrac{E}{9K} = \nu$ | őĹ from E and K | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ | Volumetric strain (dilatation) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{\sigma(1 - 2\nu)}{E} \cdot 3$ | Volumetric strain under hydrostatic ŌÉ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{3\sigma_h(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$ | For hydrostatic pressure $p$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $\nu_{max} = 0.5$ | Incompressible limit (rubber-like) | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $\nu_{min} = -1$ | Auxetic materials (theoretical) | Dimensionless |
| 13 | $K_0 = \nu / (1 - \nu)$ | At-rest earth pressure coefficient | Dimensionless |
| 14 | $\Delta V / V = \varepsilon_x(1 - 2\nu)$ | Vol. strain in uniaxial tension | Dimensionless |

> [!CAUTION]
> **LaTeX Rendering Tip:** In your answer scripts, always write absolute values and norms as $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ ‚ÄĒ never using the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside LaTeX ‚ÄĒ to avoid markdown/LaTeX parsing issues in online platforms.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Volumetric Strain under Uniaxial Stress

Consider a rectangular block of dimensions $L \times b \times d$ subjected to a uniaxial tensile stress $\sigma_x$ along the x-axis (lateral surfaces are stress-free).

**Step 1:** Initial volume of the block:

$$V_0 = L \cdot b \cdot d$$

**Step 2:** Under stress $\sigma_x$, the longitudinal strain in x-direction is:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}$$

**Step 3:** The lateral strains in y and z directions (free surfaces, only Poisson effect):

$$\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\nu \cdot \frac{\sigma_x}{E}$$

**Step 4:** New dimensions after deformation:

$$L' = L(1 + \varepsilon_x), \quad b' = b(1 + \varepsilon_y), \quad d' = d(1 + \varepsilon_z)$$

**Step 5:** New volume:

$$V = L \cdot b \cdot d \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_x)(1 + \varepsilon_y)(1 + \varepsilon_z)$$

**Step 6:** Divide new volume by original:

$$\frac{V}{V_0} = (1 + \varepsilon_x)(1 + \varepsilon_y)(1 + \varepsilon_z)$$

**Step 7:** Expand using $(1+a)(1+b)(1+c) = 1 + a + b + c + ab + ac + bc + abc$:

$$\frac{V}{V_0} = 1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z + \varepsilon_x\varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_y\varepsilon_z + \varepsilon_z\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_x\varepsilon_y\varepsilon_z$$

**Step 8:** Since strains are infinitesimally small (typically $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$), the products of two or more strains are negligible (second-order small):

$$\frac{V}{V_0} \approx 1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

**Step 9:** The volumetric strain is defined as:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{V - V_0}{V_0} = \frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

**Step 10:** Substitute the values of $\varepsilon_x$, $\varepsilon_y$, $\varepsilon_z$:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\sigma_x}{E} - \nu\frac{\sigma_x}{E} - \nu\frac{\sigma_x}{E}$$

**Step 11:** Final result:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_v = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}(1 - 2\nu)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight from Derivation 1:**
> - If $\nu < 0.5$, the volume **increases** under tension (typical for metals).
> - If $\nu = 0.5$, the volume **remains constant** (incompressible material like rubber).
> - If $\nu > 0.5$ (theoretically impossible for stable materials), the volume would **decrease** under tension.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Volumetric Strain under Hydrostatic (3D Equal) Stress

A hydrostatic stress means equal pressure on all sides. Let the principal stresses all equal $\sigma_h$ (this is a **compressive** hydrostatic state; for tensile hydrostatic, use $+\sigma_h$).

**Step 1:** Apply $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = \sigma_h$ in the generalized Hooke's Law:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_h - \nu(\sigma_h + \sigma_h)\right] = \frac{\sigma_h}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$$

**Step 2:** By symmetry, $\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z$.

**Step 3:** Sum all three strains:

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma_h}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$$

**Step 4:** Final result:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_v = \frac{3\sigma_h(1 - 2\nu)}{E}}$$

**Step 5:** By definition, Bulk Modulus $K = \dfrac{\sigma_h}{\varepsilon_v}$ (with compressive sign convention):

$$K = \frac{\sigma_h}{\frac{3\sigma_h(1 - 2\nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

**Step 6:** Rearranging gives the famous relationship:

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$
