Consider a **pure shear stress** $\tau$ applied on a small cubic element. By complementary shear, shear stresses $\tau$ also appear on the perpendicular faces, producing a state of pure shear.

**Step 1:** The principal stresses for pure shear are:
- Maximum principal stress: $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ (along 45¬į direction)
- Minimum principal stress: $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ (along 135¬į direction)
- Out-of-plane: $\sigma_3 = 0$

**Step 2:** Principal strain along the 45¬į direction (using generalized Hooke's Law for 2D):

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu \cdot \sigma_2\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau - \nu(-\tau)\right] = \frac{\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 3:** Principal strain along the 135¬į direction:

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_2 - \nu \cdot \sigma_1\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-\tau - \nu(\tau)\right] = -\frac{\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 4:** The shear strain $\gamma$ in pure shear is related to the principal strains by:

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{\tau}{E}(1 + \nu) - \left(-\frac{\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)\right) = \frac{2\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 5:** By Hooke's Law in shear, $\tau = G \cdot \gamma$, hence $\gamma = \tau / G$.

**Step 6:** Equating the two expressions for $\gamma$:

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 7:** Cancel $\tau$ from both sides and rearrange:

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Lateral Strain in a Bar under Axial Load

A cylindrical steel bar of length $L$ and diameter $d$ is subjected to an axial tensile load $P$. Determine the change in diameter $\Delta d$.

**Step 1:** Axial stress:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2}$$

**Step 2:** Axial (longitudinal) strain:

$$\varepsilon_{long} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2 E}$$

**Step 3:** Lateral strain (in diameter direction):

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{long} = -\frac{4\nu P}{\pi d^2 E}$$

**Step 4:** Change in diameter:

$$\Delta d = \varepsilon_{lat} \cdot d = -\frac{4\nu P}{\pi d E}$$

**Step 5:** New diameter:

$$d' = d + \Delta d = d\left(1 - \frac{4\nu P}{\pi d^2 E}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern):**
>
> *A steel rod of 20 mm diameter and 2 m length is subjected to an axial tensile load of 50 kN. Given E = 200 GPa and őĹ = 0.3, find: (i) longitudinal strain, (ii) lateral strain, (iii) change in length, (iv) change in diameter, (v) volumetric strain.*
>
> **Solution:**
>
> Cross-sectional area:
> $$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.020)^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2$$
>
> Axial stress:
> $$\sigma = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 159.15 \, \text{MPa}$$
>
> (i) Longitudinal strain:
> $$\varepsilon_{long} = \frac{159.15 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 7.958 \times 10^{-4}$$
>
> (ii) Lateral strain:
> $$\varepsilon_{lat} = -0.3 \times 7.958 \times 10^{-4} = -2.387 \times 10^{-4}$$
>
> (iii) Change in length:
> $$\Delta L = \varepsilon_{long} \cdot L = 7.958 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 = 1.5916 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m} = 1.5916 \, \text{mm}$$
>
> (iv) Change in diameter:
> $$\Delta d = \varepsilon_{lat} \cdot d = -2.387 \times 10^{-4} \times 20 = -4.775 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{mm} = -4.775 \, \mu\text{m}$$
>
> (v) Volumetric strain:
> $$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_{long} + 2\varepsilon_{lat} = 7.958 \times 10^{-4} + 2(-2.387 \times 10^{-4}) = 3.184 \times 10^{-4}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Deformation Geometry under Uniaxial Tension

This diagram visualizes how a prismatic bar undergoes both longitudinal elongation and lateral contraction simultaneously, which is the physical basis of Poisson's ratio.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ORIGINAL BLOCK\nLength L\nWidth b\nDepth d\nVolume V0] -->|Apply Tensile Force P| B[DEFORMED BLOCK\nLength L+DeltaL\nWidth b minus DeltaB\nDepth d minus DeltaD\nVolume V0 plus DeltaV]
    B --> C[STRAIN COMPONENTS]
    C --> D1[Longitudinal Strain\nepsilon_long = DeltaL by L\n= sigma by E\nPOSITIVE]
    C --> D2[Lateral Strain\nepsilon_lat = minus DeltaB by b\n= minus nu times sigma by E\nNEGATIVE]
    C --> D3[Volumetric Strain\nepsilon_v = epsilon_x plus epsilon_y plus epsilon_z\n= sigma times 1 minus 2 nu by E]
    D1 --> E[POISSONS RATIO\nnu = minus epsilon_lat by epsilon_long\nTYPICAL RANGE 0 TO 0.5]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[ELASTIC CONSTANT MATRIX]
    F --> F1[E equals 2G times 1 plus nu]
    F --> F2[E equals 3K times 1 minus 2 nu]
    F --> F3[G equals 3K times 1 minus 2 nu by 2 times 1 plus nu]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture ‚ÄĒ Inter-Relationships of Elastic Constants

This block diagram maps out how Poisson's ratio links the three other elastic constants (E, G, K) and the typical engineering inputs/outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INPUT[APPLIED LOAD\nP in N\nSTRESS sigma in MPa] --> STRESS_CALC[Stress Computation\nsigma equals P by A]
    STRESS_CALC --> HOOKE[HOOKES LAW\ngeneralized 3D form]
    HOOKE --> E_USE[YOUNGS MODULUS E\ngoverns axial strain]
    HOOKE --> NU_USE[POISSONS RATIO nu\ngoverns lateral coupling]
    E_USE --> G_REL[E equals 2G times 1 plus nu\nG equals E by 2 times 1 plus nu]
    E_USE --> K_REL[E equals 3K times 1 minus 2 nu\nK equals E by 3 times 1 minus 2 nu]
    NU_USE --> G_REL
    NU_USE --> K_REL
    G_REL --> SHEAR_OUT[SHEAR BEHAVIOR\ntau equals G gamma\ntorsion of shafts]
    K_REL --> BULK_OUT[BULK BEHAVIOR\np equals K times epsilon_v\nhydrostatic pressure vessels]
    NU_USE --> LATERAL_OUT[LATERAL STRAIN\nDelta D calculation\ncolumn shortening effects]
    E_USE --> AXIAL_OUT[AXIAL STRAIN\nDelta L calculation\nelongation and deflection]
    SHEAR_OUT --> DESIGN[CIVIL ENGINEERING DESIGN\nbeams, columns, slabs, foundations]
    BULK_OUT --> DESIGN
    LATERAL_OUT --> DESIGN
    AXIAL_OUT --> DESIGN
```

## 4.3 ASCII Schematic: Bar under Axial Tension Showing Deformation

```
        APPLIED TENSILE LOAD P (downward arrow at top)
              |
              v
        +-----------+
        |    BAR    |   ---->  Initial length L
        | diameter d|
        |           |
        +-----------+

              AFTER DEFORMATION (Stretched)
              |
              v
        +-----------+
        |  /     \  |   ---->  New length L + DeltaL (INCREASED)
        |  \  d' /  |   ---->  New diameter d' = d - DeltaD (DECREASED)
        |    \   /  |
        +-----------+

    Longitudinal strain:  epsilon_long  = + DeltaL / L    (POSITIVE)
    Lateral strain:        epsilon_lat   = - DeltaD / d    (NEGATIVE)
    Poisson's ratio:       nu = - epsilon_lat / epsilon_long
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Logic for Solving Poisson's Ratio Problems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Problem Statement] --> B{What is given?}
    B -->|E and G| C1[Use nu equals E by 2G minus 1]
    B -->|E and K| C2[Use nu equals 1 by 3 minus E by 9K]
    B -->|G and K| C3[Use nu equals 3K minus 2G by 2 times 3K plus G]
    B -->|nu and stress| C4[Use epsilon_lat equals minus nu sigma by E]
    B -->|Strains given| C5[Use nu equals minus epsilon_lat by epsilon_long]
    C1 --> D[Compute nu]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E{Verify Range}
    E -->|minus 1 less than nu less than 0.5| F[VALID ANSWER]
    E -->|nu less than 0 or nu greater than 0.5| G[CHECK CALCULATION\nor non standard material]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024] Define Poisson's ratio. What is its theoretical range?

**Model Answer:**

Poisson's ratio (őĹ) is defined as the **negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain** for a material subjected to uniaxial stress within the elastic limit.

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}}$$

It is a **dimensionless** quantity. The theoretical range is **‚ąí1 ‚Č§ őĹ ‚Č§ 0.5**.

- The **lower limit (őĹ = ‚ąí1)** corresponds to **auxetic materials** (which expand laterally when stretched).
- The **upper limit (őĹ = 0.5)** corresponds to **incompressible materials** (such as rubber and fluids).
- For most engineering materials (steel, aluminum, concrete), **0 < őĹ < 0.35**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only "0 to 0.5" ‚ÄĒ losing 1 mark. Always state the **full theoretical range (‚ąí1 to 0.5)** and explain both endpoints.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023] Derive the relationship between Young's modulus (E), Bulk modulus (K), and Poisson's ratio (őĹ).

**Model Answer:**

Under hydrostatic stress $\sigma_h$ applied equally on all three axes, the principal stresses are $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = \sigma_h$.

From the generalized Hooke's Law:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_h - \nu(\sigma_h + \sigma_h)\right] = \frac{\sigma_h}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$$

By symmetry, $\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z$. The volumetric strain is:

$$\varepsilon_v = 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma_h}{E}(1 - 2\nu) = \frac{3\sigma_h(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

By definition of Bulk Modulus, $K = \dfrac{\sigma_h}{\varepsilon_v}$:

$$K = \frac{\sigma_h}{\frac{3\sigma_h(1 - 2\nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ July 2024]

**Question A(a) [7 Marks]:** A solid steel cylinder of diameter 30 mm and length 200 mm is subjected to an axial compressive load of 80 kN. If E = 2 √ó 10‚ĀĶ N/mm¬≤ and őĹ = 0.3, determine:
  (i) the longitudinal strain,
  (ii) the lateral strain,
  (iii) the change in length,
  (iv) the change in diameter.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Diameter, d = 30 mm
- Length, L = 200 mm
- Compressive load, P = 80 kN = 80,000 N
- Young's Modulus, E = 2 √ó 10‚ĀĶ N/mm¬≤
- Poisson's ratio, őĹ = 0.3

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(30)^2 = 706.86 \, \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Axial stress** (compressive, taken as negative):

$$\sigma = -\frac{P}{A} = -\frac{80,000}{706.86} = -113.18 \, \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Longitudinal strain**

$$\varepsilon_{long} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{-113.18}{2 \times 10^5} = -5.659 \times 10^{-4}$$

*(Negative sign indicates contraction in the longitudinal direction under compression.)*

**Step 4: Lateral strain**

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{long} = -0.3 \times (-5.659 \times 10^{-4}) = +1.698 \times 10^{-4}$$

*(Positive sign indicates expansion in the lateral direction under compression ‚ÄĒ the bar bulges.)*

**Step 5: Change in length**

$$\Delta L = \varepsilon_{long} \cdot L = -5.659 \times 10^{-4} \times 200 = -0.1132 \, \text{mm}$$

*(The bar shortens by 0.1132 mm.)*

**Step 6: Change in diameter**

$$\Delta d = \varepsilon_{lat} \cdot d = 1.698 \times 10^{-4} \times 30 = +0.005094 \, \text{mm} = 5.094 \, \mu\text{m}$$

*(The diameter increases by 5.094 őľm.)*

---

**Question A(b) [7 Marks]:** For a given material, Young's modulus is 210 GPa and Shear modulus is 84 GPa. Find:
  (i) Poisson's ratio,
  (ii) Bulk modulus,
  (iii) Volumetric strain when the material is subjected to a hydrostatic pressure of 200 MPa.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- E = 210 GPa = 210 √ó 10¬≥ MPa
- G = 84 GPa = 84 √ó 10¬≥ MPa
- Hydrostatic pressure, ŌÉ‚āē = 200 MPa (compressive)

**Step 1: Poisson's ratio using E = 2G(1 + őĹ)**

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu) \implies 1 + \nu = \frac{E}{2G} = \frac{210}{2 \times 84} = \frac{210}{168} = 1.25$$

$$\nu = 1.25 - 1 = \boxed{0.25}$$

**Step 2: Bulk modulus using E = 3K(1 ‚ąí 2őĹ)**

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{210}{3(1 - 0.5)} = \frac{210}{1.5} = \boxed{140 \, \text{GPa}}$$

**Step 3: Volumetric strain under hydrostatic pressure**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{3\sigma_h(1 - 2\nu)}{E} = \frac{3 \times (-200)(1 - 0.5)}{210 \times 10^3}$$

*(Note: Pressure is compressive, hence negative sign.)*

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{3 \times (-200) \times 0.5}{210 \times 10^3} = \frac{-300}{210,000} = -1.4286 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_v = -1.4286 \times 10^{-3}}$$

*(Negative volumetric strain indicates volume contraction under pressure ‚ÄĒ physically consistent.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice Alternative)** [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2022]

**Question B(a) [7 Marks]:** Define Poisson's ratio. For a material with E = 1.5 √ó 10‚ĀĶ N/mm¬≤ and őĹ = 0.25, determine the values of G and K. Also, find the volumetric strain when the material is subjected to a uniaxial tensile stress of 100 N/mm¬≤.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition**

Poisson's ratio is the negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain within the elastic limit:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{long}}$$

**Step 2: Compute G from E and őĹ**

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^5}{2(1 + 0.25)} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^5}{2.5} = \boxed{6 \times 10^4 \, \text{N/mm}^2 = 60 \, \text{GPa}}$$

**Step 3: Compute K from E and őĹ**

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^5}{3(1 - 0.5)} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^5}{1.5} = \boxed{1 \times 10^5 \, \text{N/mm}^2 = 100 \, \text{GPa}}$$

**Step 4: Volumetric strain under uniaxial tension**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\sigma}{E}(1 - 2\nu) = \frac{100}{1.5 \times 10^5}(1 - 0.5) = \frac{100}{1.5 \times 10^5} \times 0.5$$

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{50}{150,000} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_v = 3.333 \times 10^{-4}}$$

---

**Question B(b) [7 Marks]:** A brass rod of 25 mm diameter is subjected to a tensile load of 40 kN. The modulus of elasticity and Poisson's ratio of brass are 100 GPa and 0.35 respectively. Find the change in length, change in diameter, and change in volume of the rod. Take length of the rod as 1.5 m.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- d = 25 mm = 0.025 m
- P = 40 kN = 40,000 N
- E = 100 GPa = 100 √ó 10‚ĀĻ N/m¬≤
- őĹ = 0.35
- L = 1.5 m

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2: Axial stress**

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{40,000}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 81.49 \times 10^6 \, \text{N/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Longitudinal strain and change in length**

$$\varepsilon_{long} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{81.49 \times 10^6}{100 \times 10^9} = 8.149 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\Delta L = \varepsilon_{long} \cdot L = 8.149 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.5 = 1.222 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m} = \boxed{1.222 \, \text{mm}}$$

**Step 4: Lateral strain and change in diameter**

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{long} = -0.35 \times 8.149 \times 10^{-4} = -2.852 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\Delta d = \varepsilon_{lat} \cdot d = -2.852 \times 10^{-4} \times 25 = -7.13 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{mm} = \boxed{-7.13 \, \mu\text{m}}$$

**Step 5: Change in volume**

Initial volume:

$$V_0 = A \cdot L = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.5 = 7.363 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3$$

Volumetric strain:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\sigma}{E}(1 - 2\nu) = \frac{81.49 \times 10^6}{100 \times 10^9}(1 - 0.7) = 8.149 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.3 = 2.445 \times 10^{-4}$$

Change in volume:

$$\Delta V = \varepsilon_v \cdot V_0 = 2.445 \times 10^{-4} \times 7.363 \times 10^{-4} = 1.800 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^3 = \boxed{180 \, \text{mm}^3}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Errors in Lateral Strain** ‚ÄĒ Students frequently forget that lateral strain is **negative** under tension and **positive** under compression. Always state the sign explicitly.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency** ‚ÄĒ Mixing SI units (m, N, Pa) with mm-based units (mm, N, N/mm¬≤) is the #1 cause of calculation errors. **Standardize units at the start.**
> 3. **Misapplying E = 2G(1 + őĹ)** ‚ÄĒ Some students write $E = G(1 + \nu)$, missing the factor of 2. **Memorize the exact formula.**
> 4. **Forgetting the 1 - 2őĹ factor in volumetric strain** ‚ÄĒ When asked for "change in volume," always multiply the volumetric strain by the **initial volume**.
> 5. **Confusing hydrostatic vs uniaxial stress** ‚ÄĒ Hydrostatic stress uses all three principal stresses equal; uniaxial uses one. The volumetric strain formulas are different.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Poisson's Ratio (KTU PCCET205 ‚ÄĒ Module 1)**

- **Definition:** $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat} / \varepsilon_{long}$ ‚ÄĒ **dimensionless**, ranges from **‚ąí1 to 0.5**.
- **Sign Convention:** Longitudinal strain under tension is **positive**; lateral strain is **negative**; the negative sign in the formula makes őĹ positive.
- **The 3 Master Formulas (memorize these 3 only ‚ÄĒ every other relation derives from them):**
  - $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$
  - $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$
  - $G = \dfrac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)}$
- **Generalized Hooke's Law (3D form) ‚ÄĒ must be memorized in full:**
  - $\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)]$
  - $\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)]$
  - $\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)]$
- **Volumetric Strain (Dilatation):** $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$
  - Under **uniaxial** stress: $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$
  - Under **hydrostatic** stress: $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{3\sigma_h(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$
- **Special Case ‚ÄĒ Incompressibility:** When $\nu = 0.5$, $\varepsilon_v = 0$ (no volume change, e.g., rubber, fluids).
- **At-Rest Earth Pressure Coefficient:** $K_0 = \dfrac{\nu}{1 - \nu}$ ‚ÄĒ useful in geotechnical engineering.
- **Auxetic Materials:** Have $\nu < 0$ ‚ÄĒ they expand laterally when stretched (rare, mostly engineered).
- **Typical KTU Values to Remember:**
  - Steel: E = 200 GPa, őĹ = 0.3, G = 76.92 GPa, K = 166.67 GPa
  - Concrete: E ‚Čą 25‚Äď30 GPa, őĹ ‚Čą 0.15‚Äď0.20
  - Aluminum: E = 70 GPa, őĹ = 0.33
- **Quick Sanity Check Formula:** $\dfrac{E}{G} = 2(1 + \nu)$ ‚ÄĒ if you forget őĹ, use this to back-calculate.
- **Pressure Vessel Note:** Poisson's ratio couples hoop and longitudinal strains ‚ÄĒ always use the **plane stress version** of Hooke's Law for thin-walled cylinders.
- **Exam Tip:** When asked for "change in volume" of a bar under axial load, **never** compute $\Delta L \times A$ directly. Use $\Delta V = \varepsilon_v \times V_0 = \dfrac{\sigma V_0}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$ ‚ÄĒ it accounts for **both** longitudinal and lateral effects.

---

topics.title: Volumetric Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Volumetric Strain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Volumetric Strain** (also called **Dilation** or **Bulk Strain**) is defined as the **ratio of the change in volume of a body to its original volume**, due to the application of an external load or system of loads. It is a **dimensionless quantity** and is denoted by $\varepsilon_v$, $\theta$, or $e_v$.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\delta V}{V} = \frac{\text{Change in Volume}}{\text{Original Volume}}$$

Where:
- $\delta V$ = Change in volume of the body (final volume ‚ąí original volume)
- $V$ = Original volume of the body
- $\varepsilon_v$ is **unitless** (since both numerator and denominator carry units of volume, typically **mm¬≥** or **m¬≥**)

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Mechanics of Solids*, volumetric strain is introduced in **Module 1** as a direct consequence of the **three normal strains** $(\varepsilon_x, \varepsilon_y, \varepsilon_z)$ acting along the three mutually perpendicular principal axes. It forms the foundation for deriving the **Bulk Modulus (K)** and the fundamental **E‚ÄďK‚ÄďG relationship** in elasticity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The Sponge in Water

Imagine a **dry kitchen sponge** with dimensions 10 cm √ó 10 cm √ó 5 cm, giving it an original volume of **500 cm¬≥**.

- **Analogy 1 (Compression):** Now, if you squeeze the sponge equally from all six sides (like a deep-sea pressure), the sponge shrinks. Its new volume might drop to 480 cm¬≥. The change in volume is ‚ąí20 cm¬≥, so the volumetric strain is:
$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{480 - 500}{500} = \frac{-20}{500} = -0.04 = -4\%$$

This is exactly what happens to objects under **hydrostatic pressure** (e.g., submarines, deep-sea divers, dam foundations).

- **Analogy 2 (Expansion):** Conversely, if a **balloon** inflates from 100 cm¬≥ to 120 cm¬≥, the volumetric strain is:
$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{120 - 100}{100} = +0.20 = +20\%$$

This is what happens in **thermal expansion** of fluids and gases.

> [!TIP]
> **Sign Convention (per KTU 2024 standard):** A **positive** volumetric strain indicates **expansion (increase in volume)**, and a **negative** volumetric strain indicates **contraction (decrease in volume)**. This follows the standard **tensile-positive** convention.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Volumetric Strain as the Sum of Linear Strains

When a 3D body is subjected to a general state of stress, the resulting strains in the **x, y, and z directions** are $\varepsilon_x$, $\varepsilon_y$, and $\varepsilon_z$ respectively. For **small deformations** (the assumption in all KTU-level strength of materials problems), the total volumetric strain is the **algebraic sum** of the three normal strains:

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

This is a consequence of the **principle of superposition** and the assumption that strains are infinitesimal (so cross-product terms like $\varepsilon_x \cdot \varepsilon_y$ are negligibly small).

## 2.2 The Three Independent Shear Strains Have No Direct Effect on Volume

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The shear strains $(\gamma_{xy}, \gamma_{yz}, \gamma_{zx})$ **do not contribute** to volumetric strain at the first-order infinitesimal strain level. They cause **shape distortion** (angular changes), not volume change. Only the **three normal strains** change volume.

## 2.3 Volumetric Strain in Terms of Principal Stresses

Using **Generalized Hooke's Law** (the constitutive equation for linear elastic isotropic materials):

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

Adding these three equations:

$$\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)(1 - 2\nu)\right]$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_v = \frac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)}$$

This is one of the **most important formulas** in the entire KTU Mechanics of Solids syllabus and is **frequently asked** in university exams.

## 2.4 Bulk Modulus (K) ‚ÄĒ The Volumetric Stiffness

> [!DEFINITION]
> **Bulk Modulus (K)** is defined as the **ratio of uniform hydrostatic pressure (or direct compressive stress) to the resulting volumetric strain**. It measures a material's resistance to **uniform compression from all sides**.

$$K = \frac{\sigma_{hydrostatic}}{\varepsilon_v} = \frac{p}{\delta V / V}$$

The unit of Bulk Modulus is the same as that of stress: **N/m¬≤** or **Pa** (or MPa, GPa).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description / Application** | **Units** |
|:-----:|:------------|:------------------------------|:---------:|
| 1 | $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{\delta V}{V}$ | Basic definition of volumetric strain | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ | Sum of normal strains (small strain theory) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$ | In terms of direct stresses and material constants | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $K = \dfrac{\sigma_{hyd}}{\varepsilon_v} = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | Bulk Modulus definition & relation to E and őĹ | N/m¬≤ or Pa |
| 5 | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | Young's Modulus in terms of K and őĹ | N/m¬≤ or Pa |
| 6 | $\nu = \dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{E}{6K}$ | Poisson's ratio in terms of E and K | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{p}{K}$ (for hydrostatic state) | Volumetric strain under pure hydrostatic pressure $p$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $E = 2G(1 + \nu) = 3K(1 - 2\nu) = \dfrac{9KG}{3K + G}$ | Universal elastic constants relation | N/m¬≤ or Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme, problems on volumetric strain typically fall into **two categories**:
> 1. **Direct strain problems** ‚ÄĒ given $\varepsilon_x, \varepsilon_y, \varepsilon_z$, find $\varepsilon_v$ (and vice versa).
> 2. **Stress-based problems** ‚ÄĒ given $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z, E, \nu$, find $\varepsilon_v$, and then use it to compute $K$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Engineering Field** | **Application of Volumetric Strain / Bulk Modulus** |
|:----------------------|:-----------------------------------------------------|
| **Dam Engineering** | Concrete in dam foundations experiences 3D compression; bulk modulus governs settlement. |
| **Submarine Design** | Hull design uses bulk modulus to predict hull collapse depth. |
| **Soil Mechanics** | Compression of soil strata under overburden pressure uses 1D analog of bulk modulus (constrained modulus). |
| **Hydraulic Systems** | Hydraulic fluid behavior under pressure is governed by its bulk modulus (compressibility). |
| **Material Science** | Distinguishes nearly incompressible materials (rubber, $\nu \approx 0.5$, $K \to \infty$) from compressible ones (cork, foam, $\nu \approx 0$). |
| **Earthquake Engineering** | P-wave velocity in soil/rock depends on bulk modulus and density. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ from First Principles

### Setup

Consider a rectangular parallelepiped (a small elemental cube) with original dimensions:

- Length along x-axis = $dx$
- Length along y-axis = $dy$
- Length along z-axis = $dz$

**Original volume:**

$$V = (dx)(dy)(dz)$$

### Step 1: Apply Normal Strains

Under deformation, the new dimensions become:

- New length along x = $dx(1 + \varepsilon_x)$
- New length along y = $dy(1 + \varepsilon_y)$
- New length along z = $dz(1 + \varepsilon_z)$

### Step 2: Compute New Volume

$$V' = dx(1 + \varepsilon_x) \cdot dy(1 + \varepsilon_y) \cdot dz(1 + \varepsilon_z)$$

### Step 3: Expand the Product

$$\begin{aligned}
V' &= (dx)(dy)(dz) \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_x)(1 + \varepsilon_y)(1 + \varepsilon_z)
\end{aligned}$$

Now expand $(1 + \varepsilon_x)(1 + \varepsilon_y)(1 + \varepsilon_z)$ term by term:

$$\begin{aligned}
(1 + \varepsilon_x)(1 + \varepsilon_y) &= 1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_x \varepsilon_y \\
(1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_x \varepsilon_y)(1 + \varepsilon_z) &= 1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z \\
&\quad + \varepsilon_x \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_y \varepsilon_z + \varepsilon_z \varepsilon_x \\
&\quad + \varepsilon_x \varepsilon_y \varepsilon_z
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Small Strain Assumption

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **small strain theory** (valid for all standard KTU problems, since strains are typically of the order $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$), the **second-order** terms $\varepsilon_x \varepsilon_y$, $\varepsilon_y \varepsilon_z$, $\varepsilon_z \varepsilon_x$, and the **third-order** term $\varepsilon_x \varepsilon_y \varepsilon_z$ are **negligibly small** and are dropped.

After neglecting higher-order terms:

$$(1 + \varepsilon_x)(1 + \varepsilon_y)(1 + \varepsilon_z) \approx 1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
V' &\approx V \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z) \\
V' &= V + V(\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Compute Change in Volume and Volumetric Strain

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta V &= V' - V = V(\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z) \\
\varepsilon_v &= \frac{\delta V}{V} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\therefore \varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$

### Starting Point ‚ÄĒ Generalized Hooke's Law

For a linear, elastic, isotropic material, the normal strains are:

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_x &= \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right] \\
\varepsilon_y &= \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right] \\
\varepsilon_z &= \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 1: Add All Three Equations

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z &= \frac{1}{E}\Big[\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y - \nu\sigma_z \\
&\quad + \sigma_y - \nu\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_z \\
&\quad + \sigma_z - \nu\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y\Big]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Group Like Terms

Rearranging the right-hand side by collecting coefficients of $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\sigma_z$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z &= \frac{1}{E}\Big[\sigma_x(1 - \nu) - \nu\sigma_y + \sigma_y(1 - \nu) - \nu\sigma_z \\
&\quad + \sigma_z(1 - \nu) - \nu\sigma_x\Big]
\end{aligned}$$

A cleaner approach: combine all stress terms:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Coefficient of } \sigma_x &: 1 - \nu - \nu = 1 - 2\nu \\
\text{Coefficient of } \sigma_y &: 1 - 2\nu \\
\text{Coefficient of } \sigma_z &: 1 - 2\nu
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Factor Out the Common Term

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z &= \frac{1 - 2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Apply Definition of Volumetric Strain

Since $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_v = \frac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z) \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$

### Special Case: Hydrostatic Stress State

When a body is subjected to **equal triaxial compression** (or tension), e.g., a submarine hull at depth:

$$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$$

(where $p$ is the hydrostatic pressure, taken positive for compression here).

### Step 1: Substitute into the Volumetric Strain Formula

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_v &= \frac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z) \\
\varepsilon_v &= \frac{1-2\nu}{E}(-p - p - p) \\
\varepsilon_v &= \frac{1-2\nu}{E}(-3p) \\
\varepsilon_v &= -\frac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Definition of Bulk Modulus

By definition, $K$ is the ratio of hydrostatic stress to volumetric strain (in magnitude, with sign convention handled carefully):

$$\begin{aligned}
K &= \frac{p}{\vert \varepsilon_v \vert} = \frac{p}{3p(1-2\nu)/E} \\
K &= \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\therefore K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}}$$

### Step 3: Important Physical Inferences from This Relation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - As $\nu \to 0.5$ (rubber, water, perfectly incompressible materials), the denominator $(1 - 2\nu) \to 0$, hence $K \to \infty$. This means an **incompressible material** has **infinite bulk modulus** (i.e., its volume cannot change, no matter how much pressure is applied).
> - As $\nu \to 0$ (cork, some ceramics), $K \to E/3$, meaning the material is highly compressible volumetrically.
> - For most metals, $\nu \approx 0.30$, so $K \approx E / 1.2 \approx 0.83 E$. Metals are roughly **5 to 10 times stiffer in bulk** than in uniaxial tension.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Worked Examples (Conceptual)

### Example A: Direct Strain Approach

> A steel cube of side 100 mm is subjected to strains:
> $\varepsilon_x = 200 \times 10^{-6}$, $\varepsilon_y = 150 \times 10^{-6}$, $\varepsilon_z = -100 \times 10^{-6}$.
> Find the volumetric strain and change in volume.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= (100)^3 = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 = 10^6 \text{ mm}^3 \\
\varepsilon_v &= \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z \\
&= (200 + 150 - 100) \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 250 \times 10^{-6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \\
\delta V &= \varepsilon_v \cdot V = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^6 \\
\delta V &= 250 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

### Example B: Stress Approach

> A brass block has $E = 100$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$, and is subjected to stresses:
> $\sigma_x = 60$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, $\sigma_z = 20$ MPa.
> Find: (i) Volumetric strain, (ii) Bulk Modulus.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_v &= \frac{1 - 2(0.30)}{100 \times 10^3}(60 + 40 + 20) \text{ MPa} \\
&= \frac{0.40}{100 \times 10^3}(120) \\
&= \frac{48}{100 \times 10^3} \\
&= 4.8 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
K &= \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} = \frac{100 \times 10^3}{3(1 - 0.60)} = \frac{100 \times 10^3}{1.2} \\
K &= 83.33 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Conceptual Architecture of Volumetric Strain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Parameters] --> B{Type of Loading}
    B -- Direct Strain Given --> C[Exx, Eyy, Ezz]
    B -- Direct Stress Given --> D[Sxx, Syy, Szz plus E, nu]
    C --> E[Sum the Three Normal Strains]
    D --> F[Apply Generalized Hookes Law]
    F --> G[Use Formula Ev equals 1 minus 2 nu divided by E times sum of stresses]
    E --> H[Compute Ev]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute Change in Volume dV]
    H --> J[Compute Bulk Modulus K]
    H --> K[Infer Material Behavior]
    K --> K1[nu close to 0.5 means nearly Incompressible]
    K --> K2[nu close to 0 means highly Compressible]
    J --> L[Relate K to E and G]
    L --> M[Universal Elastic Constant Relation]
    I --> N[Final Output Report]
    J --> N
    H --> N
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Flow from Stresses to Volumetric Strain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Direct Stress Sxx] --> H1[Hookes Law Application]
    S2[Direct Stress Syy] --> H1
    S3[Direct Stress Szz] --> H1
    H1 --> H2[Compute Exx, Eyy, Ezz]
    H2 --> H3[Add Three Linear Strains]
    H3 --> H4[Volumetric Strain Ev]
    H4 --> H5[Bulk Modulus K]
    H4 --> H6[Change in Volume dV]
    H5 --> H7[Engineering Application]
    H6 --> H7
```

## 4.3 Schematic Interpretation ‚ÄĒ Deformation of a Unit Cube

> Since the **deformation of a 3D cube under strain** is best represented geometrically (which is outside the scope of Mermaid block diagrams), the following textual schematic is provided:

**Original Cube (Side = a):**
- Vertices at: $(0,0,0)$, $(a,0,0)$, $(0,a,0)$, $(0,0,a)$, etc.
- Volume: $V_0 = a^3$

**Deformed Parallelepiped (after strains $\varepsilon_x, \varepsilon_y, \varepsilon_z$):**
- Vertices shift to: $(a(1+\varepsilon_x), 0, 0)$, etc.
- New sides: $a(1+\varepsilon_x)$, $a(1+\varepsilon_y)$, $a(1+\varepsilon_z)$
- New volume: $V' = a^3(1+\varepsilon_x)(1+\varepsilon_y)(1+\varepsilon_z) \approx a^3(1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z)$

**Visual cue:** The cube **stretches in x and y** (tension) and **contracts in z** (compression), resulting in a **slight net increase in volume** if $\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y > \vert \varepsilon_z \vert$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no physical drawing in Mermaid?** Mermaid is fundamentally a **flowchart and graph rendering engine**, not a CAD/3D geometry tool. Attempting to render a 3D cube with deformation vectors in Mermaid produces cluttered, non-informative diagrams. The block-level functional architecture above faithfully captures the **logical workflow** that a student must follow to solve any volumetric strain problem.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (2 √ó 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023 Style Tag]**

### Q1. Define volumetric strain. State its mathematical expression in terms of normal strains and direct stresses. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> Volumetric strain is defined as the **ratio of change in volume of a body to its original volume** when subjected to external loads. It is a dimensionless quantity.

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\delta V}{V}$$

In terms of the three normal strains (for small deformations):

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

In terms of direct stresses, using Generalized Hooke's Law:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$$

where $E$ is Young's Modulus and $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio. **[3 Marks]**

---

### Q2. What is Bulk Modulus? How is it related to Young's Modulus and Poisson's ratio? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Bulk Modulus (K)** is defined as the **ratio of uniform hydrostatic pressure to the resulting volumetric strain**. It represents a material's resistance to **uniform compression from all directions**.

$$K = \frac{\text{Hydrostatic stress}}{\text{Volumetric strain}} = \frac{p}{\varepsilon_v}$$

Its relationship to Young's Modulus ($E$) and Poisson's ratio ($\nu$) is:

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad E = 3K(1-2\nu)$$

For a perfectly **incompressible material** ($\nu = 0.5$), the bulk modulus $K$ tends to **infinity**, since the material cannot change its volume regardless of the applied pressure. **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE)

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Model Paper Style Tag]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **A brass bar of dimensions 80 mm √ó 60 mm √ó 40 mm is subjected to a triaxial state of stress. The normal stresses acting on the bar are: $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa (tensile), $\sigma_y = 50$ MPa (compressive), and $\sigma_z = 30$ MPa (tensile). For brass, take Young's Modulus $E = 100$ GPa and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.32$.**
>
> **(a)** Determine the volumetric strain in the bar. **[7 Marks]**
> **(b)** Calculate the change in volume of the bar and the Bulk Modulus of the material. **[7 Marks]**

#### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- $\sigma_x = +80$ MPa (tensile, positive)
- $\sigma_y = -50$ MPa (compressive, negative)
- $\sigma_z = +30$ MPa (tensile, positive)
- $E = 100$ GPa $= 100 \times 10^3$ MPa
- $\nu = 0.32$

**Step 1: Apply the volumetric strain formula in terms of stresses.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_v &= \frac{1 - 2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z) \\
&= \frac{1 - 2(0.32)}{100 \times 10^3} \times [80 + (-50) + 30] \\
&= \frac{1 - 0.64}{100 \times 10^3} \times [80 - 50 + 30] \\
&= \frac{0.36}{100 \times 10^3} \times 60
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute the result.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_v &= \frac{0.36 \times 60}{100 \times 10^3} = \frac{21.6}{100 \times 10^3} \\
\varepsilon_v &= 2.16 \times 10^{-4} \text{ (dimensionless)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** $\varepsilon_v = 2.16 \times 10^{-4}$ **[7 Marks]**

#### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Compute the original volume of the bar.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= 80 \text{ mm} \times 60 \text{ mm} \times 40 \text{ mm} \\
V &= 192{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 = 1.92 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute the change in volume.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta V &= \varepsilon_v \times V \\
&= 2.16 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.92 \times 10^5 \\
\delta V &= 41.472 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute the Bulk Modulus.**

$$\begin{aligned}
K &= \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} \\
&= \frac{100 \times 10^3}{3(1 - 0.64)} \\
&= \frac{100 \times 10^3}{3 \times 0.36} \\
&= \frac{100 \times 10^3}{1.08}
\end{aligned}$$

$$K = 92{,}592.59 \text{ MPa} \approx 92.59 \text{ GPa}$$

**Answer:** $\delta V = 41.47$ mm¬≥ (increase), and $K \approx 92.59$ GPa **[7 Marks]**

---

### Question B (Alternative ‚ÄĒ 14 Marks)

> **A steel cube of side 200 mm is subjected to three mutually perpendicular normal strains: $\varepsilon_x = 3 \times 10^{-4}$, $\varepsilon_y = 2 \times 10^{-4}$, and $\varepsilon_z = -1 \times 10^{-4}$. For steel, $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.30$.**
>
> **(a)** Compute the volumetric strain and the change in volume of the cube. **[7 Marks]**
> **(b)** Determine the principal stresses using the given strains. **[7 Marks]**

#### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- Cube side $a = 200$ mm
- $\varepsilon_x = 3 \times 10^{-4}$
- $\varepsilon_y = 2 \times 10^{-4}$
- $\varepsilon_z = -1 \times 10^{-4}$

**Step 1: Compute the volumetric strain.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_v &= \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z \\
&= (3 + 2 - 1) \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 4 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute the original volume.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= a^3 = (200)^3 \\
V &= 8 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute the change in volume.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta V &= \varepsilon_v \times V \\
&= 4 \times 10^{-4} \times 8 \times 10^6 \\
\delta V &= 3200 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** $\varepsilon_v = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ and $\delta V = 3200$ mm¬≥ (volume increases since net strain is positive) **[7 Marks]**

#### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Invert Generalized Hooke's Law to find the stresses.**

From the strain relations:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_x &= \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}\left[(1-\nu)\varepsilon_x + \nu(\varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

Substitute the values. First, compute the common factor:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)} &= \frac{200 \times 10^3}{(1.30)(0.40)} = \frac{200 \times 10^3}{0.52} \\
&= 384{,}615.38 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\sigma_x$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_x &= 384{,}615.38 \times \left[(0.70)(3 \times 10^{-4}) + (0.30)(1 \times 10^{-4})\right] \\
&= 384{,}615.38 \times \left[2.1 \times 10^{-4} + 0.3 \times 10^{-4}\right] \\
&= 384{,}615.38 \times 2.4 \times 10^{-4} \\
\sigma_x &= 92.31 \text{ MPa (tensile)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute $\sigma_y$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_y &= 384{,}615.38 \times \left[(0.70)(2 \times 10^{-4}) + (0.30)(2 \times 10^{-4})\right] \\
&= 384{,}615.38 \times \left[1.4 \times 10^{-4} + 0.6 \times 10^{-4}\right] \\
&= 384{,}615.38 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \\
\sigma_y &= 76.92 \text{ MPa (tensile)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute $\sigma_z$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_z &= 384{,}615.38 \times \left[(0.70)(-1 \times 10^{-4}) + (0.30)(5 \times 10^{-4})\right] \\
&= 384{,}615.38 \times \left[-0.7 \times 10^{-4} + 1.5 \times 10^{-4}\right] \\
&= 384{,}615.38 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-4} \\
\sigma_z &= 30.77 \text{ MPa (tensile)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** $\sigma_x \approx 92.31$ MPa, $\sigma_y \approx 76.92$ MPa, $\sigma_z \approx 30.77$ MPa **[7 Marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Students frequently drop the negative sign for compressive stresses. Compressive stress is **negative** in the standard KTU convention ‚ÄĒ writing it as positive gives a **completely wrong answer** and costs **2‚Äď3 marks**.
> 2. **Unit Mismatch:** Forgetting to convert GPa to MPa (or mm to m) is the #1 reason for numerical errors. Always write units explicitly in the substitution step.
> 3. **Confusing Bulk Modulus with Young's Modulus:** $K \neq E$. A common mistake is writing $K = E$ directly. The correct relation is $K = E / [3(1-2\nu)]$.
> 4. **Neglecting the Sign in $\delta V$:** If $\varepsilon_v$ is negative, $\delta V$ is also negative (volume decreases). Students often report a **positive change** even when contraction occurs, losing **1 mark**.
> 5. **Forgetting to Apply Small-Strain Assumption:** In derivations, students sometimes retain second-order terms like $\varepsilon_x \varepsilon_y$, which is **not required** in the KTU 2024 syllabus and complicates the answer unnecessarily.
> 6. **Incorrect use of Hydrostatic Stress:** For pure hydrostatic loading, $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$. Students sometimes write $\sigma_x = p$ (positive) and then wrongly conclude that the material expands under compression.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **The 10 Golden Points on Volumetric Strain ‚ÄĒ Must Memorize for KTU Exams**

- **Definition:** Volumetric strain $\varepsilon_v = \delta V / V$ ‚ÄĒ ratio of volume change to original volume. **Dimensionless**.

- **Direct Formula:** $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ ‚ÄĒ valid **only under small strain** (infinitesimal deformation) assumption.

- **Stress Formula:** $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$ ‚ÄĒ derived from Generalized Hooke's Law.

- **Shear strains DO NOT contribute** to $\varepsilon_v$ at first order ‚ÄĒ only **three normal strains** affect volume change.

- **Bulk Modulus:** $K = \dfrac{p}{\varepsilon_v} = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ ‚ÄĒ measures resistance to **uniform compression from all sides**.

- **Incompressibility Limit:** As $\nu \to 0.5$, $K \to \infty$ ‚ÄĒ material cannot change volume (rubber, water, ideal fluids).

- **Cork's Anomaly:** Cork has $\nu \approx 0$ (does not expand laterally when compressed) ‚ÄĒ this is why cork is used as a **bottle stopper**.

- **Universal Relation:** $E = 2G(1+\nu) = 3K(1-2\nu)$ ‚ÄĒ connects **all three** elastic constants.

- **Sign Convention:** Tensile stresses/strains = **positive**; Compressive = **negative** (per KTU standard).

- **Typical Exam Pattern:**
  - **3-mark question** ‚Üí define $\varepsilon_v$ and state formula.
  - **7-mark sub-part** ‚Üí compute $\varepsilon_v$ and $\delta V$ given either strains or stresses.
  - **7-mark sub-part** ‚Üí compute $K$ using $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$.
  - **14-mark full question** ‚Üí combines both, often with a follow-up on elastic constants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Aspirants:** Master the three derivations (Sections 3.1, 3.2, 3.3) thoroughly. The examiner often awards **2 bonus marks** for clear derivation steps. Always end your long answers with a **boxed final answer** and **explicit units** to maximize your score.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bulk Modulus of Elasticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Bulk Modulus of Elasticity ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Bulk Modulus of Elasticity (K)** is defined as the ratio of **hydrostatic (volumetric) stress** applied on a body to the corresponding **volumetric strain** produced, taken within the elastic limit. It quantifies a material's resistance to uniform compression from all sides (i.e., its *incompressibility*). $$K = \frac{\text{Direct (Hydrostatic) Stress}}{\text{Volumetric Strain}} = \frac{p}{\displaystyle -\frac{\Delta V}{V}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Bulk Modulus is the ratio of change in hydrostatic pressure to the corresponding volumetric strain.* The negative sign in the denominator ensures that **K remains a positive quantity**, since an increase in compressive pressure causes a **decrease** in volume (őĒV is negative). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Sponge-in-the-Ocean" Intuition Imagine dropping a dry sponge into the deep ocean. - At the **surface** (low pressure), the sponge retains its full, puffed-up volume. Water molecules barely squeeze it. - As the sponge descends to **greater depths** (high hydrostatic pressure), the immense, equal pressure from all directions squeezes water out of its pores, **shrinking its volume dramatically**. - A **stiff, industrial rubber sponge** barely changes volume even at crushing depths ‚ÄĒ it has a **high Bulk Modulus** (highly incompressible). - A **soft household sponge**, however, compresses significantly ‚ÄĒ it has a **low Bulk Modulus** (highly compressible). In this analogy: - The **ocean pressure** = Hydrostatic stress (p) - The **shrinkage of the sponge** = Volumetric strain (őĒV/V) - The **stiffness of the sponge material** = Bulk Modulus (K) ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Property | Standard Unit | Alternate / SI Equivalent | |---|---|---| | Bulk Modulus (K) | **N/m¬≤** or **Pa** | **GPa** (Giga-Pascal) for solids | | Hydrostatic Stress (p) | **N/m¬≤** or **Pa** | MPa in civil engineering practice | | Volumetric Strain (őĒV/V) | Dimensionless | unitless ratio | > [!NOTE] > **Typical KTU-Reference Values (Must Memorise):** > - Steel: **‚Čą 160 GPa** > - Water: **‚Čą 2.2 GPa** > - Air (at STP): **‚Čą 0.000101 GPa (101 kPa)** > - Rubber: **‚Čą 2 GPa** ### 1.4 Governing Compressibility Relation The reciprocal of Bulk Modulus is termed the **Compressibility (ő≤)**: $$\beta = \frac{1}{K}$$ It measures how easily a material's volume can be reduced under pressure. Fluids (which have no shear stiffness) are characterised almost entirely by K and ő≤. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Decomposition ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Logic To master Bulk Modulus for KTU 2024 examinations, the concept must be broken into the following logical chain: 1. **Identify the Loading Type** - A body is subjected to **uniform normal stress (p)** on **all six faces** simultaneously. - This is called a **triaxial state of stress** with equal principal stresses: $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = -p$. 2. **Quantify the Resulting Strain** - Because the stress is hydrostatic, the body **deforms equally in all three orthogonal directions**. - The **linear strain** along each axis is: $$\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\frac{p}{3K}$$ > *Negative sign indicates compression (volume reduction).* 3. **Compute the Volumetric Strain** - Volumetric strain is the **sum of the three linear strains**: $$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = -\frac{3p}{3K} = -\frac{p}{K}$$ 4. **Finalize the Definition** - Inverting gives the canonical form of Bulk Modulus: $$K = \frac{p}{-\varepsilon_v} = \frac{p}{\displaystyle -\frac{\Delta V}{V}}$$ 5. **Inter-Elastic-Constant Relationships** - The KTU syllabus (Module 1) **mandates** familiarity with how K relates to Young's Modulus (E), Shear Modulus (G), and Poisson's Ratio (őĹ). These are derived from the **generalised Hooke's Law for 3D stress states**. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Negative Sign When you push on a balloon (or any closed volume) from all sides equally: - Pressure **increases** (p ‚ÜĎ) - Volume **decreases** (őĒV < 0) - Therefore $\frac{\Delta V}{V}$ is **negative**. By inserting the **negative sign** in the definition, we mathematically cancel the negative, ensuring that **K is intrinsically positive** ‚ÄĒ a universal convention in solid mechanics. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Table ‚ÄĒ Bulk Modulus (Memorise Before Exam)** | # | Formula | Meaning | KTU Frequency | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $K = \dfrac{p}{-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}}$ | Fundamental definition | ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ | | 2 | $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = -\dfrac{p}{K}$ | Volumetric strain expression | ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ | | 3 | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | K‚ÄďE‚ÄďőĹ relationship | ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ | | 4 | $K = \dfrac{EG}{3(3G-E)}$ | K‚ÄďE‚ÄďG relationship | ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ | | 5 | $K = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)\cdot(1-2\nu)}\cdot$ wait, simplification gives $K = \dfrac{2G(1+\nu)}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | K‚ÄďG‚ÄďőĹ relationship | ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ | | 6 | $K = \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ | Reciprocal of compressibility | ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ | | 7 | $\varepsilon_x = -\dfrac{p}{3K}$ | Linear strain under hydrostatic stress | ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ | | 8 | $dV = V_0\left(1-e^{-p/K}\right)$ | Volume change at pressure *p* (integration form) | ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ | | 9 | $K = \dfrac{E}{9}$ (approx. for $\nu = 0.25$) | Quick estimate rule of thumb | ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ | > [!NOTE] > **Convention Reminder:** Throughout KTU papers, Bulk Modulus is universally denoted as **K** (sometimes also **B** in international texts, but **K** is the KTU standard). ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Bulk Modulus is **not a textbook abstraction** ‚ÄĒ it is mission-critical in: - **Hydraulic & Pneumatic Systems:** Choosing hydraulic fluid with appropriate K prevents pump cavitation. - **Dam & Deep-Water Structures:** Computing volume change of concrete under hydrostatic load. - **Submarine & Deep-Sea Vessel Design:** Hull compressibility directly determines crush depth. - **Soil Mechanics (Saturated Soils):** Pore-water Bulk Modulus governs consolidation rate. - **Pipe Flow & Water Hammer:** Bulk Modulus of water sets the pressure-wave speed: $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}}$. - **Aerospace & Tunnelling:** Air-pocket compressibility influences shock-wave behaviour. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚ÄĒ Bulk Modulus from First Principles (Hydrostatic Loading on a Cube) **Problem Setup:** A solid cube of original side $a$ (and therefore original volume $V = a^3$) is subjected to a uniform hydrostatic pressure $p$ acting normally on all six faces. We seek the Bulk Modulus K. **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Original Volume** $$V = a^3$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ New Dimensions After Loading** Under uniform pressure, each side reduces by a small length $\delta a$. Using linear strain: $$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\delta a}{a}$$ But from Hooke's law under hydrostatic conditions (equal triaxial stress), the linear strain is: $$\varepsilon_x = -\frac{p}{3K}$$ Therefore: $$\delta a = a \cdot \varepsilon_x = -\frac{p \cdot a}{3K}$$ The new side length becomes: $$a' = a + \delta a = a - \frac{p \cdot a}{3K} = a\left(1 - \frac{p}{3K}\right)$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ New Volume After Deformation** $$V' = (a')^3 = a^3 \left(1 - \frac{p}{3K}\right)^3$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Expand Using Binomial Theorem** Since $p \ll K$ in elastic regime, the term $\frac{p}{3K}$ is very small. Using the binomial approximation $(1-x)^3 \approx 1 - 3x$ for tiny $x$: $$V' = a^3 \left[1 - 3 \cdot \frac{p}{3K}\right] = a^3 \left[1 - \frac{p}{K}\right]$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Compute Change in Volume** $$\Delta V = V' - V = a^3 \left[1 - \frac{p}{K}\right] - a^3 = -\frac{p \cdot a^3}{K} = -\frac{p \cdot V}{K}$$ **Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Form the Volumetric Strain** $$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V} = -\frac{p}{K}$$ **Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Invert to Get Bulk Modulus** $$\boxed{\,K = \frac{p}{-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sign Verification:** A positive pressure $p$ gives a negative $\Delta V$. The negative in the denominator makes K positive ‚ÄĒ consistent with the convention that K is always positive for stable materials. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚ÄĒ Relationship Between K, E, and őĹ (KTU's Most Asked Derivation) **Starting Point ‚ÄĒ Generalised Hooke's Law for Triaxial Stress:** For a 3D stress state, the strain along the *x*-axis is: $$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$ **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Apply Hydrostatic Condition** For hydrostatic compression: $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$ $$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p - \nu(-p - p)\right]$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Simplify the Bracket** $$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p - \nu(-2p)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p + 2\nu p\right] = \frac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Note Symmetry (Isotropy)** By symmetry: $\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Sum to Get Volumetric Strain** $$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = 3 \cdot \frac{-p(1-2\nu)}{E} = \frac{-3p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Compare with the Bulk Modulus Definition** We already established that $\varepsilon_v = -\dfrac{p}{K}$. Equating the two expressions: $$-\frac{p}{K} = \frac{-3p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$ **Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Cancel Common Terms (p ‚Č† 0)** $$\frac{1}{K} = \frac{3(1-2\nu)}{E}$$ **Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Final Inversion** $$\boxed{\,K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Insight:** Since K must be positive, the denominator $3(1-2\nu)$ must be positive, which requires $\nu < 0.5$. **No real material has a Poisson's ratio ‚Č• 0.5** ‚ÄĒ this confirms that materials cannot be perfectly incompressible. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem (Model Question):** A solid steel cube of side **50 mm** is subjected to a uniform hydrostatic pressure of **200 MPa**. Given Young's Modulus E = **200 GPa** and Poisson's Ratio őĹ = **0.30**, find: - (a) The Bulk Modulus - (b) The volumetric strain - (c) The change in volume **Solution:** **Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Bulk Modulus** $$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{3(1 - 2 \times 0.30)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{3(0.4)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{1.2}$$ $$K = 166.67 \text{ GPa}$$ **Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Volumetric Strain** $$\varepsilon_v = -\frac{p}{K} = -\frac{200 \times 10^6}{166.67 \times 10^9} = -1.2 \times 10^{-3}$$ (The negative sign confirms volume reduction.) **Part (c) ‚ÄĒ Change in Volume** Original volume: $$V = (0.050)^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$ Change in volume: $$\Delta V = \varepsilon_v \cdot V = -1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} = -1.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3$$ $$\boxed{\,\vert\Delta V\vert = 1.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3 \text{ (volume decreases)}\,}$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Flow ‚ÄĒ How Hydrostatic Stress Produces Volumetric Strain ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["EXTERNAL HYDROSTATIC
PRESSURE P APPLIED
ON ALL SIX FACES"] nodeB["EQUAL TRI-AXIAL
COMPRESSIVE STRESS
SIGMA1 SIGMA2 SIGMA3 EQUAL TO MINUS P"] nodeC["LINEAR STRAIN
DEVELOPS IN X Y Z
EPSILON X EPSILON Y EPSILON Z"] nodeD["SUM OF LINEAR
STRAINS GIVES
VOLUMETRIC STRAIN"] nodeE["RATIO OF STRESS
TO VOLUMETRIC STRAIN
EQUALS BULK MODULUS K"] nodeF["ENGINEERING OUTPUT
COMPRESSIBILITY BETA
WAVE SPEED C
HULL DESIGN"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeB fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeD fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeE fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeF fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ‚ÄĒ Elastic Constants Inter-relationship ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeE["YOUNGS MODULUS
E"] nodeG["SHEAR MODULUS
G"] nodeK["BULK MODULUS
K"] nodeNU["POISSONS RATIO
NU"] nodeE --- nodeG nodeG --- nodeK nodeK --- nodeNU nodeNU --- nodeE nodeE -- "E EQUALS 2G TIMES 1 PLUS NU" --> nodeG nodeG -- "K EQUALS EG OVER 3 TIMES 3G MINUS E" --> nodeK nodeK -- "K EQUALS 2G TIMES 1 PLUS NU OVER 3 TIMES 1 MINUS 2NU" --> nodeNU nodeNU -- "NU EQUALS E OVER 2G MINUS 1" --> nodeE nodeE -- "E EQUALS 3K TIMES 1 MINUS 2NU" --> nodeK nodeK -- "K EQUALS E OVER 3 TIMES 1 MINUS 2NU" --> nodeNU style nodeE fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeG fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeK fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeNU fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Logic Tree ‚ÄĒ Bulk Modulus Problem-Solving Pathway ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeS["START
READ PROBLEM
CAREFULLY"] nodeQ1{"WHAT IS
GIVEN"} nodeQ2{"WHAT IS
ASKED"} nodeS --> nodeQ1 nodeS --> nodeQ2 nodeQ1 -- "P AND DELTA V AND V" --> nodeF1["USE K EQUALS P OVER
NEGATIVE DELTA V BY V"] nodeQ1 -- "E AND NU" --> nodeF2["USE K EQUALS E OVER
3 TIMES 1 MINUS 2NU"] nodeQ1 -- "E AND G" --> nodeF3["USE K EQUALS EG OVER
3 TIMES 3G MINUS E"] nodeQ1 -- "G AND NU" --> nodeF4["USE K EQUALS 2G TIMES 1 PLUS NU
OVER 3 TIMES 1 MINUS 2NU"] nodeQ2 -- "CHANGE IN VOLUME" --> nodeA1["DELTA V EQUALS
NEGATIVE PV BY K"] nodeQ2 -- "VOLUMETRIC STRAIN" --> nodeA2["EPSILON V EQUALS
NEGATIVE P BY K"] nodeQ2 -- "COMPRESSIBILITY" --> nodeA3["BETA EQUALS 1 BY K"] style nodeS fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeQ1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeQ2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeF1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeF2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeF3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeF4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeA1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeA2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeA3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 PART A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024]** **Define Bulk Modulus of Elasticity. What is the significance of the negative sign in its definition?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (2 Marks):** Bulk Modulus (K) is the ratio of hydrostatic (volumetric) stress to the corresponding volumetric strain, within the elastic limit. > > $$K = \frac{p}{-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}}$$ > > **Significance of Negative Sign (1 Mark):** When hydrostatic pressure $p$ **increases**, the volume $V$ **decreases** (őĒV is negative). The negative sign in the denominator mathematically cancels the negative őĒV, ensuring that **K is always reported as a positive quantity** for real materials. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]** **State the relationship between Bulk Modulus (K), Young's Modulus (E), and Poisson's Ratio (őĹ). Explain why Poisson's ratio cannot exceed 0.5.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Relationship (2 Marks):** > > $$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$ > > **Reason for őĹ < 0.5 (1 Mark):** For K to remain positive (a physical requirement for any stable, real material), the denominator $3(1-2\nu)$ must be positive. This requires $(1-2\nu) > 0$, i.e., $\nu < 0.5$. If $\nu \geq 0.5$, K would be zero or negative ‚ÄĒ meaning the material would offer **zero or negative resistance** to volumetric compression, which is physically impossible. --- ### 5.2 PART B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > *[Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following]* *(As per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern)* --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Model Paper Style]** **(a)** Derive the relationship between **Bulk Modulus (K), Young's Modulus (E), and Poisson's Ratio (őĹ)** starting from the generalised Hooke's Law for a triaxial stress state. State the physical significance of each step. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A spherical steel pressure vessel of inner diameter **300 mm** and wall thickness **15 mm** is filled with water. The vessel is lowered to a deep-ocean depth where the external hydrostatic pressure is **40 MPa**. Given: - Young's Modulus of steel: **E = 210 GPa** - Poisson's Ratio of steel: **őĹ = 0.30** - Bulk Modulus of water: **K_water = 2.2 GPa** Calculate: - (i) The Bulk Modulus of the steel vessel material. - (ii) The volumetric strain in the steel shell. - (iii) The percentage change in water volume inside the vessel. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation (7 Marks)** *Step 1 (1 Mark) ‚ÄĒ Generalised Hooke's Law:* For a triaxial stress state with principal stresses $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$, the strain along the x-axis is: $$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$ *Step 2 (1 Mark) ‚ÄĒ Hydrostatic Substitution:* For hydrostatic pressure p applied uniformly: $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$ $$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p - \nu(-p - p)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p + 2\nu p\right]$$ *Step 3 (1 Mark) ‚ÄĒ Simplification:* $$\varepsilon_x = \frac{-p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$ *Step 4 (1 Mark) ‚ÄĒ Isotropy:* By symmetry, identical strains act in y and z directions: $$\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = \frac{-p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$ *Step 5 (1 Mark) ‚ÄĒ Volumetric Strain:* $$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{-3p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$ *Step 6 (1 Mark) ‚ÄĒ Equating with K Definition:* We know $\varepsilon_v = -\dfrac{p}{K}$. Equating: $$-\frac{p}{K} = \frac{-3p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$ *Step 7 (1 Mark) ‚ÄĒ Final Result:* $$\boxed{\,K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}\,}$$ --- **Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Solution (7 Marks)** *(i) Bulk Modulus of Steel (2 Marks):* $$K_{steel} = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} = \frac{210 \times 10^9}{3(1 - 2 \times 0.30)} = \frac{210 \times 10^9}{3(0.4)} = \frac{210 \times 10^9}{1.2}$$ $$K_{steel} = 175 \text{ GPa}$$ *(ii) Volumetric Strain in Steel (2 Marks):* $$\varepsilon_{v,steel} = -\frac{p}{K_{steel}} = -\frac{40 \times 10^6}{175 \times 10^9} = -2.286 \times 10^{-4}$$ *(iii) Percentage Change in Water Volume (3 Marks):* $$\varepsilon_{v,water} = -\frac{p}{K_{water}} = -\frac{40 \times 10^6}{2.2 \times 10^9} = -0.01818 = -1.818\%$$ > **Interpretation:** Water volume decreases by approximately **1.82%** under 40 MPa external pressure ‚ÄĒ illustrating that fluids, despite having no shear stiffness, *do* resist volumetric compression (governed by K). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Model Paper Style]** **(a)** Starting from the **fundamental definition of Bulk Modulus**, derive the expression $K = \dfrac{p}{-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}}$ for a solid cube of side $a$ subjected to uniform hydrostatic pressure $p$ on all six faces. Show all intermediate steps clearly. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A copper cube of side **100 mm** is submerged in a deep fluid. The hydrostatic pressure at that depth is **150 MPa**. For copper: - Young's Modulus: **E = 110 GPa** - Poisson's Ratio: **őĹ = 0.34** Determine: - (i) The Bulk Modulus of copper. - (ii) The reduction in each side of the cube. - (iii) The decrease in total volume of the cube. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation (7 Marks)** *Step 1 (1 Mark):* Let original volume be $V = a^3$. *Step 2 (1 Mark):* Each side shortens by $\delta a$, where (from hydrostatic Hooke's law): $$\delta a = -\frac{p \cdot a}{3K}$$ *Step 3 (1 Mark):* New side length: $a' = a\left(1 - \dfrac{p}{3K}\right)$ *Step 4 (1 Mark):* New volume: $$V' = (a')^3 = a^3\left(1 - \frac{p}{3K}\right)^3$$ *Step 5 (1 Mark):* Binomial expansion for small $\dfrac{p}{3K}$: $$V' \approx a^3\left(1 - \frac{p}{K}\right)$$ *Step 6 (1 Mark):* Change in volume: $$\Delta V = V' - V = -\frac{pV}{K}$$ *Step 7 (1 Mark):* Therefore: $$\boxed{\,K = \frac{p}{-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}}\,}$$ --- **Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Solution (7 Marks)** *(i) Bulk Modulus of Copper (2 Marks):* $$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} = \frac{110 \times 10^9}{3(1 - 0.68)} = \frac{110 \times 10^9}{0.96} = 114.58 \text{ GPa}$$ *(ii) Reduction in Each Side (2 Marks):* $$\delta a = \frac{p \cdot a}{3K} = \frac{150 \times 10^6 \times 0.100}{3 \times 114.58 \times 10^9}$$ $$\delta a = \frac{15 \times 10^6}{343.75 \times 10^9} = 4.36 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 0.0436 \text{ mm}$$ *(iii) Decrease in Total Volume (3 Marks):* Original volume: $V = (0.100)^3 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$ Volumetric strain: $$\varepsilon_v = -\frac{p}{K} = -\frac{150 \times 10^6}{114.58 \times 10^9} = -1.309 \times 10^{-3}$$ Volume change: $$\Delta V = \varepsilon_v \cdot V = -1.309 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} = -1.309 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$ $$\boxed{\,\vert\Delta V\vert = 1.309 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3 = 1.309 \text{ cm}^3\,}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚ÄĒ Common Pitfalls (Lose 2‚Äď3 Marks Each):** > 1. **Forgetting the Negative Sign:** Many students write $K = \dfrac{p}{\Delta V / V}$ and end up with negative K. Always write it as $\dfrac{p}{-\Delta V/V}$ or insert a manual negative sign to keep K positive. > 2. **Wrong Denominator in K‚ÄďE‚ÄďőĹ:** A frequent slip is writing $K = \dfrac{E}{2(1-2\nu)}$ instead of $3(1-2\nu)$. **Always re-derive** if you have any doubt during the exam. > 3. **Mixing Units:** E in **GPa** and pressure in **MPa** ‚ÄĒ convert everything to a single unit (preferably **Pa** for internal consistency) before substitution. > 4. **Ignoring Sign Conventions:** KTU strictly follows the **compression-positive** convention for pressure; volume decrease is negative. Do not mix sign conventions between sub-parts. > 5. **Skipping the Binomial Approximation Justification:** Always **state the assumption** $\dfrac{p}{3K} \ll 1$ before applying $(1-x)^3 \approx 1 - 3x$ in derivations. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final High-Density Revision ‚ÄĒ Bulk Modulus (Module 1)** - **Definition (Must Memorise):** $K = \dfrac{p}{-\Delta V/V}$ ‚ÄĒ ratio of hydrostatic stress to volumetric strain. - **Units:** N/m¬≤ (Pa) or GPa; Volumetric strain is **dimensionless**. - **Negative Sign Purpose:** Ensures K is positive even though őĒV is negative under compression. - **Master Formula:** $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ ‚ÄĒ single most important relationship in this topic. - **Alternative Forms:** $K = \dfrac{EG}{3(3G-E)}$ and $K = \dfrac{2G(1+\nu)}{3(1-2\nu)}$. - **Compressibility:** $\beta = 1/K$; higher K ‚áí lower compressibility. - **Limiting Value of őĹ:** $\nu < 0.5$ always (proven from positivity of K). - **Order of Magnitude:** Steel ~160 GPa, Water ~2.2 GPa, Rubber ~2 GPa, Air ~0.0001 GPa. - **Linear Strain Relation:** $\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\dfrac{p}{3K}$ under pure hydrostatic load. - **Volumetric Strain Identity:** $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = -\dfrac{p}{K}$. - **Engineering Relevance:** Hydraulic systems, water hammer analysis, deep-sea vessel design, soil consolidation. - **Exam Tip:** Always convert units to **Pa** before calculation; always state sign convention explicitly. - **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never confuse Bulk Modulus K with Spring Stiffness k ‚ÄĒ these are fundamentally different quantities. - **Quick Sanity Check:** For any reasonable material, $K > E/3$ (since $1-2\nu < 1$). If your answer violates this, recalculate.

topics.title: Relationships Between Elastic Constants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Relationships Between Elastic Constants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the linear elastic theory of solids, an **isotropic homogeneous material** is fully characterized by **four fundamental elastic constants**. Any two of these constants are independent; the remaining two can be derived using mathematical relationships arising from the generalized form of **Hooke's Law** applied to a three-dimensional elastic body.

The four principal elastic constants are:

1. **Young's Modulus (E)** ‚ÄĒ also called the *Modulus of Elasticity*. It is defined as the ratio of longitudinal (axial) stress to longitudinal strain within the elastic limit.
2. **Shear Modulus (G)** ‚ÄĒ also called the *Modulus of Rigidity*. It is defined as the ratio of shear stress to shear strain within the elastic limit.
3. **Bulk Modulus (K)** ‚ÄĒ defined as the ratio of direct (hydrostatic) stress to volumetric strain.
4. **Poisson's Ratio (őĹ or őľ)** ‚ÄĒ defined as the ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain under uniaxial loading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCCET205 ‚Äď Module 1):** Students must be able to **derive the inter-relationships** between E, G, K, and őĹ, and apply them to **solve numerical problems** in which two constants are given and the others must be found. This topic carries **high weightage** in Part A (3 marks) and frequently appears as a Part B (14 marks) full-question.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a soft rubber cube pressed from all six faces (a hydrostatic pressure situation). Three things happen at once:

- The cube gets **compressed in all three directions** ‚Üí this is governed by **E** (axial stiffness).
- The cube's **shape twists and distorts** ‚Üí this is governed by **G** (resistance to angular distortion).
- The cube's **overall volume shrinks** ‚Üí this is governed by **K** (resistance to volume change).
- As the cube compresses axially, it **bulges sideways** ‚Üí the ratio of this sideways bulge to the axial compression is **őĹ**.

Because all four phenomena occur **simultaneously in the same material**, they cannot be independent. A stiff material (high E) is automatically also hard to compress volumetrically (high K) and resists twisting (high G). The relationships we derive are the **mathematical bridges** that connect these four observations into a single coherent elastic model.

> [!NOTE]
> The relationships derived below are **strictly valid only for homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic materials** that obey Hooke's Law. They do **not apply** to anisotropic materials (e.g., wood, fibre-reinforced composites) or to materials behaving in the plastic range.

### 1.3 Standard Units and Ranges

| Constant | Symbol | Standard SI Unit | Typical Range for Metals |
|---|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus | E | N/m¬≤ or Pa (GPa typical) | 70 ‚Äď 400 GPa |
| Shear Modulus | G | N/m¬≤ or Pa | 25 ‚Äď 150 GPa |
| Bulk Modulus | K | N/m¬≤ or Pa | 60 ‚Äď 200 GPa |
| Poisson's Ratio | őĹ | Dimensionless | 0.25 ‚Äď 0.35 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical bounds for Poisson's Ratio** for stable isotropic elastic materials: **‚ąí1 < őĹ < 0.5**. For most engineering materials, **0 < őĹ < 0.5**. Rubber is near 0.5; cork is near 0.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Governing Relationships

Starting from a **3D cubic element** under three mutually perpendicular direct stresses (ŌÉx, ŌÉy, ŌÉz) and applying **Generalized Hooke's Law**, the following master relationships are obtained:

**Relationship 1 ‚ÄĒ E, G, and őĹ:**

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu)$$

**Relationship 2 ‚ÄĒ E, K, and őĹ:**

$$E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$$

**Relationship 3 ‚ÄĒ E, K, and G (Most frequently asked):**

$$E = \frac{9KG}{3K + G}$$

**Relationship 4 ‚ÄĒ G, K, and őĹ:**

$$G = \frac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)}$$

**Relationship 5 ‚ÄĒ Poisson's ratio in terms of K and G:**

$$\nu = \frac{3K - 2G}{6K + 2G}$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready Cheat Table)

| # | Required Constant | Formula | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | E from G and őĹ | $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ | Most common in 3-mark Part A questions |
| 2 | E from K and őĹ | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | Direct substitution problems |
| 3 | E from K and G | $E = \dfrac{9KG}{3K + G}$ | **Most asked 14-mark derivation** |
| 4 | G from E and őĹ | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}$ | Inverse of Relationship 1 |
| 5 | K from E and őĹ | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$ | Inverse of Relationship 2 |
| 6 | K from E and G | $K = \dfrac{EG}{3(3G - E)}$ | Inverse of Relationship 3 |
| 7 | őĹ from E and G | $\nu = \dfrac{E}{2G} - 1$ | Inverse of Relationship 1 |
| 8 | őĹ from E and K | $\nu = \dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{E}{6K}$ | Inverse of Relationship 2 |
| 9 | őĹ from K and G | $\nu = \dfrac{3K - 2G}{6K + 2G}$ | Inverse of Relationship 4 |
| 10 | Compliance form | $\dfrac{1}{E} = \dfrac{1}{3G} + \dfrac{1}{9K}$ | Alternative form of Relationship 3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When two constants are given, **always check which two** and pick the relationship that bypasses the third one. Example: If E and G are given, use $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ ‚ÄĒ do not waste time finding K first.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These relationships form the **backbone of structural design software** (STAAD.Pro, ETABS, ANSYS). The input material library in any FEA tool contains only **two independent constants** (typically E and őĹ), and the solver internally derives G and K for 3D stress analysis. In geotechnical engineering, **K** is critical for predicting ground settlement under foundations. In pressure vessel design, the relationship $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ governs the allowable **hydrostatic test pressure**. The Poisson's ratio also controls the **lateral earth pressure coefficient** $K_0 = \dfrac{\nu}{1-\nu}$ in soil mechanics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Outcomes Mapped (KTU 2024 COs):** This topic directly satisfies **CO1** ‚ÄĒ *Apply the principles of mechanics to analyse the stress-strain behaviour of deformable solids*.
