## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**
> 
> **Q1.** Define **strain energy** and **strain energy density**. State the units of each. (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

> **Strain Energy (U):** It is the energy stored in a body due to elastic deformation. It is equal to the work done by external forces in deforming the body, provided the deformation is fully recoverable (within the elastic limit). Mathematically, $U = \frac{1}{2} P \delta$ for an axially loaded bar. **Unit:** Joules (J) or N¬∑m.
> 
> **Strain Energy Density (u):** It is the strain energy stored per unit volume of the material. $u = \frac{U}{V} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2 E} = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \epsilon$. **Unit:** J/m¬≥ (or N/m¬≤ = Pa).

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> 
> **Q2.** What is **proof resilience**? Derive the expression for the **modulus of resilience** in terms of yield stress. (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

> **Proof Resilience ($U_p$):** It is defined as the maximum strain energy that can be stored in a body **up to the elastic limit** (i.e., up to the yield point) without undergoing permanent deformation.
> 
> Mathematically, $U_p = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{\,2} \cdot V}{2 E}$.
> 
> **Modulus of Resilience ($\mu$):** It is the proof resilience *per unit volume*:
> $$\mu = \frac{U_p}{V} = \frac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2 E}$$
> 
> It represents the area under the stress‚Äďstrain diagram up to the elastic limit. **Unit:** J/m¬≥.

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> 
> **Q1 (A). (a)** Derive the expression for the strain energy stored in a uniform bar of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and Young's modulus $E$ when subjected to a **gradually applied axial load** $P$. (7 marks)
> 
> **(b)** A circular bar of length **2 m** and diameter **25 mm** is subjected to an axial pull of **50 kN** applied gradually. If $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm¬≤, determine the **strain energy** and **strain energy density** in the bar. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation:**

Step 1: Consider a uniform prismatic bar fixed at one end. Apply a gradually increasing axial load that reaches a final value $P$.

Step 2: At an intermediate load $P_x$, the corresponding elongation is:
$$\delta_x = \frac{P_x L}{A E}$$

Step 3: The small work done as the load increases by $dP_x$ is:
$$dW = P_x \, d\delta_x = P_x \cdot \frac{L}{A E} \, dP_x$$

Step 4: Total work done (= strain energy stored):
$$U = \int_{0}^{P} \frac{L}{A E} P_x \, dP_x = \frac{L}{A E} \cdot \frac{P^{2}}{2} = \frac{P^{2} L}{2 A E}$$

Step 5: Alternative forms:
$$U = \frac{1}{2} P \delta = \frac{\sigma^{2} V}{2 E} = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \epsilon V$$

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical:**

Given: $L = 2 \, \text{m} = 2000 \, \text{mm}$; $d = 25 \, \text{mm}$ ‚Üí $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (25)^{2} = 490.87 \, \text{mm}^{2}$

$P = 50 \, \text{kN} = 50 \times 10^{3} \, \text{N}$; $E = 2 \times 10^{5} \, \text{N/mm}^{2}$

**Strain Energy:**
$$U = \frac{P^{2} L}{2 A E} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{3})^{2} \times 2000}{2 \times 490.87 \times 2 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{2.5 \times 10^{9} \times 2000}{1.9635 \times 10^{8}} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 10^{12}}{1.9635 \times 10^{8}} \\
&= 25{,}464.5 \, \text{N¬∑mm} \\
&\approx 25.46 \, \text{J}
\end{aligned}$$

**Strain Energy Density:**

Volume: $V = A L = 490.87 \times 2000 = 9.8175 \times 10^{5} \, \text{mm}^{3}$

$$u = \frac{U}{V} = \frac{25{,}464.5}{9.8175 \times 10^{5}} = 0.0259 \, \text{N/mm}^{2} = 0.0259 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Verification via stress formula:**
$\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{490.87} = 101.86 \, \text{N/mm}^{2}$

$$u = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E} = \frac{(101.86)^{2}}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{10{,}375.4}{4 \times 10^{5}} = 0.0259 \, \text{MPa} \;\checkmark$$

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> 
> **Q1 (B). (a)** A weight of **5 kN** falls through a height of **100 mm** on to a collar rigidly attached to the end of a vertical bar of length **3 m**, cross-section **600 mm¬≤**, and $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm¬≤. Find the **maximum instantaneous stress** induced in the bar and the **maximum elongation**. (7 marks)
> 
> **(b)** Explain the terms **proof resilience** and **modulus of resilience**. A steel bar of **200 mm¬≤** cross-section and **1.5 m** length is subjected to a gradually applied axial tensile load. If $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm¬≤ and the yield stress is **300 N/mm¬≤**, find the proof resilience of the bar. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Impact Load:**

Given: $W = 5 \, \text{kN} = 5 \times 10^{3} \, \text{N}$; $h = 100 \, \text{mm}$; $L = 3 \, \text{m} = 3000 \, \text{mm}$; $A = 600 \, \text{mm}^{2}$; $E = 2 \times 10^{5} \, \text{N/mm}^{2}$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Energy balance:**
$$W (h + \delta_{max}) = \frac{P_{max}^{2} L}{2 A E}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Hooke's law for dynamic force:**
$$P_{max} = \frac{\delta_{max} A E}{L} = \frac{\delta_{max} \times 600 \times 2 \times 10^{5}}{3000} = 40{,}000 \, \delta_{max}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Substitute into energy equation:**
$$5 \times 10^{3} \, (100 + \delta_{max}) = \frac{(40{,}000)^{2} \, \delta_{max}^{2} \times 3000}{2 \times 600 \times 2 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
5 \times 10^{6} + 5 \times 10^{3} \, \delta_{max} &= \frac{1.6 \times 10^{9} \, \delta_{max}^{2} \times 3000}{2.4 \times 10^{8}} \\
5 \times 10^{6} + 5 \times 10^{3} \, \delta_{max} &= 20{,}000 \, \delta_{max}^{2} \\
20{,}000 \, \delta_{max}^{2} - 5 \times 10^{3} \, \delta_{max} - 5 \times 10^{6} &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Solve the quadratic** ($a = 20{,}000$; $b = -5 \times 10^{3}$; $c = -5 \times 10^{6}$):
$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5 \times 10^{3} + \sqrt{(5 \times 10^{3})^{2} + 4 \times 20{,}000 \times 5 \times 10^{6}}}{2 \times 20{,}000}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_{max} &= \frac{5 \times 10^{3} + \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{7} + 4 \times 10^{11}}}{40{,}000} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 10^{3} + \sqrt{4.00025 \times 10^{11}}}{40{,}000} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 10^{3} + 632{,}479.1}{40{,}000} \\
&= \frac{637{,}479.1}{40{,}000} \\
&\approx 15.94 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Maximum instantaneous stress:**
$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{P_{max}}{A} = \frac{40{,}000 \times 15.94}{600} = \frac{6.376 \times 10^{5}}{600} \approx 1062.6 \, \text{N/mm}^{2}$$

> [!WARNING] 
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ‚ÄĒ Question (a):** A common error is forgetting to **convert the units consistently**. If you use $L$ in m, then $h$ must be in m, and $E$ in N/m¬≤. Mixing m and mm in the same formula leads to dimensional chaos. KTU deducts 2 marks for unit inconsistency. Also, students often forget the $\sqrt{}$ term or take the negative root ‚ÄĒ **always take the positive root** for $\delta_{max}$ (the negative root is physically meaningless).

---

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Definitions + Proof Resilience:**

**Proof Resilience ($U_p$):** Maximum strain energy that can be stored in a body within its elastic limit (up to yield stress) without undergoing permanent deformation. It is the area under the stress‚Äďstrain curve from origin to yield point, multiplied by the volume.

**Modulus of Resilience ($\mu$):** Proof resilience per unit volume. It is a *material property* indicating the energy-absorbing capacity of the material within its elastic range. $\mu = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2 E}$.

**Numerical:**

Given: $A = 200 \, \text{mm}^{2}$; $L = 1.5 \, \text{m} = 1500 \, \text{mm}$; $E = 2 \times 10^{5} \, \text{N/mm}^{2}$; $\sigma_y = 300 \, \text{N/mm}^{2}$.

Volume: $V = A L = 200 \times 1500 = 3 \times 10^{5} \, \text{mm}^{3}$

$$\begin{aligned}
U_p &= \frac{\sigma_y^{\,2} \cdot V}{2 E} \\
&= \frac{(300)^{2} \times 3 \times 10^{5}}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{5}} \\
&= \frac{90{,}000 \times 3 \times 10^{5}}{4 \times 10^{5}} \\
&= \frac{2.7 \times 10^{10}}{4 \times 10^{5}} \\
&= 67{,}500 \, \text{N¬∑mm} = 67.5 \, \text{J}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING] 
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ‚ÄĒ Question (b):** Marks are often lost in two places:
> 1. **Forgetting the "√ó V"** ‚ÄĒ Students compute only $\mu$ (modulus of resilience) but the question asks for $U_p$ (proof resilience, *not* per unit volume). KTU deducts 2‚Äď3 marks for this.
> 2. **Mixing up the formula** ‚ÄĒ Some students write $U_p = \frac{1}{2} \sigma_y \cdot \epsilon_y \cdot V$ but then use the wrong $\epsilon_y$ value. Always derive $\epsilon_y = \sigma_y / E$ first, *then* plug in.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision ‚ÄĒ Strain Energy Due to Normal Stress**

- **Strain Energy (U)** = work done by external load during elastic deformation = area under $P$‚Äď$\delta$ curve.
- **Master Formula (Gradual):** $U = \dfrac{P^{2} L}{2 A E} = \dfrac{1}{2} P \delta = \dfrac{\sigma^{2} V}{2 E} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sigma \epsilon V$.
- **Strain Energy Density (u):** $u = \dfrac{U}{V} = \dfrac{\sigma^{2}}{2 E} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sigma \epsilon$. Units: J/m¬≥ or MPa.
- **Proof Resilience ($U_p$):** Max strain energy up to elastic limit. $U_p = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{\,2} V}{2 E}$.
- **Modulus of Resilience ($\mu$):** Proof resilience per unit volume. $\mu = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2 E}$.
- **Sudden Load DMF = 2:** $\delta_{max} = 2 \delta_{static}$, $\sigma_{max} = 2 \sigma_{static}$, $U_{sudden} = 2 U_{gradual}$.
- **Impact Load Energy Balance:** $W(h + \delta_{max}) = \dfrac{\delta_{max}^{2} A E}{2 L}$. Solve quadratic in $\delta_{max}$, take **positive root**.
- **Impact Load Max Force:** $P_{max} = W \!\left( 1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2 h A E}{W L}} \right)$.
- **Units Discipline (KTU strict):** Use **N, mm, N/mm¬≤, N¬∑mm** consistently. Mixing units is a guaranteed 1‚Äď2 mark penalty.
- **Sanity Checks (Board-exam safety net):**
  - Strain energy must be **positive** (always).
  - Strain energy density unit must match stress unit (Pa = N/m¬≤ = N¬∑m/m¬≥).
  - In impact problems, $\delta_{max} \geq 2 \delta_{static}$ (i.e., more than sudden-load case). If you get a smaller value, recheck.
- **Forgetting $\frac{1}{2}$ factor** is the single most common error ‚ÄĒ it costs **1 mark** in every derivation question.
- **Strain energy is recoverable** only if the material remains in the elastic range; otherwise, plastic deformation dissipates the energy as heat and sound.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strain Energy in Bars Carrying Axial Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Strain Energy in Bars Carrying Axial Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STRAIN ENERGY (U):** Strain energy is the internal energy stored within a deformable body (such as an axially loaded bar) due to the work done by external forces in producing elastic deformation. Mathematically, it is defined as the area under the **Load (P) vs. Elongation (őī)** curve up to the elastic limit, given by:
>
> $$U = \int_{0}^{\delta} P \, d\delta$$

For a bar of length **L**, cross-sectional area **A**, and Young's Modulus **E**, subjected to an axial tensile or compressive force **P** (within the elastic limit), the strain energy is expressed as:

$$U = \frac{P^{2} L}{2 A E} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2 E} \cdot (A L) = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon \cdot V$$

where **V = AL** is the volume of the bar, **ŌÉ** is the induced axial stress, and **őĶ** is the corresponding axial strain.

### Key Related Terminologies

| Term | Definition | Standard Unit |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **Resilience** | Total strain energy stored within the elastic limit (volume-integrated). | N¬∑m (Joules) |
| **Proof Resilience** | Maximum strain energy stored up to the **elastic limit** without permanent deformation. | N¬∑m (Joules) |
| **Modulus of Resilience (u_r)** | Strain energy stored per unit volume up to the elastic limit. | N¬∑m/m¬≥ (Pa) |
| **Strain Energy Density (u)** | Instantaneous strain energy stored per unit volume at any stress ŌÉ. | N¬∑m/m¬≥ (Pa) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modulus of Resilience Formula:** $u_r = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}^{2}}{2E}$, where **ŌÉ_y** is the yield stress. This is a key design parameter for materials that must absorb shocks (e.g., railway buffers, vehicle springs).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine stretching a **rubber band** between your fingers. As you pull harder, you feel a resisting force grow, and the rubber becomes slightly longer. The work your muscles do on the rubber band does not vanish ‚ÄĒ it is **stored as internal energy** within the molecular structure of the rubber. The instant you release, the rubber "snaps back" because the stored energy converts back into kinetic energy of motion.

A **steel bar under axial load** behaves on the same principle:
- The **load (P)** is your pulling force.
- The **elongation (őī)** is the stretch you observe.
- The **stored energy (U)** is the integral of (force √ó tiny extension) at every stage of loading.
- If you unload the bar, the energy is given back ‚ÄĒ this is what makes the material **elastic** and the system **conservative**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why engineers care about strain energy:** It determines a structure's ability to absorb shocks (e.g., during earthquakes, vehicle impacts, or pile driving). Materials with high resilience protect against sudden failure. This is why **railway couplings, automobile bumpers, and structural dampers** are designed using the principles of strain energy storage.

---

## 1.3 Three Categories of Axial Loading for Energy Analysis

In KTU board examinations, the analysis of strain energy in axial loading is **always** contextualized under three classical load types. Each produces a different stress and energy distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Gradually Applied (Static) Load:** Load increases slowly from 0 to P. Stress = P/A, Strain Energy = P¬≤L/(2AE).
> **2. Suddenly Applied Load:** Load instantly switches from 0 to full P. Stress = **2P/A** (twice the static value), Strain Energy = P¬≤L/(2AE) ‚ÄĒ same energy, but stress is doubled.
> **3. Impact / Falling Load:** Load is dropped from a height **h** onto the bar. Stress = P √ó [1 + ‚ąö(1 + 2h/őī_st)], inducing far higher dynamic stresses than the static equivalent.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation Logic ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Reasoning

### **Case 1: Uniform Bar under Axial Load (Constant Cross-Section)**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Consider an elemental slice:**
Take an infinitesimal element of length **dx** at any location along a uniform bar of cross-section **A** and total length **L**, subjected to axial force **P**.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Stress and strain in the element:**
The axial stress in the element is uniform:
$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A}$$
Using Hooke's Law, the corresponding strain is:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{P}{AE}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Elongation of the element:**
The infinitesimal extension **dőī** of the element is:
$$d\delta = \varepsilon \cdot dx = \frac{P \cdot dx}{AE}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Work done on the element (Strain energy in element):**
Since the element extends by **dőī** under force **P**, the work stored as strain energy **dU** is:
$$dU = \frac{1}{2} \cdot P \cdot d\delta = \frac{1}{2} \cdot P \cdot \frac{P \, dx}{AE} = \frac{P^{2}}{2AE} \cdot dx$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Total strain energy by integration over L:**
$$U = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{P^{2}}{2AE} \, dx = \frac{P^{2}}{2AE} \cdot L = \frac{P^{2} L}{2AE}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The factor **¬Ĺ** appears because for an **elastic** body the load grows linearly with deformation (P ‚ąĚ őī). Energy is the area of the **triangle** under the P‚Äďőī curve, not a rectangle. The total energy is the area of this triangle: ¬Ĺ √ó base √ó height = ¬Ĺ √ó őī √ó P.

---

### **Case 2: Non-Uniform (Varying Cross-Section) Bar**

For a bar where cross-section **A(x)** varies along its length, the energy in an element of length **dx** is:
$$dU = \frac{P^{2}}{2 A(x) E} \, dx$$

**Total Strain Energy:**
$$U = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{P^{2}}{2 A(x) E} \, dx = \frac{P^{2}}{2E} \int_{0}^{L} \frac{dx}{A(x)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This integral form is **highly tested** in KTU for problems involving stepped bars, tapered bars, or bars with holes. A common simplification is to break the bar into **n segments** of constant cross-section, then sum:
> $$U = \frac{P^{2}}{2E} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_{i}}{A_{i}}$$

---

### **Case 3: Bar with Self-Weight (Dead Load)**

When a bar is vertical and its **own weight W** induces the load, the stress is not constant. At a depth **x** from the free end:
$$P(x) = \rho g A x \quad \text{where} \quad \rho = \text{density}, \quad g = \text{gravity}$$

Strain energy stored:
$$U = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{(\rho g A x)^{2}}{2 A E} \, dx = \frac{\rho^{2} g^{2} A L^{3}}{6 E}$$

In terms of total weight **W = ŌĀgAL**:
$$U = \frac{W^{2} L}{6 A E}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Comparison with External Load (P = W at end):** $U_{end} = \dfrac{W^{2}L}{2AE}$ is **3 times** the self-weight energy $U_{self} = \dfrac{W^{2}L}{6AE}$. This means self-weight loading is a *more gradual* application ‚ÄĒ like a graduated load.

---

### **Case 4: Shear Strain Energy Density (Brief Note)**

For completeness, in a 3D stress state, the **volumetric strain energy density** combines normal and shear components:
$$u = \frac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_{x}^{2} + \sigma_{y}^{2} + \sigma_{z}^{2} - 2\nu(\sigma_{x}\sigma_{y} + \sigma_{y}\sigma_{z} + \sigma_{z}\sigma_{x})\right] + \frac{1}{2G}(\tau_{xy}^{2} + \tau_{yz}^{2} + \tau_{zx}^{2})$$

For uniaxial tension (ŌÉ_x = ŌÉ, all others zero):
$$u = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E}$$

This matches our axial formula. ‚úď

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEEP THIS TABLE FOR EXAM DAY ‚ÄĒ HIGH-FREQUENCY FORMULAS**

| # | Scenario | Governing Formula | Stress / Energy Multiplier |
|---|----------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Uniform bar, axial load P | $U = \dfrac{P^{2}L}{2AE}$ | ŌÉ = P/A (static) |
| 2 | Bar with axial load P (alternative form) | $U = \dfrac{1}{2} P \delta$ where $\delta = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$ | Same as #1 |
| 3 | Stress-strain energy density | $u = \dfrac{\sigma^{2}}{2E} = \dfrac{1}{2}\sigma\varepsilon$ | Per unit volume |
| 4 | Varying cross-section (continuous) | $U = \dfrac{P^{2}}{2E} \int_{0}^{L} \dfrac{dx}{A(x)}$ | For tapered/stepped bars |
| 5 | Stepped bar (n segments) | $U = \dfrac{P^{2}}{2E} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_{i}}{A_{i}}$ | Discrete sections |
| 6 | Bar under its own weight W | $U = \dfrac{W^{2} L}{6 A E}$ | Stress varies linearly with depth |
| 7 | **Gradually applied load** | $U = \dfrac{P^{2} L}{2 A E}$ (same form) | ŌÉ_static = P/A |
| 8 | **Suddenly applied load** | $U = \dfrac{P^{2} L}{2 A E}$ (energy same!) | **ŌÉ = 2P/A** (doubled) |
| 9 | **Impact load from height h** | $U = \dfrac{P^{2} L}{2 A E} \left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right)^{2}$ | $\sigma_{impact} = \sigma_{st} \left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right)$ |
| 10 | Castigliano's Theorem (axial) | $\delta = \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial P}$ | Deflection = partial derivative of U w.r.t. load |
| 11 | Proof Resilience | $U_{p} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}^{2}}{2E} \cdot V$ | Up to yield |
| 12 | Modulus of Resilience | $u_{r} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}^{2}}{2E}$ | Per unit volume |

**Standard Constants & Units:**
- **Young's Modulus (E):** Steel ‚Čą **200 GPa**, Concrete ‚Čą **30 GPa**, Aluminum ‚Čą **70 GPa**, Copper ‚Čą **100 GPa**.
- **Strain Energy (U):** N¬∑m or **Joules (J)**.
- **Strain Energy Density (u):** **N¬∑m/m¬≥** or **J/m¬≥** (equivalent to **Pa**).
- **Gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s¬≤**.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!TIP]
> **Where is strain energy analysis used in actual civil engineering practice?**

1. **Pile Driving & Foundation Engineering:** An impact load (pile hammer) drives a precast concrete pile into the ground. Engineers must check that the induced dynamic stress does not exceed the compressive strength of the pile ‚ÄĒ direct application of impact loading formulas.
2. **Earthquake-Resistant Design:** Structures absorb seismic energy through controlled elastic deformation. The **resilience** of structural steel (high E, good yield strength) is a key selection criterion.
3. **Pre-stressed Concrete:** Tendons are pre-tensioned; strain energy is locked into the steel and transferred to concrete as pre-compression. The strain energy analysis governs the loss of prestress over time.
4. **Crane and Hoist Cables:** Sudden snatching of a load (e.g., during lifting) is a *suddenly applied load* case. The factor-of-2 stress amplification is why operators are trained to lift smoothly.
5. **Railway Buffer Stops and Shock Absorbers:** Designed to maximize strain energy absorption using the modulus of resilience criterion.
6. **Aerospace and Mechanical Springs:** Helical and leaf springs are sized such that their stored strain energy matches required impact energy absorption.

---

## 2.4 The Concept of "Equivalent Static Load" (Why Gradual, Sudden & Impact Differ)

A central idea in KTU board problems is the concept of an **equivalent gradual (static) load** that would produce the *same strain energy* as a dynamic loading scenario.

- For **suddenly applied load P:** The bar does *not* deform gradually. The full elongation **őī** is reached instantly, and elastic recovery kicks the load up. Energy conservation requires: $\frac{1}{2} P_{equiv} \cdot \delta = \frac{1}{2} P \cdot \delta$, so **P_equiv = P** for energy, but stress is doubled because of momentum reversal.
- For **impact from height h:** The falling weight **W** gains kinetic energy **W¬∑h** before striking. This KE is added to the strain energy: $U = W \cdot h + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{P^2 L}{AE}$ (if P is the static preload). Solving yields the **dynamic stress multiplier** $\left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right)$.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Derivation ‚ÄĒ Strain Energy in a Uniform Axially Loaded Bar

We start from the **fundamental definition**: strain energy is the work done by the gradually applied external force in producing deformation. We use a small slice of the bar and integrate.

**Step 1: Define the element.**
Consider a uniform bar of length **L**, cross-sectional area **A**, Young's Modulus **E**, fixed at the lower end. An axial tensile force **P** is applied gradually at the upper (free) end.

**Step 2: Stress analysis.**
At any cross-section, the internal axial force is constant and equal to **P** (since the bar is in equilibrium under a single point load at the free end). The induced normal stress is:
$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A}$$

**Step 3: Strain via Hooke's Law.**
Within the elastic limit:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{P}{AE}$$

**Step 4: Total elongation of the bar.**
The total elongation is obtained by integrating the elemental strain over the length:
$$\delta = \int_{0}^{L} \varepsilon \, dx = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{P}{AE} \, dx = \frac{P}{AE} \cdot L = \frac{PL}{AE}$$

**Step 5: Construct the Load‚ÄďElongation (P‚Äďőī) curve.**
Since **P** is proportional to **őī** (both are linear in P, with constant slope AE/L), the P‚Äďőī diagram is a **straight line** through the origin with slope k = AE/L (the axial stiffness).

**Step 6: Compute work done = Area under the P‚Äďőī curve.**
Because **P** is applied *gradually* (from 0 to P), at any intermediate elongation **őī'**, the load is **P' = (AE/L)¬∑őī'**. The work done in producing a further infinitesimal extension **dőī** is:
$$dU = P' \cdot d\delta = \frac{AE}{L} \cdot \delta' \cdot d\delta$$

**Step 7: Integrate from 0 to őī (or 0 to P).**
$$U = \int_{0}^{\delta} \frac{AE}{L} \cdot \delta' \, d\delta' = \frac{AE}{L} \cdot \frac{\delta^{2}}{2} = \frac{AE \delta^{2}}{2L}$$

**Step 8: Substitute őī = PL/AE and simplify.**
$$U = \frac{AE}{2L} \cdot \left(\frac{PL}{AE}\right)^{2} = \frac{AE}{2L} \cdot \frac{P^{2}L^{2}}{A^{2}E^{2}} = \frac{P^{2} L}{2 A E}$$

$$\boxed{U = \frac{P^{2} L}{2 A E}}$$

**Step 9: Express in terms of stress and volume.**
Multiplying numerator and denominator by **A**:
$$U = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{A} \cdot \frac{PL}{AE} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sigma \cdot \delta \cdot \text{(using chain)}$$

A cleaner form: write the **strain energy density**:
$$u = \frac{U}{V} = \frac{U}{A \cdot L} = \frac{1}{E} \cdot \left(\frac{P}{A}\right)^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{U = u \cdot V = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E} \cdot AL = \frac{1}{2}\sigma\varepsilon \cdot AL}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Units: $\dfrac{N^{2} \cdot m}{m^{2} \cdot \dfrac{N}{m^{2}}} = N \cdot m = J$ ‚úď

---

## 3.2 Derivation ‚ÄĒ Suddenly Applied Load (Stress = 2P/A)

A weight **P** is applied **instantly** (no time for gradual deformation). The bar suddenly elongates by **őī_st = PL/AE**, the static equilibrium elongation.

**Step 1: Apply energy balance.**
- External work done by the suddenly applied load P: This is **P √ó őī** (a rectangle, not a triangle), because the load is constant at P throughout the deformation.
- Strain energy stored in bar: **U = ¬Ĺ √ó k √ó őī¬≤ = ¬Ĺ √ó (AE/L) √ó őī¬≤**.

**Step 2: Conservation of energy (assuming no losses).**
$$P \cdot \delta = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{AE}{L} \cdot \delta^{2}$$

Wait ‚ÄĒ this is incorrect for suddenly applied load. In sudden loading, the load P remains constant, and the bar **oscillates** between 0 and 2őī_st before settling. The maximum stress occurs at the maximum elongation **őī_max = 2őī_st**. Let's derive this properly:

**Step 2 (Corrected):** When the load P is suddenly applied at the free end of a massless bar:
- At the instant of application, elongation is **0** (load is suddenly there but bar hasn't moved).
- The bar accelerates, overshoots equilibrium, and oscillates.
- The peak elongation is **őī_max = 2őī_st** (a classical result in dynamics of elastic systems).
- The peak stress is:
$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{P \cdot \delta_{max}}{A \cdot \delta_{st}/L} \cdot \frac{1}{A} = \frac{P \cdot 2 \delta_{st}}{A \delta_{st}} = \frac{2P}{A}$$

Equivalently, using **Castigliano's theorem for dynamic systems**, the equivalent static load is **P_equiv = 2P**, so:
$$\sigma_{max} = 2 \cdot \frac{P}{A} = \frac{2P}{A}$$

**Step 3: Strain energy at peak deformation.**
The strain energy at the maximum elongation **2őī_st** is:
$$U_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{AE}{L} \cdot (2\delta_{st})^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{AE}{L} \cdot 4 \delta_{st}^{2} = 2 \cdot \frac{P^{2}L}{2AE} = \frac{P^{2}L}{AE}$$

This equals the work done by P over the maximum elongation 2őī_st:
$$W = P \cdot 2\delta_{st} = \frac{2P \cdot PL}{AE} = \frac{P^{2}L}{AE} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = \frac{2P^{2}L}{2AE} \cdot 2$$

Wait ‚ÄĒ let me redo. Static elongation: $\delta_{st} = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$. Then $P \cdot 2\delta_{st} = \dfrac{2P^{2}L}{AE}$. The strain energy formula gives $U_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{AE}{L} \cdot (2\delta_{st})^{2} = \dfrac{2AE \delta_{st}^{2}}{L} = \dfrac{2AE}{L} \cdot \dfrac{P^{2}L^{2}}{A^{2}E^{2}} = \dfrac{2P^{2}L}{AE}$ ‚úď.

So $U_{max} = W = \dfrac{2P^{2}L}{AE}$, which is **twice** the static energy $\dfrac{P^{2}L}{2AE}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Board-Exam Point:** For a **suddenly applied** load, the maximum *stress* is **2P/A**, but the maximum *strain energy* is $\dfrac{2P^{2}L}{AE}$ (not $\dfrac{P^{2}L}{2AE}$). The factor of 2 in energy is a frequent trap. Always remember: **energy stored = work done by constant force over its actual displacement**, not the static equilibrium value alone.

---

## 3.3 Derivation ‚ÄĒ Impact Load (Stress Multiplier from Height h)

A weight **W** falls from a height **h** onto a collar attached to the free end of a vertical bar (length L, area A, modulus E). The bar is also preloaded by a static weight **P** at the bottom (or top ‚ÄĒ depending on problem framing). Find the maximum stress and deformation.

**Step 1: Energy balance (Work‚ÄďEnergy Theorem).**
- Potential energy lost by weight **W** falling through total distance **(h + őī_max)**: $E_{PE} = W(h + \delta_{max})$
- Strain energy stored in bar at max elongation **őī_max**:
$$U_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{AE}{L} \cdot \delta_{max}^{2}$$
- Energy dissipated (if any preload P works through őī_max): $E_{preload} = \frac{1}{2} P \cdot \delta_{max}$ (this depends on the boundary conditions; in simplest case W is the only load).

**Step 2: Equate PE lost to strain energy stored.**
For simplicity, assume only W acts (no preload P):
$$W(h + \delta_{max}) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{AE}{L} \cdot \delta_{max}^{2}$$

**Step 3: Rearrange into a quadratic in őī_max.**
$$\frac{AE}{2L} \cdot \delta_{max}^{2} - W \cdot \delta_{max} - W \cdot h = 0$$

Multiply by 2L/AE:
$$\delta_{max}^{2} - \frac{2 W L}{A E} \cdot \delta_{max} - \frac{2 W L h}{A E} = 0$$

Note that **W¬∑L/(AE) = őī_st** (the static elongation produced by W alone). Substitute:
$$\delta_{max}^{2} - 2 \delta_{st} \cdot \delta_{max} - 2 h \cdot \delta_{st} = 0$$

**Step 4: Solve the quadratic using the formula.**
$$\delta_{max} = \frac{2\delta_{st} \pm \sqrt{4\delta_{st}^{2} + 8 h \cdot \delta_{st}}}{2} = \delta_{st} \pm \sqrt{\delta_{st}^{2} + 2 h \cdot \delta_{st}}$$

Take the **positive root** (extension is positive, + sign):
$$\delta_{max} = \delta_{st} \left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right)$$

**Step 5: Derive the maximum stress.**
Since stress is proportional to elongation in a linear elastic bar:
$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{E \cdot \delta_{max}}{L} = \frac{E \cdot \delta_{st}}{L} \cdot \left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right)$$

Recognizing that $\sigma_{st} = \dfrac{W}{A} = \dfrac{E \cdot \delta_{st}}{L}$:
$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = \sigma_{st} \cdot \left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right)}$$

**Step 6: Limiting cases.**
- If **h = 0** (load just placed without drop): $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_{st} \cdot (1 + 1) = 2\sigma_{st}$ ‚úď (matches suddenly applied load).
- If **h ‚Üí ‚ąě** (very large drop): $\sigma_{max} \approx \sigma_{st} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}$ ‚ÄĒ grows without bound.
- If **h ‚Čę őī_st**: $\sigma_{max} \approx \sigma_{st} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}$ ‚ÄĒ large dynamic amplification.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Comparative Summary of Load Types

> [!NOTE]
> **MASTER COMPARISON TABLE (Save for the exam)**

| Parameter | Gradually Applied | Suddenly Applied | Impact (Drop from height h) |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------|------------------------------|
| **Force-time profile** | P rises linearly from 0 to P | 0 to P instantly | 0, then P (= W) on contact |
| **Peak elongation** | őī_st = PL/AE | őī_max = **2őī_st** | $\delta_{max} = \delta_{st}\left(1+\sqrt{1+\dfrac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right)$ |
| **Peak stress** | ŌÉ_st = P/A | ŌÉ_max = **2P/A** | $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_{st}\left(1+\sqrt{1+\dfrac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right)$ |
| **Strain energy at peak** | $U = \dfrac{P^2L}{2AE}$ | $U_{max} = \dfrac{2P^2L}{2AE} = \dfrac{P^2L}{AE}$ | $U_{max} = W(h + \delta_{max})$ |
| **Work done by external force** | Triangle: ¬Ĺ¬∑P¬∑őī_st | Rectangle: P¬∑2őī_st | $W \cdot (h + \delta_{max})$ |
| **Stress multiplier** | **1√ó** | **2√ó** | $\left(1+\sqrt{1+\dfrac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right)$ |
| **Typical scenario** | Slowly placed weight | Drop from infinitesimal height | Hammer strike, pile driving |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Insight ‚ÄĒ Why the "Factor of 2" Exists for Sudden Loads

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Check:** When a weight is placed *gradually* on a scale, the reading stabilizes at **W** and the spring (or bar) extends by **őī_st**. The energy stored is **¬ĹWőī_st** (triangle area under the load-extension curve).
>
> When a weight is *suddenly* placed (e.g., dropped from 1 mm), the bar is forced to absorb the same final energy but with a constant force throughout the deformation. To reach the same final potential-energy state, the system must oscillate ‚ÄĒ first overshooting to 2őī_st, then back to őī_st, then to 0, etc. (in absence of damping).
>
> At the **peak of overshoot** (őī_max = 2őī_st), the strain energy is **4 times** the static value ($U = \frac{1}{2}k(2\delta_{st})^2 = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{2}k\delta_{st}^{2}$), and stress is **2 times** the static stress.

This is why **rigid lifting equipment** (cranes) require **smooth acceleration profiles** ‚ÄĒ sudden jerk would double the cable stress and risk snapping the cable.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture ‚ÄĒ Strain Energy Analysis Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram maps out the **decision logic** an engineer follows when analyzing strain energy in a bar under axial load.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Bar Under Axial Load] --> B{Is the bar Uniform Cross-Section?}
    B -- YES --> C{Is the Load Gradual, Sudden, or Impact?}
    B -- NO --> D[Divide Bar into n Segments of Constant A]
    D --> E[For each segment, compute Stress, Strain, Energy]
    E --> F[Sum Energies over all segments: U equals Sum of Pi squared Li over 2AiE]
    
    C -- GRADUAL --> G[Stress equals P over A]
    C -- SUDDEN --> H[Stress equals 2P over A, Max Energy equals P squared L over AE]
    C -- IMPACT --> I[Compute Static Elongation delta_st equals WL over AE]
    I --> J[Peak Stress equals sigma_st times Open Paren 1 plus sqrt Open Paren 1 plus 2h over delta_st Close Paren Close Paren]
    
    G --> K[Compute Strain Energy U equals P squared L over 2AE]
    H --> K
    J --> L[Compute Maximum Strain Energy equals W times Open Paren h plus delta_max Close Paren]
    F --> M[Total Strain Energy equals Sum of Pi squared Li over 2AiE]
    
    K --> N[OUTPUT: Stress, Elongation, Strain Energy]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[END: Design Check: Stress within Elastic Limit?]
```

## 4.2 Schematic ‚ÄĒ Load vs. Elongation (P‚Äďőī) Curves for the Three Load Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GRADUAL [Gradual Loading]
        A1[Origin] --> B1[Linear Rise 0 to P]
        B1 --> C1[Final Point P, delta_st]
    end
    subgraph SUDDEN [Sudden Loading]
        A2[Origin] --> B2[Vertical Jump 0 to P at delta equals 0]
        B2 --> C2[Overshoot to P at delta equals 2 delta_st]
        C2 --> D2[Oscillate back to P at delta equals delta_st]
    end
    subgraph IMPACT [Impact from height h]
        A3[Origin] --> B3[Vertical Jump 0 to W at delta equals 0]
        B3 --> C3[Overshoot to W at delta equals delta_max]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In **gradual** loading, the load rises smoothly along a straight line to (P, őī_st). In **sudden** loading, the load is applied as a vertical step at őī = 0, the system overshoots equilibrium, and oscillates. In **impact** loading, the maximum elongation őī_max is far greater than őī_st depending on the drop height h.

## 4.3 Schematic ‚ÄĒ Energy Distribution in a Tapered (Varying Cross-Section) Bar

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tapered Bar: A varies from A1 to A2 over Length L] --> B[Choose Elemental Slice of Thickness dx at position x]
    B --> C[Elemental Area: A of x]
    C --> D[Stress in Element: sigma equals P over A of x]
    D --> E[Strain in Element: epsilon equals P over A of x times E]
    E --> F[Elemental Elongation: d delta equals P dx over A of x times E]
    F --> G[Elemental Strain Energy: dU equals P squared dx over 2 A of x E]
    G --> H[Integrate over L: U equals P squared over 2E times Integral dx over A of x from 0 to L]
```

> [!TIP]
> **For a linear taper** A(x) = A‚āĀ + (A‚āā ‚ąí A‚āĀ)¬∑(x/L), the integral simplifies to:
> $$\int_{0}^{L} \frac{dx}{A(x)} = \frac{L}{A_2 - A_1} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$$
>
> So the strain energy becomes:
> $$U = \frac{P^{2} L}{2E (A_2 - A_1)} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$$

## 4.4 Free-Body-Diagram Reference (Text Description)

For a vertical bar of length **L** fixed at the bottom and loaded at the top:

```
        P (axial load at top)
        ‚Üď
       ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
       |   |  ‚Üź Top: free end
       ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
       |   |
       |   |  ‚Üź Bar segments with stress ŌÉ = P/A
       |   |     at every cross-section
       |   |
       |   |
       ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
       ‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď  ‚Üź Bottom: fixed support (rigid wall / ground)
```

The **internal force** at any horizontal cut is **P** (in tension). The **strain energy** is distributed uniformly along the bar when the cross-section is constant.
