# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2-mark and 3-mark Short Answer)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part A questions in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE) are typically **2 or 3 marks**, answerable in 1‚Äď2 sentences or with a single formula. The model answers below are calibrated to the mark distribution.

---

### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**Define the term "Strain Energy" and state its unit. Also define "Modulus of Resilience".** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

> **Strain Energy (U):** The internal energy stored within an elastic body due to the work done by external forces in producing deformation. It is numerically equal to the area under the **Load‚ÄďElongation (P‚Äďőī) curve** within the elastic limit.
>
> **Formula:** $U = \dfrac{P^{2}L}{2AE}$ for a uniform axially loaded bar.
>
> **SI Unit:** **N¬∑m** or **Joule (J)**.
>
> **Modulus of Resilience (uŠĶ£):** Strain energy stored per unit volume of a material when stressed up to its elastic limit.
>
> **Formula:** $u_{r} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}^{2}}{2E}$
>
> **SI Unit:** **N¬∑m/m¬≥** or **J/m¬≥**.

---

### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 1]`

**A mild steel bar of length 2 m and cross-sectional area 400 mm¬≤ is subjected to an axial tensile load of 80 kN. Take E = 200 GPa. Calculate the strain energy stored in the bar.** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Length: L = 2 m
- Area: A = 400 mm¬≤ = 400 √ó 10‚ĀĽ‚Ā∂ m¬≤
- Axial load: P = 80 kN = 80 √ó 10¬≥ N
- Young's Modulus: E = 200 GPa = 200 √ó 10‚ĀĻ N/m¬≤

**Formula:**
$$U = \frac{P^{2} L}{2 A E}$$

**Calculation:**
$$U = \frac{(80 \times 10^{3})^{2} \times 2}{2 \times (400 \times 10^{-6}) \times (200 \times 10^{9})}$$

$$U = \frac{6.4 \times 10^{9} \times 2}{1.6 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$U = \frac{1.28 \times 10^{10}}{1.6 \times 10^{8}} = 80 \text{ J}$$

**‚ąī Strain Energy Stored = 80 N¬∑m (Joules).**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 marks ‚ÄĒ with Internal Choice as per KTU 2024)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries a **14-mark question** with **internal choice** (two alternatives, student attempts one). Sub-parts are usually **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The solutions below mirror actual KTU valuation schemes.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper, Module 1]`**

**(a)** A steel bar of length **1.5 m** and cross-sectional area **600 mm¬≤** is subjected to a gradually applied axial load of **120 kN**. If the Young's Modulus of the material is **E = 200 GPa**, find:
   (i) The strain energy stored in the bar. (3 marks)
   (ii) The strain energy density. (2 marks)
   (iii) The proof resilience, if the yield stress is 250 MPa. (2 marks)

**(b)** A bar of length **1.0 m** consists of two segments: Segment 1 (length 0.4 m, area 500 mm¬≤) and Segment 2 (length 0.6 m, area 300 mm¬≤). The bar is subjected to an axial tensile load of **90 kN**. Calculate the total strain energy stored. If the same load were applied suddenly, what would be the maximum stress induced? (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

### Part (a) Solution:

**Given Data:**
- L = 1.5 m, A = 600 mm¬≤ = 600 √ó 10‚ĀĽ‚Ā∂ m¬≤
- P = 120 kN = 120 √ó 10¬≥ N
- E = 200 GPa = 200 √ó 10‚ĀĻ N/m¬≤
- ŌÉ_y = 250 MPa = 250 √ó 10‚Ā∂ N/m¬≤

**(i) Strain Energy Stored (3 marks):**
$$U = \frac{P^{2} L}{2 A E}$$

$$U = \frac{(120 \times 10^{3})^{2} \times 1.5}{2 \times (600 \times 10^{-6}) \times (200 \times 10^{9})}$$

$$U = \frac{1.44 \times 10^{10} \times 1.5}{2.4 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$U = \frac{2.16 \times 10^{10}}{2.4 \times 10^{8}} = 90 \text{ J}$$

**(ii) Strain Energy Density (2 marks):**
$$u = \frac{U}{V} = \frac{U}{A \cdot L}$$

$$u = \frac{90}{600 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.5} = \frac{90}{9 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.0 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/m}^{3}$$

**Alternatively:**
$$u = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E} = \frac{(P/A)^{2}}{2E} = \frac{(200 \times 10^{6})^{2}}{2 \times 200 \times 10^{9}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{16}}{4 \times 10^{11}} = 1.0 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/m}^{3} ‚úď$$

**(iii) Proof Resilience (2 marks):**
$$U_{p} = \frac{\sigma_{y}^{2}}{2E} \cdot V = u_{r} \cdot V$$

$$U_{p} = 1.0 \times 10^{5} \times 9 \times 10^{-4} = 90 \text{ J}$$

Interesting observation: Since the applied stress (200 MPa) is below the yield (250 MPa), the bar's stored energy (90 J) is *less* than the proof resilience. To achieve full proof resilience, the load would need to be $P = \sigma_{y} \cdot A = 250 \times 10^{6} \times 600 \times 10^{-6} = 150$ kN.

---

### Part (b) Solution:

**Given:**
- Segment 1: L‚āĀ = 0.4 m, A‚āĀ = 500 mm¬≤ = 500 √ó 10‚ĀĽ‚Ā∂ m¬≤
- Segment 2: L‚āā = 0.6 m, A‚āā = 300 mm¬≤ = 300 √ó 10‚ĀĽ‚Ā∂ m¬≤
- P = 90 kN = 90 √ó 10¬≥ N
- E = 200 √ó 10‚ĀĻ N/m¬≤ (assumed standard for steel)

**Step 1: Total Strain Energy (4 marks):**
For a stepped bar:
$$U = \frac{P^{2}}{2E} \left[ \frac{L_{1}}{A_{1}} + \frac{L_{2}}{A_{2}} \right]$$

$$U = \frac{(90 \times 10^{3})^{2}}{2 \times 200 \times 10^{9}} \left[ \frac{0.4}{500 \times 10^{-6}} + \frac{0.6}{300 \times 10^{-6}} \right]$$

$$U = \frac{8.1 \times 10^{9}}{4 \times 10^{11}} \left[ 800 + 2000 \right]$$

$$U = 0.02025 \times 2800 = 56.7 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2: Static Stress in Each Segment (1 mark):**
$$\sigma_{1} = \frac{P}{A_{1}} = \frac{90 \times 10^{3}}{500 \times 10^{-6}} = 180 \text{ MPa}$$
$$\sigma_{2} = \frac{P}{A_{2}} = \frac{90 \times 10^{3}}{300 \times 10^{-6}} = 300 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Sudden Load ‚ÄĒ Maximum Stress (2 marks):**
For sudden loading, the maximum stress is **double** the static stress in each segment:
$$\sigma_{1, max} = 2 \times 180 = 360 \text{ MPa}$$
$$\sigma_{2, max} = 2 \times 300 = 600 \text{ MPa}$$

**Critical location:** Segment 2 (smaller cross-section) governs with maximum stress = **600 MPa**. If this exceeds the material's yield stress, the bar fails.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper, Module 1]`**

**(a)** A vertical steel bar of length **2.0 m**, cross-sectional area **500 mm¬≤**, and **E = 200 GPa** is fixed at the lower end. A weight of **20 kN** falls on a rigid collar at the free end from a height of **80 mm**. Determine:
   (i) The static elongation due to the 20 kN load. (2 marks)
   (ii) The maximum stress induced by the impact. (3 marks)
   (iii) The maximum elongation. (2 marks)

**(b)** Derive the expression for the strain energy stored in a uniform bar subjected to an axial load, starting from the definition $U = \int P \, d\delta$. Hence, show that the strain energy density is given by $u = \dfrac{\sigma^{2}}{2E}$. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

### Part (a) Solution:

**Given:**
- L = 2.0 m, A = 500 mm¬≤ = 500 √ó 10‚ĀĽ‚Ā∂ m¬≤
- E = 200 √ó 10‚ĀĻ N/m¬≤
- W = 20 kN = 20 √ó 10¬≥ N
- h = 80 mm = 0.08 m

**(i) Static Elongation (2 marks):**
$$\delta_{st} = \frac{WL}{AE} = \frac{20 \times 10^{3} \times 2.0}{500 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^{9}}$$

$$\delta_{st} = \frac{4 \times 10^{4}}{1 \times 10^{8}} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.4 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Maximum Stress by Impact (3 marks):**
Static stress:
$$\sigma_{st} = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{20 \times 10^{3}}{500 \times 10^{-6}} = 40 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 40 \text{ MPa}$$

Impact stress formula:
$$\sigma_{max} = \sigma_{st} \cdot \left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right)$$

Compute the term:
$$\frac{2h}{\delta_{st}} = \frac{2 \times 0.08}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.16}{0.0004} = 400$$

$$\sigma_{max} = 40 \times \left(1 + \sqrt{1 + 400}\right) = 40 \times (1 + \sqrt{401})$$

$$\sqrt{401} \approx 20.025$$

$$\sigma_{max} = 40 \times (1 + 20.025) = 40 \times 21.025 = 841 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) Maximum Elongation (2 marks):**
$$\delta_{max} = \delta_{st} \cdot \left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right) = 4 \times 10^{-4} \times 21.025$$

$$\delta_{max} = 8.41 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 8.41 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚ÄĒ Common Pitfalls in Impact Load Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** ‚ÄĒ h in mm and őī_st in mm is acceptable, but mixing mm and m will lose 2 marks. Always use **SI units (m, N, Pa)** consistently.
> 2. **Sign error in square root** ‚ÄĒ students sometimes take the negative root, giving a negative elongation. Always take the **positive** root.
> 3. **Missing the "1 +" inside the bracket** ‚ÄĒ the formula is $(1 + \sqrt{1 + 2h/\delta_{st}})$, not just $\sqrt{1 + 2h/\delta_{st}}$. This single error halves the answer and loses 3 marks.
> 4. **Assuming strain energy equals static energy** ‚ÄĒ for impact loads, the strain energy at maximum elongation is $U_{max} = W(h + \delta_{max})$, not $\frac{W^2 L}{2AE}$. Use the energy balance equation directly.

---

### Part (b) Solution:

**Derivation (7 marks ‚ÄĒ Allocate ~3.5 marks for derivation, ~2 marks for the density derivation, ~1.5 marks for the final boxed result and units):**

**Step 1: Setup and definition.**
Consider a uniform bar of length **L**, cross-sectional area **A**, fixed at one end and subjected to an axial tensile load **P** at the free end. The bar extends by an amount **őī** proportional to P (Hooke's Law, linear elastic regime). The strain energy stored is the work done by the external force as the bar deforms gradually:

$$U = \int_{0}^{\delta} P \, d\delta$$

**Step 2: Apply Hooke's Law for linear elastic bar.**
The relationship between load and elongation is:
$$\delta = \frac{PL}{AE} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P = \frac{AE}{L} \cdot \delta$$

**Step 3: Substitute and integrate.**
$$U = \int_{0}^{\delta} \frac{AE}{L} \cdot \delta \, d\delta = \frac{AE}{L} \cdot \frac{\delta^{2}}{2}$$

$$U = \frac{AE \delta^{2}}{2L}$$

**Step 4: Eliminate őī to get the energy in terms of P, L, A, E.**
Substituting $\delta = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$:

$$U = \frac{AE}{2L} \cdot \left(\frac{PL}{AE}\right)^{2} = \frac{AE}{2L} \cdot \frac{P^{2}L^{2}}{A^{2}E^{2}} = \frac{P^{2} L}{2 A E}$$

**Step 5: Derive the strain energy density.**
The volume of the bar is $V = A \cdot L$. The strain energy density is energy per unit volume:

$$u = \frac{U}{V} = \frac{P^{2} L}{2 A E} \cdot \frac{1}{A L} = \frac{P^{2}}{2 A^{2} E} = \frac{1}{2E} \cdot \left(\frac{P}{A}\right)^{2} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E}$$

**Step 6: Alternative derivation using ŌÉ and őĶ.**
By Hooke's Law: $\sigma = E \varepsilon$, so:
$$u = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E} = \frac{\sigma \cdot (E\varepsilon)}{2E} = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{U = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E} \cdot V = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon \cdot V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Missing the factor ¬Ĺ** ‚ÄĒ students often write $U = P \delta$ instead of $U = \frac{1}{2} P \delta$. This is a 1-mark deduction in Part (a) and a 2-mark deduction in derivations. The **¬Ĺ** comes from the gradual (incremental) application of load.
> 2. **Confusing strain energy with complementary energy** ‚ÄĒ for linearly elastic systems, both are equal, but for non-linear systems they are not. KTU Module 1 typically restricts to linear cases.
> 3. **Wrong sign convention** ‚ÄĒ compressive loads *also* store strain energy; the formula $U = \frac{P^2L}{2AE}$ is valid for both tension and compression because $P^2$ is always positive. Do not insert a negative sign.
> 4. **In Stepped bar problems**, students often forget to sum the energies segment-wise. Always write the integral form first: $U = \frac{P^2}{2E} \sum L_i / A_i$, then substitute.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **HIGH-DENSITY BULLET REVIEW ‚ÄĒ Read 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

### **Core Formulas (Memorize These)**

- [ ] **Uniform bar:** $U = \dfrac{P^{2} L}{2 A E} = \dfrac{1}{2} P \delta = \dfrac{\sigma^{2}}{2E} \cdot V$
- [ ] **Strain energy density:** $u = \dfrac{\sigma^{2}}{2E} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon$
- [ ] **Varying cross-section:** $U = \dfrac{P^{2}}{2E} \int_{0}^{L} \dfrac{dx}{A(x)}$
- [ ] **Stepped bar:** $U = \dfrac{P^{2}}{2E} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_{i}}{A_{i}}$
- [ ] **Self-weight bar:** $U = \dfrac{W^{2} L}{6 A E}$ (where W = total weight)

### **Three Load Types ‚ÄĒ Stress Multipliers**

- [ ] **Gradual:** ŌÉ = P/A (baseline)
- [ ] **Sudden:** ŌÉ = **2P/A** (DOUBLED)
- [ ] **Impact from height h:** $\sigma = \sigma_{st} \left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right)$

### **Resilience Formulas**

- [ ] **Proof Resilience** (energy to yield): $U_{p} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}^{2}}{2E} \cdot V$
- [ ] **Modulus of Resilience:** $u_{r} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}^{2}}{2E}$

### **Key Physical Constants & Materials**

- [ ] **E (Steel) = 200 GPa**, **E (Aluminum) = 70 GPa**, **E (Concrete) = 30 GPa**
- [ ] **g = 9.81 m/s¬≤** (for self-weight problems)
- [ ] **1 GPa = 10‚ĀĻ N/m¬≤ = 10‚ĀĻ Pa**, **1 MPa = 10‚Ā∂ Pa = 1 N/mm¬≤**

### **Conceptual Watch-Points**

- [ ] Strain energy is **always positive** (since $P^2$, $\sigma^2$ are positive) ‚ÄĒ applies to both tension and compression.
- [ ] The factor **¬Ĺ** is non-negotiable for *gradual* loading; it represents the area of the **triangle** under the P‚Äďőī curve.
- [ ] For *sudden* loading, stress doubles but energy goes **up by 4√ó** at peak oscillation ($U = 4 \times U_{static}$). However, the *final equilibrium* energy is the same as gradual loading ‚ÄĒ the excess energy is dissipated through oscillation/damping.
- [ ] The *Castigliano's theorem* in axial form: $\delta = \partial U / \partial P$ ‚ÄĒ useful for finding deflections in statically determinate systems.
- [ ] **Energy = Complementary Energy** for linearly elastic systems, but this is NOT true in general. KTU 2024 Module 1 only requires linear elastic analysis.

### **Common Mistake Categories to Avoid**

1. **Unit errors** ‚ÄĒ never mix mm with m in formula evaluation.
2. **Missing the ¬Ĺ factor** in strain energy.
3. **Forgetting to sum** for stepped/tapered bars.
4. **Misapplying** the impact formula ‚ÄĒ verify the sign of the square root.
5. **Confusing** proof resilience (volume-integrated) with modulus of resilience (per unit volume).

### **Engineering Relevance (For 1-mark context questions)**

> Strain energy in axial bars underpins:
> - **Pile driving** in foundation engineering.
> - **Pre-stressed concrete** design.
> - **Earthquake-resistant** structural design.
> - **Crane and cable** design.
> - **Shock absorber** and **buffer** design.

---

**END OF MODULE 1 NOTES ‚ÄĒ STRAIN ENERGY IN AXIALLY LOADED BARS**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strain Energy Due to Shear Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Strain Energy Due to Shear Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Strain Energy Due to Shear Stress** is the internal potential energy stored within a deformable body when it is subjected to **shear loading**. Mathematically, it represents the work done by the gradually applied tangential (shear) forces in producing the corresponding angular deformation (shear strain), assuming the loading is **static** and the material obeys **Hooke's Law** in shear.

When a body experiences a shear stress $\tau$ across a plane, the complementary faces undergo a relative sliding displacement, producing an **angular distortion** $\phi$ (or $\gamma$). The work done by the shear force during this gradual, elastic deformation is irreversibly stored as **elastic strain energy**, and is fully recoverable upon unloading (provided the **elastic limit**, typically **G = 80 GPa for structural steel**, is not exceeded).

## 1.2 Key Terminology Map

| Term | Symbol | SI Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shear Stress | $\tau$ | $\text{N/m}^2$ (Pa) | Tangential force per unit area |
| Shear Strain | $\phi$ or $\gamma$ | radian (dimensionless) | Angular deformation in radians |
| Modulus of Rigidity | $G$ | $\text{N/m}^2$ (Pa) | Shear stiffness of the material |
| Volume of Element | $V$ | $\text{m}^3$ | Total volume over which strain energy is integrated |
| Strain Energy | $U$ | $\text{N¬∑m}$ (Joules) | Total elastic energy stored in the body |
| Strain Energy Density | $u$ | $\text{N¬∑m/m}^3$ (J/m¬≥) | Strain energy per unit volume |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Sliding Book Stack" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Stack of Books on a Sliding Tray**
>
> Imagine a thick stack of paperback books resting on a smooth table. If you push the top book **horizontally** with a slow, steady hand, the top book slides over the one below it. Each page experiences a small **tangential force** over its top surface (a shear force) and a small **sliding displacement** relative to the page below (a shear strain).
>
> Your hand is doing **work** against the friction-like inter-page resistance ‚ÄĒ that work is not lost as heat (ideally, in elastic conditions); it is **stored as elastic strain energy** in the slight bending and untidiness of the paper fibers at every interface.
>
> - The harder you push (greater $\tau$), the more energy is stored ‚ÄĒ but the relationship is **quadratic**, not linear. Double the shear stress ‚Üí **four times** the strain energy.
> - The stiffer the material (greater $G$), the LESS energy is stored for the same stress ‚ÄĒ because the body distorts less.
> - This is precisely why a **stiff shear connection** (high $G$) is "brittle" in energy terms, while a **compliant one** (low $G$) absorbs more impact energy.

This concept governs the design of every **riveted joint**, **bolted connection**, **shear connector** in composite beams, **punching operation**, and **torsional shaft** in mechanical and civil engineering practice.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Setup of the Shear Element

Consider a small elemental cube of side length $dx$, $dy$, $dz$ carved out of a deformable body. Let us apply a pure **shear stress** $\tau$ on the top face in the $x$-direction. By complementary shear, an equal shear stress $\tau$ also acts on the right face in the $y$-direction, on the bottom face in the $-x$-direction, and on the left face in the $-y$-direction.

The top face displaces by a small amount $\delta$ relative to the bottom face. The corresponding **shear strain** is:

$$\phi = \frac{\delta}{dx}$$

This shear strain is a small angle (in radians) and is the fundamental kinematic variable in shear deformation.

## 2.2 Operative Logic Flow (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

The derivation follows this logical chain, which KTU examiners frequently test:

1. **Kinematics (Geometry of Deformation):** A shear stress $\tau$ produces a shear strain $\phi$ such that the top face slides by $\delta = \phi \cdot dx$ relative to the bottom face.

2. **Equilibrium (Internal Force):** The shear stress $\tau$ acting on a face of area $A$ produces a shear force $F = \tau \cdot A$. On a differential area $dA$, the differential force is $dF = \tau \cdot dA$.

3. **Constitutive Law (Material Behaviour):** Within the elastic limit, the material obeys **Hooke's Law in shear**:
$$\tau = G \cdot \phi$$
where $G$ is the **Modulus of Rigidity** (or Shear Modulus).

4. **Work-Energy Equivalence:** The work done by a gradually applied force $F$ producing a displacement $\delta$ in a linearly elastic system is:
$$W = \frac{1}{2} \cdot F \cdot \delta$$
The factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ arises because the force builds up linearly from zero to $F$ as the displacement increases from zero to $\delta$.

5. **Strain Energy Density:** Substituting the shear force and shear displacement into the work expression and dividing by volume gives the strain energy stored **per unit volume**:
$$u = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \tau \cdot \phi$$

6. **Total Strain Energy:** Integrate (or simply multiply) the density by the total volume $V$ of the stressed region:
$$U = u \cdot V = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \tau \cdot \phi \cdot V$$

7. **Elimination of $\phi$ using Hooke's Law:** Since $\phi = \tau / G$, we can rewrite the total strain energy in three equivalent forms.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight ‚ÄĒ Why the factor ¬Ĺ?**
> In static, linearly elastic loading, the shear stress does **not** jump instantly to its final value. It builds up **gradually from zero** to $\tau$ as the deformation $\phi$ grows. The work done is the area under the **triangular** load‚Äďdeformation diagram, which is exactly $\frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = \frac{1}{2} \tau \phi$ per unit volume. Forgetting this $\frac{1}{2}$ is a classic KTU board-exam trap and costs full marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is the energy proportional to $G$, not $1/G$, in the strain-form version?**
> When we express strain energy in terms of *strain* instead of *stress*, we get $U = \frac{1}{2} G \phi^2 V$. A **stiffer** material (larger $G$) stores **more** energy at the same strain because the internal restoring forces are larger. Conversely, when expressed in terms of *stress* ($U = \frac{\tau^2}{2G} V$), a stiffer material stores **less** energy at the same applied stress because it deforms less.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Three Master Formulas (Memorize for the Board Exam)**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi = \dfrac{\tau}{G}$ | Hooke's Law in Shear | Converting stress to strain |
| 2 | $u = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\tau\,\phi$ | Strain Energy Density (J/m¬≥) | Energy per unit volume |
| 3 | $U = \dfrac{\tau^2}{2G}\,V$ | Total Strain Energy in terms of stress (J) | Stress is uniform/known |
| 4 | $U = \dfrac{1}{2}\,G\,\phi^2\,V$ | Total Strain Energy in terms of strain (J) | Strain is uniform/known |
| 5 | $U = \dfrac{1}{2}\,V\,\tau\,\phi$ | General Total Strain Energy (J) | Mixed (stress & strain both given) |
| 6 | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Relation to Young's Modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$ | Converting between elastic constants |
| 7 | $P_s = \dfrac{U}{V}$ | Proof Resilience (Pa) | Maximum energy/unit volume up to elastic limit |

### Numerical Reference Values (Memorize These Standard Values)

| Material | $E$ (GPa) | $G$ (GPa) | Poisson's Ratio $\nu$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Structural Steel | **200** | **80** | **0.30** |
| Concrete | **30** | **12** | **0.20** |
| Aluminium Alloy | **70** | **26** | **0.33** |
| Timber (along grain) | **12** | **0.6** | **0.30** |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Riveted/Bolted Steel Connections:** When a rivet is loaded in single or double shear, the energy absorbed in the shear planes governs the **ductility** and **impact resistance** of the connection. KTU syllabus explicitly covers rivet shear and bolt shear in subsequent modules.
- **Punching & Shearing Operations:** The work required to punch a hole through a metal plate is the strain energy integrated over the cylindrical shear surface ‚ÄĒ this is the basis of **metal forming** and **sheet-metal cutting** calculations.
- **Torsion of Circular Shafts:** The total strain energy of a shaft under torque is the integrated form of the shear strain energy density. The result, $U = \dfrac{T^2 L}{2 G J}$, is a **direct extension** of the formula derived here.
- **Earthquake Engineering:** The **hysteretic strain energy** dissipated in shear panels and braces is computed using the same $\tau^2 / 2G$ density concept.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation ‚ÄĒ From Elemental Cube to Final Formula

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation is the **golden derivation** for the KTU 2024 Scheme exam. Master every line. The board examiner will accept equivalent reasoning, but the **final boxed formula** is mandatory.

### **Setup**

Consider a small rectangular element (a parallelepiped) of dimensions $dx \times dy \times dz$ extracted from a body subjected to a uniform shear stress $\tau$ on its top face (and complementary shear on the other faces).

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Shear Force on the Top Face**

The area of the top face is $A = dy \cdot dz$.

The shear force on this face is:
$$F = \tau \cdot A = \tau \cdot (dy \cdot dz)$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Shear Displacement of the Top Face**

The top face displaces by a small amount $\delta$ relative to the bottom (fixed) face, in the direction of the shear force. The shear strain is the angle $\phi$ (in radians) such that:
$$\phi = \frac{\delta}{dx} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \delta = \phi \cdot dx$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Work Done by the Gradually Applied Shear Force**

Since the load is applied gradually and the material is linearly elastic, the force grows from $0$ to $F$ as the displacement grows from $0$ to $\delta$. The work done (area of the triangular load‚Äďdisplacement diagram) is:
$$dW = \frac{1}{2} \cdot F \cdot \delta$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Substitute $F$ and $\delta$**

$$dW = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left[\tau \cdot (dy \cdot dz)\right] \cdot \left[\phi \cdot dx\right]$$

Rearranging the differentials:
$$dW = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \tau \cdot \phi \cdot (dx \cdot dy \cdot dz)$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Recognise the Differential Volume**

The product $dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$ is the differential volume $dV$ of the element. Therefore:
$$dW = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \tau \cdot \phi \cdot dV$$

By the work‚Äďenergy principle, this work is stored as strain energy in the element:
$$dU = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \tau \cdot \phi \cdot dV$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Integrate Over the Whole Volume**

$$U = \int_V \frac{1}{2} \cdot \tau \cdot \phi \cdot dV$$

If the shear stress $\tau$ and shear strain $\phi$ are **uniform** throughout the volume $V$, the integrand is constant and can be taken outside the integral:
$$U = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \tau \cdot \phi \cdot V$$

> This is the **General Form** of the strain energy due to pure shear. ‚≠ź

**Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Apply Hooke's Law in Shear**

Within the elastic limit:
$$\phi = \frac{\tau}{G}$$

Substitute this into the General Form:
$$U = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \tau \cdot \left(\frac{\tau}{G}\right) \cdot V$$

**Step 8 ‚ÄĒ Simplify the Final Result (Stress Form)**

$$\boxed{\,U = \frac{\tau^2}{2G} \cdot V\,}$$

This is the **canonical formula** for strain energy due to uniform shear stress.

**Step 9 ‚ÄĒ Alternative Form (Strain Form)**

We can also eliminate $\tau$ instead of $\phi$ using $\tau = G \phi$:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (G \phi) \cdot \phi \cdot V$$

$$\boxed{\,U = \frac{G \phi^2}{2} \cdot V\,}$$

**Step 10 ‚ÄĒ Strain Energy Density (Energy per Unit Volume)**

Dividing the total strain energy by volume:

$$u = \frac{U}{V} = \frac{\tau^2}{2G} = \frac{1}{2}\,G\,\phi^2 = \frac{1}{2}\,\tau\,\phi$$

$$\boxed{\,u = \frac{\tau^2}{2G}\, \quad \left[\frac{\text{J}}{\text{m}^3}\right]\,}$$

## 3.2 The Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Three Equivalent Forms at a Glance

| Form | Equation | Most Useful When |
|---|---|---|
| **Mixed Form** | $U = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\tau\,\phi\,V$ | Both $\tau$ and $\phi$ are given directly |
| **Stress Form** | $U = \dfrac{\tau^2}{2G}\,V$ | Only applied shear stress is known |
| **Strain Form** | $U = \dfrac{G\,\phi^2}{2}\,V$ | Only measured shear strain is known |
| **Density Form** | $u = \dfrac{\tau^2}{2G}$ | Computing energy per unit volume (proof resilience) |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Problem:** A solid steel cube of side $a = 50\,\text{mm}$ is fixed on its bottom face and subjected to a tangential force $F = 50\,\text{kN}$ on its top face. Find the strain energy stored in the cube. Take $G = 80\,\text{GPa}$ for steel.

**Solution:**

Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Shear stress on the top face:
$$\tau = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{F}{a^2} = \frac{50 \times 10^3\,\text{N}}{(0.050\,\text{m})^2} = \frac{50{,}000}{0.0025} = 20 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa} = 20\,\text{MPa}$$

Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Volume of the cube:
$$V = a^3 = (0.050\,\text{m})^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3$$

Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Apply the canonical formula:
$$U = \frac{\tau^2}{2G}\,V = \frac{(20 \times 10^6)^2}{2 \times 80 \times 10^9} \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Evaluate the numerator and denominator separately:
$$\tau^2 = (20 \times 10^6)^2 = 400 \times 10^{12}\,\text{Pa}^2 = 4 \times 10^{14}\,\text{Pa}^2$$

$$2G = 2 \times 80 \times 10^9 = 160 \times 10^9\,\text{Pa} = 1.6 \times 10^{11}\,\text{Pa}$$

Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Combine and compute:
$$U = \frac{4 \times 10^{14}}{1.6 \times 10^{11}} \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$U = 2500 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} = 0.3125\,\text{J}$$

$$\boxed{\,U = 0.3125\,\text{J}\,}$$

**Cross-verification using strain form:**

Shear strain:
$$\phi = \frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{20 \times 10^6}{80 \times 10^9} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{rad}$$

Strain energy:
$$U = \frac{1}{2}\,G\,\phi^2\,V = \frac{1}{2} \times 80 \times 10^9 \times (2.5 \times 10^{-4})^2 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \times 80 \times 10^9 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$U = 0.5 \times 80 \times 6.25 \times 1.25 \times 10^{9-8-4} = 0.5 \times 80 \times 6.25 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$U = 0.3125\,\text{J} \quad \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart ‚ÄĒ Energy Storage Pathway Under Shear

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Shear Force F applied gradually] --> B[Shear Stress tau = F by A on cross-section]
    B --> C[Material deforms - Shear Strain phi develops]
    C --> D[Hookes Law in Shear: tau = G x phi]
    D --> E[Strain Energy Density: u = 0.5 x tau x phi]
    E --> F{Uniform Stress in Volume V?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Total Strain Energy U = 0.5 x tau x phi x V]
    F -- No --> H[Integrate: U = integral of 0.5 tau phi dV over V]
    G --> I[Stress Form: U = tau squared by 2G times V]
    H --> I
    G --> J[Strain Form: U = G phi squared by 2 times V]
    H --> J
    I --> K[Strain Energy stored elastically in the body]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Recoverable on unloading if elastic limit not exceeded]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Physical Interpretation of the Elemental Cube

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Element[Small Elemental Cube under Pure Shear]
        E1[Bottom face: fixed in space] 
        E2[Top face: displaces by delta in x-direction]
        E1 --- E2
        E3[Side face: complementary shear stress tau acts]
        E2 --- E3
    end
    Element --> O1[Shear Stress tau = F by A]
    Element --> O2[Shear Strain phi = delta by dx = tau by G]
    Element --> O3[Strain Energy Density u = 0.5 tau phi = tau squared by 2G]
    O3 --> O4[Total U = u times V = tau squared V by 2G]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram ‚ÄĒ From Load Application to Energy Release

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Undeformed
    Undeformed --> Loading: Apply shear force F gradually
    Loading --> Strained: Shear strain phi develops, energy being stored
    Strained --> PeakLoaded: Force reaches F, strain reaches phi_max
    PeakLoaded --> Unloading: Remove force gradually
    Unloading --> Recovered: Strain energy U released as recoverable work
    Recovered --> Undeformed: Body returns to original shape
    Undeformed --> [*]
    note right of Strained: Energy stored = 0.5 x tau x phi x V
    note right of Recovered: Energy returned = same U (elastic)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1, Q1.a]**
>
> **Q1.** Define **strain energy due to shear stress**. State the formula for the same.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Strain energy due to shear stress is the **internal elastic potential energy** stored in a deformable body when it is subjected to a **shear stress** $\tau$, producing an **angular deformation** called **shear strain** $\phi$, under static, linearly elastic conditions.

The formula is:
$$U = \frac{\tau^2}{2G} \cdot V = \frac{1}{2} \cdot G \cdot \phi^2 \cdot V = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \tau \cdot \phi \cdot V$$

where $G$ is the **Modulus of Rigidity** and $V$ is the volume of the stressed region. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the meaning of symbols.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2, Q1.b]**
>
> **Q2.** Derive the expression for **strain energy density** in a body subjected to pure shear.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Consider a small element of volume $dV$ subjected to a shear stress $\tau$ that produces a shear strain $\phi$. The shear force is $dF = \tau \, dA$ and the relative displacement is $d\delta = \phi \cdot dx$, where $dx$ is the thickness of the element.

The differential work done by the gradually applied force is:
$$dW = \frac{1}{2} \cdot dF \cdot d\delta = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \tau \cdot \phi \cdot dV$$

Strain energy density is the energy per unit volume:
$$u = \frac{dU}{dV} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \tau \cdot \phi$$

Using Hooke's Law in shear ($\phi = \tau/G$):
$$u = \frac{\tau^2}{2G} \quad \text{J/m}^3$$

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Q2]**
>
> ### **Question A ‚ÄĒ 14 Marks**
>
> **(a)** A mild steel block of dimensions $60\,\text{mm} \times 60\,\text{mm} \times 40\,\text{mm}$ is fixed on its bottom face ($60 \times 40\,\text{mm}^2$). A tangential force of $F = 90\,\text{kN}$ is applied uniformly on the top face. Take $G = 80\,\text{GPa}$. Calculate:
>   1. The shear stress induced in the block. **(2 marks)**
>   2. The shear strain developed. **(2 marks)**
>   3. The total strain energy stored in the block. **(3 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A circular shaft of diameter $d = 50\,\text{mm}$ and length $L = 1.5\,\text{m}$ is subjected to a torque $T = 1.2\,\text{kN \cdot m}$. The maximum permissible shear stress is $\tau_{\max} = 60\,\text{MPa}$. Using the shear strain energy concept, determine the **modulus of rigidity** $G$ of the shaft material if the strain energy stored is $U = 18\,\text{J}$. Verify your result using the torsion formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Block dimensions: $60\,\text{mm} \times 60\,\text{mm} \times 40\,\text{mm}$
- Fixed face area: $A = 60\,\text{mm} \times 40\,\text{mm} = 2400\,\text{mm}^2 = 2400 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2$
- Tangential force: $F = 90\,\text{kN} = 90{,}000\,\text{N}$
- Modulus of rigidity: $G = 80\,\text{GPa} = 80 \times 10^9\,\text{Pa}$
- Height of the block (distance between fixed and loaded face): $dx = 60\,\text{mm} = 0.060\,\text{m}$

**Step 1: Shear stress** $\tau$ **(2 marks)**

The shear force acts on the top face of area $A = 2400 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2$:
$$\tau = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{90{,}000}{2400 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{90{,}000}{0.0024} = 37.5 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 37.5\,\text{MPa}}$$

**Step 2: Shear strain** $\phi$ **(2 marks)**

Applying Hooke's Law in shear:
$$\phi = \frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{37.5 \times 10^6}{80 \times 10^9} = 4.6875 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{rad}$$

$$\boxed{\phi = 4.6875 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{rad}}$$

**Step 3: Total strain energy** $U$ **(3 marks)**

Volume of the block:
$$V = 0.060 \times 0.060 \times 0.040 = 1.44 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3$$

Strain energy stored:
$$U = \frac{\tau^2}{2G} \cdot V = \frac{(37.5 \times 10^6)^2}{2 \times 80 \times 10^9} \times 1.44 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\tau^2 = (37.5 \times 10^6)^2 = 1406.25 \times 10^{12} = 1.40625 \times 10^{15}\,\text{Pa}^2$$

$$2G = 1.6 \times 10^{11}\,\text{Pa}$$

$$U = \frac{1.40625 \times 10^{15}}{1.6 \times 10^{11}} \times 1.44 \times 10^{-4} = 8790.625 \times 1.44 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$U = 1.26585 \times 10^{0} \approx 1.266\,\text{J}$$

$$\boxed{U \approx 1.266\,\text{J}}$$

### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks

**Given:** $d = 50\,\text{mm} = 0.050\,\text{m}$, $L = 1.5\,\text{m}$, $T = 1.2\,\text{kN \cdot m} = 1200\,\text{N \cdot m}$, $\tau_{\max} = 60\,\text{MPa}$, $U = 18\,\text{J}$.

**Recall the torsion strain energy formula** (extension of shear strain energy to shafts):
$$U = \frac{T^2 L}{2 G J}$$

where the polar moment of inertia for a solid circular shaft is:
$$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.050)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{32} = 6.1359 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^4$$

**Solving for G:**
$$G = \frac{T^2 L}{2 U J} = \frac{(1200)^2 \times 1.5}{2 \times 18 \times 6.1359 \times 10^{-7}}$$

Numerator:
$$T^2 \cdot L = 1.44 \times 10^6 \times 1.5 = 2.16 \times 10^6$$

Denominator:
$$2 \cdot U \cdot J = 2 \times 18 \times 6.1359 \times 10^{-7} = 2.2089 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$G = \frac{2.16 \times 10^6}{2.2089 \times 10^{-5}} = 9.78 \times 10^{10}\,\text{Pa} \approx 97.8\,\text{GPa}$$

**Verification using the torsion formula:**

Maximum shear stress in a circular shaft:
$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{J} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$$

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{16 \times 1200}{\pi \times (0.050)^3} = \frac{19{,}200}{\pi \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{19{,}200}{3.927 \times 10^{-4}} = 48.9 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa} \approx 48.9\,\text{MPa}$$

This is less than the permissible $60\,\text{MPa}$, so the shaft is **safe**. The computed $G \approx 97.8\,\text{GPa}$ is consistent with a high-strength steel alloy.

$$\boxed{G \approx 97.8\,\text{GPa}, \quad \tau_{\max} \approx 48.9\,\text{MPa} \leq 60\,\text{MPa} \quad \text{(Safe)}}$$

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper, Q2 Alt]**
>
> ### **Question B ‚ÄĒ 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
>
> **(a)** Two rivets, each of diameter $d = 20\,\text{mm}$, are used in **double shear** to connect three steel plates. Each plate has thickness $t = 12\,\text{mm}$. A tensile load of $P = 120\,\text{kN}$ is transmitted through the joint. Using the shear strain energy concept, calculate the **strain energy stored in both rivets** if the modulus of rigidity of the rivet material is $G = 80\,\text{GPa}$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive, in detail, the **three equivalent forms** of the strain energy expression in pure shear. Explain when each form is most conveniently used in engineering practice. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Rivet diameter: $d = 20\,\text{mm} = 0.020\,\text{m}$
- Number of rivets: $n = 2$
- Type of shear: double shear ‚Üí each rivet has **2 shear planes**
- Total number of shear planes: $n_s = 2 \times 2 = 4$
- Plate thickness (height over which shear acts): $t = 12\,\text{mm} = 0.012\,\text{m}$
- Tensile load: $P = 120\,\text{kN} = 120{,}000\,\text{N}$
- Modulus of rigidity: $G = 80\,\text{GPa} = 80 \times 10^9\,\text{Pa}$

**Step 1: Shear force on each rivet plane**

Assuming the load is equally shared among the 4 shear planes:
$$F_{\text{plane}} = \frac{P}{n_s} = \frac{120{,}000}{4} = 30{,}000\,\text{N}$$

**Step 2: Shear stress on each rivet plane**

Cross-sectional area of one rivet:
$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.020)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{4} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2$$

Shear stress:
$$\tau = \frac{F_{\text{plane}}}{A} = \frac{30{,}000}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 95.49 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa} \approx 95.49\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 3: Volume of one shear plane (cylindrical shear surface)**

The shear acts over a cylindrical surface of diameter $d$ and height $t$ (the plate thickness):
$$V_{\text{plane}} = A \cdot t = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.012 = 3.770 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^3$$

**Step 4: Strain energy in one shear plane**

$$U_{\text{plane}} = \frac{\tau^2}{2G} \cdot V_{\text{plane}} = \frac{(95.49 \times 10^6)^2}{2 \times 80 \times 10^9} \times 3.770 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\tau^2 = (95.49 \times 10^6)^2 = 9118.3 \times 10^{12} = 9.1183 \times 10^{15}\,\text{Pa}^2$$

$$2G = 1.6 \times 10^{11}\,\text{Pa}$$

$$U_{\text{plane}} = \frac{9.1183 \times 10^{15}}{1.6 \times 10^{11}} \times 3.770 \times 10^{-6} = 56{,}989 \times 3.770 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$U_{\text{plane}} \approx 0.2149\,\text{J}$$

**Step 5: Total strain energy in 4 shear planes**

$$U_{\text{total}} = n_s \times U_{\text{plane}} = 4 \times 0.2149$$

$$\boxed{U_{\text{total}} \approx 0.860\,\text{J}}$$

### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks

The **three equivalent forms** of strain energy in pure shear are:

**Form 1 ‚ÄĒ Mixed Form:** $U = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\tau\,\phi\,V$

This is the most general expression. It is most useful when **both** the shear stress $\tau$ and the shear strain $\phi$ are directly known (e.g., from direct measurement using strain gauges). It does not require knowledge of the material property $G$.

**Form 2 ‚ÄĒ Stress Form:** $U = \dfrac{\tau^2}{2G}\,V$

This form is obtained by substituting $\phi = \tau / G$ (Hooke's Law). It is **the most widely used** in design problems because the applied **load** (and hence stress $\tau$) is usually given, while the strain $\phi$ is not directly measured. This is the form to default to in board exams.

**Form 3 ‚ÄĒ Strain Form:** $U = \dfrac{G\,\phi^2}{2}\,V$

This is obtained by substituting $\tau = G\phi$. It is most useful in **experimental mechanics** and **vibration analysis**, where strains are measured (e.g., dynamically) and one wishes to compute the energy stored.

**Engineering Practice:** In typical KTU board exam problems, the load is given, so $\tau$ is calculated first and the **Stress Form** is the recommended choice. The Strain Form is used when a torque-angle curve is given for a shaft. The Mixed Form is rarely the optimal choice in exam settings but is conceptually the most general.

---

## 5.3 ‚ö†ÔłŹ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor ¬Ĺ:** Students frequently write $U = \tau \phi V$ or $U = \frac{\tau^2}{G} V$. Always remember the factor of **¬Ĺ** in strain energy expressions. It arises from the gradual (quasi-static) application of load. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** $G$ must be in **Pa** (not GPa) when $\tau$ is in Pa and $V$ is in $\text{m}^3$. Common error: $G = 80$, $\tau = 37.5$ (mixed GPa and MPa). Always convert to **base SI units** before substituting. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 3. **Using the wrong area for shear in rivets:** In **single shear**, there is **one** shear plane per rivet. In **double shear**, there are **two** shear planes per rivet. Forgetting to multiply by 2 in double shear is a classic 1‚Äď2 mark loss. **(Lose 1‚Äď2 marks)**
>
> 4. **Confusing $E$ and $G$:** Students sometimes write $U = \frac{\tau^2}{2E} V$ ‚ÄĒ this is **wrong**. Shear strain energy uses $G$, not $E$. The relationship is $G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$, so for steel $G \approx 0.4 E$, not equal to $E$. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 5. **Forgetting to integrate non-uniform stress:** If $\tau$ varies with position (e.g., in a tapered bar or non-uniform shaft), the simple formula $U = \frac{\tau^2 V}{2G}$ **does not apply directly**. You must integrate: $U = \int \frac{\tau^2}{2G} dV$. **(Lose 2‚Äď3 marks)**
>
> 6. **Mixing up shear and torsional strain energy:** Shear strain energy is $U = \frac{\tau^2 V}{2G}$. Torsional strain energy for a circular shaft is $U = \frac{T^2 L}{2 G J}$. They are related but **not the same formula**. The shaft formula is derived by integrating the shear formula over the volume. **(Lose 1‚Äď2 marks)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain Energy Due to Shear Stress ‚ÄĒ One-Page Rapid Recap**
>
> - **Definition:** Internal elastic energy stored in a body subjected to shear stress $\tau$, producing shear strain $\phi$. **(Know the exact wording.)**
> - **Hooke's Law in Shear:** $\tau = G \phi$ ‚ÄĒ this is the constitutive backbone of the entire derivation. **(Memorize.)**
> - **Master Formula (Stress Form):** $U = \dfrac{\tau^2}{2G} \cdot V$ ‚ÄĒ **default choice for board problems**. **(Memorize.)**
> - **Strain Form:** $U = \dfrac{G \phi^2}{2} \cdot V$ ‚ÄĒ used when strain is measured. **(Memorize.)**
> - **Mixed Form:** $U = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tau \cdot \phi \cdot V$ ‚ÄĒ the most general form. **(Memorize.)**
> - **Strain Energy Density:** $u = \dfrac{\tau^2}{2G} = \dfrac{1}{2} G \phi^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \tau \phi$ ‚ÄĒ units: $\text{J/m}^3$ or Pa. **(Memorize.)**
> - **Always include the factor of ¬Ĺ:** Strain energy = $\frac{1}{2} \times \text{stress} \times \text{strain} \times \text{volume}$. **(Memorize.)**
> - **Standard value to memorize:** $G_{\text{steel}} = 80\,\text{GPa}$, $G_{\text{concrete}} = 12\,\text{GPa}$. **(Memorize.)**
> - **Relation to Young's Modulus:** $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ ‚ÄĒ use this if $G$ is not directly given. **(Memorize.)**
> - **Units check (always!):** $\tau$ in Pa, $G$ in Pa, $V$ in $\text{m}^3$ $\rightarrow$ $U$ in J. **(Never mix GPa with Pa.)**
> - **Torsion connection:** $U_{\text{torsion}} = \dfrac{T^2 L}{2 G J}$ is the integrated form of the shear strain energy for circular shafts. **(Memorize for Part B.)**
> - **Rivet convention:** Single shear = 1 shear plane/rivet; Double shear = 2 shear planes/rivet. **(Memorize.)**
> - **Stress state:** Pure shear state has **NO normal stress** on the principal planes ‚ÄĒ the principal stresses are $+\tau$ and $-\tau$ at $45¬į$ to the shear plane. **(Remember for compound-stress follow-up questions.)**
> - **Real-world use:** Riveted joints, bolted connections, punching operations, torsion of shafts, shear connectors in composite construction. **(Cite in viva.)**
> - **Validity limit:** Formula valid only within the **elastic limit** of the material. For elastoplastic analysis, replace $G$ with the **secant** or **tangent** modulus as appropriate. **(Higher-order concept.)**
> - **Quadratic dependence on stress:** Doubling $\tau$ quadruples $U$. This is why high-stress regions dominate the total stored energy. **(Engineering insight.)**
> - **Inverse dependence on $G$:** A stiffer material stores LESS energy for a given applied stress (it deforms less). This governs **energy absorption** selection in impact-resistant design. **(Engineering insight.)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Beams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Beams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Beam

A **beam** is a structural member which is primarily designed to carry transverse (lateral) loads acting perpendicular to its longitudinal axis. The load applied to a beam produces **bending stresses** (tensile on one side, compressive on the other) and **shear stresses** along its cross-section. Beams are one of the most fundamental and frequently encountered load-carrying elements in civil engineering structures, forming the skeleton of buildings, bridges, floor systems, and machine frames.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A beam is a structural member of considerable length built to support transverse loading, which causes bending combined with shearing. The transverse loads cause the beam to deform in a manner dependent on the type of support and the nature of loading.

**Geometric Axis Convention (Standard KTU Sign Convention):**

- The longitudinal axis of the beam is designated as the **x-axis** (horizontal).
- The vertical direction (along which transverse loads act) is the **y-axis**.
- The cross-sectional dimensions are referenced along the **z-axis**.

## 1.2 Types of Beams (Based on Support Conditions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why support conditions matter:** The nature of the support determines the **degree of static indeterminacy**, the magnitude of **reactions**, the shape of the **deflection curve**, and the values of **shear force and bending moment** along the span.

The following table summarizes the canonical beam classifications used across the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Beam Type | Description | Number of Reactions | Static Indeterminacy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simply Supported Beam** | Supported by a pin (hinge) at one end and a roller at the other end | 3 (R_A_x, R_A_y, R_B_y) | Determinate (0¬į) |
| **Overhanging Beam** | A simply supported beam that extends beyond one or both supports | 3 (R_A_x, R_A_y, R_B_y) | Determinate (0¬į) |
| **Cantilever Beam** | Fixed at one end, completely free at the other end | 3 (H_A, V_A, M_A) | Determinate (0¬į) |
| **Propped Cantilever** | Cantilever fixed at one end, simply supported (roller) at the other | 4 (H_A, V_A, M_A, R_B) | Indeterminate to the 1st degree |
| **Fixed Beam** | Built-in (clamped) at both ends | 6 (H_A, V_A, M_A, H_B, V_B, M_B) | Indeterminate to the 3rd degree |
| **Continuous Beam** | Has more than two supports along its length | ‚Č• 4 (one per support) | Indeterminate to (n‚ąí3) degrees |

## 1.3 Types of Loading

A beam may be subjected to a wide range of loadings, but in KTU problems, the following three are most common:

1. **Concentrated (Point) Load (P):** A load applied over a negligibly small contact area. Units: **kN** or **N**.
2. **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL, w):** A load spread uniformly along a finite length of the beam. Units: **kN/m** or **N/m**.
3. **Uniformly Varying Load (UVL):** A load that varies linearly from zero at one end to a maximum value at the other end (triangular load). Units: **kN/m**.

## 1.4 Real-World Analogy ‚ÄĒ "The Diving Board"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a **diving board at a swimming pool**. The board is fixed (clamped) into the concrete at one end ‚ÄĒ this is your **fixed support** (cantilever). When a person walks out toward the free end, the board sags downward. The top fibers stretch (tension) and the bottom fibers compress. If you instead imagine a **wooden plank laid across two bricks**, the plank bends when you stand on it. That is a **simply supported beam**. The internal "twisting-off" tendency at the support is the **shear force**, and the "bending-over" tendency at midspan is the **bending moment**. Every beam in real life ‚ÄĒ floors, bridges, machine tool beds, railway sleepers, crane girders ‚ÄĒ behaves exactly like this.

## 1.5 Shear Force and Bending Moment ‚ÄĒ The Two Internal Resultants

When a beam is cut at any cross-section, the internal stresses can be reduced to two resultant internal forces/moments:

- **Shear Force (V or S):** The algebraic sum of the vertical force components on either side of the section. It is the internal force that tends to **slide** one portion of the beam past the adjacent portion.
- **Bending Moment (M or BM):** The algebraic sum of the moments of the force components about the centroid of the section. It is the internal moment that tends to **bend** the beam.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Section Equilibrium Principle:**
> At any cross-section, sum of vertical forces = 0 ‚áí **V** is the internal shear resisting the applied load.
> At any cross-section, sum of moments = 0 ‚áí **M** is the internal bending moment resisting the applied moment.
> Therefore, for every beam, drawing the **Shear Force Diagram (SFD)** and **Bending Moment Diagram (BMD)** is the FIRST step in design.

## 1.6 Standard KTU Sign Convention

The 2024 KTU scheme universally adopts the following sign convention (this is the convention checked in board exams):

| Quantity | Positive Sense | Physical Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Shear Force (V)** | Left part pushed **up** OR Right part pushed **down** | Clockwise rotation of beam element |
| **Bending Moment (M)** | Tension on **bottom** fiber OR Compression on top fiber | Sagging (concave upward) |
| **Sagging Moment** | Hogging ‚áí Top fibers in tension | Negative moment |
| **Hogging Moment** | Sagging ‚áí Bottom fibers in tension | Positive moment |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** A very common mistake students make is reversing the sign convention for shear force. If you use a different convention (e.g., the older "right part pushed up" convention), your SFD will be inverted and you will lose 3-4 marks per problem. **Memorize and stick to the KTU convention above.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Differential Relationships

These three differential equations form the **mathematical backbone** of every SFD/BMD problem in KTU exams. They tie the rate of change of one diagram to the value of the next.

Let:
- $w(x)$ = intensity of distributed load (positive upward, **kN/m**)
- $V(x)$ = shear force at section $x$ (**kN**)
- $M(x)$ = bending moment at section $x$ (**kN¬∑m**)
- $E$ = Young's Modulus (**N/mm¬≤** or **MPa**)
- $I$ = Moment of Inertia of cross-section about the neutral axis (**mm‚Āī** or **m‚Āī**)
- $EI$ = Flexural Rigidity (**N¬∑m¬≤**)

### Relationship 1: Load to Shear

The rate of change of shear force with respect to distance along the beam equals the negative of the load intensity:

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$

> **Interpretation:** Where $w = 0$ (no load region), shear force is **constant** (horizontal line on SFD). Where $w = $ constant (UDL), shear varies **linearly** (sloped line). Where $w = $ linear (UVL), shear varies **parabolically**.

### Relationship 2: Shear to Moment

The rate of change of bending moment with respect to distance along the beam equals the shear force:

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$$

> **Interpretation:** Where $V = 0$, the bending moment is at an **extremum** (local maximum/minimum). This is how we locate points of maximum bending moment.

### Relationship 3: Combined

By direct differentiation, the second derivative of moment equals the negative of the load intensity:

$$\frac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w(x)$$

> **Interpretation (Curve Shape from Loading):**
> - If $w = 0$ (no load): $M$ varies **linearly** (straight line BMD).
> - If $w = $ constant (UDL): $M$ varies as a **parabola** (2nd degree curve).
> - If $w = $ linear (UVL): $M$ varies as a **cubic** (3rd degree curve).

## 2.2 Standard Results for Simply Supported Beams (Quick Reference)

These appear in **>70% of KTU problems** and are a guaranteed 2-mark direct question:

| Loading Type | Max Bending Moment (M_max) | Max Shear (V_max) | Location of M_max |
|---|---|---|---|
| Point Load **P** at center of span L | $M_{max} = \dfrac{PL}{4}$ | $V_{max} = \dfrac{P}{2}$ | At midspan ($x = L/2$) |
| Point Load **P** at distance 'a' from A, 'b' from B (a + b = L) | $M_{max} = \dfrac{Pab}{L}$ | $V_{max} = P\cdot\dfrac{a}{L}$ (left side) | Under the load ($x = a$) |
| UDL **w** over entire span L | $M_{max} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8}$ | $V_{max} = \dfrac{wL}{2}$ | At midspan ($x = L/2$) |
| UVL (zero at one end, $w_0$ at other) over span L | $M_{max} = \dfrac{w_0 L^2}{9\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.0642\,w_0 L^2$ | $V_{max} = \dfrac{w_0 L}{6}$ (at zero end) | At $x = L/\sqrt{3}$ from zero end |
| Couple **M‚āÄ** at midspan | $M_{max} = M_0$ | $V = 0$ (no shear!) | At the section of couple |

## 2.3 Cantilever Beam Standard Results

| Loading Type | Max Bending Moment (M_max) | Max Shear (V_max) |
|---|---|---|
| Point Load **P** at free end | $M_{max} = -PL$ (hogging) | $V_{max} = P$ |
| UDL **w** over entire length L | $M_{max} = -\dfrac{wL^2}{2}$ (hogging) | $V_{max} = wL$ |
| UVL (zero at fixed end, $w_0$ at free end) | $M_{max} = -\dfrac{w_0 L^2}{6}$ (hogging) | $V_{max} = \dfrac{w_0 L}{2}$ |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following table ‚ÄĒ every KTU Part A and Part B question on beams will draw from these.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Key Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Load-Shear relation | $dV/dx = -w$ | kN/m | SFD slope = $-$load |
| 2 | Shear-Moment relation | $dM/dx = V$ | kN | BMD slope = shear |
| 3 | Concavity of BMD | $d^2M/dx^2 = -w$ | ‚ÄĒ | If $w \downarrow$, BMD concave up |
| 4 | Flexure formula | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | Pa, m‚Āī | Fundamental bending stress |
| 5 | Max bending stress | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Pa (or MPa) | $Z = I/y_{max}$ = section modulus |
| 6 | Shear stress | $\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot A\bar{y}}{I \cdot b}$ | Pa | $\tau_{max}$ at neutral axis |
| 7 | Slope equation | $EI\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ | N¬∑m¬≤ | The Euler-Bernoulli beam equation |
| 8 | Deflection (max, simply sup., UDL) | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$ | m | At midspan |
| 9 | Deflection (max, simply sup., central pt. load) | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$ | m | At midspan |
| 10 | Deflection (max, cantilever, UDL) | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{wL^4}{8EI}$ | m | At free end |
| 11 | Deflection (max, cantilever, end load) | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}$ | m | At free end |
| 12 | Slope (max, simply sup., UDL) | $\theta_{max} = \dfrac{wL^3}{24EI}$ | rad | At supports |
| 13 | Point of Contraflexure | $M(x) = 0$ AND sign change of $M$ | ‚ÄĒ | Where curvature reverses |
| 14 | Total UDL on length L | $W = wL$ | kN | Acts at midspan of UDL |
| 15 | Total UVL on length L | $W = \dfrac{1}{2}w_0 L$ | kN | Acts at L/3 from max end |
| 16 | SFD area to BMD | $\Delta M = \int V\,dx$ | kN¬∑m | Change in M = area under SFD |
| 17 | BMD area to deflection | $\theta = \dfrac{1}{EI}\int M\,dx$ | rad | Change in slope = M-area/EI |
| 18 | Macaulay's method step load | Use $\langle x-a \rangle^n$ brackets | ‚ÄĒ | For discontinuous loads |
| 19 | Stiffness (simply supported) | $k = \dfrac{48EI}{L^3}$ | N/m | For central point load |
| 20 | Stiffness (cantilever) | $k = \dfrac{3EI}{L^3}$ | N/m | For end load |

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> 1. **Building Design** ‚ÄĒ Beams in RCC frames are first designed for **maximum bending moment** (to determine steel reinforcement) and then checked for **maximum shear** (to design stirrups).
> 2. **Bridge Girders** ‚ÄĒ Highway and railway bridges are essentially long beams. The SFD/BMD under moving vehicle loads determines the critical loading positions (influence line diagrams).
> 3. **Machine Tool Beds** ‚ÄĒ Deflection of a lathe bed directly affects machining accuracy. The $\delta_{max} = 5wL^4 / 384EI$ formula sets the precision tolerance.
> 4. **Crane Jibs and Booms** ‚ÄĒ Cantilever-type beams under combined bending and self-weight require careful SF/BM analysis for the hoist load.
> 5. **Aerospace Wings** ‚ÄĒ Aircraft wings are cantilever beams; the SF/BM and deflection under gust loads govern their structural design.

## 2.6 Point of Contraflexure (Inflection Point)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A point of contraflexure (also called **inflection point** or **point of zero moment**) is a section of the beam where the bending moment is zero AND the curvature of the deflection curve changes sign.

- **Mathematical condition:** $M(x) = 0$ with sign change on either side.
- **Physical meaning:** At this point, the beam changes from **sagging** (concave up, bottom in tension) to **hogging** (concave down, top in tension) or vice versa.
- **Engineering significance:** Points of contraflexure are **locations of zero flexural stress** but are often the **locations of maximum shear** ‚ÄĒ so they need careful design for shear.

## 2.7 Quick-Reference Theorem List

1. **Area-Shear Theorem:** The change in bending moment between two sections = area of the SFD between those two sections. $\Delta M_{AB} = \int_A^B V\,dx$.
2. **Load-Shear Theorem:** The change in shear force between two sections = area of the load diagram between those two sections. $\Delta V_{AB} = -\int_A^B w\,dx$.
3. **Mohr's Theorems for Beams:** (a) Change in slope between two points = area of M/EI diagram between those points. (b) Deflection between two tangents = moment of M/EI area about the point.
4. **Macaulay's Method:** Uses Macaulay brackets $\langle x-a \rangle^n$ to handle discontinuous point loads/UDLs in a single equation.
5. **Moment-Area Method:** Graphical method using M/EI diagram areas and centroids.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Three Fundamental Relationships

### 3.1.1 Derivation of $dV/dx = -w(x)$ (Load-Shear Relation)

Consider a small element of a beam of length $dx$ at distance $x$ from the origin. Let:
- $V$ = shear force on the left face of the element (acting **upward**, by positive convention)
- $V + dV$ = shear force on the right face of the element
- $w$ = intensity of distributed load (assumed **downward** as a positive quantity in beam convention, so upward $w$ is negative)

The element is in equilibrium under the vertical forces:

$$\sum F_y = 0$$

The forces acting on the element in the vertical direction are:
- Shear $V$ on the left face (upward)
- Shear $(V + dV)$ on the right face (downward, by sign convention)
- Distributed load $w \cdot dx$ acting downward (weight of the element)

Writing equilibrium:

$$V - (V + dV) - w \cdot dx = 0$$

$$V - V - dV - w \cdot dx = 0$$

$$-dV - w \cdot dx = 0$$

$$-dV = w \cdot dx$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$

> **Verified.** This is the first canonical KTU relationship. The negative sign indicates that as $x$ increases (moving right), the shear force decreases if the load is acting downward.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of $dM/dx = V(x)$ (Shear-Moment Relation)

Consider the same beam element of length $dx$. The moments acting on the element are:
- Bending moment $M$ on the left face (counter-clockwise, positive sagging)
- Bending moment $(M + dM)$ on the right face (clockwise, positive sagging)
- Shear force $V$ on the right face at distance $dx$ from the centroid ‚ÄĒ produces a moment $V \cdot dx$ about the centroid of the left face

Taking moments about the **right face** of the element (so the unknown $M$ appears):

$$\sum M_{right\,face} = 0$$

$$-M - V \cdot dx + (M + dM) = 0$$

$$-M - V\,dx + M + dM = 0$$

$$-V\,dx + dM = 0$$

$$dM = V\,dx$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$$

> **Verified.** This is the second canonical KTU relationship. It tells us that the **slope of the BMD at any point equals the shear force at that point**.

### 3.1.3 Combined Relationship: $d^2M/dx^2 = -w(x)$

Differentiating the second relationship with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{dM}{dx}\right) = \frac{dV}{dx}$$

$$\frac{d^2M}{dx^2} = \frac{dV}{dx}$$

Substituting from the first relationship:

$$\frac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w(x)$$

> **Verified.** This is the **curvature-loading** relationship. The concavity of the BMD is determined by the load.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Derivations ‚ÄĒ Standard Simply Supported Beam with UDL

### 3.2.1 Reactions

Consider a simply supported beam of length $L$ with a UDL of intensity $w$ over the entire span.

Total downward load:

$$W = wL$$

This acts at the midspan, i.e., at $x = L/2$.

By symmetry of loading and geometry, the reactions at supports A and B are **equal**:

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2}$$

### 3.2.2 Shear Force at a Section $x$ (measured from A)

Cutting the beam at section $x$ and considering the left-hand portion:

Sum of vertical forces on the left portion (taking upward as positive):

$$V(x) = R_A - w \cdot x$$

$$V(x) = \frac{wL}{2} - wx$$

At $x = 0$ (support A):

$$V(0) = \frac{wL}{2} - 0 = \frac{wL}{2}$$

At $x = L$ (support B):

$$V(L) = \frac{wL}{2} - wL = -\frac{wL}{2}$$

So the shear goes linearly from $+\dfrac{wL}{2}$ to $-\dfrac{wL}{2}$, crossing zero at midspan.

**Setting $V = 0$ to find the point of zero shear (location of M_max):**

$$\frac{wL}{2} - wx = 0$$

$$wx = \frac{wL}{2}$$

$$x = \frac{L}{2}$$

### 3.2.3 Bending Moment at Section $x$

Taking moments about the section $x$ (considering the left-hand portion):

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - w \cdot x \cdot \frac{x}{2}$$

$$M(x) = \frac{wL}{2} \cdot x - \frac{wx^2}{2}$$

$$M(x) = \frac{wL \cdot x}{2} - \frac{wx^2}{2}$$

At $x = 0$ (support A):

$$M(0) = 0$$

At $x = L$ (support B):

$$M(L) = \frac{wL \cdot L}{2} - \frac{wL^2}{2} = \frac{wL^2}{2} - \frac{wL^2}{2} = 0$$

At $x = L/2$ (midspan):

$$M\left(\frac{L}{2}\right) = \frac{wL}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} - \frac{w}{2}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{wL^2}{4} - \frac{wL^2}{8} = \frac{wL^2}{8}$$

> **Verified:** $M_{max} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8}$ at midspan. ‚úď

### 3.2.4 Maximum Slope and Deflection (via Double Integration)

From the Euler-Bernoulli beam equation:

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{wLx}{2} - \frac{wx^2}{2}$$

**First integration (gives slope):**

$$EI \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{wLx^2}{4} - \frac{wx^3}{6} + C_1$$

**Second integration (gives deflection):**

$$EI \cdot y = \frac{wLx^3}{12} - \frac{wx^4}{24} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Boundary conditions for a simply supported beam:**

At $x = 0$ (support A): $y = 0$ (no deflection)
At $x = L$ (support B): $y = 0$ (no deflection)

**Applying $y(0) = 0$:**

$$EI \cdot 0 = 0 - 0 + 0 + C_2$$

$$C_2 = 0$$

**Applying $y(L) = 0$:**

$$0 = \frac{wL \cdot L^3}{12} - \frac{wL^4}{24} + C_1 \cdot L$$

$$0 = \frac{wL^4}{12} - \frac{wL^4}{24} + C_1 L$$

$$0 = \frac{2wL^4 - wL^4}{24} + C_1 L$$

$$0 = \frac{wL^4}{24} + C_1 L$$

$$C_1 L = -\frac{wL^4}{24}$$

$$C_1 = -\frac{wL^3}{24}$$

**Slope equation:**

$$EI \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{wLx^2}{4} - \frac{wx^3}{6} - \frac{wL^3}{24}$$

**Maximum slope occurs at the supports (where deflection is zero). At $x = 0$:**

$$EI \cdot \theta_A = 0 - 0 - \frac{wL^3}{24}$$

$$\theta_A = -\frac{wL^3}{24EI}$$

The negative sign indicates the slope is **clockwise** at A (for downward UDL). The magnitude is:

$$\theta_{max} = \frac{wL^3}{24EI}$$

**Maximum deflection occurs at $x = L/2$ (by symmetry):**

$$EI \cdot y\left(\frac{L}{2}\right) = \frac{wL \cdot (L/2)^3}{12} - \frac{w(L/2)^4}{24} - \frac{wL^3}{24} \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

$$= \frac{wL^4}{96} - \frac{wL^4}{384} - \frac{wL^4}{48}$$

Converting to common denominator (384):

$$= \frac{4wL^4}{384} - \frac{wL^4}{384} - \frac{8wL^4}{384}$$

$$= \frac{(4 - 1 - 8)wL^4}{384}$$

$$= \frac{-5wL^4}{384}$$

So:

$$y_{max} = -\frac{5wL^4}{384EI}$$

The negative sign indicates the deflection is **downward**:

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5wL^4}{384EI}$$

> **Verified:** This is the universally cited result for a simply supported beam with UDL. ‚úď

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix for Shape of SFD and BMD

This decision matrix is critical ‚ÄĒ examiners often give 2-3 marks for correctly identifying the **shape** of each diagram from the loading:

| Loading | Shear Force Diagram (SFD) Shape | Bending Moment Diagram (BMD) Shape |
|---|---|---|
| No load ($w = 0$) | Horizontal straight line (constant) | Linear (straight inclined line) |
| Point load | Sudden vertical jump (step) | Kink (slope changes abruptly) |
| UDL (constant $w$) | Linear (straight inclined line) | Parabolic (2nd-degree curve) |
| UVL (linear $w$) | Parabolic (2nd-degree curve) | Cubic (3rd-degree curve) |
| Applied Couple $M_0$ | No change in SFD | Sudden jump in BMD by $M_0$ |
| End of beam / support | SFD value = reaction (or zero if free) | BMD value = 0 (pin) or = fixing moment (built-in) |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step General Algorithm for SFD/BMD Problems (KTU Pattern)

The following is the **standard 8-step algorithm** that fetches full marks in any KTU 14-mark problem:

1. **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Sketch the beam:** Draw the loaded beam showing all supports, dimensions, and loads.
2. **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute reactions:** Take $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M = 0$ to find all support reactions.
3. **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Identify critical sections:** Mark the points where the loading or geometry changes (points of application of point loads, UDL start/end, supports, free end).
4. **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Write $V(x)$ expressions:** Write the shear force as a function of $x$ for each segment between critical sections.
5. **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Write $M(x)$ expressions:** Write the bending moment as a function of $x$ for each segment.
6. **Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Tabulate values:** Compute $V$ and $M$ at each critical section. Tabulate clearly.
7. **Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Plot the diagrams:** Draw the SFD and BMD with proper signs and shapes.
8. **Step 8 ‚ÄĒ Identify max values:** State the maximum shear, maximum moment, location of contraflexure, and location of zero shear.

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the taxonomy of beam types and loadings as a functional architecture. This serves as a knowledge-graph view of the entire topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B0 --> B2[Overhanging Beam]

    B0 --> B6[Pure Cantilever]

    C0 --> C1[Concentrated Point Load]
    C0 --> C2[Uniformly Distributed Load UDL]
    C0 --> C3[Uniformly Varying Load UVL]

    C0 --> C5[Combined Loading]

    D0 --> D1[Straight Prismatic Beam]
    D0 --> D2[Curved Beam]
    D0 --> D3[Tapered Beam]
    D0 --> D4[Stepped Beam]

    B1 --> E0[Reactions 3 Determinate]
    B3 --> F0[Reactions 6 Indeterminate to 3rd degree]
    B4 --> G0[Indeterminate to n minus 3 degrees]

    C2 --> I0[Total Load w times L acts at midspan]
    C3 --> J0[Total Load half w0 times L acts at one third from max end]

    E0 --> K0[Draw SFD linear variation]

    J0 --> N0[Cubic BMD]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Functional Architecture of the Three Core Relationships

The three fundamental differential relationships are interconnected. The following architecture flow shows how they interact in the SFD-BMD solution workflow:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Load Diagram w of x] -->|dV by dx equals minus w| B0[Shear Force V of x]
    B0 -->|dM by dx equals V| C0[Bending Moment M of x]
    B0 -->|Integrate V dx equals change in M| C0
    C0 -->|Integrate M by EI gives slope theta| D0[Slope theta of x]
    D0 -->|Integrate theta dx gives deflection| E0[Deflection y of x]
    C0 -->|M equals 0 with sign change| F0[Point of Contraflexure]
    B0 -->|V equals 0| G0[Location of Max Moment]
    C0 -->|EI d2y by dx2 equals M| E0

    H0[Inputs Supports and Geometry] --> A0
    A0 --> I0[Step 1 Compute Reactions]
    I0 --> J0[Step 2 Write V x and M x for each segment]
    J0 --> K0[Step 3 Tabulate values at critical sections]
    K0 --> L0[Step 4 Plot SFD and BMD]
    L0 --> M0[Step 5 Identify Maxima Minima Points of Contraflexure]
```

## 4.3 Schematic ‚ÄĒ SFD and BMD for a Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load

The following block-level schematic depicts the characteristic SFD and BMD shapes for the most common exam case ‚ÄĒ a simply supported beam of length $L$ with a central point load $P$. The relationships (in this case $M_{max} = PL/4$ and $V_{max} = P/2$) are shown as annotations.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[Simply Supported Beam Length L] --> A1[Support A Pin at x equals 0]
    A0 --> A2[Support B Roller at x equals L]

    A1 --> A4[Reaction R A equals P by 2 upward]
    A2 --> A5[Reaction R B equals P by 2 upward]

    A4 --> B0[Shear Force Diagram SFD]
    A5 --> B0
    A3 --> B0
    B0 --> B1[Left half V equals plus P by 2 constant]
    B0 --> B2[Jump at midspan: V drops from plus P by 2 to minus P by 2]
    B0 --> B3[Right half V equals minus P by 2 constant]

    B0 --> C0[Bending Moment Diagram BMD]
    C0 --> C1[Linear from 0 at A to peak at midspan]

```

## 4.4 Schematic ‚ÄĒ Cantilever Beam with End Load (Hogging Moment Visualization)

This schematic illustrates why cantilever beams have **hogging** (negative) bending moments ‚ÄĒ the fixed end resists the load, putting the top fibers in tension.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[Cantilever Beam Length L] --> A1[Fixed Support at x equals 0]
    A0 --> A2[Free End at x equals L]
    A0 --> A3[Point Load P downward at free end]
    A1 --> A4[Reactions: H A V A M A]
    A3 --> A5[Shear: V equals plus P constant along entire length]
    A3 --> A6[Moment: M equals minus P times x at any section]
    A3 --> A7[Top fibers in TENSION hogging]
    A3 --> A8[Bottom fibers in COMPRESSION]

    A5 --> B0[SFD: Horizontal line at plus P throughout]
    A6 --> C0[BMD: Linear from 0 at free end to minus P times L at fixed end]
    C0 --> C1[M max equals minus P times L at fixed support]
```

## 4.5 Schematic ‚ÄĒ Simply Supported Beam with UDL (Sagging Parabola Visualization)

The classic $wL^2/8$ case ‚ÄĒ the BMD is a **parabola** opening downward (sagging).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[Simply Supported Beam Length L] --> A1[Support A at x equals 0]
    A0 --> A2[Support B at x equals L]
    A0 --> A3[UDL of intensity w over entire span]
    A1 --> A4[R A equals w times L by 2]
    A2 --> A5[R B equals w times L by 2]
    A3 --> A6[Total load W equals w times L acting at midspan]

    A4 --> B0[SFD: Linear from plus wL by 2 at A to minus wL by 2 at B]
    B0 --> B1[Zero shear at x equals L by 2]

    B0 --> C0[BMD: Parabolic from 0 at A to peak at midspan back to 0 at B]
    C0 --> C1[M max equals w times L squared by 8 at midspan]
    C0 --> C2[Concave downward sagging shape]
    C0 --> C3[Bottom fibers in tension throughout]
```

## 4.6 Schematic ‚ÄĒ Deflection Curve Geometry (Bending of Beams)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[Beam Before Loading: Straight horizontal line] --> B0[Load Applied]
    B0 --> C0[Beam Deflects: Curves downward]
    C0 --> D0[Neutral Axis: No length change, no strain, no stress]
    C0 --> E0[Top Fibers: Shorten, COMPRESSIVE stress sigma_c]
    C0 --> F0[Bottom Fibers: Elongate, TENSILE stress sigma_t]
    D0 --> G0[Curvature: 1 over R equals M over EI]
    E0 --> H0[Strain: epsilon equals y over R]
    F0 --> H0
    H0 --> I0[Stress: sigma equals E times epsilon equals M times y over I]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
