# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2019 Scheme, Frequently Asked]**

### **Part A ‚ÄĒ Question 1**

**Q: Distinguish between a cantilever beam and a simply supported beam. State any two practical examples for each type.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Cantilever Beam | Simply Supported Beam |
|---|---|---|
| **Support condition** | One end is **fixed (clamped)**, the other end is **free** | One end is **pinned (hinged)**, the other end is on a **roller** |
| **Reactions** | 3 reactions (H, V, M at fixed end) | 3 reactions (H at pin, V at pin, V at roller) |
| **Maximum BM** | At the **fixed end** (hogging/negative) | At the **midspan** (sagging/positive) |
| **Practical examples** | (1) Diving board, (2) Balcony slab projecting out of a building, (3) Aircraft wing, (4) Flag pole | (1) Bridge deck resting on two piers, (2) Wooden plank across two bricks, (3) Floor beam resting on two columns, (4) Railway sleeper on two rails |

> *Awarding:* 1 mark for correct distinction, 1 mark each for examples (2 marks).

---

### **Part A ‚ÄĒ Question 2**

**Q: Define the terms (i) Shear Force, and (ii) Bending Moment, with the standard KTU sign convention.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(i) Shear Force (1.5 marks):** Shear force at a section of a beam is the algebraic sum of the vertical (transverse) forces on either side of the section. By KTU sign convention, it is taken as **positive** if the left portion of the beam is pushed **upward** (or the right portion pushed **downward**), causing clockwise rotation of the beam element.

**(ii) Bending Moment (1.5 marks):** Bending moment at a section of a beam is the algebraic sum of the moments of all forces on either side of the section about that section. By KTU sign convention, it is **positive** when the moment causes **sagging** of the beam (tension on bottom fibers, compression on top fibers) and **negative** when it causes **hogging** (tension on top fibers, compression on bottom fibers).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ‚ÄĒ With Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

---

### **Part B ‚ÄĒ Question 1: OR Group (Choose Either A or B)**

---

#### **Question 1(A) (14 Marks)** ‚ÄĒ *KTU University Exam - May 2024*

A simply supported beam of length **6 m** carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of **20 kN/m** over the entire span. Draw the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD). Also determine the **maximum bending moment** and the **point where it occurs**.

**Sub-part (a) ‚ÄĒ Compute Reactions and Tabulate SFD/BMD (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Reactions**

Total load on the beam:

$$W = w \times L = 20 \times 6 = 120 \text{ kN}$$

By symmetry, both reactions are equal:

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{W}{2} = \frac{120}{2} = 60 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Shear Force at Section $x$ (measured from A)**

$$V(x) = R_A - w \cdot x = 60 - 20x$$

**Tabulated Values:**

| $x$ (m) | $V(x)$ (kN) |
|---|---|
| 0 (at A) | $60$ |
| 1 | $60 - 20 = 40$ |
| 2 | $60 - 40 = 20$ |
| 3 | $60 - 60 = 0$ |
| 4 | $60 - 80 = -20$ |
| 5 | $60 - 100 = -40$ |
| 6 (at B) | $60 - 120 = -60$ |

**Step 3: Bending Moment at Section $x$**

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - \frac{w \cdot x^2}{2} = 60x - 10x^2$$

**Tabulated Values:**

| $x$ (m) | $M(x)$ (kN¬∑m) |
|---|---|
| 0 | $0$ |
| 1 | $60 - 10 = 50$ |
| 2 | $120 - 40 = 80$ |
| 3 | $180 - 90 = 90$ |
| 4 | $240 - 160 = 80$ |
| 5 | $300 - 250 = 50$ |
| 6 | $360 - 360 = 0$ |

**Sub-part (b) ‚ÄĒ Plot Diagrams and Identify Max BM (7 marks)**

**Step 4: Point of Zero Shear (Location of M_max)**

Setting $V(x) = 0$:

$$60 - 20x = 0 \implies x = 3 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Maximum Bending Moment**

At $x = 3$ m:

$$M_{max} = 60(3) - 10(3)^2 = 180 - 90 = 90 \text{ kN¬∑m}$$

**Step 6: Diagram Characteristics**

- **SFD:** Linear (straight line) from $+60$ kN at A to $-60$ kN at B, crossing zero at $x = 3$ m.
- **BMD:** Parabolic curve (concave down), starting at 0, peaking at 90 kN¬∑m at midspan, returning to 0 at B.
- **Maximum BM occurs at the center of the beam** (at $x = 3$ m) and is **$M_{max} = 90$ kN¬∑m**.

**Verification using standard formula:**

$$M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{8} = \frac{20 \times 6^2}{8} = \frac{720}{8} = 90 \text{ kN¬∑m} \quad \checkmark$$

$$V_{max} = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{20 \times 6}{2} = 60 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark$$

> **Final Answer:** $M_{max} = 90$ kN¬∑m at midspan ($x = 3$ m). ‚úď

---

#### **Question 1(B) (14 Marks)** ‚ÄĒ *KTU University Exam - Dec 2023*

A simply supported beam of length **8 m** carries two point loads: **30 kN at 2 m from the left support** and **50 kN at 5 m from the left support**. Draw the SFD and BMD. Determine the **maximum bending moment** and the **position of the point of contraflexure** (if any).

**Sub-part (a) ‚ÄĒ Reactions and SFD Construction (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Reactions**

Let $R_A$ and $R_B$ be the vertical reactions at supports A ($x = 0$) and B ($x = 8$ m).

$\sum M_A = 0$ (taking counter-clockwise as positive):

$$R_B \times 8 - 30 \times 2 - 50 \times 5 = 0$$

$$8 R_B = 60 + 250 = 310$$

$$R_B = \frac{310}{8} = 38.75 \text{ kN}$$

$\sum F_y = 0$:

$$R_A + R_B = 30 + 50 = 80$$

$$R_A = 80 - 38.75 = 41.25 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Shear Force Calculation at Each Section**

**Segment 1: $0 \le x < 2$ m (from A to first load)**

$$V(x) = R_A = 41.25 \text{ kN} \quad (\text{constant})$$

At $x = 0^+$: $V = +41.25$ kN
At $x = 2^-$: $V = +41.25$ kN

**At $x = 2$ m (under 30 kN load): Sudden drop of 30 kN**

$$V(2^+) = 41.25 - 30 = +11.25 \text{ kN}$$

**Segment 2: $2 \le x < 5$ m (between loads)**

$$V(x) = 41.25 - 30 = 11.25 \text{ kN} \quad (\text{constant})$$

**At $x = 5$ m (under 50 kN load): Sudden drop of 50 kN**

$$V(5^+) = 11.25 - 50 = -38.75 \text{ kN}$$

**Segment 3: $5 \le x \le 8$ m (from second load to B)**

$$V(x) = 41.25 - 30 - 50 = -38.75 \text{ kN} \quad (\text{constant})$$

At $x = 8^-$: $V = -38.75$ kN
At $x = 8$: jumps by $+R_B = 38.75$ kN back to 0. ‚úď

**SFD Tabulation:**

| Section | $x$ (m) | $V$ (kN) |
|---|---|---|
| Just right of A | 0 | $+41.25$ |
| Just left of 30 kN load | 2 | $+41.25$ |
| Just right of 30 kN load | 2 | $+11.25$ |
| Just left of 50 kN load | 5 | $+11.25$ |
| Just right of 50 kN load | 5 | $-38.75$ |
| Just left of B | 8 | $-38.75$ |
| Just right of B | 8 | $0$ |

**Sub-part (b) ‚ÄĒ BMD Construction and Maxima (7 marks)**

**Step 3: Bending Moment at Each Section**

**At A ($x = 0$):**

$$M(0) = 0 \text{ kN¬∑m}$$

**At $x = 2$ m (under 30 kN load):**

$$M(2) = R_A \times 2 = 41.25 \times 2 = 82.5 \text{ kN¬∑m}$$

**At $x = 5$ m (under 50 kN load):**

$$M(5) = R_A \times 5 - 30 \times (5-2)$$

$$M(5) = 41.25 \times 5 - 30 \times 3$$

$$M(5) = 206.25 - 90 = 116.25 \text{ kN¬∑m}$$

**At B ($x = 8$ m):**

$$M(8) = 41.25 \times 8 - 30 \times 6 - 50 \times 3$$

$$M(8) = 330 - 180 - 150 = 0 \text{ kN¬∑m} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Locate Maximum Bending Moment**

In segment 2 ($2 \le x \le 5$ m), $V = +11.25$ kN (positive constant). The BMD slope in this region is positive, so M is increasing throughout this region. Therefore, the **maximum moment in this region occurs at the right end** (at $x = 5$ m).

In segment 3 ($5 \le x \le 8$ m), $V = -38.75$ kN (negative constant). The BMD slope in this region is negative, so M is decreasing throughout. So M is at a **local maximum at $x = 5$ m**.

**Therefore, the maximum bending moment occurs at $x = 5$ m, where the 50 kN load is applied.**

$$\boxed{M_{max} = 116.25 \text{ kN¬∑m at } x = 5 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5: Point of Contraflexure**

The bending moment is positive (sagging) throughout the entire span. The BMD is a series of straight lines all above the $x$-axis. The moment is zero only at the two supports ($x = 0$ and $x = 8$ m). Since the moment does **not** change sign anywhere in the span (no transition from positive to negative), there is **no point of contraflexure** in this beam.

**Step 6: Summary**

- $V_{max} = 41.25$ kN (just right of A)
- $M_{max} = 116.25$ kN¬∑m at $x = 5$ m
- **No point of contraflexure** (since the moment is positive throughout)

---

### **Part B ‚ÄĒ Question 2: OR Group (Choose Either A or B)**

---

#### **Question 2(A) (14 Marks)** ‚ÄĒ *KTU University Exam - May 2023*

A cantilever beam of length **4 m** is fixed at end A and carries:
- A point load of **10 kN** at the **free end B**
- A UDL of **5 kN/m** over the **entire span**

Draw the SFD and BMD. Determine the **maximum bending moment** and the **maximum deflection** at the free end. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm¬≤ and $I = 1 \times 10^8$ mm‚Āī.

**Sub-part (a) ‚ÄĒ Reactions, SFD, and BMD (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Reactions at Fixed End A**

By equilibrium:

$$V_A = 10 + (5 \times 4) = 10 + 20 = 30 \text{ kN} \quad (\text{upward})$$

$$M_A = 10 \times 4 + 5 \times 4 \times 2 = 40 + 40 = 80 \text{ kN¬∑m} \quad (\text{counter-clockwise, hogging})$$

**Step 2: Shear Force at Section $x$ (measured from free end B)**

Considering the **right (free) side** of the section:

**At any section $x$ from B:**

$$V(x) = -[10 + 5x] = -(10 + 5x) \text{ kN}$$

(Here, the sign convention for cantilever is taken from the free end since it has no reactions.)

**SFD Tabulation:**

| $x$ (m) from B | $V(x)$ (kN) |
|---|---|
| 0 (at B) | $-10$ |
| 1 | $-15$ |
| 2 | $-20$ |
| 3 | $-25$ |
| 4 (at A) | $-30$ |

The SFD is **linear** (UDL causes linear variation) with a **jump of -10 kN at B** and a constant slope of $-5$ kN/m thereafter. Total drop of 20 kN due to UDL over 4 m. Final value at A: $-30$ kN, which equals the reaction $V_A = +30$ kN (when read from left). ‚úď

**Step 3: Bending Moment at Section $x$ (measured from B)**

$$M(x) = -[10 \cdot x + 5 \cdot x \cdot (x/2)] = -(10x + 2.5x^2) \text{ kN¬∑m}$$

**BMD Tabulation:**

| $x$ (m) from B | $M(x)$ (kN¬∑m) |
|---|---|
| 0 (at B) | $0$ |
| 1 | $-(10 + 2.5) = -12.5$ |
| 2 | $-(20 + 10) = -30$ |
| 3 | $-(30 + 22.5) = -52.5$ |
| 4 (at A) | $-(40 + 40) = -80$ |

**Step 4: Maximum Bending Moment**

$$M_{max} = -80 \text{ kN¬∑m} \quad \text{at fixed end A} \quad (\text{hogging, top fibers in tension})$$

**Sub-part (b) ‚ÄĒ Maximum Deflection at Free End (7 marks)**

**Step 5: Apply Superposition Principle**

For a cantilever with end point load $P$:

$$\delta_1 = \frac{PL^3}{3EI}$$

For a cantilever with UDL $w$ over the entire length:

$$\delta_2 = \frac{wL^4}{8EI}$$

**Step 6: Convert Units**

- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm¬≤
- $I = 1 \times 10^8$ mm‚Āī
- $EI = 2 \times 10^{13}$ N¬∑mm¬≤ = $2 \times 10^4$ N¬∑m¬≤ = $20,000$ kN¬∑m¬≤
- $L = 4$ m

**Step 7: Compute Each Deflection**

$$\delta_1 = \frac{PL^3}{3EI} = \frac{10 \times 4^3}{3 \times 2 \times 10^4} = \frac{640}{6 \times 10^4} = \frac{640}{60,000}$$

$$\delta_1 = 0.01067 \text{ m} = 10.67 \text{ mm}$$

$$\delta_2 = \frac{wL^4}{8EI} = \frac{5 \times 4^4}{8 \times 2 \times 10^4} = \frac{5 \times 256}{160,000} = \frac{1280}{160,000}$$

$$\delta_2 = 0.008 \text{ m} = 8 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 8: Total Deflection at Free End**

$$\delta_{max} = \delta_1 + \delta_2 = 10.67 + 8.00 = 18.67 \text{ mm (downward)}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{max} = 18.67 \text{ mm (downward)}}$$

---

#### **Question 2(B) (14 Marks)** ‚ÄĒ *KTU University Exam - Dec 2022*

A simply supported beam of length **10 m** carries a UVL (Uniformly Varying Load) that varies from **0 at the left support A** to **30 kN/m at the right support B**. Draw the SFD and BMD. Determine:
- The **reactions** at A and B
- The **maximum bending moment** and its **location**
- The **points of contraflexure** (if any)

**Sub-part (a) ‚ÄĒ Reactions and SFD (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the Total Load**

For a triangular (UVL) load, the total load is the area of the triangle:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} \times w_0 \times L = \frac{1}{2} \times 30 \times 10 = 150 \text{ kN}$$

The centroid of a triangle is located at $\frac{L}{3}$ from the **maximum** end (i.e., from B) or at $\frac{2L}{3}$ from the **zero** end (A). So the centroid is at $x = 10/3$ m from A or at $x = 20/3$ m from B.

**Step 2: Compute Reactions**

Taking moments about A (counter-clockwise positive):

$$R_B \times 10 = 150 \times \frac{2 \times 10}{3} = 150 \times 6.667 = 1000$$

Wait, the centroid from A is at $\frac{2L}{3} = \frac{20}{3}$ m. So:

$$R_B \times 10 = 150 \times \frac{20}{3}$$

$$R_B \times 10 = 1000$$

$$R_B = 100 \text{ kN}$$

By $\sum F_y = 0$:

$$R_A + R_B = 150$$

$$R_A = 150 - 100 = 50 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Load Intensity at Section $x$**

The load varies linearly from 0 at A to 30 kN/m at B. So at any section $x$ from A:

$$w(x) = \frac{30}{10} \cdot x = 3x \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 4: Shear Force at Section $x$**

Considering the **left portion**, the total load on this portion is the area of the smaller triangle from 0 to $x$:

$$\text{Load on left} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot x \cdot (3x) = \frac{3x^2}{2}$$

$$V(x) = R_A - \frac{3x^2}{2} = 50 - 1.5 x^2 \text{ kN}$$

**SFD Tabulation:**

| $x$ (m) | $w(x)$ (kN/m) | $V(x)$ (kN) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 (at A) | 0 | $+50$ |
| 1 | 3 | $50 - 1.5 = 48.5$ |
| 2 | 6 | $50 - 6 = 44$ |
| 3 | 9 | $50 - 13.5 = 36.5$ |
| 4 | 12 | $50 - 24 = 26$ |
| 5 | 15 | $50 - 37.5 = 12.5$ |
| 5.77 (zero shear) | ~17.32 | $\approx 0$ |
| 6 | 18 | $50 - 54 = -4$ |
| 7 | 21 | $50 - 73.5 = -23.5$ |
| 8 | 24 | $50 - 96 = -46$ |
| 9 | 27 | $50 - 121.5 = -71.5$ |
| 10 (at B) | 30 | $50 - 150 = -100$ |

**Verification:** $V(10) = -100$ kN equals $-R_B = -100$ kN. ‚úď

The SFD is a **parabolic curve** (because load is linear in $x$, $V$ is quadratic in $x$).

**Sub-part (b) ‚ÄĒ BMD, Max BM, and Contraflexure (7 marks)**

**Step 5: Bending Moment at Section $x$**

The load on the left portion is a triangle of height $w(x) = 3x$ and base $x$. Its area is $\frac{1}{2} \cdot x \cdot 3x = 1.5 x^2$. Its centroid is at $\frac{2x}{3}$ from A.

Taking moments about the section $x$:

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - (\text{total load on left}) \cdot (\text{distance from section})$$

$$M(x) = 50x - (1.5x^2) \cdot \left(x - \frac{2x}{3}\right)$$

$$M(x) = 50x - 1.5 x^2 \cdot \frac{x}{3}$$

$$M(x) = 50x - 0.5 x^3$$

**BMD Tabulation:**

| $x$ (m) | $M(x)$ (kN¬∑m) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0 |
| 1 | $50 - 0.5 = 49.5$ |
| 2 | $100 - 4 = 96$ |
| 3 | $150 - 13.5 = 136.5$ |
| 4 | $200 - 32 = 168$ |
| 5 | $250 - 62.5 = 187.5$ |
| 6 | $300 - 108 = 192$ |
| 7 | $350 - 171.5 = 178.5$ |
| 8 | $400 - 256 = 144$ |
| 9 | $450 - 364.5 = 85.5$ |
| 10 | $500 - 500 = 0$ |

The BMD is a **cubic curve** (because $w$ is linear, $M$ is cubic in $x$).

**Step 6: Locate Maximum Bending Moment**

Setting $V(x) = 0$:

$$50 - 1.5 x^2 = 0$$

$$x^2 = \frac{50}{1.5} = 33.33$$

$$x = 5.77 \text{ m}$$

**Maximum Bending Moment:**

$$M_{max} = 50(5.77) - 0.5 (5.77)^3$$

$$= 288.5 - 0.5 \times 192.2$$

$$= 288.5 - 96.1 = 192.4 \text{ kN¬∑m}$$

$$\boxed{M_{max} \approx 192 \text{ kN¬∑m at } x \approx 5.77 \text{ m from A}}$$

**Verification using standard formula:**

For a simply supported beam with UVL (zero at A, $w_0$ at B):

$$M_{max} = \frac{w_0 L^2}{9\sqrt{3}} = \frac{30 \times 100}{9 \times 1.732} = \frac{3000}{15.588} = 192.5 \text{ kN¬∑m} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 7: Points of Contraflexure**

Setting $M(x) = 0$:

$$50x - 0.5 x^3 = 0$$

$$x(50 - 0.5 x^2) = 0$$

Solutions:

$$x = 0 \quad \text{(support A, trivially a zero moment point)}$$

$$50 - 0.5 x^2 = 0 \implies x^2 = 100 \implies x = 10 \text{ m} \quad \text{(support B, trivially a zero moment point)}$$

These are the **supports** themselves, not interior points of contraflexure. Since the bending moment is **positive throughout the entire span** (sagging), there is **no interior point of contraflexure** in this beam.

> **Final Answer Summary:**
> - $R_A = 50$ kN, $R_B = 100$ kN
> - $M_{max} = 192.5$ kN¬∑m at $x \approx 5.77$ m from A
> - **No interior points of contraflexure**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚ÄĒ Common Pitfalls for Beam Problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Mismatch (‚Äď2 to ‚Äď3 marks):** Mixing up the sign convention between shear and moment leads to a fully incorrect SFD/BMD. **Stick to the KTU standard convention** (sagging positive).
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert Units (‚Äď2 marks):** When computing deflection, ensure $E$ is in **N/mm¬≤ or kN/m¬≤** consistently with $I$ in **mm‚Āī or m‚Āī** and $L$ in **mm or m**. Mismatched units are a guaranteed zero on the numerical answer.
>
> 3. **Confusing SFD Shape vs. BMD Shape (‚Äď2 marks):** UDL ‚Üí **linear SFD**, **parabolic BMD**. UVL ‚Üí **parabolic SFD**, **cubic BMD**. The differentiation between them is a very common 2-mark question.
>
> 4. **Misidentifying Point of Contraflexure (‚Äď1 mark):** A point of contraflexure requires **$M = 0$ AND a sign change**. Simply finding $M = 0$ at the supports is not a contraflexure; it is just a zero-moment boundary condition.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Self-Weight (‚Äď2 marks):** In long-span beams, the self-weight (as a UDL) must be included. Forgetting this is a common oversight in exam problems.
>
> 6. **Jumping the SFD at the wrong place:** Point loads cause **vertical jumps** in the SFD at their point of application. Applied **couples** cause **vertical jumps** in the BMD but **do NOT** change the SFD. Conflating these two cases is a frequent error.
>
> 7. **Forgetting Units in Final Answer (‚Äď1 mark):** Always quote $V$ in kN, $M$ in kN¬∑m, and $\delta$ in mm or m. A numerical answer without units loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ Beams (Module 2, PCCET205)**

- [ ] A **beam** is a structural member carrying transverse loads; it bends and shears but does not carry axial loads as its primary function.
- [ ] **Six types of beams** by support: simply supported, overhanging, cantilever, propped cantilever, fixed, continuous.
- [ ] **Three types of loads**: point load (kN), UDL (kN/m), UVL (kN/m, varying linearly).
- [ ] **Shear Force** = algebraic sum of vertical forces on either side of a section; sign convention: left-up positive.
- [ ] **Bending Moment** = algebraic sum of moments on either side of a section; sign convention: sagging positive.
- [ ] **Three fundamental relationships** to memorize cold: $dV/dx = -w$, $dM/dx = V$, $d^2M/dx^2 = -w$.
- [ ] **SFD shape vs. BMD shape rule**: UDL gives linear SFD and parabolic BMD; UVL gives parabolic SFD and cubic BMD; point load gives jump in SFD and kink in BMD.
- [ ] **Standard results to memorize**: $PL/4$ for central point load, $wL^2/8$ for full-span UDL, $PL$ for cantilever end load, $wL^2/2$ for cantilever full-span UDL.
- [ ] **Point of contraflexure** = location where $M = 0$ with a **sign change** of $M$ on either side; it is where curvature reverses.
- [ ] **Maximum bending moment** occurs at the point where **shear force = 0** (or at a concentrated moment application).
- [ ] **Flexure formula** (bending stress): $\sigma = My/I = M/Z$, where $Z$ is the section modulus.
- [ ] **Euler-Bernoulli beam equation** (for deflection): $EI \cdot d^2y/dx^2 = M(x)$.
- [ ] **Standard deflection results**: $5wL^4/384EI$ (SS UDL), $PL^3/48EI$ (SS central point load), $wL^4/8EI$ (cantilever UDL), $PL^3/3EI$ (cantilever end load).
- [ ] **Macaulay's method** uses $\langle x - a \rangle^n$ brackets to write a single equation for $V(x)$ and $M(x)$ across discontinuous loading.
- [ ] **Superposition principle** is valid for linearly elastic beams: deflections due to multiple loads can be summed.
- [ ] **Area-shear theorem**: change in $M$ between two sections = area of SFD between those sections.
- [ ] **Slope-deflection sign convention**: clockwise slope at left end and counter-clockwise slope at right end are taken positive (or any consistent convention, but KTU-specific convention must be followed).
- [ ] **Units check**: force in kN, moment in kN¬∑m, stress in N/mm¬≤ (= MPa), $I$ in mm‚Āī, $E$ in N/mm¬≤, deflection in mm.
- [ ] **Hogging vs. Sagging**: hogging = top fibers in tension = negative $M$; sagging = bottom fibers in tension = positive $M$.
- [ ] **At a pin/roller support**: $M = 0$ (always). At a **fixed support**: $M = $ fixing moment (‚Č† 0).
- [ ] **In a propped cantilever**, the degree of indeterminacy is **1**. Use the consistent deformation method or three-moment theorem.
- [ ] **For a fixed beam** (both ends built-in), the degree of indeterminacy is **3**. The fixed-end moments are the standard primary unknowns.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When you get a beam problem, **always draw the SFD and BMD on the same plot**, label all key values clearly, and **box the final numerical answer** with units. A neat, well-labeled diagram can earn 1-2 partial marks even if your algebra is slightly off.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **beam** is a structural member subjected to transverse loading (loads perpendicular to its longitudinal axis), which produces **bending moments**, **shear forces**, and in some cases **axial forces** and **torsional moments**. Beams are the most fundamental and widely used load-carrying elements in civil, mechanical, and aerospace engineering structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim for Exam):**
> *"A beam is a structural member of long span (length >> cross-sectional dimension) designed to support transverse loads applied perpendicular to its axis, transferring them to the supports."*

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, beams are classified based on **three primary criteria**:

1. **Classification by Support Conditions** (Most Important for Exam)
2. **Classification by Geometry**
3. **Classification by Equilibrium/Statically Determinate vs. Indeterminate**

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **ūüéĮ Think of a beam as a "horizontal spine" of a structure.**
> Just like your spine supports the weight of your body and transfers it to your legs, a beam supports the loads (people, furniture, vehicles, machinery) and transfers them to walls, columns, or foundations.

| Real-World Analogy | Beam Type |
|---|---|
| A **diving board** fixed at one end, free at the other | Cantilever Beam |
| A **bridge** resting on two piers | Simply Supported Beam |
| A **balcony slab** extending beyond a wall | Overhanging Beam |
| A **continuous slab of a building floor** | Continuous Beam |
| A **table leg** fixed in concrete on both ends | Fixed Beam |

## 1.3 Key Physical Parameters

- **Span (L)**: Longitudinal length between supports, measured in **metres (m)** or **millimetres (mm)**.
- **Young's Modulus (E)**: Material stiffness property, measured in **N/m¬≤** or **Pa** (e.g., Steel: **E = 2 √ó 10‚ĀĶ N/mm¬≤**).
- **Moment of Inertia (I)**: Cross-sectional geometric property, measured in **mm‚Āī** or **m‚Āī**.
- **Flexural Rigidity (EI)**: Resistance to bending deformation, in **N¬∑m¬≤** or **N¬∑mm¬≤**.

## 1.4 The Big Picture ‚ÄĒ Why This Topic Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> Every building, bridge, flyover, crane, aircraft wing, and machine frame is built using beams of some type. **Choosing the correct beam type directly affects:**
> - Safety factor
> - Material economy (cost optimization)
> - Construction methodology
> - Deflection and vibration control

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification Tree

> [!NOTE]
> **ūüĒĎ This is the most important diagram for Module 2. Memorize this hierarchy.**

```
BEAMS
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ 1. By Support Conditions
‚Ēā   ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ (a) Simply Supported Beam
‚Ēā   ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ (b) Cantilever Beam
‚Ēā   ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ (c) Overhanging Beam
‚Ēā   ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ (d) Fixed Beam (Built-in / Encastre Beam)
‚Ēā   ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ (e) Propped Cantilever Beam
‚Ēā   ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ (f) Continuous Beam
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ 2. By Geometry
‚Ēā   ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ (a) Straight Beam
‚Ēā   ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ (b) Curved Beam
‚Ēā   ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ (c) Tapered Beam
‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ 3. By Equilibrium
    ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ (a) Statically Determinate Beams
    ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ (b) Statically Indeterminate Beams
```

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Each Beam Type

### 2.2.1 Simply Supported Beam (SSB)

- **Support Configuration:** Pin support at one end + Roller support at the other end.
- **Reactions:** 2 unknown vertical reactions (no horizontal load assumed); $R_A$ and $R_B$.
- **Equilibrium Equations Available:** $\sum F_y = 0$ and $\sum M = 0$ ‚Üí 2 equations.
- **Degree of Static Indeterminacy (DSI):** 0 (Statically Determinate).
- **Moment at Supports:** Zero ($M_A = M_B = 0$).
- **Bending Moment Diagram (BMD):** Smooth curve (parabolic for UDL).
- **Real Example:** A wooden plank placed across two bricks.

### 2.2.2 Cantilever Beam

- **Support Configuration:** Fixed (built-in) at one end, completely free at the other.
- **Reactions:** 3 unknowns ‚Üí $R_A$ (vertical), $H_A$ (horizontal), $M_A$ (fixing moment).
- **Degree of Static Indeterminacy:** 0.
- **Moment at Fixed End:** Maximum (carries the entire load effect).
- **Moment at Free End:** Zero.
- **Deflection:** Maximum at the free end, zero at fixed end.
- **Real Example:** A flagpole, a balcony, the wings of an aeroplane, a diving board.

### 2.2.3 Overhanging Beam

- **Support Configuration:** Two supports (one pin, one roller) but the beam extends beyond one or both supports.
- **Reactions:** 2 unknowns ($R_A$, $R_B$).
- **Degree of Static Indeterminacy:** 0.
- **Key Feature:** A **Hogging (Negative) Bending Moment** develops over the support(s) due to the overhanging portion, while the central span experiences **Sagging (Positive) Bending Moment**.
- **Real Example:** A bus body chassis, a roof slab projecting beyond a wall.

### 2.2.4 Fixed Beam (Encastre / Built-in Beam)

- **Support Configuration:** Both ends are rigidly built-in (no rotation, no translation).
- **Reactions:** 6 unknowns ‚Üí $R_A$, $H_A$, $M_A$, $R_B$, $H_B$, $M_B$ (3 per end).
- **Equilibrium Equations:** Only 3.
- **Degree of Static Indeterminacy:** 3 (Highly Indeterminate).
- **Key Feature:** Maximum stiffness, minimum deflection (often zero at both ends), carries the **least bending moment** for the same loading (most economical in terms of stress).
- **Real Example:** Reinforced concrete portal frames, machine tool beds.

### 2.2.5 Propped Cantilever Beam

- **Support Configuration:** Fixed at one end + roller (or pin) prop at the other end.
- **Reactions:** 4 unknowns ‚Üí $R_A$, $H_A$, $M_A$, $R_B$.
- **Equilibrium Equations:** 3.
- **Degree of Static Indeterminacy:** 1 (Once Indeterminate).
- **Key Feature:** Combines stiffness of cantilever with simple support ‚ÄĒ common in real construction.
- **Real Example:** A continuous beam bridge deck with one end fixed into the abutment.

### 2.2.6 Continuous Beam

- **Support Configuration:** More than two supports along its length (3 or more spans).
- **Reactions:** Equal to number of supports.
- **Degree of Static Indeterminacy:** $(n-2)$ for a continuous beam with $n$ supports, assuming no axial loads.
- **Key Feature:** Smaller bending moments and deflections than a series of simply supported beams, but requires additional analysis (e.g., Three-Moment Theorem, Moment Distribution Method, or Stiffness Matrix).
- **Real Example:** Multi-span bridges, continuous floor beams in buildings.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Beam Type | No. of Reactions (r) | Equilibrium Eqs (e) | DSI = (r ‚ąí e) | BMD Shape (UDL) | Max Bending Moment Location |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Simply Supported | **2** | 2 | **0** | Parabolic | Mid-span |
| Cantilever | **3** | 3 | **0** | Parabolic (cubic slope) | Fixed End |
| Overhanging | **2** | 2 | **0** | Parabolic + Linear | Over Support |
| Fixed Beam | **6** | 3 | **3** | Parabolic (with inflect. pt.) | End regions |
| Propped Cantilever | **4** | 3 | **1** | Parabolic + Cubic | Fixed End (largest) |
| Continuous | **n** (‚Č•3) | 3 | **n ‚ąí 3** | Smooth wave-like | Over intermediate supports |

### 2.3.1 Standard Bending Moment & Reactions for Common Cases

| Beam | Loading | Reactions | Max BM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simply Supported, Length L, Point Load W at centre | W at L/2 | $R_A = R_B = W/2$ | $WL/4$ at mid-span |
| Simply Supported, UDL w over L | ‚ÄĒ | $R_A = R_B = wL/2$ | $wL^2/8$ at mid-span |
| Cantilever, Length L, Point Load W at free end | W at tip | $R_A = W$, $M_A = WL$ | $WL$ at fixed end |
| Cantilever, UDL w over L | ‚ÄĒ | $R_A = wL$, $M_A = wL^2/2$ | $wL^2/2$ at fixed end |
| Fixed Beam, UDL w over L | ‚ÄĒ | $R_A = R_B = wL/2$ | $wL^2/12$ at ends, $wL^2/24$ at centre |
| Propped Cantilever, UDL w over L | ‚ÄĒ | $R_A = 3wL/8$, $R_B = 5wL/8$ | $9wL^2/128$ at $x = 3L/8$ from propped end |

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **ūüŹóÔłŹ Practical Decision-Making in Design:**
> - **Cantilevers** ‚Üí Maximum moment at fixed end ‚Üí Use this end as the design critical section.
> - **Simply Supported** ‚Üí Easy to analyze, easy to construct, but higher deflections.
> - **Fixed Beams** ‚Üí Most efficient in material use, but construction is difficult (requires rigid joints).
> - **Continuous Beams** ‚Üí Preferred in bridges and multi-storey buildings for stiffness and economy.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Degree of Static Indeterminacy (DSI) for a Beam

### 3.1.1 The Governing Equation

For a 2D beam structure, the general formula for DSI is:

$$DSI = r - e - c$$

where:
- $r$ = Total number of support reaction components
- $e$ = Number of available equilibrium equations (always 3 for a 2D rigid body)
- $c$ = Number of internal condition equations (e.g., internal hinges)

For a typical beam (no internal hinges), $c = 0$, hence:

$$DSI = r - 3$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Application

**Case 1: Simply Supported Beam**
- Pin: 2 reactions (vertical + horizontal)
- Roller: 1 reaction (vertical only)
- Total: $r = 3$
- $DSI = 3 - 3 = 0$ ‚Üí **Statically Determinate** ‚úď

**Case 2: Fixed Beam**
- Fixed end A: 3 reactions (vertical, horizontal, moment)
- Fixed end B: 3 reactions (vertical, horizontal, moment)
- Total: $r = 6$
- $DSI = 6 - 3 = 3$ ‚Üí **Statically Indeterminate to Degree 3** ‚úď

**Case 3: Propped Cantilever**
- Fixed end: 3 reactions
- Roller prop: 1 reaction
- Total: $r = 4$
- $DSI = 4 - 3 = 1$ ‚Üí **Indeterminate to Degree 1** ‚úď

**Case 4: Continuous Beam with 4 Supports (No Axial Load)**
- Each support provides 1 vertical reaction
- Total: $r = 4$
- $DSI = 4 - 3 = 1$ ‚Üí **Indeterminate to Degree 1** ‚úď

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bending Moment Equation for a Cantilever with UDL

### 3.2.1 Setup

Consider a cantilever beam of length $L$, fixed at the left end (A) and free at the right end (B), carrying a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of intensity $w$ (N/m) over its entire length.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Free Body Diagram and Reactions**

Total downward load = $w \times L$

Sum of vertical forces:
$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies R_A = wL$$

Sum of moments about A:
$$\sum M_A = 0 \implies M_A - wL \cdot \frac{L}{2} = 0$$
$$M_A = \frac{wL^2}{2}$$

**Step 2: Bending Moment at a Section at Distance x from the Free End**

We consider the right portion (from $x$ to $L$) because it has fewer loads and avoids the unknown $M_A$.

The Bending Moment at section $x$ (using the right-hand side free body):

$$M_x = -w \cdot x \cdot \frac{x}{2}$$

$$M_x = -\frac{wx^2}{2}$$

The negative sign indicates a **hogging moment** (concave-down curvature), which is characteristic of cantilevers.

**Step 3: Maximum Bending Moment**

Setting $x = L$ (at the fixed end):

$$M_{max} = -\frac{wL^2}{2}$$

**Step 4: Shear Force Equation**

For the same right portion:
$$V_x = w \cdot x$$

At $x = 0$ (free end): $V = 0$
At $x = L$ (fixed end): $V = wL$

This matches the vertical reaction at A.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Slope and Deflection for a Cantilever with UDL

Using the **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Equation**:

$$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M_x$$

**Step 1: Substitute the Moment Equation**

$$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -\frac{wx^2}{2}$$

**Step 2: Integrate Once to Get Slope**

$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{wx^3}{6} + C_1$$

**Step 3: Apply Boundary Condition at the Fixed End**

At $x = L$, slope $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$ (no rotation at fixed end):

$$0 = -\frac{wL^3}{6} + C_1$$
$$C_1 = \frac{wL^3}{6}$$

**Step 4: Integrate Again to Get Deflection**

$$EI \cdot y = -\frac{wx^3}{6} \cdot \frac{x}{4} + C_1 \cdot x + C_2$$

Wait ‚ÄĒ let me redo this more carefully:

$$EI \cdot y = -\frac{w x^4}{24} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 5: Apply Boundary Condition at Fixed End (No Deflection at x = L)**

At $x = L$, $y = 0$:
$$0 = -\frac{wL^4}{24} + \frac{wL^3}{6} \cdot L + C_2$$
$$0 = -\frac{wL^4}{24} + \frac{wL^4}{6} + C_2$$

Converting to common denominator (24):
$$0 = -\frac{wL^4}{24} + \frac{4wL^4}{24} + C_2$$
$$0 = \frac{3wL^4}{24} + C_2$$
$$C_2 = -\frac{wL^4}{8}$$

**Step 6: Final Deflection Equation**

$$EI \cdot y = -\frac{wx^4}{24} + \frac{wL^3 x}{6} - \frac{wL^4}{8}$$

**Step 7: Maximum Deflection at the Free End (x = 0)**

At $x = 0$:
$$EI \cdot y_{max} = -\frac{wL^4}{8}$$
$$y_{max} = -\frac{wL^4}{8EI}$$

The negative sign indicates downward deflection. The magnitude:

$$|y_{max}| = \frac{wL^4}{8EI}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ūüéĮ KTU Examination Tip:** The maximum deflection of a cantilever with UDL is $\frac{wL^4}{8EI}$ ‚ÄĒ this is a **HIGH-FREQUENCY** exam question. Commit it to memory.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Slope and Deflection of a Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load

A simply supported beam of length $L$ carries a concentrated load $W$ at mid-span.

By symmetry:
$$R_A = R_B = \frac{W}{2}$$

**Bending Moment at distance $x$ from A (for $0 \le x \le L/2$):**
$$M_x = \frac{W}{2} x$$

**Step 1: Apply Beam Equation**

$$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{Wx}{2}$$

**Step 2: Integrate Once**
$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{Wx^2}{4} + C_1$$

**Step 3: Boundary Condition at x = 0 (Deflection = 0)**
$$y(0) = 0 \implies C_2 = 0$$

**Step 4: Symmetry Condition at x = L/2 (Slope = 0)**
$$0 = \frac{W(L/2)^2}{4} + C_1$$
$$C_1 = -\frac{WL^2}{16}$$

**Step 5: Integrate Again**
$$EI \cdot y = \frac{Wx^3}{12} - \frac{WL^2 x}{16}$$

**Step 6: Maximum Deflection at x = L/2**
$$EI \cdot y_{max} = \frac{W(L/2)^3}{12} - \frac{WL^2 (L/2)}{16}$$
$$= \frac{WL^3}{96} - \frac{WL^3}{32}$$
$$= \frac{WL^3}{96} - \frac{3WL^3}{96}$$
$$= -\frac{WL^3}{48}$$

$$\boxed{|y_{max}| = \frac{WL^3}{48EI}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **ūüďĆ KTU Examination Tip:** This is the **most-asked** deflection formula ‚ÄĒ $\delta_{max} = \frac{WL^3}{48EI}$ for a centrally loaded simply supported beam.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix ‚ÄĒ All Key Formulas at a Glance

| Beam Configuration | Loading | Max Deflection ($\delta_{max}$) | Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cantilever | Point Load W at tip | $\frac{WL^3}{3EI}$ | Free end |
| Cantilever | UDL w | $\frac{wL^4}{8EI}$ | Free end |
| Simply Supported | Central Point Load W | $\frac{WL^3}{48EI}$ | Mid-span |
| Simply Supported | UDL w | $\frac{5wL^4}{384EI}$ | Mid-span |
| Fixed Beam | Central Point Load W | $\frac{WL^3}{192EI}$ | Mid-span |
| Fixed Beam | UDL w | $\frac{wL^4}{384EI}$ | Mid-span |
| Propped Cantilever | UDL w | $\frac{wL^4}{185EI}$ | At $x = 0.5785L$ from propped end |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a simply supported beam and a fixed beam with neat sketches.**

**Model Answer:**

A **simply supported beam** has a pin support at one end and a roller support at the other. The bending moment at both supports is zero, and the supports allow rotation. It is **statically determinate** (DSI = 0).

A **fixed beam** is rigidly built-in at both ends, preventing both rotation and translation. It carries fixing moments at both ends and is **statically indeterminate to the 3rd degree** (DSI = 3).

| Property | Simply Supported | Fixed |
|---|---|---|
| End Conditions | Pin + Roller | Both Built-in |
| DSI | 0 | 3 |
| End Moments | Zero | Non-zero |
| Max BM (UDL) | $wL^2/8$ | $wL^2/12$ |
| Construction | Easy | Difficult |

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2023]
**What is the degree of static indeterminacy of a propped cantilever beam? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer:**

The **degree of static indeterminacy (DSI)** of a propped cantilever beam is **1**.

**Justification:**
- A propped cantilever has a fixed support at one end (providing 3 reaction components: $R_A$, $H_A$, $M_A$) and a roller prop at the other (providing 1 reaction component: $R_B$).
- Total number of reactions: $r = 4$
- Number of available equilibrium equations: $e = 3$
- DSI $= r - e = 4 - 3 = \mathbf{1}$

Hence, it is **once statically indeterminate** and requires one compatibility equation (e.g., deflection at the prop = 0) for analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A cantilever beam of length 4 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 10 kN/m over its entire span and a point load of 20 kN at the free end. Draw the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD), and find the maximum bending moment.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define a continuous beam. For a continuous beam ABC of two equal spans (each 6 m) with a uniformly distributed load of 5 kN/m over both spans, find the support reactions and the bending moment at the central support B using the Three-Moment Theorem.

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Given:**
- Cantilever length: $L = 4$ m
- UDL: $w = 10$ kN/m over the entire length
- Point load at free end: $W = 20$ kN

**Step 1: Calculate Support Reactions at the Fixed End A**

Vertical reaction:
$$R_A = wL + W = 10 \times 4 + 20 = 60 \text{ kN}$$

Fixing moment at A:
$$M_A = wL \cdot \frac{L}{2} + W \cdot L = 10 \times 4 \times 2 + 20 \times 4$$
$$M_A = 80 + 80 = 160 \text{ kN¬∑m}$$

**Step 2: Bending Moment at a General Section x from the Free End**

For $0 \le x \le 4$ m:
$$M_x = -W \cdot x - w \cdot \frac{x^2}{2}$$
$$M_x = -20x - 5x^2$$

**Step 3: Shear Force Equation**

$$V_x = -(W + wx) = -(20 + 10x)$$

**Step 4: Key Values**

| $x$ (m) | Shear Force (kN) | Bending Moment (kN¬∑m) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 (free end) | $-20$ | $0$ |
| 1 | $-30$ | $-25$ |
| 2 | $-40$ | $-60$ |
| 3 | $-50$ | $-105$ |
| 4 (fixed end) | $-60$ | $-160$ |

**Step 5: Maximum Bending Moment**

The maximum bending moment occurs at the fixed end:
$$\boxed{M_{max} = 160 \text{ kN¬∑m (Hogging)}}$$

**Step 6: SFD and BMD Description**

- SFD: Linear variation from $-20$ kN at free end to $-60$ kN at fixed end.
- BMD: Parabolic curve from $0$ at free end to $-160$ kN¬∑m at fixed end.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Definition:** A continuous beam is a beam that extends continuously over more than two supports, with intermediate supports providing additional restraints. It is statically indeterminate to degree $(n-3)$ where $n$ is the number of supports.

**Given:**
- Two equal spans: $L_1 = L_2 = 6$ m
- UDL: $w = 5$ kN/m over both spans
- Supports: A (end), B (intermediate), C (end)

**Step 1: Apply the Three-Moment Theorem (Clapeyron's Theorem)**

For supports A, B, C (where A and C are end supports, $M_A = M_C = 0$):

$$M_A L_1 + 2M_B(L_1 + L_2) + M_C L_2 = -\frac{w L_1^3}{4} - \frac{w L_2^3}{4}$$

Substituting $M_A = M_C = 0$ and $L_1 = L_2 = 6$ m:

$$2M_B(6 + 6) = -\frac{5 \times 6^3}{4} - \frac{5 \times 6^3}{4}$$

$$24 M_B = -2 \times \frac{5 \times 216}{4} = -2 \times 270 = -540$$

$$M_B = -\frac{540}{24} = -22.5 \text{ kN¬∑m}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Support Reactions**

For span AB:
$$R_A = \frac{w L_1}{2} - \frac{M_B - M_A}{L_1} = \frac{5 \times 6}{2} - \frac{-22.5 - 0}{6}$$
$$R_A = 15 + 3.75 = 18.75 \text{ kN}$$

By symmetry, $R_C = 18.75$ kN.

$$R_B = w(L_1 + L_2) - R_A - R_C = 5 \times 12 - 18.75 - 18.75$$
$$R_B = 60 - 37.5 = 22.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Final Answer:**
- $R_A = R_C = 18.75$ kN
- $R_B = 22.5$ kN
- $M_B = -22.5$ kN¬∑m (Hogging)

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A simply supported beam of span 6 m carries a point load of 30 kN at 2 m from the left support. Calculate the support reactions and draw the SFD and BMD. Also find the maximum bending moment.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the difference between a statically determinate and statically indeterminate beam with two examples of each. A fixed beam of span 5 m carries a UDL of 8 kN/m over its entire length. Calculate the fixing moments and the reactions at the supports using the standard results.

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Given:**
- Span: $L = 6$ m
- Point load: $W = 30$ kN at $a = 2$ m from left support A

**Step 1: Calculate Support Reactions**

Taking moments about A:
$$R_B \times 6 = 30 \times 2$$
$$R_B = 10 \text{ kN}$$

Sum of vertical forces:
$$R_A + R_B = 30 \implies R_A = 20 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Bending Moment Equations**

For $0 \le x \le 2$ m:
$$M_x = R_A \cdot x = 20x$$

At $x = 2$: $M = 40$ kN¬∑m

For $2 \le x \le 6$ m:
$$M_x = R_A \cdot x - 30(x - 2) = 20x - 30x + 60$$
$$M_x = -10x + 60$$

At $x = 6$: $M = 0$ ‚úď

**Step 3: Maximum Bending Moment**

The maximum bending moment occurs at the point of load application ($x = 2$ m):
$$\boxed{M_{max} = 40 \text{ kN¬∑m (Sagging)}}$$

This is also verified by the formula: $M_{max} = \frac{Wab}{L} = \frac{30 \times 2 \times 4}{6} = 40$ kN¬∑m ‚úď

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Difference Between Determinate and Indeterminate Beams:**

A **statically determinate beam** can be analyzed using only the equations of static equilibrium, because the number of unknown reactions equals the number of available equilibrium equations.

A **statically indeterminate beam** has more unknown reactions than equilibrium equations, requiring additional compatibility (deformation) equations for analysis.

| Determinate Examples | Indeterminate Examples |
|---|---|
| Simply Supported Beam | Fixed Beam |
| Cantilever Beam | Propped Cantilever |

**Fixed Beam Problem:**

**Given:** $L = 5$ m, $w = 8$ kN/m

**Standard Formula for Fixing Moments of a Fixed Beam with UDL:**

$$M_A = M_B = \frac{wL^2}{12}$$

$$M_A = M_B = \frac{8 \times 5^2}{12} = \frac{200}{12} = 16.67 \text{ kN¬∑m}$$

**Support Reactions:**

By symmetry:
$$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{8 \times 5}{2} = 20 \text{ kN}$$

**Verification using Bending Moment at Mid-span:**

$$M_{mid} = \frac{wL^2}{24} = \frac{8 \times 25}{24} = 8.33 \text{ kN¬∑m (Sagging)}$$

The BMD is parabolic, with hogging moments of $16.67$ kN¬∑m at the ends and sagging moment of $8.33$ kN¬∑m at mid-span.

> [!WARNING]
> **‚ö†ÔłŹ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mistakes):**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** In cantilever beams, the moment is **hogging (negative)**. Many students write the magnitude but forget the negative sign, losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting Maximum Deflection Location:** Maximum deflection in a simply supported beam with central point load is at the **centre**, not at the supports. Marks deducted for wrong location.
> 3. **Wrong Units:** Always maintain consistency. If span is in **m**, then deflection will be in **m**. Convert to **mm** only at the final answer.
> 4. **Skipping BMD/SFD Diagrams:** KTU examiners allocate 2‚Äď3 marks purely for the diagram. Even if your calculations are correct, missing or sloppy diagrams lead to mark loss.
> 5. **Misidentifying Indeterminacy:** A fixed beam has DSI = **3** (not 1). Students commonly confuse it with a propped cantilever. Always count: $r - 3$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ūüéĮ Final Exam Quick-Recall Points:**

- ‚úÖ **Simply Supported Beam** ‚Üí Pin + Roller ‚Üí 2 reactions ‚Üí **DSI = 0** ‚Üí Max BM at mid-span.
- ‚úÖ **Cantilever Beam** ‚Üí Fixed at one end only ‚Üí 3 reactions ‚Üí **DSI = 0** ‚Üí Max BM at fixed end.
- ‚úÖ **Overhanging Beam** ‚Üí 2 supports + projection ‚Üí **DSI = 0** ‚Üí Has BOTH sagging and hogging moments.
- ‚úÖ **Fixed Beam** ‚Üí Both ends built-in ‚Üí 6 reactions ‚Üí **DSI = 3** ‚Üí Most rigid, smallest deflections.
- ‚úÖ **Propped Cantilever** ‚Üí 1 fixed end + 1 simple support ‚Üí **DSI = 1** ‚Üí Requires 1 compatibility equation.
- ‚úÖ **Continuous Beam** ‚Üí > 2 supports ‚Üí **DSI = (n ‚ąí 3)** ‚Üí Analyzed by Three-Moment Theorem or Moment Distribution.

### ūüĒĎ Must-Memorize Formulas for the Exam:

$$\delta_{SSB,central\ load} = \frac{WL^3}{48EI}$$

$$\delta_{Cantilever,UDL} = \frac{wL^4}{8EI}$$

$$\delta_{Cantilever,point\ load} = \frac{WL^3}{3EI}$$

$$M_{SSB,UDL} = \frac{wL^2}{8}, \quad M_{Cantilever,UDL} = \frac{wL^2}{2}$$

$$M_{Fixed\ Beam,UDL} = \frac{wL^2}{12}\ \text{(at ends)}, \quad \frac{wL^2}{24}\ \text{(at centre)}$$

### ūüß† Conceptual Traps to Avoid:

1. ‚ĚĆ Don't confuse **"degree of indeterminacy"** with **"number of supports"**.
2. ‚ĚĆ A continuous beam with 3 supports is indeterminate to degree **1**, not 3.
3. ‚ĚĆ The slope at the fixed end of a cantilever is **zero**, not infinity.
4. ‚ĚĆ The shear force is **maximum** at the supports, not at the mid-span (for UDL on SSB, it IS maximum at supports but zero at mid-span).
5. ‚ĚĆ In an overhanging beam, the point of contraflexure (where BM = 0) lies between the central support and the load.

### ūüďö Recommended Self-Study Order:

1. Draw free body diagrams of each beam type.
2. Practice calculating reactions for each.
3. Move to SFD and BMD plotting.
4. Apply standard deflection formulas to verify computations.
5. Attempt past KTU university questions (Dec 2019 onwards in 2024 scheme).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Loading on Beams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Loading on Beams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ‚ÄĒ Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Beam?

A **beam** is a structural member, typically straight and horizontal, whose primary function is to resist loads applied perpendicular (transverse) to its longitudinal axis. Beams transfer the applied transverse loads to supports through **bending**, **shear**, and in some cases **torsion**. The principal internal responses of a beam to transverse loading are:

- **Bending Moment (BM)** ‚ÄĒ represented as $M$ in $\text{N¬∑m}$ or $\text{kN¬∑m}$
- **Shear Force (SF)** ‚ÄĒ represented as $V$ in $\text{N}$ or $\text{kN}$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *A beam is a flexural member subjected primarily to transverse loads, producing internal shear forces and bending moments. The intensity, distribution, and nature of the applied load govern the internal force distribution along the beam length.*

## 1.2 Classification of Loads on Beams (KTU Module 2 ‚ÄĒ Core Topic)

Loads acting on a beam are classified primarily by their **mode of application**, **distribution**, and **nature of variation** along the span. The complete taxonomy as prescribed by the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 is:

| S.No. | Type of Load | Quick Description | Common KTU Symbol |
|:-----:|:-------------|:------------------|:-----------------:|
| 1 | **Concentrated (Point) Load** | A single force acting at one point | $P$ (N or kN) |
| 2 | **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)** | Constant intensity along a length | $w$ (N/m or kN/m) |
| 3 | **Uniformly Varying Load (UVL)** | Linearly varying intensity | Triangular or Trapezoidal |
| 4 | **Couple / Moment Load** | Pure rotation, no translation | $M_0$ (N¬∑m) |
| 5 | **Impact Load** | Suddenly applied, dynamic | $P_{impact}$ |
| 6 | **Eccentric / Axial Load** | Off-axis or along beam axis | $P_{axial}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** For Module 2, the three most frequently tested loading types in End Semester Examinations (ESE) are **Point Load**, **UDL**, and **UVL (Triangular)**. A typical 14-mark question converts UVL/UDL into an equivalent point load and then determines SF and BM.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The Diving Board

Imagine a **diving board** at a swimming pool:

- A child standing on the *exact end* of the board is analogous to a **point load**.
- A row of children standing shoulder-to-shoulder along the board is analogous to a **UDL** ‚ÄĒ every part of the board supports equal weight per unit length.
- A child sliding off the edge such that more weight sits near the fixed end and less at the free end is analogous to a **triangular UVL**.
- An adult twisting the end of the board (without standing on it) creates a pure **moment/couple load**.

The board's reaction at the fixed support will change shape (deflection) and internal stress depending on *how* the load is applied ‚ÄĒ this is the essence of the **Types of Loading** topic.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$
- **Self-weight of standard materials (kN/m¬≥):** Steel $= 78.5$, Concrete $= 24$, Timber $= 5$ to $8$
- **Standard residential floor live load (per IS 875):** $2.0 \, \text{kN/m}^2$ to $5.0 \, \text{kN/m}^2$

> [!TIP]
> Always remember to **convert the area load (kN/m¬≤) into a line load (kN/m)** by multiplying it with the **tributary width** of the beam. For example, $3 \, \text{kN/m}^2 \times 4 \, \text{m} = 12 \, \text{kN/m}$ UDL on the beam.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ‚ÄĒ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Type 1: Concentrated (Point) Load

### Definition
A **concentrated load** is theoretically an idealisation where the entire load is assumed to act at a **single point** on the beam. In practice, the contact area is negligibly small compared to the beam span.

### Characteristics
- Represented as a **single straight arrow** in diagrams
- The SF diagram exhibits a **vertical jump (discontinuity)** equal to $P$ at the point of application
- The BM diagram exhibits a **kink (slope discontinuity)** at the point of application

### Why does this happen?
Because the shear force is the *summation of forces* on either side of a section, adding a concentrated force causes an immediate change ‚ÄĒ exactly the same way a transaction immediately changes your bank balance.

## 2.2 Type 2: Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)

### Definition
A load whose intensity $w$ is **constant over a finite length** of the beam. Units: $\text{N/m}$ or $\text{kN/m}$.

### Equivalent Resultant (KTU High-Yield Concept)
The entire UDL can be replaced by an **equivalent point load** of magnitude $W = w \cdot L$ acting at the **centroid (mid-point)** of the loaded length.

$$W_{eq} = w \times L_{loaded}$$

The location of this equivalent point is at the **midpoint** of the UDL region.

### Physical Intuition
Think of a **stack of books** placed uniformly along a table. The total weight equals (weight per book √ó number of books), and the *average* location is in the middle. Replacing all books with a single "phantom" book of the same total weight at the middle gives the same downward force and the same moment effect.

## 2.3 Type 3: Uniformly Varying Load (UVL)

### Sub-type 3a: Triangular Load
- Intensity is **zero at one end** and **maximum $w$ at the other end** (or vice versa).
- Varies **linearly** with distance $x$ from the origin.

$$w(x) = \frac{w_0}{L} \cdot x$$

### Equivalent Resultant
The total load is the area of the triangle:

$$W_{eq} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot w_0 \cdot L$$

The centroid of a triangle lies at $\frac{L}{3}$ from the **base of maximum intensity** and $\frac{2L}{3}$ from the **zero-intensity end**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Students frequently place the centroid at $L/2$ ‚ÄĒ *this is wrong*. For a triangle, the centroid is at $L/3$ from the larger side, NOT at the middle.

### Sub-type 3b: Trapezoidal Load
A trapezoidal load is a **superposition** of a UDL and a triangular load. It can be split into:
- A **rectangle** of intensity $w_1$ (UDL portion) over length $L$
- A **triangle** with height $(w_2 - w_1)$ over length $L$

$$W_{eq,total} = w_1 \cdot L + \tfrac{1}{2}(w_2 - w_1) \cdot L = \tfrac{L}{2}(w_1 + w_2)$$

## 2.4 Type 4: Couple / Moment Load

A **couple** is a pair of equal, opposite, and parallel forces separated by a small distance, producing a **pure rotational effect** with no net translational force.

- Represented as a **curved arrow** in diagrams
- Units: $\text{N¬∑m}$ or $\text{kN¬∑m}$
- Causes an **instantaneous jump in the bending moment diagram** equal to the magnitude of the applied couple

## 2.5 Type 5: Impact Load

A load applied **suddenly** with a finite velocity. The dynamic amplification factor is given by:

$$P_{impact} = P_{static} \times \text{Impact Factor}$$

Common impact factors (per IS 875):
- For lift loads: **1.2 to 1.5**
- For bridge deck live loads: **1.0 to 1.1**

> [!NOTE]
> While impact loads are mentioned in KTU Module 2, detailed dynamic analysis belongs to Module 4/5. For Module 2, treat them as **statically equivalent loads** unless specified otherwise.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| S.No. | Loading Type | Total Load (Resultant) | Location of Resultant (from relevant reference) | Unit |
|:-----:|:-------------|:----------------------|:------------------------------------------------|:----:|
| 1 | Single Point Load $P$ | $W = P$ | At the point of application | $\text{N}$ |
| 2 | UDL $w$ over length $L$ | $W = w L$ | At $L/2$ from either end | $\text{N}$ |
| 3 | Triangular UVL (max $w$ at far end) | $W = \tfrac{1}{2} w L$ | At $L/3$ from the max end | $\text{N}$ |
| 4 | Trapezoidal ($w_1$ to $w_2$) over $L$ | $W = \tfrac{L}{2}(w_1 + w_2)$ | $x = \tfrac{L}{3} \cdot \tfrac{(w_1 + 2w_2)}{(w_1 + w_2)}$ | $\text{N}$ |
| 5 | Couple / Moment $M_0$ | $W = 0$ (no translation) | N/A (pure rotation) | $\text{N¬∑m}$ |
| 6 | Impact Load | $P_{impact} = P \times I_F$ | At the point of application | $\text{N}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip ‚ÄĒ KTU Board Pattern:** Examiners often ask *"Find the equivalent point load and its location for the given UDL/UVL"* for 3 to 7 marks. Memorize the **triangular centroid at $L/3$** ‚ÄĒ it is the single most-asked concept in this topic.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Building floors:** UDL from uniform live load + self-weight of slab
- **Cantilever chajjas/sunshades:** Trapezoidal load from masonry + triangular wind/UDL
- **Bridge decks:** Combination of moving point loads (vehicle wheels) and UDL (lane load)
- **Retaining walls (modeled as cantilever beams):** Trapezoidal earth pressure = UVL
- **Crane girders:** Moving concentrated loads
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equivalent Resultant of a UDL

**Statement:** A UDL of intensity $w$ acting over a length $L$ of a beam is statically equivalent to a point load $W = wL$ acting at the centroid (midpoint) of the loaded region.

**Proof:**

Consider an elementary strip of length $dx$ at distance $x$ from the left end of the loaded zone. The force on this strip is:

$$dF = w \cdot dx$$

The total force is obtained by integrating over the loaded length:

$$W = \int_{0}^{L} dF = \int_{0}^{L} w \, dx = w \cdot L$$

To find the centroid, equate the moment of the equivalent load about the left end to the moment of the distributed load:

$$W \cdot \bar{x} = \int_{0}^{L} x \cdot dF = \int_{0}^{L} x \cdot w \, dx$$

$$w L \cdot \bar{x} = w \cdot \int_{0}^{L} x \, dx = w \cdot \left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{L} = w \cdot \frac{L^2}{2}$$

$$\bar{x} = \frac{L^2}{2L} = \frac{L}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{x} = \frac{L}{2} \quad \text{and} \quad W_{eq} = wL}$$

> **Logic Step:** The *first integral* of an elemental force gives the *total force*; the *first moment* of the elemental force about a reference gives the *centroid location*. This is the same principle used in finding the centroid of any 2D area.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Equivalent Resultant of a Triangular UVL

**Statement:** A triangular load varying from $0$ at one end to $w_0$ at the other end over a length $L$ is equivalent to a point load $W = \tfrac{1}{2} w_0 L$ acting at $L/3$ from the *maximum-intensity* end.

**Proof:**

Let $x$ be measured from the **zero-intensity** end. The load intensity at position $x$ is:

$$w(x) = \frac{w_0}{L} \cdot x$$

The force on an elementary strip $dx$:

$$dF = w(x) \cdot dx = \frac{w_0}{L} x \, dx$$

Total force:

$$W = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{w_0}{L} x \, dx = \frac{w_0}{L} \cdot \frac{L^2}{2} = \frac{w_0 L}{2}$$

Centroid (taking moment about the zero-intensity end):

$$W \cdot \bar{x} = \int_{0}^{L} x \cdot dF = \int_{0}^{L} x \cdot \frac{w_0}{L} x \, dx = \frac{w_0}{L} \int_{0}^{L} x^2 \, dx$$

$$W \cdot \bar{x} = \frac{w_0}{L} \cdot \frac{L^3}{3} = \frac{w_0 L^2}{3}$$

Substituting $W = \frac{w_0 L}{2}$:

$$\frac{w_0 L}{2} \cdot \bar{x} = \frac{w_0 L^2}{3}$$

$$\bar{x} = \frac{2L}{3} \quad \text{(from the zero-intensity end)}$$

Therefore, the distance from the **maximum-intensity** end is:

$$\bar{x}' = L - \frac{2L}{3} = \frac{L}{3}$$

$$\boxed{W_{eq} = \frac{w_0 L}{2}, \quad \text{located at } \frac{L}{3} \text{ from the maximum-intensity end}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalent Resultant of a Trapezoidal Load

A trapezoidal load with intensities $w_1$ (left) and $w_2$ (right) over length $L$ can be split into:
- **Component A:** UDL of intensity $w_1$ over length $L$
- **Component B:** Triangular load of height $(w_2 - w_1)$ over length $L$

**Resultant of A:**

$$W_A = w_1 \cdot L, \quad x_A = \frac{L}{2}$$

**Resultant of B (triangular, max $(w_2 - w_1)$ at the right end):**

$$W_B = \frac{1}{2}(w_2 - w_1) \cdot L, \quad x_B = \frac{2L}{3} \text{ from the left end}$$

**Total Resultant:**

$$W = W_A + W_B = w_1 L + \frac{1}{2}(w_2 - w_1)L = \frac{L}{2}(w_1 + w_2)$$

**Centroid (from left end):**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{W_A \cdot x_A + W_B \cdot x_B}{W_A + W_B}$$

$$\bar{x} = \frac{w_1 L \cdot \frac{L}{2} + \frac{1}{2}(w_2 - w_1)L \cdot \frac{2L}{3}}{\frac{L}{2}(w_1 + w_2)}$$

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\frac{w_1 L^2}{2} + \frac{(w_2 - w_1)L^2}{3}}{\frac{L}{2}(w_1 + w_2)}$$

Taking $L^2$ common in the numerator:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} w_1 + \frac{1}{3}(w_2 - w_1)}{\frac{1}{2}(w_1 + w_2)} \cdot L$$

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\frac{3w_1 + 2(w_2 - w_1)}{6}}{\frac{w_1 + w_2}{2}} \cdot L = \frac{3w_1 + 2w_2 - 2w_1}{3(w_1 + w_2)} \cdot L$$

$$\boxed{\bar{x} = \frac{L(w_1 + 2w_2)}{3(w_1 + w_2)} \quad \text{(from the } w_1 \text{ end)}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Effect on SF and BM at the Point of Application

| Load Type | Effect on Shear Force $V$ at load point | Effect on Bending Moment $M$ at load point |
|:---------:|:----------------------------------------|:-------------------------------------------|
| Point Load $P$ | Sudden jump of magnitude $P$ | Slope (kink) changes by $P$ |
| UDL $w$ over $dx$ | Continuous linear change | Continuous parabolic (curvature $\neq 0$) |
| Triangular UVL | Continuous (slope of V is linear) | Cubic variation of $M$ |
| Couple $M_0$ | **No effect on V** | Sudden jump of magnitude $M_0$ |
| Impact $P \times I_F$ | Same as point load of magnitude $P \cdot I_F$ | Same as point load |

> **Reading the Table:** The SF diagram of a UDL is a **straight line** (linear), while its BM diagram is a **second-degree parabola**. For a triangular UVL, SF is linear with a non-zero starting value, and BM is **cubic** (curved twice).

## 3.5 Worked Example: Combined Loading on a Simply Supported Beam

**Problem:** A simply supported beam of span $6 \, \text{m}$ carries:
- A point load of $20 \, \text{kN}$ at $2 \, \text{m}$ from the left support
- A UDL of $10 \, \text{kN/m}$ from $x = 2 \, \text{m}$ to $x = 5 \, \text{m}$
- A triangular load from $x = 5 \, \text{m}$ to $x = 6 \, \text{m}$ with maximum intensity $12 \, \text{kN/m}$ at $x = 6 \, \text{m}$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Convert distributed loads to equivalent point loads:**

$$W_{UDL} = 10 \times (5 - 2) = 30 \, \text{kN}, \quad \text{at} \; x = \frac{2 + 5}{2} = 3.5 \, \text{m}$$

$$W_{UVL} = \frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times 1 = 6 \, \text{kN}, \quad \text{at} \; x = 5 + \frac{1}{3} = 5.333 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Find reactions** (using moment about A):

$$R_B \times 6 = 20 \times 2 + 30 \times 3.5 + 6 \times 5.333$$

$$R_B \times 6 = 40 + 105 + 32 = 177$$

$$R_B = 29.5 \, \text{kN}, \quad R_A = 20 + 30 + 6 - 29.5 = 26.5 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Maximum BM at point of concentrated load ($x = 2$ m):**

$$M_{max} = R_A \times 2 = 26.5 \times 2 = 53 \, \text{kN¬∑m}$$

> **Verification:** The reactions sum to $R_A + R_B = 26.5 + 29.5 = 56 \, \text{kN}$, which equals the total applied load of $20 + 30 + 6 = 56 \, \text{kN}$. ‚úď
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 ‚ÄĒ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow ‚ÄĒ Loading Types Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Loading on Beams] --> B[Concentrated Load P]
    A --> C[Distributed Load]
    A --> D[Couple Moment M0]
    A --> E[Impact Load]
    A --> F[Axial Load]

    C --> C1[UDL Intensity w]
    C --> C2[Triangular UVL]
    C --> C3[Trapezoidal UVL]

    B --> B1[Resultant W = P]
    B1 --> B2[Acts at point of application]

    C1 --> C1a[Resultant W = wL]
    C1a --> C1b[Centroid at L by 2]

    C2 --> C2a[Resultant W = 0.5 w0 L]
    C2a --> C2b[Centroid at L by 3 from max end]

    C3 --> C3a[Split into UDL plus Triangular]
    C3a --> C3b[Superpose resultants]

    D --> D1[No shear effect]
    D --> D2[BM jumps by M0]

    E --> E1[Multiply static by Impact Factor]
    E --> E2[Treat as equivalent static]

    F --> F1[Produces axial stress only]
    F --> F2[No bending if centric]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic ‚ÄĒ Typical SF and BM Diagrams for Each Load Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Simply Supported Beam with UDL] --> S1[SF Diagram Linear Slope]
    L1 --> M1[BM Diagram Parabolic]

    L2[Simply Supported Beam with Point Load] --> S2[SF Diagram Rectangular Jump]
    L2 --> M2[BM Diagram Triangular Peak]

    L3[Simply Supported Beam with Triangular UVL] --> S3[SF Diagram Curved]
    L3 --> M3[BM Diagram Cubic Curve]

    L4[Beam with Applied Couple at Midspan] --> S4[SF Diagram Unchanged]
    L4 --> M4[BM Diagram Sudden Jump]
```

## 4.3 ASCII Schematic ‚ÄĒ Trapezoidal Load Decomposition

```
   w2 |--------\
      |         \         <-- Triangle: height (w2 - w1), base L
   w1 |__________\___    
      |              |   <-- Rectangle: height w1, base L
      |______________|
      0              L

   Decomposition:
   Part A (UDL) : Total = w1 * L          at L/2
   Part B (Tri) : Total = 0.5(w2-w1)*L    at 2L/3 from the left
   Combined     : Total = 0.5* L * (w1 + w2) at L*(w1+2w2) / 3(w1+w2)
```

## 4.4 Visual Mapping ‚ÄĒ How Loads Transform SF/BM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Applied Load] --> L1[Equivalent Point Load]
    L1 --> L2[Reactions at Supports]
    L2 --> L3[Shear Force Diagram]
    L2 --> L4[Bending Moment Diagram]

    L3 --> R1[Shape Depends on Load Type]
    L4 --> R2[Peak BM at Critical Section]

    R1 --> R1a[UDL gives Linear SF]
    R1 --> R1b[Point Load gives Step SF]
    R1 --> R1c[UVL gives Curved SF]

    R2 --> R2a[UDL gives Parabolic BM]
    R2 --> R2b[Point Load gives Triangular BM]
    R2 --> R2c[UVL gives Cubic BM]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Takeaway:** Engineers use these load equivalences to perform **quick hand calculations** before detailed computer-aided analysis. They are also the basis of all structural design codes (IS 875, Eurocode, ACI) where complex loads are idealized as combinations of UDL, point loads, and UVLs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 ‚ÄĒ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024, Series-B]**
*Define a uniformly distributed load. A beam of length $8 \, \text{m}$ carries a UDL of $6 \, \text{kN/m}$ over its entire span. Find the magnitude of the equivalent point load and its location.*

**Model Answer:**

A **uniformly distributed load (UDL)** is a load whose intensity remains **constant per unit length** along the loaded portion of a beam. It is measured in $\text{N/m}$ or $\text{kN/m}$.

Given: $w = 6 \, \text{kN/m}$, $L = 8 \, \text{m}$.

$$W_{eq} = w \times L = 6 \times 8 = 48 \, \text{kN}$$

The equivalent point load acts at the **centroid** of the UDL, which is at the **mid-span**:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{L}{2} = \frac{8}{2} = 4 \, \text{m from either support}$$

**Result:** $W_{eq} = 48 \, \text{kN}$ acting at $4 \, \text{m}$ from the left support. **(3 Marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between a uniformly distributed load (UDL) and a uniformly varying load (UVL) with neat sketches.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | UDL | UVL |
|:---------:|:---:|:---:|
| **Definition** | Load of constant intensity per unit length | Load whose intensity varies linearly with length |
| **Intensity profile** | Rectangle (constant) | Triangle or Trapezoid (linearly varying) |
| **Mathematical form** | $w(x) = \text{constant}$ | $w(x) = m \cdot x + c$ |
| **Resultant magnitude** | $W = w L$ | $W = \tfrac{1}{2} w_0 L$ (triangular) |
| **Centroid location** | At $L/2$ | At $L/3$ from the max end (triangular) |
| **Example** | Self-weight of a flat slab | Earth pressure on a retaining wall |

**Sketches:**

```
   UDL                       Triangular UVL
   |  w  |                          |\
   |     |                          | \
   |_____|                          |__\
   0     L                          0    L
```

**(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2024, Module 2]**

A simply supported beam $AB$ of span $8 \, \text{m}$ is loaded as follows:
- A point load of $30 \, \text{kN}$ at $2 \, \text{m}$ from support $A$
- A UDL of $12 \, \text{kN/m}$ from $x = 2 \, \text{m}$ to $x = 6 \, \text{m}$
- A triangular load varying from $0$ at $x = 6 \, \text{m}$ to $20 \, \text{kN/m}$ at support $B$ ($x = 8 \, \text{m}$)

**Find:**
- **(a)** [7 Marks] The equivalent point loads and their locations for the UDL and UVL.
- **(b)** [7 Marks] The reactions at supports $A$ and $B$.

---

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

**For the UDL (intensity $w = 12 \, \text{kN/m}$ over $4 \, \text{m}$, from $x = 2$ to $x = 6$):**

$$W_{UDL} = w \times L_{UDL} = 12 \times 4 = 48 \, \text{kN}$$

Location (centroid of the rectangular region):

$$\bar{x}_{UDL} = \frac{2 + 6}{2} = 4 \, \text{m from A}$$

**For the Triangular UVL (max intensity $w_0 = 20 \, \text{kN/m}$ at $x = 8 \, \text{m}$, zero at $x = 6 \, \text{m}$, length $L = 2 \, \text{m}$):**

$$W_{UVL} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times w_0 \times L = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 20 \times 2 = 20 \, \text{kN}$$

Location (centroid at $L/3 = 2/3 \, \text{m}$ from the maximum-intensity end $B$):

$$\bar{x}_{UVL} = 8 - \tfrac{2}{3} = 7.333 \, \text{m from A}$$

**(7 Marks Distribution:** Resultant magnitudes ‚ÄĒ 3 marks, Centroid locations ‚ÄĒ 4 marks)

---

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

**Free Body Diagram after equivalent load conversion:**

```
   A           2m              6m        7.333m  B
   ^                                       
   |---------|-------------|------|--------|
   Ra                                               
   |  30 kN  |  48 kN (UDL) |    20 kN(UVL)|
   |   at 2m |    at 4m     |    at 7.333m |
```

**Total downward load:**

$$\Sigma F_{down} = 30 + 48 + 20 = 98 \, \text{kN}$$

**Taking moments about $A$** ($\Sigma M_A = 0$):

$$R_B \times 8 = 30 \times 2 + 48 \times 4 + 20 \times 7.333$$

$$R_B \times 8 = 60 + 192 + 146.66 = 398.66$$

$$R_B = \frac{398.66}{8} = 49.83 \, \text{kN}$$

**Vertical equilibrium** ($\Sigma F_y = 0$):

$$R_A + R_B = 98 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_A = 98 - 49.83 = 48.17 \, \text{kN}$$

**Verification:** $\Sigma M_B = R_A \times 8 - 30 \times 6 - 48 \times 4 - 20 \times 0.667 = 385.36 - 180 - 192 - 13.34 \approx 0$ ‚úď

**(7 Marks Distribution:** Moment equation setup ‚ÄĒ 3 marks, Reaction calculations ‚ÄĒ 3 marks, Verification ‚ÄĒ 1 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023 Alternate Pattern]**

A cantilever beam of length $5 \, \text{m}$, fixed at $A$ and free at $B$, carries the following loads:
- A UDL of $8 \, \text{kN/m}$ over the entire span
- A trapezoidal load from $x = 1 \, \text{m}$ to $x = 3 \, \text{m}$, with intensity $4 \, \text{kN/m}$ at $x = 1 \, \text{m}$ and $10 \, \text{kN/m}$ at $x = 3 \, \text{m}$

**Find:**
- **(a)** [7 Marks] The total equivalent point load and its location from the fixed end.
- **(b)** [7 Marks] The fixed-end reactions (vertical force and moment) at support $A$.

---

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

**For the UDL (full span, $w = 8 \, \text{kN/m}$, $L = 5 \, \text{m}$):**

$$W_1 = 8 \times 5 = 40 \, \text{kN}, \quad \text{at} \; \bar{x}_1 = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5 \, \text{m from A}$$

**For the Trapezoidal Load** (split into rectangle + triangle):

**Rectangle** (intensity $4 \, \text{kN/m}$, length $2 \, \text{m}$):

$$W_{2a} = 4 \times 2 = 8 \, \text{kN}, \quad \text{at} \; \bar{x}_{2a} = \frac{1 + 3}{2} = 2.0 \, \text{m from A}$$

**Triangle** (height $= 10 - 4 = 6 \, \text{kN/m}$, length $2 \, \text{m}$, max at $x = 3 \, \text{m}$):

$$W_{2b} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 2 = 6 \, \text{kN}, \quad \text{at} \; \bar{x}_{2b} = 1 + \tfrac{2 \times 2}{3} = 2.333 \, \text{m from A}$$

*Note:* The centroid of the triangle is at $L/3$ from the max-intensity end, so distance from $x = 1$ m is $L - L/3 = 2L/3 = 4/3 \, \text{m}$, giving $1 + 4/3 = 7/3 = 2.333 \, \text{m}$. ‚úď

**Total Equivalent Load:**

$$W_{eq} = W_1 + W_{2a} + W_{2b} = 40 + 8 + 6 = 54 \, \text{kN}$$

**Centroid of total load from A:**

$$\bar{x}_{eq} = \frac{W_1 \cdot \bar{x}_1 + W_{2a} \cdot \bar{x}_{2a} + W_{2b} \cdot \bar{x}_{2b}}{W_{eq}}$$

$$\bar{x}_{eq} = \frac{40 \times 2.5 + 8 \times 2.0 + 6 \times 2.333}{54}$$

$$\bar{x}_{eq} = \frac{100 + 16 + 14}{54} = \frac{130}{54} = 2.407 \, \text{m from A}$$

**(7 Marks Distribution:** UDL conversion ‚ÄĒ 2 marks, Trapezoid split ‚ÄĒ 3 marks, Final centroid ‚ÄĒ 2 marks)

---

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

For a cantilever fixed at $A$ and free at $B$, the **fixed-end reactions** are:

**Vertical Reaction at A (upward):**

$$V_A = W_{eq} = 54 \, \text{kN (upward)}$$

**Fixed-End Moment at A (resisting the bending caused by downward load):**

$$M_A = W_1 \cdot \bar{x}_1 + W_{2a} \cdot \bar{x}_{2a} + W_{2b} \cdot \bar{x}_{2b} = 130 \, \text{kN¬∑m}$$

The fixed-end moment acts **counter-clockwise** to resist the clockwise rotation tendency of the applied loads.

$$\boxed{V_A = 54 \, \text{kN} \uparrow, \quad M_A = 130 \, \text{kN¬∑m} \; (\text{counter-clockwise})}$$

**(7 Marks Distribution:** Vertical reaction ‚ÄĒ 2 marks, Moment equation setup ‚ÄĒ 3 marks, Final answer with direction ‚ÄĒ 2 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ‚ÄĒ Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Triangular centroid at $L/2$ instead of $L/3$** ‚ÄĒ loses 1 to 2 marks immediately. The triangle centroid is **always** at $L/3$ from the larger side.
> 2. **Forgetting to decompose the trapezoidal load** into rectangle + triangle ‚ÄĒ directly using a wrong formula loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Not stating units** in the final answer ‚ÄĒ strict KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 mark for "$\text{kN}$" or "$\text{kN¬∑m}$" missing.
> 4. **Sign convention errors** for fixed-end moment direction in cantilevers ‚ÄĒ examiners expect a clear diagram with the moment direction marked.
> 5. **Skipping the verification step** (sum of forces or sum of moments) ‚ÄĒ losing 1 mark that students often take for granted.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **Point Load $P$** ‚Üí Resultant $= P$, location = point of application. Causes **step jump** in SF, **kink** in BM.
- **UDL $w$** over length $L$ ‚Üí $W = wL$, location $= L/2$. SF = **linear**, BM = **parabolic** ($2^{\circ}$ curve).
- **Triangular UVL** (max $w_0$ at one end) ‚Üí $W = \tfrac{1}{2} w_0 L$, location $= L/3$ from the **max end**.
- **Trapezoidal Load** ‚Üí Always **split** into rectangle + triangle, then superpose.
- **Couple $M_0$** ‚Üí Has **no effect on SF** but causes an **instantaneous jump of $M_0$** in BM.
- **Impact Load** ‚Üí Multiply static load by **Impact Factor** (1.2 to 1.5 for lifts).
- **Equivalent Point Load** concept is valid for finding reactions and global equilibrium, but **NOT** for local stress calculations near the actual distributed region.
- **Unit consistency** is non-negotiable: $\text{kN/m} \times \text{m} = \text{kN}$ for force, $\text{kN} \times \text{m} = \text{kN¬∑m}$ for moment.
- **Always draw a free-body diagram** before calculating ‚ÄĒ KTU examiners explicitly look for it and award 1 to 2 marks for it.
- **Verify your answer** using $\Sigma F_y = 0$ and $\Sigma M = 0$ ‚ÄĒ 1 mark is reserved for this check.
- The **three most-tested concepts** for KTU ESE Module 2 are: (i) Centroid of triangular UVL, (ii) Trapezoidal decomposition, (iii) Reaction calculations for combined loads on simply supported beams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Bending Moment and Shear Force
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of Bending Moment and Shear Force

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A beam is a structural member primarily designed to resist **transverse loads** acting perpendicular to its longitudinal axis. When such loads are applied, two internal reaction components are developed at any cross-section of the beam:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shear Force (V or SF)** at a section is the **algebraic sum of the vertical (transverse) forces** acting on either side of the section.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bending Moment (M or BM)** at a section is the **algebraic sum of the moments** of all the forces (about the section) acting on either side of the section.

Both quantities are **internal resultant forces** and are essential for the design of beams against **bending stresses** and **shear stresses** as per KTU Module 2 (Strength of Materials) outcomes.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a wooden ruler (the beam) horizontally with both hands and placing a small weight (a load) in the middle:

- Your hands must push **upward** to keep the ruler from falling. This upward push at the support, and the downward load in the middle, create a tendency for the ruler to **slide vertically** at any imaginary cut. This sliding tendency is the **Shear Force**.

- At the same time, the middle of the ruler sags downward (bends into a smile-shape). The fibers at the **bottom** of the ruler are stretched (tension), and the fibers at the **top** are squeezed (compression). This curling/bending tendency is the **Bending Moment**.

> [!NOTE]
> Think of **Shear Force** as the "sliding force" and **Bending Moment** as the "bending tendency" at any cross-section. They are internal reactions resisting the external loads.

The standard SI units are:
- **Shear Force (V):** **kN** (or N)
- **Bending Moment (M):** **kN¬∑m** (or N¬∑mm)

---

### 1.3 Standard Sign Convention (IS 800 / Standard KTU Convention)

A **positive sign convention** is universally adopted so that engineers across the world interpret diagrams identically.

| Quantity | Positive Convention | Resulting Deformation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Shear Force (V)** | Left portion pushed **UP** OR Right portion pushed **DOWN** | Clockwise rotation of the element |
| **Bending Moment (M)** | Causes the beam to **SAG** (smile-shape, concave upward) | Compression on **TOP** fiber, Tension on **BOTTOM** fiber |

> [!NOTE]
> A **negative bending moment** is called a **hogging moment** (frown-shape, concave downward), producing tension on the top fiber and compression on the bottom.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Behind the Sign Convention

The KTU sign convention is consistent with the **right-hand rule** applied to a small beam element of length $dx$:

1. **Positive Shear:** Acts **downward** on the right face and **upward** on the left face of the element. This tends to rotate the element **clockwise**.
2. **Positive Moment:** Acts **counter-clockwise** on the right face and **clockwise** on the left face. This bends the element into a **U-shape (sagging)**, which is the natural shape of a simply supported beam under gravity loads.

### 2.2 The Fundamental Differential Relationships

Consider an infinitesimal beam element of length $dx$ subjected to a distributed load $w(x)$ (positive upward, per KTU convention in some texts; the standard KTU convention uses $w$ positive **downward**). The internal shear and moment vary as $V$ and $M$ on the left face, and $V + dV$ and $M + dM$ on the right face.

Equilibrium gives the **three most important equations in beam analysis**:

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$$

$$\frac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w(x)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These three relationships are the **backbone** of shear force and bending moment diagram (SFD & BMD) problems. KTU examiners frequently ask derivations or applications of these.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Used For |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V = \sum F_{\text{left}} \downarrow$ (or upward on right) | Net vertical force on left segment | Computing shear at a section |
| 2 | $M = \sum M_{\text{section, left}}$ | Net moment of left-side forces about section | Computing moment at a section |
| 3 | $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$ | Slope of SFD equals negative of load intensity | Drawing SFD shapes |
| 4 | $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$ | Slope of BMD equals the shear force | Drawing BMD shapes |
| 5 | $\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w$ | Curvature of BMD relates to load | Identifying points of inflection |
| 6 | $\Delta V = -\int w \, dx$ | Sudden change in V due to a point load | Step changes in SFD |
| 7 | $\Delta M = \int V \, dx$ | Area under the SFD between two points | Computing change in M |
| 8 | $R_A + R_B = \sum \text{downward loads}$ | Global vertical equilibrium | Finding reactions |
| 9 | $\sum M_A = 0$ | Moment equilibrium about a support | Finding reactions |
| 10 | For point load $P$: $V$ jumps by $P$ at load | Vertical step in SFD | Locating jump points |
| 11 | For couple $M_0$: $M$ jumps by $M_0$ at couple | Vertical step in BMD | Locating jump points |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Design of building beams, bridge girders, slabs, and lintels.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Design of machine shafts, crane jibs, and leaf springs.
- **Aerospace:** Sizing of wing spars and fuselage frames.
- **Shipbuilding:** Hull girder strength analysis.

The **Bending Moment Diagram (BMD)** identifies the section of **maximum bending stress** (the location where $M$ is maximum), which dictates the required cross-section size (Section Modulus $Z$):

$$\sigma_{\text{bending}} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z}$$

The **Shear Force Diagram (SFD)** identifies the section of **maximum shear stress**, which dictates web thickness in I-beams:

$$\tau_{\text{max}} = \frac{V_{\max} \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Three Fundamental Relationships
