**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Bending Moment at Section $x$ from Left Support (where $0 \le x \le L/2$):**

$$M_x = R_A \cdot x = \frac{W}{2} \cdot x = \frac{W\,x}{2}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Section Modulus:**

$$Z_x = \frac{b \cdot d_x^{\,2}}{6}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Uniform Strength Condition:**

$$\sigma = \frac{M_x}{Z_x} = \frac{W\,x/2}{b\,d_x^{\,2}/6} = \frac{3\,W\,x}{b\,d_x^{\,2}}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Solve for $d_x$:**

$$d_x^{\,2} = \frac{3\,W\,x}{b\,\sigma}$$

$$\boxed{\,d_x = \sqrt{\frac{3\,W\,x}{b\,\sigma}}\,}$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Profile Interpretation:**
Two **parabolic segments** meeting at the centre.

- At supports ($x = 0$): $d = 0$
- At centre ($x = L/2$): $d_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3\,W\,L}{2\,b\,\sigma}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetry Note (KTU Favourite)**
> For the right half ($L/2 \le x \le L$), the BM expression is $M_x = \dfrac{W}{2}(L - x)$. Substituting gives a mirror-image parabolic profile ‚ÄĒ the **full beam profile is two parabolic halves**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ‚ÄĒ Simply Supported Beam with UDL (Rectangular Section, Constant Width $b$)

**Given:** Simply supported beam of span $L$, UDL $w$ per unit length over the entire span.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Reactions:**

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{w\,L}{2}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Bending Moment at Section $x$ from Left Support:**

$$M_x = R_A \cdot x - \frac{w\,x^{2}}{2} = \frac{w\,L\,x}{2} - \frac{w\,x^{2}}{2} = \frac{w}{2}\left(L\,x - x^{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Uniform Strength Condition:**

$$\sigma = \frac{M_x}{Z_x} = \frac{\dfrac{w}{2}\left(L\,x - x^{2}\right)}{\dfrac{b\,d_x^{\,2}}{6}} = \frac{3\,w\left(L\,x - x^{2}\right)}{b\,d_x^{\,2}}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Solve for $d_x$:**

$$d_x^{\,2} = \frac{3\,w\left(L\,x - x^{2}\right)}{b\,\sigma}$$

$$\boxed{\,d_x = \sqrt{\frac{3\,w\left(L\,x - x^{2}\right)}{b\,\sigma}}\,}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Profile Interpretation:**
The expression $L\,x - x^{2}$ is the equation of a **parabola**, so the depth profile is also parabolic ‚ÄĒ the **overall shape of the beam is elliptical**.

- At supports ($x = 0$ or $x = L$): $d = 0$
- At centre ($x = L/2$): $d_{\max} = \dfrac{L}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{3\,w}{b\,\sigma}}$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 ‚ÄĒ Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load (Circular Cross-Section)

**Given:** Simply supported beam of span $L$, point load $W$ at centre, circular cross-section of varying diameter $d_x$.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Bending Moment at Section $x$ from Support (for $0 \le x \le L/2$):**

$$M_x = \frac{W}{2} \cdot x = \frac{W\,x}{2}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Section Modulus for Circular Section:**

$$Z_x = \frac{\pi\,d_x^{\,3}}{32}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Uniform Strength Condition:**

$$\sigma = \frac{M_x}{Z_x} = \frac{W\,x/2}{\pi\,d_x^{\,3}/32} = \frac{16\,W\,x}{\pi\,d_x^{\,3}}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Solve for $d_x$:**

$$d_x^{\,3} = \frac{16\,W\,x}{\pi\,\sigma}$$

$$\boxed{\,d_x = \sqrt[3]{\frac{16\,W\,x}{\pi\,\sigma}}\,}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Profile Interpretation:**
This is a **cubic parabolic** profile.

- At supports ($x = 0$): $d = 0$
- At centre ($x = L/2$): $d_{\max} = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{8\,W\,L}{\pi\,\sigma}}$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Design Methodology Flowchart (KTU Problem-Solving Framework)

The following Mermaid flowchart encapsulates the **canonical KTU approach** to designing a beam of uniform strength. This is the same logical sequence a Board examiner expects to see in your answer script.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1([Beam Design Problem Statement]) --> s2[Step 1: Identify Support and Loading Type]
    s2 --> s3{Beam Configuration}
    s3 -->|Cantilever| s4[Load at Free End or UDL]
    s3 -->|Simply Supported| s5[Central Load or UDL]
    s4 --> s6[Step 2: Establish Bending Moment Mx]
    s5 --> s6
    s6 --> s7[Step 3: Apply Uniform Strength Condition]
    s7 --> s8["Mx divided by Zx equals sigma_max constant"]
    s8 --> s9[Step 4: Select Cross Section Geometry]
    s9 --> s10{Rectangular or Circular}
    s10 -->|Rectangular| s11["Zx equals b dx squared over 6"]
    s10 -->|Circular| s12["Zx equals pi dx cubed over 32"]
    s11 --> s13[Step 5: Solve Algebraically for dx]
    s12 --> s13
    s13 --> s14[Step 6: Evaluate at Critical Sections]
    s14 --> s15[Step 7: Apply Practical Minimum Depth at Supports]
    s15 --> e1([Final Beam Profile and Dimensions])
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Uniform Strength Concept

The schematic below shows the **interactions between the physical loads, internal moment, geometric profile, and stress state** that drive the design of a uniform strength beam.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1[External Loads W and w] --> a2[Reactions R_A and R_B]
    a2 --> a3[Bending Moment Field Mx varies with x]
    a3 --> a4[Section Modulus Demand Zx proportional to Mx]
    a4 --> a5[Geometric Profile dx and bx]
    a5 --> a6[Uniform Stress sigma_max constant]
    a6 -.Feedback.-> a5
    a6 --> a7[Verification against Shear Stress]
    a7 --> a8[Final Design Profile]
```

## 4.3 Typical Beam Profile Summary (Tabular Schematic)

| Configuration | Visual Profile Shape | Mathematical Character |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Cantilever + Point Load | Parabolic taper | $d \propto \sqrt{x}$ |
| Cantilever + UDL | Straight taper (triangular) | $d \propto x$ |
| SS Beam + Central Point Load | Two parabolic halves (lens) | $d \propto \sqrt{x}$ each half |
| SS Beam + UDL | Elliptical | $d \propto \sqrt{Lx - x^{2}}$ |
| SS Beam + Central Point Load (Circular) | Cubic parabolic | $d \propto x^{1/3}$ |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Snapshot** ‚ÄĒ Part A: 2 √ó 3 = 6 marks, Part B: 1 √ó 14 = 14 marks (with internal choice). Module 3 typically contributes one full 14-mark question in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]`
**[3 Marks | CO3 | Bloom Level: Understand]**
**Define a beam of uniform strength. State the fundamental mathematical condition that must be satisfied at every cross-section of such a beam.**

**Model Answer:**
A beam of uniform strength is a beam whose cross-sectional dimensions are **varied along the length** such that the **maximum bending stress at every section is the same** and equal to the allowable working stress of the material.

**Governing Condition:**

$$\frac{M_x}{Z_x} = \sigma_{\max} = \text{constant}$$

where $M_x$ is the bending moment at a section located at distance $x$, $Z_x$ is the section modulus at that section, and $\sigma_{\max}$ is the maximum allowable bending stress. The condition ensures that no part of the beam is over-stressed or under-stressed, leading to an **economical and safe design**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024]`
**[3 Marks | CO3 | Bloom Level: Understand]**
**For a cantilever of uniform strength with rectangular cross-section of constant width $b$, write the equation for the depth $d_x$ at a section $x$ from the free end for the following loadings:**
**(i) A point load $W$ at the free end.**
**(ii) A uniformly distributed load $w$ per unit length over the entire span.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Point Load $W$ at Free End:**

$$d_x = \sqrt{\frac{6\,W\,x}{b\,\sigma}}$$

The depth varies **parabolically**, being maximum at the fixed end and zero (theoretically) at the free end.

**(ii) Uniformly Distributed Load $w$:**

$$d_x = x\,\sqrt{\frac{3\,w}{b\,\sigma}}$$

The depth varies **linearly** with $x$, producing a **triangular profile** with maximum depth at the fixed end.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme)** ‚ÄĒ Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as Part (a) = 7 marks and Part (b) = 7 marks. You must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023 | Model Paper]`
**[14 Marks | CO3 | Bloom Level: Apply]**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Derive the expression for the depth of a beam of uniform strength for a cantilever of length $L$ carrying a point load $W$ at the free end. The beam has a rectangular cross-section of constant width $b$. Sketch the resulting profile.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Bending Moment:** At section $x$ from free end, $M_x = W \cdot x$.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Section Modulus:** $Z_x = \dfrac{b \cdot d_x^{\,2}}{6}$.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Uniform Strength:** $\sigma = \dfrac{M_x}{Z_x} = \dfrac{6\,W\,x}{b\,d_x^{\,2}}$.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Solving for $d_x$:** $\quad d_x^{\,2} = \dfrac{6\,W\,x}{b\,\sigma}$

$$\boxed{\,d_x = \sqrt{\frac{6\,W\,x}{b\,\sigma}}\,}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Profile:** Parabolic ‚ÄĒ maximum depth at fixed end: $d_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{6\,W\,L}{b\,\sigma}}$, zero at free end.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**A cantilever of length 2 m carries a point load of 20 kN at the free end. The beam is of uniform strength with rectangular cross-section of width 100 mm. Determine the depth at the fixed end and at 1 m from the free end. Take $\sigma = 80$ N/mm¬≤.**

**Given Data:**
$L = 2000$ mm, $W = 20\,000$ N, $b = 100$ mm, $\sigma = 80$ N/mm¬≤

**Formula:** $d_x = \sqrt{\dfrac{6\,W\,x}{b\,\sigma}}$

**At Fixed End ($x = L = 2000$ mm):**

$$d_{\text{fixed}} = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 20\,000 \times 2000}{100 \times 80}} = \sqrt{\frac{240 \times 10^{6}}{8000}} = \sqrt{30\,000}$$

$$\boxed{\,d_{\text{fixed}} = 173.21 \text{ mm}\,}$$

**At 1 m from Free End ($x = 1000$ mm):**

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 20\,000 \times 1000}{100 \times 80}} = \sqrt{\frac{120 \times 10^{6}}{8000}} = \sqrt{15\,000}$$

$$\boxed{\,d = 122.47 \text{ mm}\,}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024 | Model Paper]`
**[14 Marks | CO3 | Bloom Level: Apply]**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**A simply supported beam of span 4 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 30 kN/m over the entire span. The beam is of uniform strength with rectangular cross-section of width 200 mm. Find the depth of the beam at the mid-span. Take allowable bending stress $\sigma = 100$ N/mm¬≤.**

**Given Data:**
$L = 4000$ mm, $w = 30$ N/mm, $b = 200$ mm, $\sigma = 100$ N/mm¬≤

**Formula (derived in Section 3.4):** $d_x = \sqrt{\dfrac{3\,w\,(L\,x - x^{2})}{b\,\sigma}}$

**At Mid-Span ($x = L/2 = 2000$ mm):**

$$L\,x - x^{2} = 4000 \times 2000 - (2000)^{2} = 8 \times 10^{6} - 4 \times 10^{6} = 4 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$d_{\text{mid}} = \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 30 \times 4 \times 10^{6}}{200 \times 100}} = \sqrt{\frac{360 \times 10^{6}}{20\,000}} = \sqrt{18\,000}$$

$$\boxed{\,d_{\text{mid}} = 134.16 \text{ mm}\,}$$

**Verification using $M_{\max} = wL^{2}/8$:**

$$M_{\max} = \frac{30 \times (4000)^{2}}{8} = 60 \times 10^{6} \text{ N¬∑mm}$$

$$d_{\text{mid}} = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 60 \times 10^{6}}{200 \times 100}} = \sqrt{18\,000} = 134.16 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark$$

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**A simply supported beam of span 5 m carries a concentrated load of 50 kN at the centre. The beam is of uniform strength with a circular cross-section. Derive the expression for the diameter at any section, and find the maximum diameter at the centre. Take allowable bending stress $\sigma = 120$ N/mm¬≤.**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Bending Moment:** $M_x = \dfrac{W}{2} \cdot x = 25\,000\,x$ N¬∑mm (for $0 \le x \le L/2$)

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Section Modulus (Circular):** $Z_x = \dfrac{\pi\,d_x^{\,3}}{32}$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Uniform Strength Condition:**

$$\sigma = \frac{M_x}{Z_x} = \frac{25\,000\,x \times 32}{\pi\,d_x^{\,3}} = \frac{800\,000\,x}{\pi\,d_x^{\,3}}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Solving for $d_x$:**

$$d_x^{\,3} = \frac{800\,000\,x}{\pi\,\sigma} = \frac{16\,W\,x}{\pi\,\sigma}$$

$$\boxed{\,d_x = \sqrt[3]{\frac{16\,W\,x}{\pi\,\sigma}}\,}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Maximum Diameter at Centre ($x = L/2 = 2500$ mm):**

$$d_{\max}^{\,3} = \frac{16 \times 50\,000 \times 2500}{\pi \times 120} = \frac{2 \times 10^{9}}{376.99} = 5.305 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{3}$$

$$d_{\max} = \sqrt[3]{5.305 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\boxed{\,d_{\max} = 174.55 \text{ mm}\,}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks (Critical Pitfalls)**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (‚Äď2 marks)** ‚ÄĒ Mixing kN¬∑m with N/mm¬≤ and mm. Convert **everything to N, mm, N/mm¬≤** before substitution. The KTU 2024 scheme is strict on SI consistency.
> 2. **Wrong Section Modulus (‚Äď2 to 3 marks)** ‚ÄĒ For circular sections, students frequently write $Z = \pi d^{2}/4$ (which is *area*, not section modulus). The correct formula is $Z = \pi d^{3}/32$.
> 3. **Confusion in Coordinate Reference (‚Äď1 mark)** ‚ÄĒ $x$ must be **explicitly defined**: from the free end for cantilevers and from the support for simply supported beams. Failure to state this loses a mark.
> 4. **Forgetting Practical Minimum Depth (‚Äď1 mark)** ‚ÄĒ When asked to "design", mention that the depth at the support is theoretically zero, but a **minimum practical depth** (e.g., 50‚Äď100 mm) must be provided to resist shear and prevent buckling.
> 5. **Skipping Intermediate Steps in Derivations (‚Äď2 marks)** ‚ÄĒ Never jump from $M_x$ directly to the final equation. The KTU Board Examiner awards step marks for showing the substitution of $Z_x$, the algebraic manipulation, and the final solving step.
> 6. **Sign of Bending Moment Ignored (‚Äď1 mark)** ‚ÄĒ The condition $M/Z = \sigma$ assumes **positive** (sagging) moment. For cantilevers, the moment is **negative** (hogging) ‚ÄĒ the magnitude is the same, and the equation still holds.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ Beams of Uniform Strength**

- **Core Condition:** $M_x / Z_x = \sigma_{\max} = \text{constant}$ for all sections.
- **Bending Moment Variations:**
  - Cantilever + Point Load: $M_x = Wx$ (linear in $x$)
  - Cantilever + UDL: $M_x = wx^{2}/2$ (parabolic in $x$)
  - SS + Central Point Load: $M_x = Wx/2$ for $0 \le x \le L/2$
  - SS + UDL: $M_x = (wL/2)x - wx^{2}/2$ (parabolic in $x$)
- **Section Modulus Memory Aids:**
  - Rectangle: $Z = bd^{2}/6$ ‚ÄĒ power of $d$ is **2**
  - Circle: $Z = \pi d^{3}/32$ ‚ÄĒ power of $d$ is **3**
- **Resulting Profiles (Memorise the Shapes):**
  - Cantilever + Point Load ‚Üí **Parabolic** (depth $\propto \sqrt{x}$)
  - Cantilever + UDL ‚Üí **Triangular/Linear** (depth $\propto x$)
  - SS + Central Point Load ‚Üí **Two parabolic halves** (lens shape)
  - SS + UDL ‚Üí **Elliptical** (depth $\propto \sqrt{Lx - x^{2}}$)
- **Key Numerical Check:** For SS beam with UDL, depth at centre = $\dfrac{L}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{3w}{b\sigma}}$.
- **Practical Caveat:** The depth at the supports (or free end) is theoretically **zero**, but a **minimum practical depth** is always provided to resist shear.
- **KTU 2024 Typical Question Types:** (a) Pure derivation, (b) Numerical with two evaluation points (e.g., "depth at fixed end and at 1 m from free end"), (c) Comparative design with rectangular vs circular section.
- **Conversion Mastery:** 1 kN = 10¬≥ N, 1 m = 10¬≥ mm, 1 N/mm¬≤ = 1 MPa. Get this right and half the battle is won.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strain Energy Due to Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Strain Energy Due to Bending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ūüďė SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain Energy Due to Bending** is the internal energy stored within a structural member as it deforms under the action of a **bending moment**. Mathematically, it represents the work done by the internal bending stresses as the beam undergoes curvature change from its initial straight (undeformed) configuration to the deflected configuration.

For a beam segment of length $dx$ subjected to a bending moment $M(x)$, the **infinitesimal strain energy** is:

$$dU = \frac{M^2}{2EI}\,dx$$

where:
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of Elasticity of the material (in **N/m¬≤** or **Pa**)
- $I$ = Second Moment of Area (Moment of Inertia) of the cross-section about the Neutral Axis (in **m‚Āī** or **mm‚Āī**)
- $M$ = Bending moment at the section (in **N¬∑m**)
- $U$ = Strain energy (in **N¬∑m** or **Joules**)

The total strain energy stored along the length $L$ of the beam is obtained by integration:

$$U = \int_0^L \frac{M^2}{2EI}\,dx$$

This expression is valid under the assumptions of **linear elastic behavior**, **small deflections**, and **Euler‚ÄďBernoulli beam theory** (plane sections remain plane and normal to the neutral axis after bending).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The Springy Diving Board

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a diver standing at the tip of a diving board.** As the diver applies load, the board bends. The fibres on the top of the board are compressed (storing compressive strain energy), while the fibres on the bottom are stretched (storing tensile strain energy). The fibres near the neutral axis store almost no energy. When the diver jumps off, the board rebounds ‚ÄĒ the stored bending strain energy is released as kinetic energy, making the board "snap back."

This simple everyday phenomenon is the physical manifestation of **bending strain energy**. The deeper the bend and the stiffer the material, the more energy is locked inside the board. In engineering practice, this is precisely the same mechanism that:
- Stores energy in **leaf springs** of vehicles,
- Allows **arch bridges** to absorb load through flexure, and
- Is exploited in **cantilever beams** of structural frames and aircraft wings.

## 1.3 Why Engineers Study This

- **Deflection Calculations**: Strain energy methods (especially **Castigliano's Theorem**) allow computation of unknown deflections without solving the differential equation of the elastic curve.
- **Impact Loading**: When loads are applied suddenly, the strain energy capacity of a beam governs its ability to resist shock.
- **Design for Resilience**: Knowing the energy a member can absorb elastically helps prevent brittle failure.
- **Structural Optimization**: Strain energy distributions guide material placement (e.g., I-beams, where more material is placed away from the neutral axis to maximize $I$).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Whenever a problem in Module 3 asks for "deflection by energy method" or "strain energy stored in a beam," the formula $U = \int M^2/(2EI)\,dx$ is your **primary starting point**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ūüďė SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principles

The derivation of bending strain energy rests on three foundational principles:

1. **Hooke's Law for Axial Strain of a Fibre**:  
   A fibre at distance $y$ from the neutral axis experiences strain $\varepsilon = y/\rho$, where $\rho$ is the radius of curvature of the deflected beam.

2. **Bending Stress Distribution (Flexure Formula)**:  
   $\sigma = \dfrac{My}{I}$ ‚ÄĒ this is linearly varying across the depth, zero at the neutral axis, and maximum at the extreme fibres.

3. **Work‚ÄďEnergy Equivalence in Elastic Deformation**:  
   For gradual, static loading, the external work done equals the internal strain energy stored: $W_{ext} = U_{int}$.

## 2.2 Structured Logic Steps

| Step | Concept | Outcome |
|:----:|:--------|:--------|
| 1 | Take an elemental slice of length $dx$ at section $x$ | Elemental volume isolated |
| 2 | Apply moment-curvature relation $\dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | Curvature linked to moment |
| 3 | Compute elemental change in slope $d\theta = \dfrac{dx}{\rho}$ | Slope change obtained |
| 4 | Work done by moment $M$ on this element $dU = \tfrac{1}{2}M\,d\theta$ | Elemental strain energy |
| 5 | Substitute and integrate over beam length | Total bending strain energy |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all standard formulas. **Memorize the boxed results** ‚ÄĒ they appear in nearly every KTU question on this topic.

| # | Beam / Loading Case | Bending Moment $M(x)$ | Strain Energy $U$ |
|:-:|:--------------------|:----------------------|:------------------|
| 1 | Pure bending (constant $M$) | $M$ | $U = \dfrac{M^2 L}{2EI}$ |
| 2 | Cantilever ‚ÄĒ point load $P$ at free end | $M = Px$ (from fixed end) | $U = \dfrac{P^2 L^3}{6EI}$ |
| 3 | Cantilever ‚ÄĒ UDL $w$ over span $L$ | $M = \dfrac{wx^2}{2}$ | $U = \dfrac{w^2 L^5}{40EI}$ |
| 4 | Simply supported ‚ÄĒ central point load $P$ | $M = \dfrac{Px}{2}$ for $0 \le x \le L/2$ | $U = \dfrac{P^2 L^3}{96EI}$ |
| 5 | Simply supported ‚ÄĒ UDL $w$ over span $L$ | $M = \dfrac{wx(L-x)}{2}$ | $U = \dfrac{w^2 L^5}{240EI}$ |
| 6 | Simply supported ‚ÄĒ moment $M_0$ at centre | $M = \dfrac{M_0 x}{L}$ | $U = \dfrac{M_0^2 L}{6EI}$ |
| 7 | Propped cantilever ‚ÄĒ UDL $w$ | $M = \dfrac{w}{2}\left(Lx - x^2 - \dfrac{L^2}{4}\right)$ | $U = \dfrac{w^2 L^5}{1280EI}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Consistency Rule:** For numerical answers in KTU papers, ensure that all quantities are in **SI units** (N, m, Pa) or consistently in **mm-N units** (N, mm, MPa). Mixing units is the single most common error in energy-method problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The bending strain energy concept is central to:

- **Aerospace structures**: Wing spars store bending strain energy during gust loading, redistributing stress smoothly.
- **Mechanical springs**: Helical and leaf springs are designed by maximizing the strain energy stored per unit volume of material.
- **Civil earthquake engineering**: Structures designed to absorb seismic energy through controlled flexure.
- **Automotive crashworthiness**: Vehicle frames deform plastically to absorb impact energy, but the elastic bending strain energy governs the pre-yield response.

## 2.5 Castigliano's Theorem ‚ÄĒ The Companion Tool

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For solving deflection problems, **Castigliano's First Theorem** states that the partial derivative of the total strain energy with respect to any point load equals the deflection at that point, in the direction of the load:
> $$\delta = \frac{\partial U}{\partial P}$$
> This is the engineer's **shortcut to deflection** ‚ÄĒ and it directly relies on the $U = \int M^2/(2EI)\,dx$ formulation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ūüďė SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Derivation: General Bending Strain Energy

We begin by isolating an elemental segment of length $dx$ located at axial distance $x$ from a reference origin along the beam axis.

### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Strain in a Generic Fibre

Consider a fibre at perpendicular distance $y$ from the **Neutral Axis (NA)**. When the beam bends with radius of curvature $\rho$, this fibre experiences axial strain given by the geometric relation:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{y}{\rho}$$

**Reasoning:** A fibre at distance $y$ on the convex side is stretched (tension), and the original length of the elemental arc $dx$ becomes $(dx + y\,d\theta)$ where $d\theta = dx/\rho$. Hence strain = $y\,d\theta / dx = y/\rho$.

### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Stress in the Fibre (Hooke's Law)

Applying Hooke's Law for uniaxial stress:

$$\sigma = E \varepsilon = \frac{Ey}{\rho}$$

**Reasoning:** Within the elastic limit, stress is proportional to strain, and the proportionality constant is Young's modulus $E$.

### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Force on an Elemental Area

For a small area $dA$ on the cross-section at distance $y$ from the NA, the axial force on the fibre is:

$$dF = \sigma\,dA = \frac{Ey}{\rho}\,dA$$

**Reasoning:** Stress times area gives the elemental axial force carried by the fibre.

### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Moment Contribution of the Fibre

The moment of this elemental force about the neutral axis is:

$$dM_f = y \cdot dF = \frac{Ey^2}{\rho}\,dA$$

**Reasoning:** Moment equals force times perpendicular distance from the NA.

### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Total Resisting Moment (Integrating Over Cross-Section)

The total internal resisting moment $M$ at the section is obtained by integrating $dM_f$ over the entire cross-sectional area $A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \int_A \frac{Ey^2}{\rho}\,dA \\
  &= \frac{E}{\rho} \int_A y^2\,dA \\
  &= \frac{E}{\rho} \cdot I
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** The integral $\int_A y^2\,dA$ is, by definition, the **Second Moment of Area** $I$ about the neutral axis.

### Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Moment‚ÄďCurvature Relationship

Rearranging the previous result, the **curvature** of the beam at section $x$ is:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{M}{EI}$$

**Reasoning:** This is the well-known *moment-curvature equation*, a cornerstone of beam theory.

### Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Change in Slope Over the Element

Over the elemental length $dx$, the change in slope between the two end-faces of the element is:

$$d\theta = \frac{dx}{\rho} = \frac{M\,dx}{EI}$$

**Reasoning:** The total change in slope over the length $dx$ equals the integral of curvature, and since curvature is locally constant over $dx$, $d\theta = \text{curvature} \times dx$.

### Step 8 ‚ÄĒ Elemental Strain Energy by Work‚ÄďEnergy Theorem

The internal moment $M$ acts through the relative rotation $d\theta$ between the two faces of the element. Assuming the moment is applied gradually from zero to its final value $M$ (linear elastic), the work done is:

$$dU = \frac{1}{2} M\,d\theta = \frac{1}{2} M \cdot \frac{M\,dx}{EI} = \frac{M^2\,dx}{2EI}$$

**Reasoning:** The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ arises because the moment builds up linearly from zero to $M$ as the element rotates; work = average force √ó displacement, and average moment = $M/2$.

### Step 9 ‚ÄĒ Total Strain Energy by Integration

Summing (integrating) the elemental strain energy over the full length $L$ of the beam:

$$
\boxed{U = \int_0^L \frac{M^2}{2EI}\,dx}
$$

**Reasoning:** The total strain energy is the cumulative internal work done across all elemental slices, each contributing $M^2/(2EI)$ per unit length.

### Step 10 ‚ÄĒ Pure Bending Special Case

If the bending moment is **constant** $M$ along the length (pure bending), the integrand is constant and:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{M^2}{2EI} \int_0^L dx \\
  &= \frac{M^2}{2EI} \cdot L \\
  &= \frac{M^2 L}{2EI}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Special Case 1: Cantilever with Point Load $P$ at Free End

**Setup:** A cantilever of length $L$, fixed at $x=0$, with point load $P$ applied at the free end $x=L$.

**Bending Moment at Section $x$** (measuring $x$ from the fixed end):

$$M(x) = Px$$

**Strain Energy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \int_0^L \frac{(Px)^2}{2EI}\,dx \\
  &= \frac{P^2}{2EI} \int_0^L x^2\,dx \\
  &= \frac{P^2}{2EI} \cdot \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_0^L \\
  &= \frac{P^2}{2EI} \cdot \frac{L^3}{3} \\
  &= \frac{P^2 L^3}{6EI}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification via Deflection:** The free-end deflection of a cantilever under tip load is $\delta = PL^3/(3EI)$. The total external work done by the gradually applied load is $W = \tfrac{1}{2}P\delta = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot P \cdot PL^3/(3EI) = P^2L^3/(6EI)$. This matches $U$ ‚ÄĒ confirming energy conservation.

---

## 3.3 Special Case 2: Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load $P$

**Setup:** A simply supported beam of span $L$ with a point load $P$ at mid-span. By symmetry, the reactions are $R_A = R_B = P/2$.

**Bending Moment for $0 \le x \le L/2$:**

$$M(x) = \frac{Px}{2}$$

**Strain Energy** (using symmetry, integrate from $0$ to $L/2$ and double):

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= 2 \int_0^{L/2} \frac{(Px/2)^2}{2EI}\,dx \\
  &= 2 \cdot \frac{P^2}{8EI} \int_0^{L/2} x^2\,dx \\
  &= \frac{P^2}{4EI} \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_0^{L/2} \\
  &= \frac{P^2}{4EI} \cdot \frac{(L/2)^3}{3} \\
  &= \frac{P^2}{4EI} \cdot \frac{L^3}{24} \\
  &= \frac{P^2 L^3}{96EI}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cross-check:** Central deflection $\delta_c = PL^3/(48EI)$. External work = $\tfrac{1}{2} P \delta_c = P^2L^3/(96EI)$. ‚úď

---

## 3.4 Special Case 3: Simply Supported Beam with UDL $w$

**Setup:** A simply supported beam of span $L$ under uniformly distributed load $w$ (force per unit length). Reactions: $R_A = R_B = wL/2$.

**Bending Moment at Section $x$:**

$$M(x) = \frac{wx}{2}(L - x) = \frac{w}{2}\left(Lx - x^2\right)$$

**Strain Energy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \int_0^L \frac{1}{2EI} \left[ \frac{w}{2}(Lx - x^2) \right]^2 dx \\
  &= \frac{w^2}{8EI} \int_0^L \left(L^2 x^2 - 2Lx^3 + x^4\right) dx \\
  &= \frac{w^2}{8EI} \left[ L^2 \cdot \frac{x^3}{3} - 2L \cdot \frac{x^4}{4} + \frac{x^5}{5} \right]_0^L \\
  &= \frac{w^2}{8EI} \left( \frac{L^5}{3} - \frac{L^5}{2} + \frac{L^5}{5} \right) \\
  &= \frac{w^2 L^5}{8EI} \left( \frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{5} \right) \\
  &= \frac{w^2 L^5}{8EI} \left( \frac{10 - 15 + 6}{30} \right) \\
  &= \frac{w^2 L^5}{8EI} \cdot \frac{1}{30} \\
  &= \frac{w^2 L^5}{240EI}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cross-check:** Central deflection $\delta_c = 5wL^4/(384EI)$. Total external work = $\tfrac{1}{2} W \delta_c$ where $W = wL$. Computation yields $w^2L^5/(240EI)$. ‚úď

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ When to Use Which Formula

| Property of Beam | Best Strategy | Reason |
|:-----------------|:--------------|:-------|
| Constant $M$ (pure bending) | Use $U = M^2L/(2EI)$ directly | Integrand is constant; integration is trivial |
| Varying $M$ (point load / UDL) | Express $M(x)$ then integrate | Need to square the moment function before integrating |
| Composite / multi-span beams | Split at points of discontinuity in $M$ | $M$ expression changes at each load/segment boundary |
| Deflection at a point with no load | Apply Castigliano with **dummy load** $Q \to 0$ | Permits use of $\partial U/\partial Q$ at locations of zero applied load |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# ūüďė SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram ‚ÄĒ Derivation Pathway

The following diagram traces the logical flow of the bending strain energy derivation, from fundamental assumptions through to the final integral expression.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Beam under bending moment M of length L] --> B[Isolate elemental slice of length dx at position x]
    B --> C[Apply strain in a fibre: eps = y over rho]
    C --> D[Apply Hooke's Law: sigma = E times y over rho]
    D --> E[Force on elemental area: dF = sigma times dA]
    E --> F[Moment contribution: dMf = y times dF]
    F --> G[Integrate over cross-section area A]
    G --> H[Define Second Moment of Area: I equals integral of y squared dA]
    H --> I[Derive moment-curvature: 1 over rho = M over EI]
    I --> J[Slope change in element: dtheta = M times dx over EI]
    J --> K[Work done by moment: dU = 0.5 times M times dtheta]
    K --> L[Elemental strain energy: dU = M squared dx over 2EI]
    L --> M[Integrate over length 0 to L]
    M --> N[Final Result: U = integral of M squared over 2EI dx]
    N --> O[Special Case: Pure bending yields U = M squared L over 2EI]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Function & Application Map

The following block diagram maps the engineering applications of bending strain energy, showing how the core formula connects to real-world structural and mechanical systems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CORE_FORMULA[Core Mathematical Foundation]
        F1[U = integral of M squared over 2EI dx]
    end
    subgraph STATIC[Static Applications]
        S1[Deflection by Castigliano Theorem]
        S2[Stiffness Coefficient Method]
        S3[Influence Line Analysis]
    end
    subgraph DYNAMIC[Dynamic Applications]
        D1[Sudden and Impact Load Analysis]
        D2[Resilience and Modulus of Resilience]
        D3[Strain Energy per Unit Volume]
    end
    subgraph REAL[Real World Engineering]
        R1[Aircraft Wing Spars]
        R2[Bridge Decks and Arches]
        R3[Leaf and Helical Springs]
        R4[Vehicle Crash Frames]
        R5[Earthquake Resistant Buildings]
    end
    F1 --> S1
    F1 --> S2
    F1 --> S3
    F1 --> D1
    F1 --> D2
    F1 --> D3
    S1 --> R1
    S1 --> R2
    D2 --> R3
    D2 --> R4
    D3 --> R5
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Stress Distribution in a Bent Beam

The figure below illustrates the linear variation of bending stress across the depth of a beam, with compression above the neutral axis and tension below. The strain energy density at each fibre is proportional to the square of the local stress.

> **Description of physical setup:**  
> A rectangular beam cross-section, depth $h$, width $b$. The neutral axis is at mid-depth. Above the NA, fibres are in **compression** (stress $\sigma_c$ on top fibre). Below the NA, fibres are in **tension** (stress $\sigma_t$ on bottom fibre). The stress varies **linearly** from $-\sigma_{max}$ at the top to $+\sigma_{max}$ at the bottom, passing through zero at the NA.

```
        ‚óĄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ b ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ėļ
        ‚ĒĆ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ēź  ‚Ė≤
        ‚Ēā ‚Ėľ ŌÉ_max (compression)        ‚Ēā  ‚Ēā
        ‚Ēā ‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď‚Ėď ‚Ēā  ‚Ēā
        ‚Ēā ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ ‚Ēā  ‚Ēā  h
        ‚Ēā ‚ĒÄ ‚ĒÄ ‚ĒÄ ‚ĒÄ ‚ĒÄ ‚ĒÄ ‚ĒÄ ‚ĒÄ NA ‚ĒÄ ‚ĒÄ ‚ĒÄ ‚ĒÄ ‚ĒÄ‚Ēā  ‚Ēā  (depth)
        ‚Ēā ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ‚ĖĎ ‚Ēā  ‚Ēā
        ‚Ēā ‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí‚Ėí ‚Ēā  ‚Ēā
        ‚Ēā ‚Ė≤ ŌÉ_max (tension)             ‚Ēā  ‚Ēā
        ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ēė  ‚Ėľ

   Strain energy density u = ŌÉ¬≤ / 2E
   Maximum at extreme fibres, zero at NA
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key insight:** Strain energy density is **quadratic** in stress, peaking at the extreme fibres and vanishing at the neutral axis. This is precisely why **I-shaped** and **hollow rectangular** sections are efficient ‚ÄĒ they remove material near the NA (where little energy is stored) and concentrate it at the extremes (where stress and energy density are highest).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# ūüďė SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ūüďĚ PART A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024]`
**Define strain energy due to bending. Derive the expression for the total strain energy stored in a beam subjected to pure bending of magnitude $M$ over a span $L$.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Strain energy due to bending is the internal work stored in a beam as it deforms under a bending moment, represented as the area under the moment‚Äďrotation curve within the elastic limit.

> **Derivation:** Consider a beam of length $L$ subjected to a constant bending moment $M$. The change in slope over an element $dx$ is $d\theta = M\,dx/(EI)$. The elemental strain energy is $dU = \tfrac{1}{2}M\,d\theta = M^2\,dx/(2EI)$. Integrating over $0$ to $L$:

$$U = \int_0^L \frac{M^2}{2EI}\,dx = \frac{M^2 L}{2EI}$$

> **Final Result:** $\boxed{U = \dfrac{M^2 L}{2EI}}$  ‚ąé

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]`
**A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a uniformly distributed load $w$ over its entire length. Find the strain energy stored in the beam, using $U = \int M^2/(2EI)\,dx$.**

**Model Answer:**

Reactions: $R_A = R_B = wL/2$. Bending moment: $M(x) = \dfrac{wx(L-x)}{2}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \int_0^L \frac{1}{2EI} \left[ \frac{wx(L-x)}{2} \right]^2 dx \\
  &= \frac{w^2}{8EI} \int_0^L (Lx - x^2)^2\,dx \\
  &= \frac{w^2}{8EI} \cdot \frac{L^5}{30} \\
  &= \frac{w^2 L^5}{240EI}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Result:** $\boxed{U = \dfrac{w^2 L^5}{240EI}}$  ‚ąé

---

## ūüďĚ PART B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### ūüÖįÔłŹ QUESTION A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Strain Energy Problem

> `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď June 2024 ‚Äď Module 3 Choice A]`

A simply supported beam $AB$ of span $L = 4$ m carries a concentrated load of $P = 20$ kN at mid-span. The beam is made of steel with $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm¬≤ and has a rectangular cross-section of width $b = 100$ mm and depth $h = 200$ mm.

**(a)** Derive the general expression for strain energy stored in the beam. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the numerical value of the strain energy stored in the beam, and verify the answer using the central deflection formula $\delta_c = PL^3/(48EI)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks

**Step 1:** For a simply supported beam of span $L$ with central point load $P$, the reactions at the supports are:

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{P}{2}$$

**Step 2:** By symmetry, the bending moment in either half-span ($0 \le x \le L/2$, measured from support $A$) is:

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x = \frac{Px}{2}$$

**Step 3:** The general expression for bending strain energy is:

$$U = \int_0^L \frac{M^2}{2EI}\,dx$$

**Step 4:** Using symmetry, integrate from $0$ to $L/2$ and multiply by 2:

$$U = 2 \int_0^{L/2} \frac{(Px/2)^2}{2EI}\,dx = 2 \cdot \frac{P^2}{8EI} \int_0^{L/2} x^2\,dx$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integral:

$$U = \frac{P^2}{4EI} \cdot \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_0^{L/2} = \frac{P^2}{4EI} \cdot \frac{L^3}{24} = \frac{P^2 L^3}{96EI}$$

> **Final Result (a):** $\boxed{U = \dfrac{P^2 L^3}{96EI}}$

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Compute the second moment of area $I$ for the rectangular cross-section:

$$I = \frac{bh^3}{12} = \frac{100 \times 200^3}{12} = \frac{100 \times 8 \times 10^6}{12} = 6.667 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2:** Convert all quantities to consistent units (N, mm):
- $P = 20$ kN $= 20{,}000$ N
- $L = 4$ m $= 4000$ mm
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm¬≤
- $I = 6.667 \times 10^7$ mm‚Āī

**Step 3:** Substitute into the strain energy formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{P^2 L^3}{96EI} \\
  &= \frac{(20{,}000)^2 \times (4000)^3}{96 \times (2 \times 10^5) \times (6.667 \times 10^7)} \\
  &= \frac{4 \times 10^8 \times 6.4 \times 10^{10}}{96 \times 1.333 \times 10^{13}} \\
  &= \frac{2.56 \times 10^{19}}{1.28 \times 10^{15}} \\
  &= 2.0 \times 10^4 \text{ N¬∑mm} \\
  &= 20 \text{ N¬∑m} = 20 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Verification using $\delta_c = PL^3/(48EI)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_c &= \frac{20{,}000 \times (4000)^3}{48 \times (2 \times 10^5) \times (6.667 \times 10^7)} \\
        &= \frac{20{,}000 \times 6.4 \times 10^{10}}{6.4 \times 10^{14}} \\
        &= 2.0 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** External work done = $\tfrac{1}{2} P \delta_c = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 20{,}000 \times 2.0 = 20{,}000$ N¬∑mm = **20 N¬∑m** ‚úď

> **Final Result (b):** $\boxed{U = 20 \text{ N¬∑m} = 20 \text{ Joules}}$ (verified by energy balance)

---

### ūüÖĪÔłŹ QUESTION B (14 Marks): Alternative Comprehensive Problem

> `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď June 2024 ‚Äď Module 3 Choice B]`

A cantilever beam of length $L = 2$ m is fixed at one end and loaded by a uniformly distributed load $w = 10$ kN/m over its entire span. The flexural rigidity of the beam is $EI = 2 \times 10^4$ N¬∑m¬≤.

**(a)** Derive the expression for strain energy stored in a cantilever under UDL, and compute its numerical value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same beam carries an additional point load $P$ at the free end such that the total strain energy becomes $U_{total} = 5$ times the strain energy due to UDL alone, find the value of $P$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks

**Step 1:** For a cantilever fixed at $x=0$ and free at $x=L$, with UDL $w$ over the span, the bending moment at section $x$ (measured from fixed end) is:

$$M(x) = \frac{wx^2}{2}$$

**Reasoning:** At a section $x$ from the fixed end, the load to the right is the UDL over length $(L-x)$. The moment arm to the centroid of this load is $(L-x)/2$. Equivalently, the load to the left is $wx$ with arm $x/2$, giving $M = wx \cdot x/2 = wx^2/2$.

**Step 2:** Strain energy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{UDL} &= \int_0^L \frac{(wx^2/2)^2}{2EI}\,dx \\
        &= \frac{w^2}{8EI} \int_0^L x^4\,dx \\
        &= \frac{w^2}{8EI} \cdot \left[ \frac{x^5}{5} \right]_0^L \\
        &= \frac{w^2 L^5}{40EI}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Numerical substitution:
- $w = 10$ kN/m $= 10{,}000$ N/m
- $L = 2$ m
- $EI = 2 \times 10^4$ N¬∑m¬≤

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{UDL} &= \frac{(10{,}000)^2 \times (2)^5}{40 \times 2 \times 10^4} \\
        &= \frac{10^8 \times 32}{8 \times 10^5} \\
        &= \frac{3.2 \times 10^9}{8 \times 10^5} \\
        &= 4{,}000 \text{ N¬∑m} = 4 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Result (a):** $\boxed{U_{UDL} = \dfrac{w^2 L^5}{40EI} = 4000 \text{ N¬∑m}}$

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Strain energy due to point load $P$ at the free end of cantilever:

$$U_P = \frac{P^2 L^3}{6EI}$$

**Step 2:** The bending moment at section $x$ due to combined loading (UDL + tip point load):

$$M_{total}(x) = \frac{wx^2}{2} + Px$$

**Step 3:** Total strain energy (using $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{total} &= \int_0^L \frac{1}{2EI}\left[ \frac{wx^2}{2} + Px \right]^2 dx \\
          &= \int_0^L \frac{1}{2EI}\left[ \frac{w^2 x^4}{4} + wPx^3 + P^2 x^2 \right] dx \\
          &= \frac{w^2 L^5}{40EI} + \frac{wPL^4}{8EI} + \frac{P^2 L^3}{6EI} \\
          &= U_{UDL} + U_{cross} + U_P
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Given $U_{total} = 5 \cdot U_{UDL}$:

$$U_{UDL} + U_{cross} + U_P = 5 U_{UDL} \implies U_{cross} + U_P = 4 U_{UDL}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute numerical values. We have $U_{UDL} = 4000$ N¬∑m and $EI = 2 \times 10^4$ N¬∑m¬≤. Then $4 U_{UDL} = 16{,}000$ N¬∑m. So:

$$\frac{wPL^4}{8EI} + \frac{P^2 L^3}{6EI} = 16{,}000$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $w = 10{,}000$, $L = 2$:

$$\frac{10{,}000 \cdot P \cdot 16}{8 \times 2 \times 10^4} + \frac{P^2 \cdot 8}{6 \times 2 \times 10^4} = 16{,}000$$

$$\frac{160{,}000\,P}{160{,}000} + \frac{8P^2}{120{,}000} = 16{,}000$$

$$P + \frac{P^2}{15{,}000} = 16{,}000$$

**Step 7:** Multiply through by $15{,}000$:

$$15{,}000\,P + P^2 = 2.4 \times 10^8$$

$$P^2 + 15{,}000\,P - 2.4 \times 10^8 = 0$$

**Step 8:** Solve the quadratic equation:

$$P = \frac{-15{,}000 \pm \sqrt{(15{,}000)^2 + 4 \times 2.4 \times 10^8}}{2}$$

$$P = \frac{-15{,}000 \pm \sqrt{2.25 \times 10^8 + 9.6 \times 10^8}}{2} = \frac{-15{,}000 \pm \sqrt{1.185 \times 10^9}}{2}$$

$$P = \frac{-15{,}000 \pm 34{,}428}{2}$$

Taking the positive root:

$$P = \frac{19{,}428}{2} = 9{,}714 \text{ N} \approx 9.71 \text{ kN}$$

> **Final Result (b):** $\boxed{P \approx 9.71 \text{ kN}}$

---

## ‚ö†ÔłŹ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Bending Strain Energy Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (‚Äď2 to ‚Äď3 marks):** Mixing mm and m, or N and kN, without conversion. Always convert to a consistent SI base unit (N, m, Pa) before substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting the Symmetry Factor (‚Äď1 mark):** In simply supported beams with central load, students often integrate over the full length $L$ but use the wrong moment expression past mid-span. The clean approach is to integrate from $0$ to $L/2$ and double the result.
> 3. **Missing the Squared Term (‚Äď2 marks):** The formula has $M^2$ in the numerator. Writing $\int M/(2EI)\,dx$ instead of $\int M^2/(2EI)\,dx$ is a critical conceptual error.
> 4. **Forgetting $1/2$ in Energy from Work Done: (‚Äď1 mark)** External work $W = \tfrac{1}{2} P \delta$ for gradual loading ‚ÄĒ omitting the half gives double the correct answer.
> 5. **Treating $EI$ as a Single Quantity Without Justification (‚Äď1 mark):** Always state that $E$ and $I$ are both constant (homogeneous material, prismatic section) before pulling them out of the integral.
> 6. **Cross-term Energy in Superposition (‚Äď2 marks):** When two loads act simultaneously, the cross-term $\int M_1 M_2 / (EI) \, dx$ must be included; do not simply add individual strain energies.

---

## ‚úÖ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ūüéĮ **Master Formula:** $U = \int_0^L \dfrac{M^2}{2EI}\,dx$ ‚ÄĒ applicable to **all** bending problems in the elastic range.
- ūüéĮ **Pure bending simplification:** When $M$ is constant, $U = M^2 L / (2EI)$ ‚ÄĒ no integration needed.
- ūüéĮ **Standard results to memorize:**  
  $P^2L^3/(6EI)$ (cantilever-tip), $P^2L^3/(96EI)$ (SS-center), $w^2L^5/(240EI)$ (SS-UDL), $w^2L^5/(40EI)$ (cantilever-UDL).
- ūüéĮ **Assumptions of the formula:** Linear elastic material, small deflections, plane sections remain plane, $EI$ constant.
- ūüéĮ **Energy Method ‚Üí Deflection:** Castigliano's theorem: $\delta = \partial U / \partial P$ ‚ÄĒ pair this with the above formula to solve most KTU Module 3 deflection questions.
- ūüéĮ **Cross-term in superposition:** For two coexisting loads, $U_{total} = U_1 + U_2 + \int M_1 M_2 / (EI)\,dx$ ‚ÄĒ the cross-term is **not** zero.
- ūüéĮ **Units:** Always work in **N‚Äďm** (or convert to mm‚ÄďN) consistently. Energy has units of **N¬∑m = Joules**.
- ūüéĮ **Symmetry shortcut:** For symmetric beams and symmetric loading, integrate from support to centre and **multiply by 2**.
- ūüéĮ **Verification trick:** Whenever you compute $U$, cross-check using $W_{ext} = \tfrac{1}{2} \sum P_i \delta_i$ for the same loading case.
- ūüéĮ **Design insight:** Strain energy density $u = \sigma^2/(2E)$ is maximum at extreme fibres ‚ÄĒ drives the rationale behind I-section and hollow-section beams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculation of Strain Energy in Beams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Strain Energy in Beams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain Energy in Beams** is the internal energy stored within a structural member (beam) as it deforms under the action of external loads, calculated by integrating the work done by internal stresses (bending moment, shear force, axial force, and torsion) over the volume of the beam.

Mathematically, for a beam subjected to bending, the total strain energy $U$ stored in the beam is expressed as:

$$U = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{M^{2}}{2EI}\, dx$$

where $M$ is the bending moment at a section, $E$ is the Young's Modulus of Elasticity (**N/m¬≤**), $I$ is the Moment of Inertia of the cross-section (**m‚Āī** or **mm‚Āī**), and $L$ is the length of the beam (**m** or **mm**).

The generalized form of strain energy accounting for **all** modes of deformation is:

$$U = \int_{V} \left( \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E} + \frac{\tau^{2}}{2G} \right) dV$$

where $\sigma$ is the normal stress, $\tau$ is the shear stress, $G$ is the Shear Modulus (**N/m¬≤**), and $V$ is the volume of the beam.

## 1.2 Component-wise Decomposition of Strain Energy

For a beam under general loading, the strain energy is the sum of contributions from each mode of deformation:

| Mode of Deformation | Strain Energy Expression | Symbol | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bending** | $U_b = \int_0^L \frac{M^2}{2EI}\,dx$ | $U_b$ | N¬∑m or J |
| **Shear** | $U_s = \int_0^L \frac{F_s^{\,2}}{2GA}\,k\,dx$ | $U_s$ | N¬∑m or J |
| **Torsion (Shafts)** | $U_t = \int_0^L \frac{T^2}{2GJ}\,dx$ | $U_t$ | N¬∑m or J |
| **Axial Loading** | $U_a = \int_0^L \frac{P^2}{2AE}\,dx$ | $U_a$ | N¬∑m or J |

where $F_s$ is the shear force, $A$ is the cross-sectional area, $k$ is the shear shape coefficient (form factor), $T$ is the torque, $J$ is the polar moment of inertia, and $P$ is the axial force.

> [!NOTE]
> For **slender beams** (span/depth ratio > 10), the shear contribution to strain energy is **negligible** compared to bending and is typically ignored in KTU board examination problems unless explicitly stated.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine stretching a **rubber band** between your fingers. The instant you stretch it, you are doing work against the internal molecular bonds of the rubber. This work is **not lost** ‚ÄĒ it is stored inside the rubber band as **potential energy**. If you release the band, this stored energy converts back into kinetic energy, snapping it back to its original shape.

A **beam in bending behaves exactly like this rubber band**, but at a much larger structural scale. As external loads push the beam downward:
1. The top fibers **compress** (storing compression energy)
2. The bottom fibers **stretch** (storing tension energy)
3. The neutral axis experiences **no strain**

When the load is removed, this accumulated **strain energy** acts as a restoring mechanism and the beam springs back. The total energy stored is precisely equal to the work done by the external load during the loading process (assuming the loading is **quasi-static** and the material remains within the **elastic limit**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The work done by external loads = Strain Energy stored in the beam (in the elastic range). This equality is the foundation of the **energy methods** in structural analysis, which form the basis for Castigliano's Theorems, the Principle of Virtual Work, and the Unit Load Method.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Young's Modulus ($E$):** Steel = $2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm¬≤, Concrete = $25 \times 10^{3}$ N/mm¬≤
- **Shear Modulus ($G$):** Steel = $0.77 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm¬≤, Concrete = $10 \times 10^{3}$ N/mm¬≤
- **Form Factor ($k$):** Rectangular = 1.5, Circular = 1.33, I-section ‚Čą 2.4 to 2.7, Thin tube ‚Čą 2.0

> [!TIP]
> For KTU board exams, **bending strain energy** is the most frequently tested component. Always verify the loading type first (point load, UDL, UVL, moment) and identify the correct $M(x)$ expression before integrating.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework for Solving Strain Energy Problems

When asked to calculate strain energy in a beam, follow this exact sequence:

- **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify the type of beam** (cantilever, simply supported, fixed, propped) and the **type of loading** (point load, UDL, UVL, moment).
- **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Draw the Bending Moment Diagram (BMD)** and derive the algebraic expression $M(x)$ for the relevant section at a distance $x$ from a chosen origin.
- **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Substitute** $M(x)$ into the strain energy formula:

$$U = \int_0^L \frac{M^{2}(x)}{2EI}\, dx$$

- **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Integrate** with respect to $x$ over the appropriate limits (or full length $L$ for uniform beams).
- **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Simplify** to obtain the final expression and assign proper **SI units** (N¬∑m or Joules).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the formula have $M^2$ and not $M$?**
> Because strain energy is a **scalar** quantity proportional to the *square* of the stress or moment, and it is always **positive** ‚ÄĒ the beam always stores energy whether bending "up" or "down" (sagging or hogging).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates the **most critical strain energy formulas** that are repeatedly tested in KTU board examinations:

| # | Beam Configuration & Loading | Bending Moment $M(x)$ | Strain Energy $U$ Stored |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cantilever, point load $W$ at free end (origin at fixed end) | $M(x) = -W(L-x)$ | $U = \dfrac{W^{2} L^{3}}{6EI}$ |
| 2 | Cantilever, UDL $w$ over full length | $M(x) = -\dfrac{w(L-x)^{2}}{2}$ | $U = \dfrac{w^{2} L^{5}}{40EI}$ |
| 3 | Simply Supported, point load $W$ at distance $a$ from left (b = L-a) | Two regions: $M_1 = \dfrac{Wbx}{L}$ and $M_2 = \dfrac{Wa(L-x)}{L}$ | $U = \dfrac{W^{2} a^{2} b^{2}}{6LEI}$ |
| 4 | Simply Supported, point load $W$ at center ($a = b = L/2$) | $M = \dfrac{W}{2} \cdot x$ (for $x \le L/2$) | $U = \dfrac{W^{2} L^{3}}{96EI}$ |
| 5 | Simply Supported, UDL $w$ over full span | $M(x) = \dfrac{w}{2}(Lx - x^{2})$ | $U = \dfrac{w^{2} L^{5}}{240EI}$ |
| 6 | Simply Supported, central moment $M_0$ | $M(x) = \dfrac{M_0 x}{L}$ (for $x \le L/2$) | $U = \dfrac{M_0^{2} L}{6EI}$ |
| 7 | Cantilever, moment $M_0$ at free end | $M(x) = M_0$ (constant) | $U = \dfrac{M_0^{2} L}{2EI}$ |
| 8 | Propped Cantilever, UDL $w$ over full length | Complex (piecewise) | $U = \dfrac{w^{2} L^{5}}{240EI} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1.6} \approx \dfrac{w^{2} L^{5}}{384EI}$ (approx.) |

> [!CAUTION]
> For **Propped Cantilever** under UDL, the exact strain energy is $U = \dfrac{w^{2}L^{5}}{184.6\,EI}$ (full derivation requires $R = \dfrac{3wL}{8}$ at the prop). Always derive the reactions first for statically indeterminate cases.

## 2.3 Castigliano's Theorem ‚ÄĒ The Bridge to Deflections

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Castigliano's First Theorem (most tested in KTU):** *The partial derivative of the total strain energy with respect to any applied force (or moment) equals the displacement (or rotation) of the point of application of that force (or moment) in the direction of the force (or moment).*

Mathematically:

$$\delta = \frac{\partial U}{\partial P} \quad \text{(deflection at point of load P)}$$

$$\theta = \frac{\partial U}{\partial M} \quad \text{(slope at point of applied moment M)}$$

For bending-dominated beams, since $U = \int_0^L \frac{M^2}{2EI}\,dx$, we obtain:

$$\delta = \frac{\partial U}{\partial P} = \int_0^L \frac{M}{EI} \cdot \frac{\partial M}{\partial P}\, dx$$

This integral is **identical** to the **Moment-Area Method** and the **Unit Load (Virtual Work) Method**, making Castigliano's Theorem an extremely powerful unified tool.

### 2.3.1 When to Use Castigliano's Theorem vs. Direct Integration

| Scenario | Recommended Method |
|---|---|
| Statically determinate beam, deflection at loaded point | Double Integration or Macaulay's Method |
| Deflection at a point where **no load exists** | Apply a **dummy load** $Q$ at that point, compute $\delta = \left.\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial Q}\right\vert_{Q=0}$ |
| Statically indeterminate beam | Castigliano's **Second Theorem** (compatibility) |
| Frame with multiple members | Castigliano's Theorem (most efficient) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Bridge Design:** Strain energy methods are used to compute camber (pre-camber) of long-span bridge girders.
> - **Aircraft Structures:** Energy methods predict wing-tip deflections under aerodynamic loads.
> - **Machine Tool Frames:** Strain energy minimization principles guide the design of rigid, low-vibration tool frames.
> - **Seismic Engineering:** Energy-based design quantifies the energy dissipation capacity of structural members during earthquakes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: General Bending Strain Energy Formula

### Objective
To derive $U = \int_0^L \dfrac{M^2}{2EI}\,dx$ from first principles.

### Starting Point ‚ÄĒ Pure Bending Stress
Consider a small element of length $dx$ in a beam. The bending stress at a fiber at distance $y$ from the neutral axis is given by the **flexure formula**:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

The corresponding longitudinal strain is $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E} = \dfrac{My}{EI}$.

### Strain Energy Density
The strain energy stored per unit volume (strain energy density) is:

$$u = \int_0^{\varepsilon} \sigma \, d\varepsilon = \int_0^{\varepsilon} E\varepsilon \, d\varepsilon = \frac{E\varepsilon^{2}}{2} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E}$$

Substituting $\sigma = \dfrac{My}{I}$:

$$u = \frac{1}{2E}\left(\frac{My}{I}\right)^{2} = \frac{M^{2} y^{2}}{2EI^{2}}$$

### Integration Over the Cross-Section
The strain energy stored in a thin horizontal strip of area $dA$ at height $y$ is $u \cdot dA$. Integrating over the entire cross-sectional area:

$$dU = \int_A \frac{M^{2} y^{2}}{2EI^{2}} \, dA = \frac{M^{2}}{2EI^{2}} \int_A y^{2}\, dA$$

By definition, $\int_A y^{2}\, dA = I$ (the second moment of area). Therefore:

$$dU = \frac{M^{2}}{2EI^{2}} \cdot I = \frac{M^{2}}{2EI}$$

### Integration Over the Length
The total strain energy in the entire beam is obtained by integrating $dU$ over the length $L$:

$$\boxed{U = \int_0^L \frac{M^{2}(x)}{2EI}\, dx}$$

This is the **fundamental strain energy equation for beams under bending** and forms the bedrock of all energy methods.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Strain Energy in a Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load

### Given
- Beam: Simply supported, length $L$
- Load: Point load $W$ at midspan

### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Support Reactions
By symmetry, $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{W}{2}$ (both reactions act upward).

### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Bending Moment Expression
For a section at distance $x$ from the left support A, considering equilibrium of the left portion:

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x = \frac{Wx}{2} \quad \text{for } 0 \le x \le \frac{L}{2}$$

By symmetry, the moment expression in the right half is the mirror image.

### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Substitute into Strain Energy Formula
For one half of the beam:

$$U_{\text{half}} = \int_0^{L/2} \frac{1}{2EI}\left(\frac{Wx}{2}\right)^{2} dx = \frac{1}{2EI} \cdot \frac{W^{2}}{4} \int_0^{L/2} x^{2}\, dx$$

### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Perform the Integration

$$U_{\text{half}} = \frac{W^{2}}{8EI} \left[ \frac{x^{3}}{3} \right]_0^{L/2} = \frac{W^{2}}{8EI} \cdot \frac{(L/2)^{3}}{3} = \frac{W^{2}}{8EI} \cdot \frac{L^{3}}{24} = \frac{W^{2} L^{3}}{192EI}$$

### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Double for Full Beam
By symmetry, the total strain energy is:

$$\boxed{U = 2 \times U_{\text{half}} = \frac{W^{2} L^{3}}{96EI}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **standard KTU result** ‚ÄĒ memorize this formula. It is the answer to a very common 3-mark question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Strain Energy in a Simply Supported Beam with UDL

### Given
- Beam: Simply supported, length $L$
- Load: Uniformly distributed load $w$ (N/m) over the entire span

### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Support Reactions
By symmetry:

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2}$$

### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Bending Moment Expression
For a section at distance $x$ from the left support A, the bending moment is:

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - \frac{wx^{2}}{2} = \frac{wL}{2} \cdot x - \frac{wx^{2}}{2} = \frac{w}{2}(Lx - x^{2})$$

### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Substitute into Strain Energy Formula

$$U = \int_0^L \frac{M^{2}}{2EI}\, dx = \frac{1}{2EI} \int_0^L \left[\frac{w}{2}(Lx - x^{2})\right]^{2} dx$$

$$U = \frac{1}{2EI} \cdot \frac{w^{2}}{4} \int_0^L (Lx - x^{2})^{2}\, dx = \frac{w^{2}}{8EI} \int_0^L (Lx - x^{2})^{2}\, dx$$

### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Expand the Integrand

$$(Lx - x^{2})^{2} = L^{2}x^{2} - 2Lx^{3} + x^{4}$$

### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Perform the Integration Term by Term

$$U = \frac{w^{2}}{8EI} \int_0^L (L^{2}x^{2} - 2Lx^{3} + x^{4})\, dx$$

$$U = \frac{w^{2}}{8EI} \left[ L^{2}\cdot\frac{x^{3}}{3} - 2L\cdot\frac{x^{4}}{4} + \frac{x^{5}}{5} \right]_0^L$$

$$U = \frac{w^{2}}{8EI} \left[ \frac{L^{5}}{3} - \frac{L^{5}}{2} + \frac{L^{5}}{5} \right]$$

### Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Simplify the Bracket (LCM = 30)

$$\frac{L^{5}}{3} - \frac{L^{5}}{2} + \frac{L^{5}}{5} = L^{5}\left(\frac{10 - 15 + 6}{30}\right) = L^{5} \cdot \frac{1}{30} = \frac{L^{5}}{30}$$

### Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Final Result

$$\boxed{U = \frac{w^{2}}{8EI} \cdot \frac{L^{5}}{30} = \frac{w^{2} L^{5}}{240EI}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **high-yield KTU result**. The derivation above is the standard model answer expected by the KTU board examiner for a 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Castigliano's Theorem ‚ÄĒ Deflection of a Cantilever with Point Load

### Given
- Cantilever beam of length $L$, fixed at A, free at B
- Point load $W$ at the free end B
- **Objective:** Find the deflection $\delta_B$ at the free end

### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Strain Energy Expression
From the KTU Formula Sheet (Row 1):

$$U = \frac{W^{2} L^{3}}{6EI}$$

### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply Castigliano's First Theorem

$$\delta_B = \frac{\partial U}{\partial W} = \frac{\partial}{\partial W}\left(\frac{W^{2} L^{3}}{6EI}\right) = \frac{2W L^{3}}{6EI} = \frac{WL^{3}}{3EI}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This result **exactly matches** the deflection of a cantilever with an end point load obtained by double integration ‚ÄĒ a beautiful confirmation of Castigliano's Theorem.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Castigliano's Theorem ‚ÄĒ Slope at Free End of a Cantilever with End Moment

### Given
- Cantilever of length $L$, fixed at A
- Applied moment $M_0$ at the free end B
- **Objective:** Find the slope $\theta_B$ at the free end

### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Strain Energy Expression
The bending moment is constant throughout the beam: $M(x) = M_0$.

$$U = \int_0^L \frac{M_0^{2}}{2EI}\, dx = \frac{M_0^{2} L}{2EI}$$

### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply Castigliano's First Theorem

$$\theta_B = \frac{\partial U}{\partial M_0} = \frac{\partial}{\partial M_0}\left(\frac{M_0^{2} L}{2EI}\right) = \frac{2M_0 L}{2EI} = \frac{M_0 L}{EI}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Comprehensive Summary

| Beam Type | Load | Max BM $M_{\max}$ | Max Deflection $\delta_{\max}$ | Strain Energy $U$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cantilever | Point $W$ at end | $WL$ | $\dfrac{WL^{3}}{3EI}$ | $\dfrac{W^{2}L^{3}}{6EI}$ |
| Cantilever | UDL $w$ | $\dfrac{wL^{2}}{2}$ | $\dfrac{wL^{4}}{8EI}$ | $\dfrac{w^{2}L^{5}}{40EI}$ |
| Simply Supported | Point $W$ at center | $\dfrac{WL}{4}$ | $\dfrac{WL^{3}}{48EI}$ | $\dfrac{W^{2}L^{3}}{96EI}$ |
| Simply Supported | UDL $w$ | $\dfrac{wL^{2}}{8}$ | $\dfrac{5wL^{4}}{384EI}$ | $\dfrac{w^{2}L^{5}}{240EI}$ |
| Simply Supported | Central $M_0$ | $\dfrac{M_0}{2}$ | $\dfrac{M_0 L^{2}}{16EI}$ (at one end) | $\dfrac{M_0^{2}L}{6EI}$ |
| Fixed-Fixed | UDL $w$ | $\dfrac{wL^{2}}{12}$ | $\dfrac{wL^{4}}{384EI}$ | $\dfrac{w^{2}L^{5}}{1440EI}$ |
| Fixed-Fixed | Center $W$ | $\dfrac{WL}{8}$ | $\dfrac{WL^{3}}{192EI}$ | $\dfrac{W^{2}L^{3}}{192 \times 8 EI} = \dfrac{W^{2}L^{3}}{1536EI}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Verification by Castigliano's Theorem:**
> For Cantilever with UDL: $U = \dfrac{w^{2}L^{5}}{40EI}$
> $$\delta_{\max} = \frac{\partial U}{\partial w} = \frac{2wL^{5}}{40EI} = \frac{wL^{4}}{20EI} \quad \text{at fixed end (slope)}$$
> $$\delta_{\text{at free end}} = \text{(integrate once more, careful!)} \to \frac{wL^{4}}{8EI} \checkmark$$
> This confirms energy methods are fully consistent with classical methods.

---

## 3.7 Deflection at a Point with No Load ‚ÄĒ Dummy Load Method

### Problem
A simply supported beam of length $L$ carries a UDL $w$ over the entire span. Find the deflection at quarter-span (i.e., at $x = L/4$) using Castigliano's Theorem.

### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Apply a Dummy Load
Since there is **no real load** at $x = L/4$, apply an **imaginary dummy load** $Q$ at that point.

### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Reactions
Taking moments about B:

$$R_A = \frac{wL}{2} + \frac{Q(L - L/4)}{L} = \frac{wL}{2} + \frac{3Q}{4}$$

$$R_B = \frac{wL}{2} + \frac{Q \cdot L/4}{L} = \frac{wL}{2} + \frac{Q}{4}$$

### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Bending Moment in Two Regions
- **Region 1** ($0 \le x \le L/4$):

$$M_1(x) = R_A \cdot x - \frac{wx^{2}}{2} = \left(\frac{wL}{2} + \frac{3Q}{4}\right)x - \frac{wx^{2}}{2}$$

- **Region 2** ($L/4 \le x \le L$):

$$M_2(x) = R_A \cdot x - \frac{wx^{2}}{2} - Q\left(x - \frac{L}{4}\right)$$

### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Partial Derivatives with Respect to Q

$$\frac{\partial M_1}{\partial Q} = \frac{3x}{4} \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial M_2}{\partial Q} = \frac{3x}{4} - \left(x - \frac{L}{4}\right) = \frac{L}{4} - \frac{x}{4}$$

### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Apply Castigliano's Theorem

$$\delta_{L/4} = \left.\int_0^{L/4} \frac{M_1}{EI}\cdot\frac{\partial M_1}{\partial Q}\,dx + \int_{L/4}^{L} \frac{M_2}{EI}\cdot\frac{\partial M_2}{\partial Q}\,dx\right\vert_{Q=0}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This integral, while lengthy, is the **standard KTU model answer** for a 14-mark question. Setting $Q = 0$ **after** differentiation is the critical step ‚ÄĒ do NOT set $Q = 0$ before differentiation, as that would erase the dummy load.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Strain Energy Calculation Workflow

The following architecture flow maps the complete procedural logic for solving any strain energy problem in beams:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Beam and Loading] --> B{Statically Determinate?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Compute Support Reactions]
    B -- No --> D[Apply Castigliano II / Compatibility]
    C --> E[Derive Bending Moment Mx]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Single BM expression or Piecewise?}
    F -- Single --> G[Apply U = Integral of M squared over 2EI dx]
    F -- Piecewise --> H[Split Integral: Sum over Regions]
    G --> I[Integrate from 0 to L]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Substitute Limits and Simplify]
    J --> K{Find Deflection also required?}
    K -- No --> L[Report Final Strain Energy in N-m or J]
    K -- Yes --> M[Apply Castigliano I: delta = partial U over partial P]
    M --> N{Load at required point?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Differentiate U w.r.t. P and substitute]
    N -- No --> P[Apply Dummy Load Q at required point]
    P --> O
    O --> Q[Integrate M times partial M over partial P dx over EI]
    Q --> L
```
