# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "strain energy" as applied to beams. State the expression for strain energy due to bending.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Strain Energy:** It is the internal energy stored in a deformed body (beam) within its elastic limit, equal to the work done by external forces in producing the deformation.

For a beam subjected to bending, the strain energy is given by:

$$U = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{M^{2}(x)}{2EI}\, dx$$

where $M(x)$ is the bending moment at section $x$, $E$ is Young's Modulus, and $I$ is the moment of inertia of the cross-section.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024]**
**State Castigliano's First Theorem and mention its two engineering applications.**

**Model Answer:**

> Castigliano's First Theorem states: *"The partial derivative of the total strain energy of an elastic structure with respect to any particular load (force or moment) gives the displacement (linear or angular) at the point of application of that load, in the direction of the load."*

Mathematically:
$$\delta = \frac{\partial U}{\partial P} \qquad \text{and} \qquad \theta = \frac{\partial U}{\partial M}$$

**Two Engineering Applications:**
1. **Determination of deflections and slopes** in statically determinate and indeterminate beams, frames, and trusses.
2. **Analysis of complex structures** (curved members, variable cross-sections) where classical double-integration methods become cumbersome.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ **Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]**

**A simply supported beam of span 6 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 20 kN/m over the entire span. Using the strain energy method, determine:**
**(a) The strain energy stored in the beam.** **(7 marks)**
**(b) The deflection at the center of the beam using Castigliano's Theorem.** **(7 marks)**

**Assume $EI = 2 \times 10^{4}$ kN¬∑m¬≤ (constant throughout).**

#### Solution

**Given:** $L = 6$ m, $w = 20$ kN/m, $EI = 2 \times 10^{4}$ kN¬∑m¬≤

##### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Strain Energy

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Reactions:**
$$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{20 \times 6}{2} = 60 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Bending Moment at distance $x$ from A:**
$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - \frac{wx^{2}}{2} = 60x - 10x^{2} = 10(6x - x^{2})$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Apply Strain Energy Formula:**
$$U = \int_0^L \frac{M^{2}}{2EI}\,dx = \frac{1}{2EI}\int_0^6 \left[10(6x - x^{2})\right]^{2} dx$$

$$U = \frac{1}{2EI} \cdot 100 \int_0^6 (6x - x^{2})^{2}\, dx$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Expand the Integrand:**
$$(6x - x^{2})^{2} = 36x^{2} - 12x^{3} + x^{4}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Perform Integration:**
$$U = \frac{100}{2EI}\int_0^6 (36x^{2} - 12x^{3} + x^{4})\, dx$$

$$U = \frac{100}{2EI}\left[36 \cdot \frac{x^{3}}{3} - 12 \cdot \frac{x^{4}}{4} + \frac{x^{5}}{5}\right]_0^6$$

$$U = \frac{100}{2EI}\left[12x^{3} - 3x^{4} + \frac{x^{5}}{5}\right]_0^6$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Substitute Limits:**
$$U = \frac{100}{2EI}\left[12(216) - 3(1296) + \frac{7776}{5}\right]$$

$$U = \frac{100}{2EI}\left[2592 - 3888 + 1555.2\right] = \frac{100}{2EI}\left[259.2\right]$$

$$U = \frac{100 \times 259.2}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{4}} = \frac{25920}{4 \times 10^{4}} = 0.648 \text{ kN¬∑m}$$

$$\boxed{U = 648 \text{ N¬∑m} = 648 \text{ J}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-verification using the standard formula:**
> $U = \dfrac{w^{2}L^{5}}{240EI} = \dfrac{(20)^{2}(6)^{5}}{240 \times 2 \times 10^{4}} = \dfrac{400 \times 7776}{4.8 \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{3.1104 \times 10^{6}}{4.8 \times 10^{6}} = 0.648$ kN¬∑m ‚úď

##### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Deflection at Center via Castigliano's Theorem

**Method:** Apply a dummy load $Q$ at the center of the beam.

**Reactions (with Q at center):**
$$R_A' = R_B' = \frac{wL + Q}{2} = \frac{20 \times 6 + Q}{2} = 60 + \frac{Q}{2} \text{ kN}$$

**Bending Moment in Left Half** ($0 \le x \le 3$ m):
$$M_1(x) = R_A' \cdot x - \frac{wx^{2}}{2} = \left(60 + \frac{Q}{2}\right)x - 10x^{2}$$

**Partial Derivative w.r.t. Q:**
$$\frac{\partial M_1}{\partial Q} = \frac{x}{2}$$

**Bending Moment in Right Half** ($3 \le x \le 6$ m):
By symmetry, $M_2 = M_1(L - x)$ and $\dfrac{\partial M_2}{\partial Q} = \dfrac{L - x}{2}$.

**Apply Castigliano's Theorem:**
$$\delta_C = \left.\int_0^L \frac{M}{EI}\cdot\frac{\partial M}{\partial Q}\,dx\right\vert_{Q=0}$$

By symmetry:
$$\delta_C = \frac{2}{EI}\int_0^3 \frac{1}{EI}\left(60x - 10x^{2}\right)\cdot\frac{x}{2}\,dx \quad \text{(at Q=0)}$$

$$\delta_C = \frac{2}{EI}\int_0^3 (30x^{2} - 5x^{3})\,dx$$

$$\delta_C = \frac{2}{EI}\left[30 \cdot \frac{x^{3}}{3} - 5 \cdot \frac{x^{4}}{4}\right]_0^3$$

$$\delta_C = \frac{2}{EI}\left[10(27) - \frac{5(81)}{4}\right] = \frac{2}{EI}\left[270 - 101.25\right] = \frac{2(168.75)}{EI} = \frac{337.5}{EI}$$

**Substituting $EI = 2 \times 10^{4}$ kN¬∑m¬≤:**
$$\delta_C = \frac{337.5}{2 \times 10^{4}} = 0.016875 \text{ m} = 16.875 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_C = 16.875 \text{ mm} \;\; (\downarrow)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-verification using the standard formula:**
> $\delta_C = \dfrac{5wL^{4}}{384EI} = \dfrac{5 \times 20 \times (6)^{4}}{384 \times 2 \times 10^{4}} = \dfrac{5 \times 20 \times 1296}{7.68 \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{129600}{7.68 \times 10^{6}} = 0.01688$ m ‚úď

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ **Choice 2 (Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2023, Supplementary 2024]**

**A cantilever beam of length 4 m is fixed at one end and carries a point load of 30 kN at the free end. Take $EI = 1.5 \times 10^{4}$ kN¬∑m¬≤.**
**(a) Calculate the strain energy stored in the beam due to bending.** **(7 marks)**
**(b) Using Castigliano's Theorem, determine the slope and deflection at the free end.** **(7 marks)**

#### Solution

**Given:** $L = 4$ m, $W = 30$ kN, $EI = 1.5 \times 10^{4}$ kN¬∑m¬≤

##### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Strain Energy

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Bending Moment at distance $x$ from the fixed end:**
$$M(x) = -W(L - x) = -30(4 - x)$$

The negative sign indicates hogging, but since $M^2$ is used, the sign is irrelevant for strain energy.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Substitute into Strain Energy Formula:**
$$U = \int_0^L \frac{M^{2}}{2EI}\, dx = \int_0^4 \frac{[30(4-x)]^{2}}{2EI}\, dx$$

$$U = \frac{900}{2EI}\int_0^4 (4-x)^{2}\, dx$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Perform Integration:**

Let $u = 4 - x$, $du = -dx$. When $x = 0$, $u = 4$; when $x = 4$, $u = 0$.

$$U = \frac{900}{2EI}\int_4^0 u^{2}(-du) = \frac{900}{2EI}\int_0^4 u^{2}\, du$$

$$U = \frac{900}{2EI}\left[\frac{u^{3}}{3}\right]_0^4 = \frac{900}{2EI} \cdot \frac{64}{3} = \frac{900 \times 64}{6 EI} = \frac{57600}{6EI} = \frac{9600}{EI}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Substitute $EI = 1.5 \times 10^{4}$ kN¬∑m¬≤:**
$$U = \frac{9600}{1.5 \times 10^{4}} = 0.64 \text{ kN¬∑m}$$

$$\boxed{U = 640 \text{ N¬∑m} = 640 \text{ J}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-check using standard formula:**
> $U = \dfrac{W^{2}L^{3}}{6EI} = \dfrac{(30)^{2}(4)^{3}}{6 \times 1.5 \times 10^{4}} = \dfrac{900 \times 64}{9 \times 10^{4}} = \dfrac{57600}{90000} = 0.64$ kN¬∑m ‚úď

##### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Slope and Deflection at Free End

**Slope at Free End** ($\theta_B$):
$$\theta_B = \frac{\partial U}{\partial W} = \frac{\partial}{\partial W}\left(\frac{W^{2}L^{3}}{6EI}\right) = \frac{2W L^{3}}{6EI} = \frac{WL^{3}}{3EI}$$

$$\theta_B = \frac{30 \times (4)^{3}}{3 \times 1.5 \times 10^{4}} = \frac{30 \times 64}{4.5 \times 10^{4}} = \frac{1920}{45000} = 0.04267 \text{ rad}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_B = 0.04267 \text{ rad} \approx 2.444^{\circ}}$$

**Deflection at Free End** ($\delta_B$):

To find deflection, we use the relation $\delta = W \cdot \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial W} \cdot \dfrac{1}{W} \cdot W$... wait, more directly:

The deflection equals the work done per unit load = (1/2) √ó W √ó $\delta_B$ (for linear elastic systems, $U = \frac{1}{2}W\delta_B$).

Therefore:
$$\delta_B = \frac{2U}{W} = \frac{2 \times 640 \text{ N¬∑m}}{30 \text{ kN}} = \frac{2 \times 0.64}{30} = \frac{1.28}{30} = 0.04267 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_B = 42.67 \text{ mm} \; (\downarrow)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-check using the standard formula:**
> $\delta_B = \dfrac{WL^{3}}{3EI} = \dfrac{30 \times 64}{3 \times 1.5 \times 10^{4}} = \dfrac{1920}{45000} = 0.04267$ m ‚úď

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to double the integration** when using symmetry ‚ÄĒ for SSB with central loading, students often integrate only over $L/2$ and forget to multiply by 2.
> 2. **Dropping the "$1/2$" in the energy formula** ‚ÄĒ the correct form is $\dfrac{M^2}{2EI}$, not $\dfrac{M^2}{EI}$. This is a 1-mark penalty per occurrence.
> 3. **Using wrong limits of integration** ‚ÄĒ for a cantilever with point load at the free end, measure $x$ from the **fixed end**, not the free end.
> 4. **Setting $Q = 0$ BEFORE differentiation** in the dummy load method ‚ÄĒ the dummy load MUST be carried through differentiation, and only set to zero at the very end.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** ‚ÄĒ mixing mm with m, or kN with N. Always convert to consistent SI units at the START of the problem.
> 6. **Forgetting to include $EI$ in the denominator** ‚ÄĒ a strain energy without $EI$ is dimensionally meaningless.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ Strain Energy in Beams:**

- ‚úÖ The **fundamental formula** for bending strain energy is $U = \int_0^L \dfrac{M^{2}}{2EI}\,dx$.

- ‚úÖ For a **cantilever with end point load:** $U = \dfrac{W^{2}L^{3}}{6EI}$.

- ‚úÖ For a **cantilever with UDL:** $U = \dfrac{w^{2}L^{5}}{40EI}$.

- ‚úÖ For a **simply supported beam with central point load:** $U = \dfrac{W^{2}L^{3}}{96EI}$.

- ‚úÖ For a **simply supported beam with UDL:** $U = \dfrac{w^{2}L^{5}}{240EI}$.

- ‚úÖ For an **off-center point load** on a simply supported beam (loads at distance $a$ and $b = L - a$ from supports): $U = \dfrac{W^{2}a^{2}b^{2}}{6LEI}$.

- ‚úÖ **Castigliano's First Theorem** (Force): $\delta = \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial P}$.

- ‚úÖ **Castigliano's First Theorem** (Moment): $\theta = \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial M}$.

- ‚úÖ For **deflection at a point with no load**, use the **dummy load method** ‚ÄĒ apply an imaginary load $Q$, differentiate, then set $Q = 0$.

- ‚úÖ The **deflection** can also be obtained from the relation $U = \dfrac{1}{2}P\delta$ (for linear elastic systems), giving $\delta = \dfrac{2U}{P}$.

- ‚úÖ **Strain energy is always positive** (note the $M^2$ term) ‚ÄĒ it does not depend on the sign of the moment.

- ‚úÖ The **bending contribution dominates** in slender beams ($L/h > 10$); shear strain energy is negligible and ignored in KTU problems unless explicitly asked.

- ‚úÖ For **statically indeterminate beams**, first find reactions using **Castigliano's Second Theorem** (compatibility) before computing strain energy.

- ‚úÖ Always write the **bending moment expression** as a function of $x$ BEFORE integrating ‚ÄĒ this is the step where most errors originate.

- ‚úÖ **Strain energy has units of N¬∑m (or Joules)** ‚ÄĒ verify units in the final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation of Differential Equation for Calculating the Deflection of Beams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Derivation of Differential Equation for Calculating the Deflection of Beams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Differential Equation of the Deflection Curve (Elastic Curve)**
> The differential equation governing the deflected shape of a prismatic beam subjected to bending is the mathematical relationship that connects the **bending moment** $M(x)$ at any section of the beam to the **deflection** $y(x)$ of the neutral axis. It is derived by combining the **bending stress equation** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ with the **mathematical expression for the radius of curvature** of a plane curve.

The most general form of the equation is:

$$EI \cdot \frac{\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}} = M(x)$$

For the vast majority of engineering problems involving **small slopes and small deflections** (the standard KTU assumption), $(dy/dx)^2 \ll 1$, and the equation simplifies to the **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Equation**:

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

Where:
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of Elasticity of the beam material (in N/m¬≤ or MPa)
- $I$ = Second Moment of Area (Moment of Inertia) of the cross-section about the neutral axis (in m‚Āī or mm‚Āī)
- $y$ = Vertical deflection of the beam at distance $x$ from the origin
- $M(x)$ = Bending moment at section $x$

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Bending Banana" Analogy**
> Imagine holding a flexible plastic ruler (the **beam**) horizontally between your palms and gently pressing your thumbs downward at the centre. The ruler bends into a smooth, continuous curve. Now, if you could somehow plot that bent shape on graph paper, you would obtain the **elastic curve** of the beam.

The mathematics of this curved shape is governed by three distinct ideas working together:

1. **The Geometry of Curves** ‚Äď Every bent shape has a *radius of curvature* $R$ at every point. A sharply bent point has a small $R$; a gently sloping point has a large $R$. Calculus provides the exact formula for $R$ in terms of the slope $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ and curvature $\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}$.

2. **The Material's Resistance** ‚Äď The beam material fights back via its stiffness $E$. A stiffer material (steel) bends less; a more compliant material (rubber) bends more.

3. **The Cross-Section's Resistance** ‚Äď The geometric arrangement of the cross-section, captured by $I$, determines how well the section resists bending. A deep I-section has a much higher $I$ than a thin rectangular strip of equal area.

> The product **$EI$** is called the **Flexural Rigidity** of the beam ‚ÄĒ the master parameter that quantifies the beam's *total* resistance to bending. A higher $EI$ means less deflection for a given load.

**Real-World Parallel:** This is precisely why railway tracks use **I-section rails** (high $I$) and not solid square bars of the same weight ‚ÄĒ the engineer is engineering the $EI$ product to minimize the deflection of the rail under the wheel load of a moving train.

---

## Standard KTU Sign Convention (Mandatory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention for Deflection Problems**
> - The **x-axis** is taken along the **undeformed (original) neutral axis** of the beam, directed from left to right.
> - The **y-axis** is taken vertically **upward**.
> - **Positive deflection** ($y > 0$) ‚Üí upward displacement.
> - **Positive bending moment** ($M > 0$) ‚Üí sagging moment (tension on the bottom fibre, compression on the top fibre).
> - **Positive load** $w$ ‚Üí distributed load acting **upward** (but in most practical problems, gravity loads point *downward*, so $w$ is taken as a negative quantity when substituted into the governing equation).

This convention aligns the second derivative $\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}$ directly with the moment $M$ through a single positive sign, eliminating the need for explicit negative signs in the core equation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Foundational Assumptions (Euler-Bernoulli Hypothesis)

The differential equation is rigorously valid **only** when the following five assumptions are satisfied. These are non-negotiable in KTU problems.

1. **The beam material is homogeneous and isotropic**, and obeys **Hooke's Law** perfectly ($\sigma = E \varepsilon$).
2. **The beam is initially straight** and has a uniform cross-section along its length (prismatic).
3. **Plane sections before bending remain plane and normal to the deformed neutral axis** after bending (no shear deformation).
4. **The beam experiences pure bending** ‚ÄĒ the only internal stress considered is the **flexural (bending) stress**. Direct (axial) stress and transverse shear stress are neglected.
5. **Deflections and slopes are infinitesimally small** ‚ÄĒ so small that $(dy/dx)^2$ is negligible compared to unity. This is the assumption that linearizes the curvature equation.

---

## Logical Step-by-Step Reasoning Behind the Derivation

The derivation is a beautiful synthesis of **three independent bodies of knowledge**:

| Step | Knowledge Domain | Key Result Used |
|------|------------------|-----------------|
| **1. Pure Bending Stress** | Strength of Materials (Module 2) | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ |
| **2. Flexure Formula Reduction** | Strength of Materials | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ |
| **3. Curvature from Calculus** | Differential Geometry (Engineering Mathematics) | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}$ |

**The "Why" Behind Each Step:**

- **Step 1** is empirically derived by observing the bending of a long slender beam in a laboratory: fibres in tension stretch proportionally to their distance from the neutral axis, and the longitudinal strain is $\varepsilon = y/R$. Hooke's Law gives $\sigma = E \varepsilon = E y / R$. Force equilibrium on a cross-section gives $M = \int \sigma \cdot y \, dA = (E/R) \int y^2 \, dA = (E/R) \cdot I$. Combining these gives $1/R = M/(EI)$.

- **Step 2** is the **flexure formula**, which says: *the curvature of a beam at any section is directly proportional to the bending moment at that section and inversely proportional to the flexural rigidity of the beam*.

- **Step 3** is the standard formula for the radius of curvature of any plane curve $y = y(x)$ in 2D analytic geometry. It is derived from the parametric definition of curvature.

Equating the curvature from **Step 2** to the curvature from **Step 3** yields the differential equation of the deflection curve.

---

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **master reference table** for solving every KTU problem on this topic. Memorize the equations and units.

| # | Equation | Name / Description | S.I. Units |
|---|----------|-------------------|-----------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | Flexure formula (curvature‚Äďmoment relation) | m‚ĀĽ¬Ļ |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}$ | Exact curvature of a plane curve | m‚ĀĽ¬Ļ |
| 3 | $EI \cdot \dfrac{\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}} = M(x)$ | **General (non-linear) differential equation of the deflection curve** | N¬∑m¬≤ / m‚Āī √ó m‚ĀĽ¬Ļ = N¬∑m / m¬≥ ... matches M |
| 4 | $EI \cdot \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ | **Simplified (linearized) differential equation** ‚ÄĒ valid for small deflections | N¬∑m |
| 5 | $EI \cdot \dfrac{d^3 y}{dx^3} = \dfrac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$ | First integration gives **slope** $\theta$ via $\theta = \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{1}{EI} \int V(x)\, dx + C_1$ | radians |
| 6 | $EI \cdot \dfrac{d^4 y}{dx^4} = \dfrac{dV}{dx} = w(x)$ | Second integration (twice) gives the **deflection** $y$ and introduces **two constants of integration** $C_1, C_2$ | N/m |
| 7 | $EI \cdot y = \iint M(x)\, dx\, dx + C_1 x + C_2$ | The double-integration form used to find the elastic curve directly | N¬∑m¬∑m¬≤ = N¬∑m¬≥ |

---

## Key Auxiliary Engineering Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Meaning |
|----------|--------|---------|---------|
| Slope | $\theta$ | $\theta = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | Angle (in radians) made by the tangent to the elastic curve with the original x-axis |
| Curvature | $\kappa$ | $\kappa = \dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}$ (linearized) | Sharpness of bending at a section |
| Flexural Rigidity | $EI$ | Product of Young's modulus and moment of inertia | Total bending stiffness of the beam |
| Shear Force | $V(x)$ | $V = \dfrac{dM}{dx}$ | Slope of the bending moment diagram |
| Intensity of Distributed Load | $w(x)$ | $w = \dfrac{dV}{dx} = \dfrac{d^2 M}{dx^2}$ | Load per unit length of the beam |

> **Standard loading values for KTU problems:**
> - Self-weight: taken as $w = \rho g A$ (N/m) for the full length.
> - Point load $P$: treated as the limit case of a distributed load over an infinitesimal length, i.e., $w \to P \, \delta(x - x_0)$.
> - Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL): constant $w$ over a span.

---

## Real-World Engineering Utility

The differential equation derived in this module is the **workhorse** of structural analysis. It is used to:

- **Design Floor Beams in Buildings**: Limit the maximum deflection to span / 325 (IS 456) or span / 360 (ACI 318) to avoid visible sagging and cracking of brittle floor finishes.
- **Design Bridge Girders**: Compute camber (pre-camber) required so the bridge deflects to a horizontal line under full traffic load.
- **Design Machine Tool Beds**: Ensure deflections are below micron-level tolerances to maintain machining accuracy.
- **Analyze Aircraft Wings and Helicopter Blades**: Predict flutter and static divergence.
- **Design Crane Jibs and Robot Arms**: Predict tip deflection under payload.
- **Diagnose Structural Failures**: Forensic engineers back-calculate the original $EI$ from observed deflections to detect corrosion, material degradation, or hidden damage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section presents the **complete, line-by-line derivation**. Every algebraic transition is justified. Read slowly, follow each line, and you will own this derivation for life.

## Part A ‚ÄĒ The Exact (Non-Linear) Differential Equation

### Pre-Derivation Setup

Consider a straight prismatic beam AB of length $L$ lying along the x-axis, subjected to a generic system of transverse loads. Under the action of these loads, the beam deflects and assumes the curved shape $y = y(x)$ shown below. The deformed configuration is called the **elastic curve** (or *deflection curve*).

We isolate an elemental segment $ds$ of the beam between two adjacent cross-sections located at arc-lengths $s$ and $s + ds$. The intersection of the two cross-sections defines an angle $d\phi$ at the centre of curvature $O$. The radius of curvature at this location is $R$.

From the geometry of a circular arc:

$$ds = R \, d\phi$$

Hence the curvature at that point is:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{d\phi}{ds}$$

### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Express the Curvature in Cartesian Coordinates

For a plane curve $y = y(x)$, the angle $\phi$ that the tangent to the curve makes with the x-axis satisfies:

$$\tan \phi = \frac{dy}{dx}$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $x$:

$$\sec^2 \phi \cdot \frac{d\phi}{dx} = \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{d\phi}{dx} = \frac{\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}}{\sec^2 \phi} = \frac{\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}}{1 + \tan^2 \phi} = \frac{\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}}{1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2}$$

Now, by the chain rule:

$$\frac{d\phi}{ds} = \frac{d\phi}{dx} \cdot \frac{dx}{ds} = \frac{\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}}{1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2} \cdot \cos \phi$$

Since $\cos \phi = \dfrac{dx}{ds}$, and from the identity $\sec^2 \phi = 1 + \tan^2 \phi$ we have $\cos \phi = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \tan^2 \phi}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (dy/dx)^2}}$, we obtain:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{d\phi}{ds} = \frac{\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}$$

This is the **exact expression for the curvature of a plane curve** in Cartesian form, derived purely from calculus.

### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Express the Curvature Using the Flexure Formula

In the earlier module on bending stresses, we derived the flexure formula, which states that under pure bending, the bending moment $M$ at a section and the radius of curvature $R$ of the neutral axis at that section are related by:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}$$

This result connects *material behaviour* ($E$), *section geometry* ($I$), and the *internal action* ($M$) to the *geometric curvature* ($1/R$).

### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Equate the Two Expressions for Curvature

Since both Step 1 and Step 2 describe the same physical curvature of the beam at the same point, they must be equal:

$$\frac{\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}} = \frac{M(x)}{EI}$$

Multiplying both sides by $EI$:

$$\boxed{EI \cdot \frac{\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}} = M(x)}$$

This is the **General Non-Linear Differential Equation of the Beam Deflection Curve** (also called the **Elastic Line Equation**). It is exact ‚ÄĒ no approximations have been introduced yet.

---

## Part B ‚ÄĒ The Simplified (Linearized) Differential Equation

### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Apply the Small-Deflection Approximation

For all practical engineering problems, the slope of the elastic curve is extremely small. Typical maximum slopes encountered in civil engineering beams are of the order of $\theta \le 1/100$ radian (about 0.6¬į). For such small angles:

$$\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 = \theta^2 \le \left(\frac{1}{100}\right)^2 = 0.0001 \ll 1$$

Hence, to an excellent engineering approximation:

$$1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 \approx 1$$

and

$$\left[1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2} \approx 1^{3/2} = 1$$

### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Reduce the General Equation to the Linear Form

Substituting this approximation into the boxed result of Step 3:

$$\boxed{EI \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)}$$

This is the **linearized differential equation of the deflection curve**, also known as the **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Equation**. It is the starting point for virtually all KTU Module 3 problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **What does "linearized" mean here?**
> The original equation is non-linear in $y$ because of the squared slope term in the denominator. Once we drop this term, the equation becomes a *linear* ordinary differential equation in $y$ ‚ÄĒ solvable by direct integration.

---

## Part C ‚ÄĒ The Successive Integration Method (Double Integration)

The standard KTU-solving procedure is to perform **two successive integrations** of the governing equation.

### Step 6 ‚ÄĒ First Integration (gives the slope $\theta$)

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

$$EI \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = \int M(x)\, dx + C_1 = \int V(x)\, dx + C_1$$

$$\theta(x) = \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{1}{EI} \left[ \int M(x)\, dx + C_1 \right]$$

where $C_1$ is the **first constant of integration**.

### Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Second Integration (gives the deflection $y$)

$$EI \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = \int M(x)\, dx + C_1$$

Integrating once more with respect to $x$:

$$EI \cdot y = \iint M(x)\, dx\, dx + C_1 x + C_2$$

$$\boxed{y(x) = \frac{1}{EI} \left[ \iint M(x)\, dx\, dx + C_1 x + C_2 \right]}$$

where $C_1$ and $C_2$ are the **two constants of integration** that arise from the two integrations. They are determined from the **boundary conditions** specific to the type of support at the ends of the beam.

### Step 8 ‚ÄĒ Common Boundary Conditions Used to Find $C_1$ and $C_2$

| Support Type | Deflection Boundary Condition | Slope Boundary Condition |
|--------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Fixed (Clamped) End** | $y = 0$ | $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$ |
| **Simply Supported (Hinge) End** | $y = 0$ | Moment $M = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = 0$ is **not** the slope condition; the slope is *unknown* |
| **Free End** | Moment $M = 0$ and Shear $V = 0$ | $y$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ are *unknown* but bounded |

### Step 9 ‚ÄĒ Successive Differentiation (Alternative Form)

Sometimes it is convenient to express the loading directly in terms of the deflection. Since $V = \dfrac{dM}{dx}$ and $w = \dfrac{dV}{dx}$:

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^3 y}{dx^3} = \frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$$

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^4 y}{dx^4} = \frac{dV}{dx} = w(x)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{EI \cdot \frac{d^4 y}{dx^4} = w(x)}$$

This is a powerful **fourth-order linear ODE** that, when integrated four times, yields $y$ directly. The four constants of integration are determined by **two geometric boundary conditions** (deflection and slope at supports) and **two natural boundary conditions** (moment and shear at free ends).

---

## Part D ‚ÄĒ Summary of the Complete Derivation Chain

For maximum clarity, here is the derivation chain summarized as an **analytical matrix**:

| Stage | Mathematical Form | Origin / Source | Approximation Introduced |
|-------|-------------------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| (a) Bending stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{E y}{R}$ | Hooke's law + plane sections | None |
| (b) Bending moment | $M = \dfrac{EI}{R}$ | Moment of stress about NA | None |
| (c) Curvature (geometry) | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{d^2 y / dx^2}{[1 + (dy/dx)^2]^{3/2}}$ | Pure calculus | None |
| (d) Equate (b) and (c) | $EI \cdot \dfrac{d^2 y / dx^2}{[1 + (dy/dx)^2]^{3/2}} = M$ | Combination of (b) and (c) | None ‚ÄĒ exact |
| (e) Linearize denominator | $[1 + (dy/dx)^2]^{3/2} \to 1$ | Small slope assumption | **Small deflection** |
| (f) Final ODE | $EI \cdot \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M$ | (d) + (e) | Small deflection |
| (g) Successive integration | $y(x) = \dfrac{1}{EI} \iint M(x)\, dx\, dx + \dfrac{C_1}{EI} x + \dfrac{C_2}{EI}$ | Direct integration of (f) | None |
| (h) Apply BCs | Solve for $C_1, C_2$ | Beam end conditions | None |

---

## Part E ‚ÄĒ Worked Example: Cantilever with Point Load at Free End

To cement the theory, here is a complete worked example applying the derived equation.

**Problem:** A cantilever beam of length $L$ is fixed at $x = 0$ and free at $x = L$. A point load $P$ acts downward at the free end. Find the deflection $y(x)$ and slope $\theta(x)$ at any section. Take $EI$ as constant.

**Step E.1 ‚ÄĒ Bending Moment Function**

At a section located at distance $x$ from the fixed end, consider the right-hand portion. The point load $P$ is at distance $(L - x)$ from the section. The bending moment is:

$$M(x) = -P(L - x) = -PL + Px$$

(negative sign due to KTU sign convention with downward load).

**Step E.2 ‚ÄĒ Governing Equation**

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -P(L - x)$$

**Step E.3 ‚ÄĒ First Integration**

$$EI \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = -P \left(Lx - \frac{x^2}{2}\right) + C_1$$

**Step E.4 ‚ÄĒ Second Integration**

$$EI \cdot y = -P \left(\frac{Lx^2}{2} - \frac{x^3}{6}\right) + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step E.5 ‚ÄĒ Apply Boundary Conditions**

- **BC 1:** At fixed end, $x = 0$: deflection $y = 0$.
  Substituting $x = 0$ and $y = 0$: $\quad 0 - 0 + 0 + C_2 = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$.

- **BC 2:** At fixed end, $x = 0$: slope $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$.
  Substituting $x = 0$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$: $\quad 0 - 0 + C_1 = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = 0$.

**Step E.6 ‚ÄĒ Final Result**

Slope:

$$\theta(x) = \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{1}{EI} \left[ -PLx + \frac{Px^2}{2} \right] = -\frac{Px}{2EI}(2L - x)$$

Deflection:

$$y(x) = \frac{1}{EI} \left[ -\frac{PLx^2}{2} + \frac{Px^3}{6} \right] = -\frac{Px^2}{6EI}(3L - x)$$

The negative sign indicates downward deflection, consistent with our coordinate convention.

**Step E.7 ‚ÄĒ Maximum Deflection (at free end, $x = L$)**

$$y_{max} = y(L) = -\frac{PL^2}{6EI}(3L - L) = -\frac{PL^3}{3EI}$$

This is the **standard formula** for maximum tip deflection of a cantilever with an end point load. ‚úď

**Maximum slope (at free end):**

$$\theta_{max} = \theta(L) = -\frac{PL}{2EI}(2L - L) = -\frac{PL^2}{2EI}$$

This is the **standard formula** for maximum tip slope of a cantilever with an end point load. ‚úď
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1 ‚ÄĒ The Three-Stage Derivation Logic Flow

This flowchart maps the logical flow of the derivation from the three independent knowledge streams to the final governing equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START DERIVATION]:::start
    A1[STAGE 1 PURE BENDING STRESS ANALYSIS]:::stage
    A2[Consider a beam fibre at distance y from neutral axis]:::step
    A3[Apply Hookes Law sigma equals E times y over R]:::step
    A4[Take moment of stress about NA]:::step
    A5[Result M over I equals E over R]:::step
    A6[STAGE 2 DIFFERENTIAL GEOMETRY OF CURVES]:::stage
    A7[Consider the elastic curve y as function of x]:::step
    A8[Define slope as tan phi equals dy over dx]:::step
    A9[Differentiate and use chain rule]:::step
    A10[Result 1 over R equals d2y over dx2 divided by 1 plus dy over dx squared to power 3 over 2]:::step
    A11[STAGE 3 EQUATION MERGER]:::stage
    A12[Both stages describe the same physical curvature at the same point]:::step
    A13[Equate the two expressions for 1 over R]:::step
    A14[STAGE 4 SMALL DEFLECTION APPROXIMATION]:::stage
    A15[Slope dy over dx is very small in practical beams]:::step
    A16[Therefore square of slope is negligible compared to 1]:::step
    A17[Denominator reduces to 1]:::step
    A18[STAGE 5 FINAL GOVERNING EQUATION]:::stage
    A19[E I d2y over dx2 equals M of x]:::final
    A20[This is the Euler Bernoulli Beam Equation]:::final
    A21[Integrate twice to get deflection y as function of x]:::final
    A22[Apply boundary conditions to find constants of integration]:::final
    A23[FINAL ELASTIC CURVE Y OF X IS OBTAINED]:::final

    A0 --> A1

    A5 --> A11

    A10 --> A11
    A11 --> A12 --> A13
    A13 --> A14

    A17 --> A18
    A18 --> A19 --> A20 --> A21 --> A22 --> A23
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Pattern (As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE):**
> - **Part A:** 2 questions √ó 3 marks = 6 marks
> - **Part B:** 1 question √ó 14 marks (with internal choice between A and B) = 14 marks
> - **Module 3 Total Weightage:** ~20 marks in End Semester Exam (combined with Module 3 full content)

---

## Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set A]`

> **Q1.** State the differential equation of the deflection curve of a beam. What is the assumption used to simplify the exact equation into the practical form?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The differential equation of the deflection curve of a beam in its **general (exact) form** is:

$$EI \cdot \frac{\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}} = M(x)$$

The **simplified (linearized) form** used in all practical KTU problems is:

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

**Key Assumption:** The slope of the beam is **very small** (typically $(dy/dx)^2 \le 0.0001$), so the quantity $(dy/dx)^2$ is **negligible** compared to unity. This causes the denominator to be approximated as:

$$\left[1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2} \approx 1$$

and the equation reduces to the linear form, which is a **second-order linear ordinary differential equation** in $y$.

> **Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for general equation, 1 mark for simplified equation, 1 mark for stating and explaining the assumption.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Question Paper]`

> **Q2.** Define **flexural rigidity** of a beam. What is its physical significance? List the **two boundary conditions** for a beam that is fixed at the left end and free at the right end.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Flexural Rigidity** is defined as the **product of Young's Modulus of Elasticity ($E$)** of the beam material and the **Second Moment of Area ($I$)** of the cross-section about the neutral axis.

$$\text{Flexural Rigidity} = E \cdot I \quad \text{(Units: N¬∑m}^2\text{)}$$

**Physical Significance:** It quantifies the **beam's total resistance to bending deformation**. A higher $EI$ value means the beam will deflect less under a given loading. For example, a steel I-beam has a much higher $EI$ than a wooden plank of the same length, so it bends much less under the same load.

**Two Boundary Conditions for a Cantilever (Fixed at left, Free at right):**

| At Fixed End ($x = 0$) | At Free End ($x = L$) |
|--------------------------|------------------------|
| Deflection: $y = 0$ | Bending moment: $M = 0$ |
| Slope: $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$ | Shear force: $V = 0$ |

> **Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for definition + significance, 1 mark for fixed-end conditions, 1 mark for free-end conditions.

---

## Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern:** Each Part B question provides **two alternative questions (A and B)** of 14 marks. The student answers **ONE** of them. Each long question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)** of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q A.(a)** Derive the differential equation of the deflection curve of a beam starting from the **bending equation** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{E}{R}$. State clearly all assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q A.(b)** A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a **uniformly distributed load (UDL)** of intensity $w$ per unit length over the entire span. Using the **double-integration method** and the differential equation of the deflection curve, determine the **maximum slope** and **maximum deflection** of the beam. Take $EI$ as constant. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Bending Equation** (1 mark)

For a beam in pure bending, the flexure formula states:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$

From this:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI} \quad \quad \quad \quad \text{--- (1)}$$

**Step 2: Exact Curvature Equation** (1.5 marks)

The exact radius of curvature of a plane curve $y(x)$ in Cartesian coordinates is:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}} \quad \quad \quad \quad \text{--- (2)}$$

**Step 3: Equating (1) and (2)** (1 mark)

Since both expressions represent the curvature of the same beam at the same section:

$$\frac{\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}} = \frac{M}{EI}$$

$$EI \cdot \frac{\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}} = M(x)$$

**Step 4: Small-Deflection Approximation** (1.5 marks)

For small slopes, $(dy/dx)^2 \ll 1$, so:

$$\left[1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2} \approx 1$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{EI \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)}$$

**Assumptions** (2 marks for clearly stating 4‚Äď5 of these):

1. Beam material is **homogeneous, isotropic**, and obeys **Hooke's Law**.
2. Beam is **initially straight** and has a **uniform cross-section** (prismatic).
3. **Plane sections** before bending remain plane and normal to the neutral axis after bending.
4. **Pure bending** condition ‚ÄĒ only bending stress is considered; shear deformation is neglected.
5. **Deflections and slopes are infinitesimally small** ‚ÄĒ enabling the linearization in Step 4.

---

#### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Simply Supported Beam with UDL (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Reactions** (0.5 mark)

By symmetry: $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2}$ (upward).

**Step 2: Bending Moment Function** (1 mark)

Cut a section at distance $x$ from the left support A. Taking the left-hand portion:

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - w \cdot x \cdot \frac{x}{2} = \frac{wL}{2} x - \frac{w x^2}{2} = \frac{w}{2}(Lx - x^2) = \frac{wx}{2}(L - x)$$

**Step 3: Governing Equation** (0.5 mark)

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = \frac{w}{2}(Lx - x^2)$$

**Step 4: First Integration** (1 mark)

$$EI \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{w}{2} \left(\frac{Lx^2}{2} - \frac{x^3}{3}\right) + C_1$$

$$EI \cdot \theta = \frac{w}{12}(3Lx^2 - 2x^3) + C_1$$

**Step 5: Second Integration** (1 mark)

$$EI \cdot y = \frac{w}{2} \left(\frac{Lx^3}{6} - \frac{x^4}{12}\right) + C_1 x + C_2$$

$$EI \cdot y = \frac{w}{72}(6Lx^3 - 2x^4) + C_1 x + C_2 = \frac{w}{12} \left(Lx^3 - \frac{x^4}{3}\right) + C_1 x + C_2$$

Wait, let me recompute carefully:

$$EI \cdot y = \frac{w}{2}\left(\frac{Lx^3}{6} - \frac{x^4}{12}\right) + C_1 x + C_2 = \frac{w L x^3}{12} - \frac{w x^4}{24} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 6: Apply Boundary Conditions** (1.5 marks)

- **BC 1:** At $x = 0$, $y = 0$ ‚Üí $C_2 = 0$.
- **BC 2:** At $x = L$, $y = 0$ ‚Üí $\dfrac{wL \cdot L^3}{12} - \dfrac{w L^4}{24} + C_1 L = 0$

Solving: $\quad \dfrac{wL^4}{12} - \dfrac{wL^4}{24} + C_1 L = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{2wL^4 - wL^4}{24} + C_1 L = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{wL^4}{24} + C_1 L = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = -\dfrac{wL^3}{24}$

**Step 7: Final Equations** (1 mark)

Slope:

$$EI \cdot \theta = \frac{w}{12}(3Lx^2 - 2x^3) - \frac{wL^3}{24} = \frac{w}{24}(6Lx^2 - 4x^3 - L^3)$$

Deflection:

$$EI \cdot y = \frac{wLx^3}{12} - \frac{wx^4}{24} - \frac{wL^3 x}{24} = \frac{w}{24}(2Lx^3 - x^4 - L^3 x)$$

Or equivalently:

$$y = \frac{wx}{24EI}(L^3 - 2Lx^2 + x^3)$$

**Step 8: Maximum Values** (0.5 mark)

By symmetry, maximum deflection occurs at the **mid-span** $x = L/2$:

$$y_{max} = y(L/2) = \frac{w \cdot (L/2)}{24EI} \left[L^3 - 2L \cdot \frac{L^2}{4} + \frac{L^3}{8}\right] = \frac{wL}{48EI}\left[L^3 - \frac{L^3}{2} + \frac{L^3}{8}\right]$$

$$= \frac{wL}{48EI} \cdot \frac{8L^3 - 4L^3 + L^3}{8} = \frac{wL}{48EI} \cdot \frac{5L^3}{8} = \frac{5wL^4}{384EI}$$

$$\boxed{y_{max} = \frac{5wL^4}{384EI} \quad \text{(downward, at mid-span)}}$$

Maximum slope occurs at the **supports** $x = 0$ and $x = L$. At $x = 0$:

$$\theta_{max} = \frac{1}{EI}\left(0 - 0 - \frac{wL^3}{24}\right) = -\frac{wL^3}{24EI}$$

By symmetry, at $x = L$ the slope is $+\dfrac{wL^3}{24EI}$ in magnitude.

$$\boxed{\vert \theta_{max} \vert = \frac{wL^3}{24EI} \quad \text{(at the supports)}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q B.(a)** Using the **Macaulay's method** (singularity functions), determine the **deflection** at the **free end** of a cantilever beam of length $L$ subjected to a **uniformly distributed load** $w$ per unit length over the entire span. The fixed end is at $x = 0$. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q B.(b)** A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a **point load $P$** at the **mid-span**. Using the differential equation of the deflection curve, find the **slope at the supports** and the **maximum deflection**. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Cantilever with UDL using Macaulay's Method (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Setup** (1 mark)

The fixed end is at $x = 0$, the free end is at $x = L$. The UDL $w$ acts downward over the entire span $0 \le x \le L$.

In Macaulay's bracket notation, the load is expressed as $w \langle x - 0 \rangle^0 = w$ (since the load starts at $x = 0$, no bracket is needed for this term, but we will keep the notation consistent).

**Step 2: Bending Moment in Macaulay's Form** (1.5 marks)

Using the sign convention that downward load creates negative moment (or whichever consistent convention the examiner adopts):

$$M(x) = -\frac{w x^2}{2}$$

**Step 3: Governing Equation in Macaulay's Form** (0.5 mark)

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -\frac{w x^2}{2}$$

**Step 4: First Integration** (0.5 mark)

$$EI \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{w x^3}{6} + C_1$$

**Step 5: Second Integration** (0.5 mark)

$$EI \cdot y = -\frac{w x^4}{24} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 6: Apply Boundary Conditions** (2 marks)

- **At fixed end** $x = 0$: $y = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$.
- **At fixed end** $x = 0$: $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = 0$.

**Step 7: Final Result** (1 mark)

$$y(x) = -\frac{w x^4}{24 EI}$$

The negative sign indicates **downward deflection**.

**Step 8: Deflection at Free End** (1 mark)

At $x = L$:

$$y_{free} = -\frac{w L^4}{24 EI}$$

$$\boxed{y_{max} = \frac{wL^4}{24EI} \quad \text{(downward at free end)}}$$

This is the **standard formula** for maximum deflection of a cantilever with UDL.

---

#### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Reactions** (0.5 mark)

By symmetry: $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{P}{2}$ (upward).

**Step 2: Bending Moment in Macaulay's Form** (1.5 marks)

Using Macaulay's brackets to handle the discontinuity at the load point $x = L/2$:

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - P \langle x - L/2 \rangle^1 = \frac{P x}{2} - P \langle x - L/2 \rangle$$

**Step 3: Governing Equation** (0.5 mark)

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = \frac{P x}{2} - P \langle x - L/2 \rangle$$

**Step 4: First Integration** (1 mark)

$$EI \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{P x^2}{4} - \frac{P \langle x - L/2 \rangle^2}{2} + C_1$$

**Step 5: Second Integration** (1 mark)

$$EI \cdot y = \frac{P x^3}{12} - \frac{P \langle x - L/2 \rangle^3}{6} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 6: Apply Boundary Conditions** (1.5 marks)

- **At $x = 0$, $y = 0$**: $\quad 0 - 0 + 0 + C_2 = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$.
- **At $x = L$, $y = 0$**: $\quad \dfrac{PL^3}{12} - \dfrac{P(L/2)^3}{6} + C_1 L = 0$

$\dfrac{PL^3}{12} - \dfrac{PL^3}{48} + C_1 L = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{4PL^3 - PL^3}{48} + C_1 L = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{3PL^3}{48} + C_1 L = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{PL^3}{16} + C_1 L = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = -\dfrac{PL^2}{16}$

**Step 7: Final Deflection Equation** (0.5 mark)

$$y(x) = \frac{1}{EI}\left[\frac{Px^3}{12} - \frac{P \langle x - L/2 \rangle^3}{6} - \frac{PL^2 x}{16}\right]$$

**Step 8: Slope at Supports** (0.5 mark)

At $x = 0$:

$$EI \cdot \theta(0) = C_1 = -\frac{PL^2}{16} \Rightarrow \theta_A = -\frac{PL^2}{16EI}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_A = -\frac{PL^2}{16EI} \quad \text{(clockwise, at left support)}}$$

By symmetry, $\theta_B = +\dfrac{PL^2}{16EI}$ (counter-clockwise, at right support).

**Step 9: Maximum Deflection at Mid-Span** (0.5 mark)

At $x = L/2$, the Macaulay term $\langle x - L/2 \rangle = 0$, so:

$$y(L/2) = \frac{1}{EI}\left[\frac{P(L/2)^3}{12} - 0 - \frac{PL^2 \cdot (L/2)}{16}\right] = \frac{1}{EI}\left[\frac{PL^3}{96} - \frac{PL^3}{32}\right] = \frac{1}{EI} \cdot \frac{PL^3 - 3PL^3}{96} = -\frac{2PL^3}{96EI} = -\frac{PL^3}{48EI}$$

$$\boxed{y_{max} = \frac{PL^3}{48EI} \quad \text{(downward, at mid-span)}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Watch out for these specific traps where students lose marks every semester:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Error (‚Äď3 to ‚Äď4 marks):** The single most common mistake is **inconsistent sign convention**. The KTU expected convention is $y$ positive upward, positive $M$ for sagging, and **downward loads yield negative $M$ in the ODE**. Many students blindly assume $M = +wL^2/8$ for a UDL on a simply supported beam without setting up the cut section. Always **derive** $M(x)$ from first principles using a free-body diagram, never memorize the formula.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Small-Deflection Approximation Justification (‚Äď2 marks):** Examiners expect a **statement of the assumption** $(dy/dx)^2 \ll 1$ and an explicit note that the denominator $[1 + (dy/dx)^2]^{3/2}$ reduces to **1** under this assumption. Skipping this step loses 2 full marks in Part A.(a) of derivation questions.
>
> 3. **Algebraic Error in Double Integration (‚Äď2 marks):** Particularly, students make sign errors when integrating $-(L-x)$ or $-\frac{wx^2}{2}$. Always re-differentiate your final $y(x)$ to check whether you recover the original $M(x)$.
>
> 4. **Macaulay's Bracket Misuse (‚Äď3 marks):** Macaulay's brackets $\langle x - a \rangle^n$ are **defined to be zero when $x < a$** and equal to $(x - a)^n$ when $x > a$. When applying boundary conditions, you must **substitute $x = a$ exactly**, not treat the bracket as a continuous function. The constant $C_1$ in a single-span problem must satisfy ALL boundary conditions, not just one.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the Two Constants of Integration (‚Äď1 to ‚Äď2 marks):** Every double integration introduces **two constants $C_1$ and $C_2$**. If you only write one or skip them entirely, marks are deducted. State the number of constants explicitly.
>
> 6. **Units Confusion (‚Äď1 mark):** $E$ in N/m¬≤ and $I$ in m‚Āī gives $EI$ in N¬∑m¬≤, but $M$ is in N¬∑m. So $EI \cdot (d^2y/dx^2)$ has units N¬∑m¬≤ √ó (m/m¬≤) = N¬∑m. ‚úď Match the moment units. State units explicitly in Part A questions.

---

## ūüďĆ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** ‚ÄĒ Read this 5 minutes before the exam.

- [x] The **General Non-Linear ODE** is: $EI \cdot \dfrac{d^2 y / dx^2}{[1 + (dy/dx)^2]^{3/2}} = M(x)$.
- [x] The **Linearized ODE** (used 99% of the time) is: $EI \cdot \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$.
- [x] The **linearization** is justified by the **small-slope assumption**: $(dy/dx)^2 \ll 1$.
- [x] **Flexural Rigidity** $EI$ has units of **N¬∑m¬≤** (or N¬∑mm¬≤). $E$ is in N/m¬≤, $I$ is in m‚Āī.
- [x] The **slope** is $\theta = dy/dx$, and the **curvature** (linearized) is $\kappa = d^2y/dx^2$.
- [x] Sign convention: $y$ up positive, $M$ positive for sagging, **downward loads create negative $M$ in the ODE**.
- [x] **Double integration** introduces **two constants of integration** $C_1$ and $C_2$.
- [x] **Boundary conditions:**
  - **Fixed end:** $y = 0$ and $dy/dx = 0$.
  - **Simply supported end:** $y = 0$ (slope is NOT zero ‚ÄĒ it is the unknown we solve for).
  - **Free end:** $M = 0$ and $V = 0$.
- [x] **Macaulay's brackets:** $\langle x - a \rangle^n$ is **zero when $x < a$** and **$(x-a)^n$ when $x > a$**.
- [x] **Successive differentiation chain:** $EI \, d^4y/dx^4 = w(x) \rightarrow V(x) \rightarrow M(x)$.
- [x] The five **Euler-Bernoulli assumptions** must be stated explicitly in derivation questions.
- [x] Always **re-differentiate** your final $y(x)$ to verify you recover $M(x)$ ‚ÄĒ this catches 80% of integration errors.
- [x] **Maximum deflection locations:**
  - **Cantilever** ‚Üí at the **free end**.
  - **Simply supported beam with central/symmetric load** ‚Üí at the **mid-span**.
  - **Simply supported beam with asymmetric load** ‚Üí at the location where $dy/dx = 0$ (slope is zero).
- [x] **Standard Results to Memorize:**

| Beam Configuration | Maximum Deflection | Maximum Slope (at supports) |
|---------------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| Cantilever, point load $P$ at free end | $\dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}$ | $\dfrac{PL^2}{2EI}$ |
| Cantilever, UDL $w$ over full span | $\dfrac{wL^4}{8EI}$ | $\dfrac{wL^3}{6EI}$ |
| Simply supported, point load $P$ at centre | $\dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$ | $\dfrac{PL^2}{16EI}$ |
| Simply supported, UDL $w$ over full span | $\dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$ | $\dfrac{wL^3}{24EI}$ |

- [x] **KTU word "derive"** means show *all* steps from the bending equation; **"obtain"** means start from the linearized form and integrate; **"find"** means just compute the result.
- [x] The **differential equation is the foundation** of the **method of superposition**, **Macaulay's method**, and the **moment-area method** ‚ÄĒ all of which extend the same basic $EI \cdot d^2y/dx^2 = M$ relation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Macaulay's Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Macaulay's Method # SECTION 1 ‚ÄĒ Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Formal Definition **Macaulay's Method** (also known as the **Method of Singularity Functions** or **Bracketed Functions**) is a mathematical technique used in the elastic analysis of beams to determine the **slope** and **deflection** at any point along the beam's span, particularly when the beam is subjected to a combination of **point loads**, **concentrated couples**, and **uniformly distributed loads (UDL)**. The method leverages the governing differential equation of the elastic curve, $$EI \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M(x)$$ and uses **singularity functions** $\langle x - a \rangle^{n}$ to represent the bending moment in a **single continuous expression** valid for the **entire span**, thereby eliminating the need to write separate moment equations for each segment of the beam. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Singularity Function Notation:** A "broken" or Macaulay bracket $\langle x - a \rangle^{n}$ is defined as: > $$\langle x - a \rangle^{n} = \begin{cases} 0, & x < a \\ (x - a)^{n}, & x \ge a \end{cases}$$ > The bracket **suppresses** the term whenever $x < a$, effectively "switching on" the contribution at the point of load application $x = a$. --- ## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a beam as a **flexible ruler** resting on two supports, and the loads (people standing on it) as **events that occur at specific locations**. In conventional methods, you would have to write *one equation* for the portion *before* the first person stands, *another equation* for the portion *between* two people, and so on. This creates a **patchwork of equations** that must be matched at the boundaries ‚ÄĒ a tedious and error-prone process. **Macaulay's brackets act like a smart light switch system**: each term in the moment equation is connected to a "switch" that turns ON only when you cross the point where the load is applied. As you walk from left to right along the beam, the switches turn on one by one, automatically building up the correct moment at each location ‚ÄĒ all within a **single, unified equation**. This dramatically simplifies the integration process and the application of boundary conditions. > [!NOTE] > **Sagging Convention:** In KTU's standard sign convention, a **sagging moment is positive**, deflections are measured **upward as positive**, and the origin $x = 0$ is conventionally taken at the **left support** of the beam. --- ## Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Young's Modulus ($E$):** Material stiffness; units **N/m¬≤** or **Pa** (typical: Steel $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ **N/mm¬≤**). - **Second Moment of Area ($I$):** Cross-sectional geometric property; units **mm‚Āī** or **m‚Āī**. - **Flexural Rigidity ($EI$):** Combined resistance to bending; units **N¬∑m¬≤** or **N¬∑mm¬≤**. > [!TIP] > Always keep $EI$ symbolic throughout the calculation. Only substitute numerical values **at the final step** to avoid computational errors and to preserve dimensional clarity. # SECTION 2 ‚ÄĒ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Macaulay's Method ### The Operational Logic ‚ÄĒ Step by Step 1. **Compute Support Reactions** using static equilibrium ($\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M = 0$). 2. **Establish a Coordinate System** with $x = 0$ at the left end (free end of cantilever preferred for cantilevers to simplify). 3. **Construct the Bending Moment Expression $M(x)$** as a single equation using Macaulay brackets, *including the reaction moments/forces and all applied loads*. 4. **Apply the Governing Differential Equation** $EI \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M(x)$. 5. **Integrate Twice** ‚ÄĒ once to obtain the **slope equation** $EI \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ and again to obtain the **deflection equation** $EIy$ ‚ÄĒ producing **two constants of integration**, $C_1$ and $C_2$. 6. **Apply Boundary Conditions** (e.g., $y = 0$ at supports, $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at fixed ends) to solve for $C_1$ and $C_2$. 7. **Retrieve Required Values** by substituting specific $x$ coordinates into the slope and deflection equations. ### Critical Integration Rules (High-Yield for KTU Exams) > [!IMPORTANT] > **RULE 1:** When integrating $\langle x - a \rangle^{n}$, you get $\dfrac{\langle x - a \rangle^{n+1}}{n+1}$ **only if $n \ge 0$**. The bracket form is **retained** at all times. > > **RULE 2:** When applying boundary conditions at $x = a$, any term containing $\langle x - a \rangle^{n}$ where $n > 0$ **vanishes** (since the bracket evaluates to zero at the boundary point). > > **RULE 3:** Terms with $n = 0$ (i.e., concentrated couples) **persist** as constants at $x = a$. > > **RULE 4:** Negative powers ($n < 0$) are *not* used in this method. --- ## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet ### Table 1 ‚ÄĒ Singularity Function Templates for Bending Moment | Load Type | Macaulay Contribution to $M(x)$ | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | UDL of intensity $w$ (downward) from $x = a$ | $-\dfrac{w}{2} \langle x - a \rangle^{2}$ | Load distributed along length, intensifies parabolically | | Point load $W$ (downward) at $x = a$ | $-W \langle x - a \rangle^{1}$ | Abrupt step in shear, linear change in moment | | Clockwise couple $M_0$ at $x = a$ | $+M_0 \langle x - a \rangle^{0}$ | Abrupt jump in moment at the section | | Reaction $R$ (upward) at $x = 0$ | $+R \cdot x$ | Linear moment build-up from reaction | ### Table 2 ‚ÄĒ Standard Integration Results (For Quick Reference) | Singularity Term | First Integral | Second Integral | |---|---|---| | $\langle x - a \rangle^{0}$ | $\langle x - a \rangle^{1}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2} \langle x - a \rangle^{2}$ | | $\langle x - a \rangle^{1}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2} \langle x - a \rangle^{2}$ | $\dfrac{1}{6} \langle x - a \rangle^{3}$ | | $\langle x - a \rangle^{2}$ | $\dfrac{1}{3} \langle x - a \rangle^{3}$ | $\dfrac{1}{12} \langle x - a \rangle^{4}$ | | $\langle x - a \rangle^{3}$ | $\dfrac{1}{4} \langle x - a \rangle^{4}$ | $\dfrac{1}{20} \langle x - a \rangle^{5}$ | ### Table 3 ‚ÄĒ Standard Boundary Conditions for Common Beams | Beam Type | Boundary Condition 1 | Boundary Condition 2 | |---|---|---| | Simply Supported | $y = 0$ at $x = 0$ | $y = 0$ at $x = L$ | | Fixed (Propped) at one end | $y = 0$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at fixed end | $y = 0$ at simply supported end | | Cantilever (origin at fixed end) | $y = 0$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at $x = 0$ | ‚ÄĒ | | Cantilever (origin at free end) | $y = 0$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at $x = L$ | ‚ÄĒ | > [!TIP] > **Pro Tip for Cantilevers:** Always take the **free end as $x = 0$** for cantilevers. This is because the bending moment at the free end is zero, eliminating the need to add a corrective moment at the origin. The UDL/contribution from the fixed end then naturally appears in your integration constants. --- ## Real-World Engineering Utility Macaulay's method is extensively used in: - **Structural Steel Design** (beams in buildings, bridges, and crane girders). - **Reinforced Concrete Beam Design** (computing serviceability deflections to satisfy IS 456 codal limits of $L/250$ or $L/350$). - **Machine Tool Frames** where deflection control is critical for precision. - **Aerospace Structures** (wing spar analysis) where analytical tractability outweighs the use of numerical methods like FEM. - **Ship Hull Girder Analysis** to evaluate hogging and sagging deflections. # SECTION 3 ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Governing Differential Equation Consider an infinitesimal beam element of length $dx$ subjected to a bending moment $M(x)$. From the Euler‚ÄďBernoulli beam theory, the curvature $\kappa$ of the neutral axis is related to the moment by: $$M(x) = \frac{EI}{\rho} = \frac{EI}{(1 + (y')^{2})^{3/2}} \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$$ For small deflections (slope $y' \ll 1$), $(y')^{2}$ is negligible, simplifying to: $$M(x) = EI \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$$ This is the **fundamental governing equation** for beam deflection. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example 1 ‚ÄĒ Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load **Problem Statement:** A simply supported beam of span $L = 6$ **m** carries a concentrated load $W = 36$ **kN** at the midspan. Using Macaulay's method, determine the **slope at the supports** and the **maximum deflection**. ### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Compute Support Reactions By symmetry: $$R_A = R_B = \frac{W}{2} = \frac{36}{2} = 18 \text{ kN}$$ ### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Write the Bending Moment Equation in Macaulay's Form Taking $x$ from the left support, the load $W$ is applied at $x = L/2 = 3$ m. The moment at any section is: $$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - W \langle x - 3 \rangle^{1}$$ Substituting $R_A = 18$ kN: $$M(x) = 18x - 36 \langle x - 3 \rangle^{1} \quad \text{(in kN¬∑m)}$$ ### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Apply the Governing Differential Equation $$EI \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = 18x - 36 \langle x - 3 \rangle^{1}$$ ### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ First Integration (Slope Equation) Using Rule 1 from Section 2: $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = 18 \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{2} - 36 \cdot \frac{\langle x - 3 \rangle^{2}}{2} + C_{1}$$ Simplifying: $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = 9x^{2} - 18 \langle x - 3 \rangle^{2} + C_{1}$$ ### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Second Integration (Deflection Equation) $$EIy = 9 \cdot \frac{x^{3}}{3} - 18 \cdot \frac{\langle x - 3 \rangle^{3}}{3} + C_{1}x + C_{2}$$ Simplifying: $$EIy = 3x^{3} - 6 \langle x - 3 \rangle^{3} + C_{1}x + C_{2}$$ ### Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Apply Boundary Conditions **BC 1:** At $x = 0$, $y = 0$: $$EIy\big|_{x=0} = 0 - 0 + 0 + C_{2} = 0 \implies C_{2} = 0$$ **BC 2:** At $x = L = 6$ m, $y = 0$ (the bracket $\langle 6 - 3 \rangle^{3}$ is now active): $$EIy\big|_{x=6} = 3(6)^{3} - 6(3)^{3} + C_{1}(6) = 0$$ $$3(216) - 6(27) + 6C_{1} = 0$$ $$648 - 162 + 6C_{1} = 0$$ $$486 + 6C_{1} = 0$$ $$C_{1} = -81 \text{ kN¬∑m}^{2}$$ ### Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Final Slope and Deflection Equations $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = 9x^{2} - 18 \langle x - 3 \rangle^{2} - 81$$ $$EIy = 3x^{3} - 6 \langle x - 3 \rangle^{3} - 81x$$ ### Step 8 ‚ÄĒ Compute Slope at the Supports **At $x = 0$ (Left support):** The bracket term vanishes. $$EI \frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = 0 - 0 - 81 = -81 \text{ kN¬∑m}^{2}$$ $$\theta_A = \frac{-81}{EI} \text{ rad}$$ **At $x = L = 6$ m (Right support):** $$EI \frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{x=6} = 9(36) - 18(9) - 81 = 324 - 162 - 81 = 81 \text{ kN¬∑m}^{2}$$ $$\theta_B = \frac{81}{EI} \text{ rad}$$ ### Step 9 ‚ÄĒ Compute Maximum Deflection at Midspan ($x = 3$ m) At $x = 3$ m, the bracket $\langle 3 - 3 \rangle^{3} = 0$ (since $3 < 3$ is false, but $3 - 3 = 0$ and $0^{3} = 0$): $$EIy\big|_{x=3} = 3(27) - 6(0) - 81(3) = 81 - 0 - 243 = -162 \text{ kN¬∑m}^{3}$$ $$y_{\max} = \frac{-162}{EI} \text{ m}$$ Substituting $W = 36$ kN and $L = 6$ m into the standard formula $y_{\max} = \dfrac{-WL^{3}}{48EI}$: $$y_{\max} = \frac{-36 \times 216}{48EI} = \frac{-7776}{48EI} = \frac{-162}{EI} \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$ > [!NOTE] > **The result matches the standard formula**, confirming the correctness of the Macaulay's method application. --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example 2 ‚ÄĒ Simply Supported Beam with UDL over Entire Span **Problem Statement:** A simply supported beam of span $L = 8$ **m** carries a UDL of $w = 10$ **kN/m** over the entire span. Determine the **maximum slope and deflection** using Macaulay's method. ### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Compute Support Reactions By symmetry: $$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{10 \times 8}{2} = 40 \text{ kN}$$ ### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Bending Moment Equation in Macaulay's Form The UDL is applied from $x = 0$ to $x = L$. Therefore: $$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - \frac{w}{2} \langle x - 0 \rangle^{2} = 40x - 5x^{2}$$ (Note: When the load starts at the origin, the Macaulay bracket $\langle x - 0 \rangle^{2}$ simply equals $x^{2}$.) ### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ First Integration $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = 40 \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{2} - 5 \cdot \frac{x^{3}}{3} + C_{1}$$ $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = 20x^{2} - \frac{5x^{3}}{3} + C_{1}$$ ### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Second Integration $$EIy = 20 \cdot \frac{x^{3}}{3} - 5 \cdot \frac{x^{4}}{12} + C_{1}x + C_{2}$$ $$EIy = \frac{20x^{3}}{3} - \frac{5x^{4}}{12} + C_{1}x + C_{2}$$ ### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Apply Boundary Conditions **BC 1:** At $x = 0$, $y = 0$: $$0 = 0 - 0 + 0 + C_{2} \implies C_{2} = 0$$ **BC 2:** At $x = L = 8$ m, $y = 0$: $$0 = \frac{20(512)}{3} - \frac{5(4096)}{12} + 8C_{1}$$ $$0 = \frac{10240}{3} - \frac{20480}{12} + 8C_{1}$$ $$0 = \frac{40960}{12} - \frac{20480}{12} + 8C_{1}$$ $$0 = \frac{20480}{12} + 8C_{1}$$ $$8C_{1} = -\frac{20480}{12} = -\frac{5120}{3}$$ $$C_{1} = -\frac{640}{3} \approx -213.33 \text{ kN¬∑m}^{2}$$ ### Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Final Equations $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = 20x^{2} - \frac{5x^{3}}{3} - \frac{640}{3}$$ $$EIy = \frac{20x^{3}}{3} - \frac{5x^{4}}{12} - \frac{640x}{3}$$ ### Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Slope and Deflection at Midspan ($x = L/2 = 4$ m) By symmetry, midspan is where slope is zero and deflection is maximum. **Slope at $x = 4$ m:** $$EI \frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{x=4} = 20(16) - \frac{5(64)}{3} - \frac{640}{3} = 320 - \frac{320}{3} - \frac{640}{3} = 320 - \frac{960}{3} = 320 - 320 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$ **Deflection at $x = 4$ m:** $$EIy\big|_{x=4} = \frac{20(64)}{3} - \frac{5(256)}{12} - \frac{640(4)}{3}$$ $$= \frac{1280}{3} - \frac{1280}{12} - \frac{2560}{3}$$ $$= \frac{5120}{12} - \frac{1280}{12} - \frac{10240}{12} = \frac{5120 - 1280 - 10240}{12} = \frac{-6400}{12} = -\frac{1600}{3} \text{ kN¬∑m}^{3}$$ **Verification with standard formula** $y_{\max} = \dfrac{5wL^{4}}{384EI}$: $$y_{\max} = \frac{5 \times 10 \times 4096}{384EI} = \frac{204800}{384EI} = \frac{1600}{3EI} \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$ --- ## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example 3 ‚ÄĒ Cantilever with UDL **Problem Statement:** A cantilever beam of length $L = 4$ **m** carries a UDL of $w = 15$ **kN/m** over the entire span. Using Macaulay's method (with origin at the free end), determine the **slope and deflection at the free end**. ### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Bending Moment Equation Taking $x = 0$ at the free end and $x = L = 4$ m at the fixed end: $$M(x) = -\frac{w}{2} \langle x - 0 \rangle^{2} = -\frac{15}{2} x^{2} = -7.5 x^{2} \text{ kN¬∑m}$$ ### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ First Integration $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{7.5 x^{3}}{3} + C_{1} = -2.5 x^{3} + C_{1}$$ ### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Second Integration $$EIy = -\frac{2.5 x^{4}}{4} + C_{1}x + C_{2} = -0.625 x^{4} + C_{1}x + C_{2}$$ ### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Apply Boundary Conditions at Fixed End ($x = L = 4$ m) **BC 1:** $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at $x = 4$ m: $$0 = -2.5(64) + C_{1} \implies C_{1} = 160 \text{ kN¬∑m}^{2}$$ **BC 2:** $y = 0$ at $x = 4$ m: $$0 = -0.625(256) + 160(4) + C_{2} = -160 + 640 + C_{2} \implies C_{2} = -480 \text{ kN¬∑m}^{3}$$ ### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Evaluate at Free End ($x = 0$) **Slope at free end:** $$EI \frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = -2.5(0) + 160 = 160 \text{ kN¬∑m}^{2}$$ $$\theta_{\text{free}} = \frac{160}{EI} \text{ rad}$$ **Deflection at free end:** $$EIy\big|_{x=0} = -0.625(0) + 0 + (-480) = -480 \text{ kN¬∑m}^{3}$$ $$\delta_{\text{free}} = \frac{-480}{EI} \text{ m (downward)}$$ **Verification with standard formulas** $\theta = \dfrac{wL^{3}}{6EI}$ and $\delta = \dfrac{wL^{4}}{8EI}$: $$\theta = \frac{15 \times 64}{6EI} = \frac{960}{6EI} = \frac{160}{EI} \text{ rad} \quad \checkmark$$ $$\delta = \frac{15 \times 256}{8EI} = \frac{3840}{8EI} = \frac{480}{EI} \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$ # SECTION 4 ‚ÄĒ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Macaulay's Method The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the systematic procedure of Macaulay's method from problem input to final solution extraction. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start: Beam with Loads and Geometry]) --> Step1[Step 1: Compute Support Reactions
using Static Equilibrium] Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Set Coordinate System
x equals 0 at Left End or Free End] Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Construct M of x
using Macaulay Brackets] Step3 --> Step4[Step 4: Apply Governing Equation
EI d2y by dx2 equals M of x] Step4 --> Step5[Step 5: First Integration
Obtain Slope Equation with C1] Step5 --> Step6[Step 6: Second Integration
Obtain Deflection Equation with C1 x and C2] Step6 --> Step7[Step 7: Apply Boundary Conditions
y equals 0 at Supports and dy by dx equals 0 at Fixed Ends] Step7 --> Step8[Step 8: Solve for C1 and C2] Step8 --> Step9[Step 9: Substitute Constants
into Slope and Deflection Equations] Step9 --> Step10[Step 10: Evaluate at Required x
to Find Slope and Deflection] Step10 --> Finish([Finish: Report Final Answers]) Step3 -.-> Note1[Note: Include Reactions as First Terms in M of x] Step5 -.-> Note2[Note: Use Rule that bracket n integrates to bracket n plus 1 over n plus 1] style Start fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Finish fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Step1 fill:#E0F6FF,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 style Step2 fill:#E0F6FF,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 style Step3 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 style Step4 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 style Step5 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B6914,color:#000 style Step6 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B6914,color:#000 style Step7 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style Step8 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style Step9 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 style Step10 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 style Note1 fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style Note2 fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Sign Convention Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Load Applied at x equals a] --> B{Type of Load?} B -->|UDL w Downward| C[Add minus w by 2 times bracket x minus a to the power 2] B -->|Point Load W Downward| D[Add minus W times bracket x minus a to the power 1] B -->|Couple M0 Clockwise| E[Add plus M0 times bracket x minus a to the power 0] B -->|Reaction R Upward at x equals 0| F[Add plus R times x] C --> G[Append to M of x Expression] D --> G E --> G F --> G style A fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 style B fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style D fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style E fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style F fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > The **Sign Convention Decision Tree** above provides a rapid visual reference for the sign of each singularity function contribution, helping students avoid the most common sign errors in KTU exams. # SECTION 5 ‚ÄĒ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023, Model Paper] **Define Macaulay's method. What are singularity functions, and how are they used in beam deflection analysis?** **Model Answer:** Macaulay's method is a technique for determining the slope and deflection of beams subjected to various loading conditions, by expressing the bending moment in a single continuous equation using singularity functions. Singularity functions, denoted by broken brackets $\langle x - a \rangle^{n}$, are mathematical constructs defined as: $$\langle x - a \rangle^{n} = \begin{cases} 0, & x < a \\ (x - a)^{n}, & x \ge a \end{cases}$$ These functions allow discontinuous loads (point loads, couples, or UDLs starting at specific points) to be incorporated into a single expression for $M(x)$, which can then be integrated twice using the beam's governing differential equation to obtain slope and deflection. **The key advantage is the elimination of multiple moment equations for different beam segments.** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2024, Model Paper] **State the rules to be followed while integrating the bending moment equation using Macaulay's method.** **Model Answer:** The rules for integrating Macaulay's brackets are: 1. **Integration Rule:** $\langle x - a \rangle^{n}$ integrates to $\dfrac{\langle x - a \rangle^{n+1}}{n+1}$ for all $n \ge 0$. The bracket form is **retained throughout** the integration process. 2. **Boundary Condition Rule:** When applying a boundary condition at $x = a$, any term containing $\langle x - a \rangle^{n}$ where $n > 0$ **vanishes** (evaluates to zero) at that point. Only $n = 0$ terms persist. 3. **Singularity Function Exponent:** The exponent $n$ must be a **non-negative integer** ($n = 0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots$). Negative exponents are not permitted in this method. 4. **Sign Convention:** A downward UDL of intensity $w$ contributes $-\dfrac{w}{2} \langle x - a \rangle^{2}$, a downward point load $W$ contributes $-W \langle x - a \rangle^{1}$, and a clockwise couple $M_0$ contributes $+M_0 \langle x - a \rangle^{0}$ to $M(x)$ (assuming the standard sagging-positive convention). --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice) ### Question A [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2023, Model Paper] **(a)** A simply supported beam of span **6 m** carries a point load of **40 kN** at **2 m from the left support**. Using Macaulay's method, find the slope at the left support and the deflection under the load. Take $EI = 2 \times 10^{4}$ **kN¬∑m¬≤**. **(7 marks)** #### Solution: **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Reactions:** Taking moments about the right support ($B$): $$R_A \times 6 = 40 \times 4 \implies R_A = \frac{160}{6} = \frac{80}{3} \approx 26.67 \text{ kN}$$ From $\sum F_y = 0$: $$R_B = 40 - 26.67 = 13.33 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Macaulay Moment Equation:** $$M(x) = \frac{80}{3}x - 40 \langle x - 2 \rangle^{1} \text{ kN¬∑m}$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Apply Governing Equation:** $$EI \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = \frac{80}{3}x - 40 \langle x - 2 \rangle^{1}$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ First Integration:** $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{40}{3} x^{2} - 20 \langle x - 2 \rangle^{2} + C_{1}$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Second Integration:** $$EIy = \frac{40}{9} x^{3} - \frac{20}{3} \langle x - 2 \rangle^{3} + C_{1}x + C_{2}$$ **Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Boundary Conditions:** At $x = 0$, $y = 0$: $\quad C_{2} = 0$ At $x = 6$ m, $y = 0$: $$0 = \frac{40}{9}(216) - \frac{20}{3}(64) + 6C_{1}$$ $$0 = 960 - \frac{1280}{3} + 6C_{1}$$ $$0 = \frac{2880 - 1280}{3} + 6C_{1} = \frac{1600}{3} + 6C_{1}$$ $$C_{1} = -\frac{800}{9} \text{ kN¬∑m}^{2}$$ **Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Slope at Left Support ($x = 0$):** $$EI \frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = 0 - 0 - \frac{800}{9} = -\frac{800}{9}$$ $$\theta_A = \frac{-800/9}{2 \times 10^{4}} = -\frac{800}{9 \times 2 \times 10^{4}} = -4.44 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$ **Step 8 ‚ÄĒ Deflection Under Load ($x = 2$ m):** At $x = 2$ m, $\langle 2 - 2 \rangle^{3} = 0$: $$EIy\big|_{x=2} = \frac{40}{9}(8) - 0 - \frac{800}{9}(2) = \frac{320}{9} - \frac{1600}{9} = -\frac{1280}{9}$$ $$y_{x=2} = \frac{-1280/9}{2 \times 10^{4}} = -7.11 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = -7.11 \text{ mm}$$ --- **(b)** A simply supported beam of span **8 m** carries a UDL of **20 kN/m** over the **left half of the span**. Find the **maximum deflection** using Macaulay's method. **(7 marks)** #### Solution: **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Reactions:** Taking moments about the right support: $$R_A \times 8 = (20 \times 4) \times 6 = 480 \implies R_A = 60 \text{ kN}$$ $$R_B = (20 \times 4) - 60 = 20 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Macaulay Moment Equation:** The UDL extends from $x = 0$ to $x = 4$ m. To express this in Macaulay form, we use the principle that a UDL of length $L'$ starting at $x = 0$ and ending at $x = a$ is equivalent to a UDL from $0$ to $\infty$ minus a UDL from $a$ to $\infty$: $$M(x) = 60x - \frac{20}{2}\langle x - 0 \rangle^{2} + \frac{20}{2}\langle x - 4 \rangle^{2}$$ $$M(x) = 60x - 10x^{2} + 10 \langle x - 4 \rangle^{2}$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ First Integration:** $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = 30x^{2} - \frac{10x^{3}}{3} + \frac{10}{3}\langle x - 4 \rangle^{3} + C_{1}$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Second Integration:** $$EIy = 10x^{3} - \frac{10x^{4}}{12} + \frac{10}{12}\langle x - 4 \rangle^{4} + C_{1}x + C_{2}$$ $$EIy = 10x^{3} - \frac{5x^{4}}{6} + \frac{5}{6}\langle x - 4 \rangle^{4} + C_{1}x + C_{2}$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Boundary Conditions:** At $x = 0$, $y = 0$: $\quad C_{2} = 0$ At $x = 8$ m, $y = 0$: $$0 = 10(512) - \frac{5(4096)}{6} + \frac{5(4)^{4}}{6} + 8C_{1}$$ $$0 = 5120 - \frac{20480}{6} + \frac{1280}{6} + 8C_{1}$$ $$0 = 5120 - \frac{19200}{6} + 8C_{1} = 5120 - 3200 + 8C_{1}$$ $$8C_{1} = -1920 \implies C_{1} = -240 \text{ kN¬∑m}^{2}$$ **Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Find Location of Maximum Deflection:** Maximum deflection occurs where slope = 0. In the region $0 \le x \le 4$: $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = 30x^{2} - \frac{10x^{3}}{3} - 240 = 0$$ Multiplying by 3: $\quad 90x^{2} - 10x^{3} - 720 = 0 \implies x^{3} - 9x^{2} + 72 = 0$ Solving (numerically): $x \approx 3.55$ m (within the UDL region ‚úď) **Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Maximum Deflection:** At $x = 3.55$ m, $\langle 3.55 - 4 \rangle^{4} = 0$: $$EIy_{\max} = 10(3.55)^{3} - \frac{5(3.55)^{4}}{6} - 240(3.55)$$ $$\approx 447.2 - 264.2 - 852.0 = -669.0 \text{ kN¬∑m}^{3}$$ $$y_{\max} = \frac{-669.0}{EI} \text{ m}$$ --- ### Question B [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2022, Model Paper] **(a)** A cantilever beam of length **4 m**, fixed at the left end, carries a UDL of **30 kN/m** over the entire span. Determine the **slope and deflection at the free end** using Macaulay's method. Take $EI = 1.2 \times 10^{4}$ **kN¬∑m¬≤**. **(7 marks)** #### Solution: **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Set Origin at Free End** ($x = 0$ at free end, $x = 4$ at fixed end): $$M(x) = -\frac{30}{2}\langle x - 0 \rangle^{2} = -15x^{2} \text{ kN¬∑m}$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ First Integration:** $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{15x^{3}}{3} + C_{1} = -5x^{3} + C_{1}$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Second Integration:** $$EIy = -\frac{5x^{4}}{4} + C_{1}x + C_{2}$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Apply Boundary Conditions at Fixed End ($x = 4$ m):** Slope = 0 at $x = 4$: $$0 = -5(64) + C_{1} \implies C_{1} = 320 \text{ kN¬∑m}^{2}$$ Deflection = 0 at $x = 4$: $$0 = -\frac{5(256)}{4} + 320(4) + C_{2} = -320 + 1280 + C_{2} \implies C_{2} = -960 \text{ kN¬∑m}^{3}$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Evaluate at Free End ($x = 0$):** Slope: $$EI \frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = 0 + 320 = 320 \text{ kN¬∑m}^{2}$$ $$\theta_{\text{free}} = \frac{320}{1.2 \times 10^{4}} = 2.667 \times 10^{-2} \text{ rad}$$ Deflection: $$EIy\big|_{x=0} = 0 + 0 - 960 = -960 \text{ kN¬∑m}^{3}$$ $$\delta_{\text{free}} = \frac{-960}{1.2 \times 10^{4}} = -0.08 \text{ m} = -80 \text{ mm (downward)}$$ --- **(b)** A simply supported beam of span **10 m** carries a point load of **50 kN** at **4 m from the left support** and a clockwise couple of **100 kN¬∑m** at **6 m from the left support**. Using Macaulay's method, determine the **deflection under the point load**. **(7 marks)** #### Solution: **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Reactions:** Taking moments about the right support: $$R_A \times 10 = 50 \times 6 + 100 \implies R_A = \frac{400}{10} = 40 \text{ kN}$$ $$R_B = 50 - 40 = 10 \text{ kN}$$ (Note: The clockwise couple at $x = 6$ produces a positive moment contribution of $+100$ to the right side, hence its inclusion in the moment equation about $B$.) **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Macaulay Moment Equation:** $$M(x) = 40x - 50 \langle x - 4 \rangle^{1} - 100 \langle x - 6 \rangle^{0} \text{ kN¬∑m}$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ First Integration:** $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = 20x^{2} - 25 \langle x - 4 \rangle^{2} - 100 \langle x - 6 \rangle^{1} + C_{1}$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Second Integration:** $$EIy = \frac{20x^{3}}{3} - \frac{25}{3} \langle x - 4 \rangle^{3} - 50 \langle x - 6 \rangle^{2} + C_{1}x + C_{2}$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Boundary Conditions:** At $x = 0$, $y = 0$: $\quad C_{2} = 0$ At $x = 10$ m, $y = 0$: $$0 = \frac{20(1000)}{3} - \frac{25(216)}{3} - 50(16) + 10C_{1}$$ $$0 = \frac{20000}{3} - 1800 - 800 + 10C_{1}$$ $$0 = \frac{20000 - 5400 - 2400}{3} + 10C_{1} = \frac{12200}{3} + 10C_{1}$$ $$C_{1} = -\frac{1220}{3} \approx -406.67 \text{ kN¬∑m}^{2}$$ **Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Deflection at $x = 4$ m:** At $x = 4$ m, $\langle 4 - 4 \rangle^{3} = 0$ and $\langle 4 - 6 \rangle^{2} = 0$ (since $4 < 6$): $$EIy\big|_{x=4} = \frac{20(64)}{3} - 0 - 0 - \frac{1220}{3}(4)$$ $$= \frac{1280}{3} - \frac{4880}{3} = -\frac{3600}{3} = -1200 \text{ kN¬∑m}^{3}$$ $$\boxed{y_{x=4} = \frac{-1200}{EI} \text{ m (downward)}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚ÄĒ Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the Sign of the Couple:** A clockwise couple applied to a beam in the standard sagging-positive convention **adds positively** to $M(x)$, while a counterclockwise couple **subtracts**. Mixing this up is a common reason for losing 3‚Äď4 marks. > 2. **Using the Bracket at the Origin:** When the UDL starts at $x = 0$, the bracket $\langle x - 0 \rangle^{2}$ simplifies to $x^{2}$, but students often forget to drop the brackets and proceed with formal integration, leading to algebraic errors. > 3. **Boundary Condition Errors in Cantilevers:** For a cantilever, the **slope and deflection are both zero at the fixed end**, not at the free end. Mixing up these two boundary conditions results in **complete loss of marks** for that question. > 4. **Neglecting Reaction Terms:** Many students include only the applied loads in $M(x)$ and forget the **support reaction moment terms** ($R_A \cdot x$). This is a frequent mistake that leads to incorrect integration constants and, ultimately, a completely wrong answer. > 5. **Inconsistent Units:** Always keep units consistent. If $L$ is in meters and load in kN, then $EI$ must be in **kN¬∑m¬≤**, and the final deflection will be in **m**.
