In engineering terms: a **column with an eccentric load** behaves exactly like this. The eccentricity $e$ of the load from the centroidal axis creates a moment $M = P \cdot e$, and the resulting stress is **Direct Stress + Bending Stress** acting together.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Civil Engineering:** Most real columns are NOT loaded perfectly centrally. Reservoirs, columns supporting offset beams, and crane pillars all face eccentric loading. The combined stress analysis is critical to **prevent tension cracks in concrete columns** (hence the famous "Middle-Third Rule" and "Middle-Fourth Rule").

### 1.3 Standard Sign Convention Used at KTU Board

| Side of Section | When Load is on the Other Side | Sign |
|---|---|---|
| Towards the eccentricity | Compression increases | **Negative (‚ąí)** |
| Away from the eccentricity | Compression reduces (or tension develops) | **Positive (+)** or smaller negative |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Conceptual Logic of Combined Stress

**Step 1: Identify the load system.**
A vertical load $P$ is applied at a distance $e$ from the centroidal axis (eccentricity). The cross-section has area $A$ and moment of inertia $I$ about the relevant neutral axis.

**Step 2: Resolve the load into two equivalent systems (Principle of Superposition).**
- A **concentric (axial) load** $P$ acting at the centroid ‚Üí produces uniform **Direct Stress** $\sigma_{d} = P/A$ across the section.
- A **couple (moment)** $M = P \cdot e$ about the centroidal axis ‚Üí produces linearly varying **Bending Stress** $\sigma_{b} = M \cdot y / I$.

**Step 3: Determine the nature of bending stress at the extreme fibres.**
On the side **closer** to the load, the bending moment adds to compression ‚Üí maximum compressive stress.
On the side **farther** from the load, bending tries to relieve compression (or induce tension).

**Step 4: Apply superposition (algebraic sum).**
The resultant stress at any point located at distance $y$ from the neutral axis is:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I}$$

**Step 5: Identify the critical (governing) stresses.**
- **Maximum compressive stress** occurs on the side of the load (compression on compression).
- **Minimum stress (may be tensile)** occurs on the opposite side.

**Step 6: Apply design conditions.**
- For **no tension** (e.g., masonry, concrete, brick piers): the minimum stress must be ‚Č• 0.
- This gives the **limiting eccentricity** condition.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units (SI) | Limiting Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Direct (Axial) Stress | $\sigma_{d} = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | N/mm¬≤ (MPa) | Uniform across section |
| 2 | Bending Stress | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | N/mm¬≤ (MPa) | Linear variation |
| 3 | Eccentric Moment | $M = P \cdot e$ | N¬∑mm | $e$ = eccentricity from centroid |
| 4 | Combined Stress (Rectangular Coords) | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A} \pm \dfrac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I}$ | N/mm¬≤ | Superposition principle |
| 5 | Combined Stress (Section Modulus form) | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}\left[1 \pm \dfrac{e \cdot y}{k^{2}}\right]$ | N/mm¬≤ | $k$ = radius of gyration |
| 6 | Radius of Gyration | $k = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | mm | $k^{2} = I/A$ |
| 7 | Section Modulus | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | mm¬≥ | Used in bending stress |
| 8 | Eccentricity in terms of k & y | $e = \dfrac{k^{2}}{y_{max}}$ | mm | Limiting value for **no tension** |
| 9 | **Middle-Third Rule** (Rectangular) | $e \leq \dfrac{d}{6}$ | mm | $d$ = dimension along eccentricity |
| 10 | **Middle-Fourth Rule** (Circular) | $e \leq \dfrac{d}{8}$ | mm | $d$ = diameter |
| 11 | Maximum Stress (Rectangular) | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{P}{A}\left[1 + \dfrac{6e}{d}\right]$ | N/mm¬≤ | Compression side |
| 12 | Minimum Stress (Rectangular) | $\sigma_{min} = \dfrac{P}{A}\left[1 - \dfrac{6e}{d}\right]$ | N/mm¬≤ | Opposite side |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used daily in the design of **short columns, chimneys, dam abutments, retaining walls, and masonry piers**. The "no-tension" condition ensures that brittle materials (which are weak in tension) do not crack on the tension side.

### 2.3 The "No-Tension" Condition ‚ÄĒ Deriving the Middle-Third Rule

For a rectangular section of breadth $b$ and depth $d$ (where $d$ is the dimension parallel to the eccentricity):
- $A = b \cdot d$
- $I = \dfrac{b \cdot d^{3}}{12}$
- $y_{max} = d/2$
- $k^{2} = I/A = d^{2}/12$

**Condition for no tension:** The minimum stress must be ‚Č• 0.

$$\sigma_{min} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y_{max}}{I} \geq 0$$

$$\frac{P}{A} \geq \frac{P \cdot e \cdot (d/2)}{b \cdot d^{3}/12}$$

$$1 \geq \frac{6e}{d} \quad \Rightarrow \quad e \leq \frac{d}{6}$$

This means the load must lie within the **middle third** of the cross-section. Hence the name **Middle-Third Rule**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The same derivation for a **circular section** yields $e \leq d/8$, known as the **Middle-Fourth Rule** (because the central region within which the load may fall is one-fourth of the diameter wide on each side, i.e., total width $d/4$).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Stress at a Point in an Eccentrically Loaded Section

**Starting Premise:**
Consider a short column of cross-sectional area $A$ subjected to a compressive load $P$ acting at an eccentricity $e$ from the centroidal $y$-axis (i.e., the load is offset by distance $e$ along $x$).

**Step 1:** The eccentric load $P$ at eccentricity $e$ is statically equivalent to a centric load $P$ at the centroid **PLUS** a moment $M = P \cdot e$ about the centroidal axis.

$$
\sigma_{resultant} = \sigma_{direct} + \sigma_{bending}
$$

**Step 2:** Direct stress is uniform across the section:

$$
\sigma_{direct} = \frac{P}{A} \quad \text{(compressive)}
$$

**Step 3:** Bending stress varies linearly with $y$:

$$
\sigma_{bending} = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} = \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I}
$$

**Step 4:** The total stress at a fibre located at distance $y$ from the neutral axis:

$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I}
$$

The sign is **positive (+)** on the side away from the load (where bending tries to relieve compression / produce tension) and **negative (‚ąí)** on the side towards the load (where bending adds to compression).

**Step 5:** Introduce the section modulus $Z = I / y_{max}$:

$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{P \cdot e}{Z}
$$

**Step 6:** Express in terms of radius of gyration $k$:

$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A} \left[ 1 \pm \frac{e \cdot y}{k^{2}} \right]
$$

**Step 7:** Identify the extreme fibre stresses. With $y = y_{max} = k^{2} / e_{limit}$:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{P}{A}\left[1 + \frac{e \cdot y_{max}}{k^{2}}\right], \quad \sigma_{min} = \frac{P}{A}\left[1 - \frac{e \cdot y_{max}}{k^{2}}\right]
$$

### 3.2 Detailed Worked Derivation: Middle-Third Rule for Rectangular Section

**Given:** Rectangular section of width $b$ and depth $d$. Load $P$ applied with eccentricity $e$ along the depth $d$.

**Section properties:**

$$
A = b \cdot d
$$

$$
I = \frac{b \cdot d^{3}}{12}
$$

$$
y_{max} = \frac{d}{2}
$$

**Apply the no-tension condition:**

$$
\sigma_{min} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y_{max}}{I} \geq 0
$$

Substitute the section properties:

$$
\frac{P}{b \cdot d} - \frac{P \cdot e \cdot (d/2)}{b \cdot d^{3}/12} \geq 0
$$

Simplify the second term:

$$
\frac{P \cdot e \cdot (d/2)}{b \cdot d^{3}/12} = \frac{P \cdot e \cdot d \cdot 12}{2 \cdot b \cdot d^{3}} = \frac{6 P \cdot e}{b \cdot d^{2}}
$$

So the inequality becomes:

$$
\frac{P}{b \cdot d} \geq \frac{6 P \cdot e}{b \cdot d^{2}}
$$

Cancel $P$ (assuming $P \neq 0$):

$$
\frac{1}{d} \geq \frac{6 e}{d^{2}}
$$

Multiply both sides by $d^{2}$:

$$
d \geq 6e \quad \Rightarrow \quad e \leq \frac{d}{6}
$$

**Geometric Interpretation:**
Since $d/6$ is one-half of $d/3$, the load can be displaced by $d/6$ on either side of the centroid. The total "no-tension zone" extends from $-d/6$ to $+d/6$, a total width of $d/3$ ‚ÄĒ exactly the **middle third** of the depth.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Trick:** When asked to prove the Middle-Third Rule, examiners expect students to write the section properties first, then substitute carefully, and **finally state the geometric interpretation**. Skipping the geometry statement costs 1 mark.

### 3.3 Detailed Worked Derivation: Middle-Fourth Rule for Circular Section

**Given:** Solid circular section of diameter $D$. Load $P$ at eccentricity $e$.

**Section properties:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{64}
$$

$$
y_{max} = \frac{D}{2}
$$

$$
k^{2} = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{\pi D^{4}/64}{\pi D^{2}/4} = \frac{D^{2}}{16}
$$

**Apply the no-tension condition:** $\sigma_{min} \geq 0$

$$
\frac{P}{A} \geq \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y_{max}}{I}
$$

$$
e \leq \frac{I}{A \cdot y_{max}} = \frac{k^{2}}{y_{max}} = \frac{D^{2}/16}{D/2} = \frac{D}{8}
$$

**Geometric Interpretation:**
The total no-tension zone width is $2 \times (D/8) = D/4$, which is the **middle fourth** of the diameter.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Limiting Eccentricities for Common Sections

| Section Type | $I$ | $A$ | $y_{max}$ | $k^{2}$ | Limiting $e$ (No Tension) | Rule Name |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangle (b √ó d) | $\dfrac{bd^{3}}{12}$ | $bd$ | $d/2$ | $d^{2}/12$ | $d/6$ | Middle-Third |
| Solid Circle (Dia $D$) | $\dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{64}$ | $\pi D^{2}/4$ | $D/2$ | $D^{2}/16$ | $D/8$ | Middle-Fourth |
| Hollow Circle (Dia $D$, $d$) | $\dfrac{\pi (D^{4}-d^{4})}{64}$ | $\pi (D^{2}-d^{2})/4$ | $D/2$ | $(D^{2}+d^{2})/16$ | $\dfrac{D^{2}+d^{2}}{8D}$ | Modified Fourth |
| Solid Square (a √ó a) | $\dfrac{a^{4}}{12}$ | $a^{2}$ | $a/2$ | $a^{2}/12$ | $a/6$ | Middle-Third |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Combined Stress Generation

The following block diagram illustrates the analytical flow from an eccentric load to the resultant stress distribution:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT Eccentric Load P at distance e from centroidal axis"] --> B["STATIC EQUIVALENCE Principle of Superposition"]
    B --> C["Component 1 Concentric Load P at centroid"]
    B --> D["Component 2 Moment M equal to P times e"]
    C --> E["DIRECT STRESS Uniform Distribution sigma_d = P by A"]
    D --> F["BENDING STRESS Linear Distribution sigma_b = M y by I"]
    E --> G["SUPERPOSITION Algebraic Addition"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["OUTPUT Resultant Stress Distribution across section"]
    H --> I["Side near load Maximum Compression"]
    H --> J["Side away from load Minimum Stress may be tensile"]
    I --> K["DESIGN CHECK Check against material limits"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["Decision No tension needed Apply Middle Third or Fourth Rule"]
    K --> M["Decision Tension allowed Calculate exact stress for design"]
```

### 4.2 Stress Distribution Block Diagram for Rectangular Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Cross Section Rectangle b by d"] --> B["Direct Stress Uniform Rectangular Block P by A"]
    A --> C["Bending Stress Linear Triangular Distribution"]
    B --> D["SUPERPOSITION Combine Algebraically"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["Resultant Trapezoidal Distribution"]
    E --> F["Maximum stress on load side"]
    E --> G["Minimum stress on opposite side"]
    F --> H["DESIGN VALUE sigma_max for material strength check"]
    G --> I["DESIGN VALUE sigma_min for crack control check"]
```

### 4.3 Eccentric Load Resolution Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Given Load P with eccentricity e"] --> B["Section Properties A and I about neutral axis"]
    B --> C["Compute Direct Stress P by A"]
    B --> D["Compute Bending Moment M equal to P times e"]
    D --> E["Compute Bending Stress at extreme fibres M times y by I"]
    C --> F["Combine with sign convention"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Compute sigma_max and sigma_min"]
    G --> H{"Check Condition"}
    H -->|No Tension Allowed| I["Verify e lies within k squared by y_max"]
    H -->|Tension Allowed| J["Report both stresses to designer"]
    I --> K["Apply Middle Third or Fourth Rule"]
    K --> L["OUTPUT Safe Design Verified"]
```

### 4.4 Cross-Section Type and Governing Rule Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Identify Cross Section Geometry"] --> B["Rectangular Section"]
    A --> C["Circular Section"]
    A --> D["I Section or Built Up Section"]
    A --> E["Hollow Circular Section"]
    B --> F["Middle Third Rule e less than d by 6"]
    C --> G["Middle Fourth Rule e less than D by 8"]
    D --> H["Compute k squared then apply general condition"]
    E --> I["Modified Fourth Rule e less than D squared plus d squared by 8 D"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid for Students:** These flow diagrams mirror the analytical sequence you should follow in the KTU answer sheet. Examiners award "method marks" (typically 2-3 out of 14) simply for showing the correct logical sequence ‚ÄĒ even if a final arithmetic slip occurs.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Part A ‚ÄĒ Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: Define direct stress and bending stress. State the formula for combined stress at any point of a section subjected to an eccentric load.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Direct Stress:** It is the intensity of internal force per unit area when an axial load is applied concentrically along the centroidal axis of a member. Formula:
$$
\sigma_{d} = \frac{P}{A}
$$

**Bending Stress:** It is the intensity of stress induced at a fibre due to the bending moment acting on the cross-section. The stress varies linearly across the section. Formula:
$$
\sigma_{b} = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}
$$

**Combined Stress:** When a load $P$ acts at an eccentricity $e$ from the centroidal axis, the stress at any point at distance $y$ from the neutral axis is:
$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{M \cdot y}{I} = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I}
$$
where $+$ corresponds to the side producing tension and $-$ to the side producing additional compression. **(3 marks: 1 + 1 + 1)**

---

**Part A ‚ÄĒ Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q: State and explain the Middle-Third Rule for a rectangular column section. Why is this rule important in the design of masonry and concrete columns?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** For a rectangular column section of depth $d$ (along the direction of eccentricity), if the eccentric load lies within the **middle third** of the section (i.e., $e \leq d/6$), the resulting stress across the entire section will be **compressive** with no tensile stress developed anywhere.

**Mathematical Expression:**
$$
e \leq \frac{d}{6}
$$

**Importance:** Masonry (brick, stone) and plain concrete are brittle materials with **negligible tensile strength**. If the load falls outside the middle third, tension develops on the far side, causing cracks. Hence, the rule ensures crack-free design by limiting eccentricity. **(3 marks: 1 + 1 + 1)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**PART B ‚ÄĒ QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model]`

**A solid rectangular column of size 200 mm √ó 300 mm (breadth √ó depth) carries a vertical load of 200 kN at an eccentricity of 50 mm along the major axis (i.e., along the 300 mm dimension). Determine:**

**(a)** The maximum and minimum intensities of stress in the section. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The maximum eccentricity for which no tension will develop in the section. Also, find the stress distribution at this limiting eccentricity. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Maximum and Minimum Stress

**Given Data:**
- Breadth $b = 200$ mm
- Depth $d = 300$ mm
- Load $P = 200$ kN $= 200 \times 10^{3}$ N
- Eccentricity $e = 50$ mm (along depth $d$)

**Step 1: Section Properties**

$$
A = b \cdot d = 200 \times 300 = 60000 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
I = \frac{b \cdot d^{3}}{12} = \frac{200 \times (300)^{3}}{12} = \frac{200 \times 27 \times 10^{6}}{12}
$$

$$
I = 4.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

$$
y_{max} = \frac{d}{2} = \frac{300}{2} = 150 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Direct (Axial) Stress**

$$
\sigma_{d} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{200 \times 10^{3}}{60000} = 3.333 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \quad \text{(compressive)}
$$

**Step 3: Bending Moment due to Eccentricity**

$$
M = P \cdot e = 200 \times 10^{3} \times 50 = 10 \times 10^{6} \text{ N¬∑mm}
$$

**Step 4: Bending Stress at Extreme Fibres**

$$
\sigma_{b} = \frac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I} = \frac{10 \times 10^{6} \times 150}{4.5 \times 10^{8}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{b} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{9}}{4.5 \times 10^{8}} = 3.333 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 5: Maximum and Minimum Stresses**

**On the side of the load** (compression + bending compression):

$$
\sigma_{max} = \sigma_{d} + \sigma_{b} = 3.333 + 3.333 = 6.667 \text{ N/mm}^{2 (compressive)}
$$

**On the opposite side** (compression ‚ąí bending compression):

$$
\sigma_{min} = \sigma_{d} - \sigma_{b} = 3.333 - 3.333 = 0 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $\sigma_{min} = 0$, the given eccentricity ($50$ mm) is **exactly the limiting eccentricity** for no tension in this section. This is a great self-check!

**Part (a) Final Answer:**
- $\sigma_{max} = 6.667$ N/mm¬≤ (compressive)
- $\sigma_{min} = 0$ N/mm¬≤
- **No tension develops, but it's right at the verge.** **(7 marks: 2 for properties + 2 for direct stress + 2 for bending stress + 1 for combination)**

---

#### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Maximum Eccentricity and Stress Distribution

**Step 1: Apply Middle-Third Rule**

For a rectangular section, limiting eccentricity:

$$
e_{lim} = \frac{d}{6} = \frac{300}{6} = 50 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Verify Using $k^{2}$ Method**

$$
k^{2} = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{8}}{60000} = 7500 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
e_{lim} = \frac{k^{2}}{y_{max}} = \frac{7500}{150} = 50 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3: Stress Distribution at Limiting Eccentricity**

At $e = e_{lim} = 50$ mm:

- Direct stress: $\sigma_{d} = 3.333$ N/mm¬≤ (uniform)
- Maximum bending stress at extreme fibre: $\sigma_{b,max} = 3.333$ N/mm¬≤

**Stress Distribution Profile:**

| Location on Section | Distance $y$ from NA | Direct Stress | Bending Stress | Resultant Stress |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Load side edge (extreme) | $+150$ mm | 3.333 | +3.333 | **6.667 N/mm¬≤ (comp.)** |
| Centroid (NA) | $0$ | 3.333 | 0 | 3.333 N/mm¬≤ (comp.) |
| Opposite side edge | $-150$ mm | 3.333 | ‚ąí3.333 | **0 N/mm¬≤** |

**Step 4: Block Diagram Representation**

```
         6.667 N/mm¬≤ (comp.)  ‚Üź load side edge
         ‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤‚Ė≤
         3.333 N/mm¬≤ (comp.)  ‚Üź neutral axis (centroid)
         ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ‚Ėľ
         0 N/mm¬≤              ‚Üź opposite side edge (no tension, no compression)
```

The stress distribution is **trapezoidal** (since $\sigma_{min} = 0$, it actually becomes triangular, with the trapezoid degenerating at the far edge).

**Part (b) Final Answer:**
- Maximum eccentricity for no tension: $e_{lim} = 50$ mm
- $\sigma_{max} = 6.667$ N/mm¬≤ (compressive)
- $\sigma_{min} = 0$ N/mm¬≤ (transition point ‚ÄĒ no tension)
- **Stress distribution: linear, varying from 6.667 to 0 N/mm¬≤ across the section.** **(7 marks: 2 for derivation + 2 for verification + 2 for distribution + 1 for diagram)**

---

**PART B ‚ÄĒ QUESTION B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]`

**A solid circular column of 300 mm diameter carries a vertical load of 450 kN at an eccentricity of 40 mm. Determine:**

**(a)** The maximum and minimum intensities of stress in the section. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The kern (core) of the circular section and the maximum eccentricity for which no tension will develop. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Maximum and Minimum Stress

**Given Data:**
- Diameter $D = 300$ mm
- Load $P = 450$ kN $= 450 \times 10^{3}$ N
- Eccentricity $e = 40$ mm

**Step 1: Section Properties of Solid Circle**

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (300)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 90000}{4} = 70685.83 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi \times (300)^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 8.1 \times 10^{9}}{64}
$$

$$
I = 3.976 \times 10^{8} \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

$$
y_{max} = \frac{D}{2} = 150 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Direct Stress**

$$
\sigma_{d} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{450 \times 10^{3}}{70685.83} = 6.366 \text{ N/mm}^{2 (compressive)}
$$

**Step 3: Bending Moment**

$$
M = P \cdot e = 450 \times 10^{3} \times 40 = 18 \times 10^{6} \text{ N¬∑mm}
$$

**Step 4: Bending Stress at Extreme Fibre**

$$
\sigma_{b} = \frac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I} = \frac{18 \times 10^{6} \times 150}{3.976 \times 10^{8}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{b} = \frac{2.7 \times 10^{9}}{3.976 \times 10^{8}} = 6.792 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 5: Maximum and Minimum Stresses**

**Load side (compressive):**
$$
\sigma_{max} = \sigma_{d} + \sigma_{b} = 6.366 + 6.792 = 13.158 \text{ N/mm}^{2 (compressive)}
$$

**Opposite side:**
$$
\sigma_{min} = \sigma_{d} - \sigma_{b} = 6.366 - 6.792 = -0.426 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

The negative sign indicates **tensile stress** of $0.426$ N/mm¬≤ on the far side.

**Part (a) Final Answer:**
- $\sigma_{max} = 13.158$ N/mm¬≤ (compressive)
- $\sigma_{min} = -0.426$ N/mm¬≤ (i.e., 0.426 N/mm¬≤ tensile)
- **Tension has developed, so the design is unsafe for masonry or plain concrete.** **(7 marks: 2 for properties + 2 for direct stress + 2 for bending stress + 1 for final stress)**

---

#### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Kern of the Section & Maximum Eccentricity

**Step 1: Define the Kern (Core)**

The kern of a cross-section is the **central region within which the application of a compressive load produces compressive stress over the entire cross-section**, with no tension anywhere. The boundary of the kern is defined by the line connecting the points where the load, if applied, would produce zero stress at the opposite extreme fibre.

**Step 2: Derive the Kern Boundary Equation**

For the no-tension condition, $\sigma_{min} \geq 0$:

$$
e \leq \frac{k^{2}}{y_{max}}
$$

For a solid circle:
$$
k^{2} = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{\pi D^{4}/64}{\pi D^{2}/4} = \frac{D^{2}}{16}
$$

$$
e_{lim} = \frac{k^{2}}{y_{max}} = \frac{D^{2}/16}{D/2} = \frac{D}{8}
$$

**Step 3: Numerical Calculation for $D = 300$ mm**

$$
e_{lim} = \frac{300}{8} = 37.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Describe the Kern Geometry**

The kern of a solid circular section is itself a **concentric circle** of diameter $D/4$ (i.e., radius $D/8$).

$$
\text{Kern diameter} = \frac{D}{4} = \frac{300}{4} = 75 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Kern radius} = \frac{D}{8} = 37.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5: Verify and Comment**

Since the given eccentricity $e = 40$ mm **exceeds** the limit $e_{lim} = 37.5$ mm, the load lies **outside the kern**, hence tension develops ‚ÄĒ consistent with the result of Part (a).

**Part (b) Final Answer:**
- Maximum eccentricity for no tension: $e_{lim} = 37.5$ mm
- Kern: a concentric circle of radius $37.5$ mm (diameter $75$ mm)
- The Middle-Fourth Rule applies: $e \leq D/8$ **(7 marks: 2 for derivation + 2 for kern concept + 2 for numerical result + 1 for verification)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign convention error (loses 1-2 marks):** Students often forget to subtract bending stress on the far side. Always draw a clear FBD with arrow indicating load position.
> 2. **Unit mismatch (loses 1 mark):** Mixing mm and cm, or kN and N. Use **N and mm** consistently (yielding N/mm¬≤) OR **kN and m** (yielding kN/m¬≤ = kPa).
> 3. **Forgetting the "no tension" condition (loses 2 marks):** KTU frequently asks for the maximum eccentricity ‚ÄĒ do not stop at computing $\sigma_{max}$ and $\sigma_{min}$ alone. Always check the design condition.
> 4. **Using the wrong axis for $I$:** For a rectangular column, if the eccentricity is along depth $d$, use $I$ about the axis perpendicular to $d$ (which has $d$ in the formula $bd^3/12$). If eccentricity is along breadth $b$, use $I = db^3/12$. **This confusion costs 3 marks.**
> 5. **Forgetting to state the kern shape:** For rectangular, kern is a rhombus. For circular, kern is a concentric circle. State it explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Direct Stress** $\sigma_{d} = P/A$ is **uniform** across the section; it is the same on every fibre.
- **Bending Stress** $\sigma_{b} = My/I$ is **linearly varying**, maximum at extreme fibres, zero at the neutral axis.
- The **principle of superposition** is valid for combined direct + bending stress, provided stresses are within elastic limits.
- The **sign convention** is: **tensile = positive (+), compressive = negative (‚ąí)**. Bending stress is added to direct stress on the compression side of the eccentricity.
- The **equivalent system** for an eccentric load $P$ at eccentricity $e$ is: a centric load $P$ at the centroid **plus** a moment $M = P \cdot e$.
- The general combined stress equation is:
$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{M \cdot y}{I} = \frac{P}{A}\left[1 \pm \frac{e \cdot y}{k^{2}}\right]
$$
- The **no-tension condition** yields the limiting eccentricity:
$$
e_{lim} = \frac{k^{2}}{y_{max}} = \frac{I}{A \cdot y_{max}}
$$
- **Middle-Third Rule (Rectangle):** $e \leq d/6$ ‚ÄĒ load must lie within the middle third of the depth.
- **Middle-Fourth Rule (Circle):** $e \leq D/8$ ‚ÄĒ load must lie within the middle fourth of the diameter.
- The **kern** is the central region of the cross-section such that any load within it produces only compressive stress.
  - **Rectangle:** Kern is a **rhombus** (diamond) with diagonals $d/3$ (vertical) and $b/3$ (horizontal).
  - **Circle:** Kern is a **concentric circle** of diameter $D/4$.
- **Section modulus form:** $\sigma = P/A \pm (P \cdot e)/Z$, where $Z = I/y_{max}$ is the section modulus.
- **Eccentricity vs. Bending:** Eccentricity $e$ refers to the geometric offset of the load; bending moment $M = P \cdot e$ is the rotational effect produced by that offset.
- The "no-tension" rule is **essential for brittle materials** like brick, stone, and plain concrete, which cannot resist tension.
- For **ductile materials** (steel), tension is permissible and the rule is not strictly required.
- Always **draw a cross-section sketch** showing the load position and label the stresses on each side ‚ÄĒ KTU examiners award method marks for this.
- The **stress distribution** for combined loading is **trapezoidal** in general, becoming **triangular** when $\sigma_{min} = 0$, and **uniform** when $e = 0$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kern of a Section
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Kern of a Section

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kern of a Section:** The *kern* (also called the **core** or **kernel**) of a structural cross-section is the central region **inside the section** within which the application of a compressive load produces **compressive stress everywhere** across the entire cross-section, with **no tensile stress** developing at any point.

Mathematically, the kern is the **locus of all points** on or within the cross-section such that if the resultant compressive load $P$ passes through any of these points, the **maximum tensile stress** in the section becomes **zero** (or, in safe design, remains compressive).

The boundary of the kern is obtained by drawing lines that are **tangential to the section** at its extreme fibers. As the load point moves outside the kern, tensile stresses begin to appear in some part of the section.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ "The Table Leg Test"

Imagine a heavy concrete column resting on a **square wooden tabletop** supported by a single leg at its center.

- If you press down on the **center of the tabletop**, every fiber of the wood experiences **compression only** ‚ÄĒ the table is safe.
- If you press down on the **edge of the tabletop**, the wood on the opposite side tends to **lift up** (tension) ‚ÄĒ the table might split.
- The **central safe zone** where you can press without breaking the wood is exactly the **kern** of the tabletop cross-section.

The kern is the **"no-tension footprint"** of the cross-section.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **No physical constants** (like $g$ or $G$) are involved in kern analysis.
> - All kern dimensions are expressed as **ratios of the section's geometric properties** (e.g., $D/8$, $b/6$, $d/6$).
> - The kern is **purely a geometric property** of the cross-section ‚ÄĒ independent of the material's strength or the magnitude of the load $P$.

## 1.4 Why Kern Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

Materials like **masonry, brick, concrete, and unreinforced stone** are **weak in tension** but strong in compression. Therefore, columns and piers made from these materials must be designed so that the **load always falls inside the kern**. This single concept governs the design of:

- Masonry dams
- Chimneys
- Bridge piers
- Retaining walls
- Footings of columns
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ‚ÄĒ Stress in an Eccentrically Loaded Column

Consider a short column of cross-sectional area $A$ subjected to an axial compressive load $P$ applied at an eccentricity $e$ from the centroidal axis. The **combined (bending + axial) stress** at any point distance $y$ from the neutral axis is given by the **secant/secant formula**:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I} = \frac{P}{A}\left(1 \pm \frac{e \cdot y}{r^2}\right)$$

where:
- $I$ = moment of inertia of the section about the neutral axis
- $r = \sqrt{I/A}$ = radius of gyration
- $y$ = distance of the extreme fiber from the neutral axis
- $e$ = eccentricity of load from centroid

## 2.2 Condition for No Tension

For **no tensile stress anywhere** in the section, the **minimum value of $\sigma$** must be $\geq 0$ (compressive or zero). The minimum stress occurs at the fiber **farthest from the load point**, i.e., at the side **opposite** to the eccentricity.

Setting the minimum stress to zero:

$$\frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y_{max}}{I} = 0$$

Solving for the limiting eccentricity $e_{max}$:

$$e_{max} = \frac{I}{A \cdot y_{max}} = \frac{r^2}{y_{max}}$$

This $e_{max}$ is the **distance from the centroid to the kern boundary** along the axis of bending. The kern boundary is the locus of all such limit points for every possible direction of eccentricity.

## 2.3 General Procedure to Construct the Kern

1. Draw the **section** to scale.
2. For each axis of symmetry, calculate $r^2/y_{max}$ to get the kern extent.
3. For inclined axes, use the general relation $e = r^2 / y_{max}$.
4. Connect these points to obtain the **kern boundary**.

## 2.4 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes all kern formulas for sections commonly tested in KTU examinations.

| Section Type | Axis of Bending | Kern Dimension (from centroid) | Kern Shape | Diagram Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rectangular** ($b \times d$) | About axis parallel to $b$ (depth $d$) | $d/6$ | Rhombus (diamond) | ‚¨• |
| **Rectangular** ($b \times d$) | About axis parallel to $d$ (width $b$) | $b/6$ | Rhombus (diamond) | ‚¨• |
| **Solid Circular** (diameter $D$) | Any diameter | $D/8$ | Circle | ‚≠ē |
| **Hollow Circular** (outer $D$, inner $d$) | Any diameter | $\dfrac{D^2 + d^2}{8D}$ | Circle | ‚≠ē |
| **Hollow Rectangular** ($B \times D$, inner $b \times d$) | About axis $\parallel$ to $B$ | $\dfrac{B D^3 - b d^3}{6(B D - b d)}$ | Diamond | ‚¨• |
| **Solid Square** ($a \times a$) | Both axes | $a/6$ | Square (rotated 45¬į) | ‚¨õ |
| **Triangular** (equilateral, side $a$) | Centroidal axis $\parallel$ to base | $h/12$ where $h = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}a$ | Triangle | ‚Ė≥ |
| **I-Section / T-Section** | Principal axes | Computed from $r^2/y_{max}$ | Irregular hexagon/polygon | ‚¨° |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** All $r^2$ and $y_{max}$ must correspond to the **same axis of bending**. A common KTU error is mixing properties from different axes.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The kern concept is the **backbone of unreinforced masonry design**:
- **Gravity dams:** The resultant of all forces (self-weight + water pressure) must fall within the **middle third** of the base ‚ÄĒ which is the kern of a rectangular base. This is the famous **"Middle Third Rule"**.
- **Chimneys & Towers:** Under wind load, the equivalent eccentricity must lie inside the kern to prevent tension in the brickwork.
- **Bridge Piers:** The kern controls the maximum permissible eccentricity of vertical loads transferred from the superstructure.
- **Spread Footings:** The column load's eccentricity relative to the footing must be within the kern to ensure the soil reaction is uniformly compressive (no uplift).

## 2.6 The Famous "Middle Third Rule" for Rectangular Sections

For a rectangular base of width $b$, the kern extends from $-b/6$ to $+b/6$ from the centroid. Since the total base width is $b$ (from $-b/2$ to $+b/2$), the kern occupies a **central zone of width $2 \times (b/6) = b/3$**, which is the **middle third** of the base.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Middle Third Rule":** If the load resultant lies within the **middle third** of a rectangular section, the stress distribution is **purely compressive (trapezoidal or triangular)** with **zero stress** at the edge at the limit. If the load moves into the **outer thirds**, **tension develops** in the section.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Kern of a Rectangular Section ($b \times d$)

### Given
- Width = $b$, Depth = $d$
- Cross-sectional area: $A = b \cdot d$
- Moment of inertia about centroidal axis parallel to $b$: $I_{xx} = b d^3 / 12$
- Distance to extreme fiber: $y_{max} = d/2$

### Step 1: Write the limiting eccentricity condition

For no tension, the load point must be at most at distance $e_{max}$ from the centroid such that the stress at the opposite edge becomes zero:

$$e_{max} = \frac{I}{A \cdot y_{max}}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the values

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{max} &= \frac{\left(\dfrac{b d^3}{12}\right)}{\left(b d\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)} \\[6pt]
e_{max} &= \frac{\dfrac{b d^3}{12}}{\dfrac{b d^2}{2}} \\[6pt]
e_{max} &= \frac{b d^3}{12} \cdot \frac{2}{b d^2} \\[6pt]
e_{max} &= \frac{2 d^3}{12 d^2} = \frac{d}{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Repeat for the other axis

By symmetry (now bending about axis $\parallel$ to $d$):
$$I_{yy} = \dfrac{d b^3}{12}, \quad y_{max} = \dfrac{b}{2}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{max} &= \frac{\left(\dfrac{d b^3}{12}\right)}{\left(b d\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{b}{2}\right)} \\[6pt]
e_{max} &= \frac{b}{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Construct the kern shape

The kern extends $\pm d/6$ along the vertical axis and $\pm b/6$ along the horizontal axis. Connecting these four points with straight lines produces a **rhombus (diamond)** whose diagonals measure $b/3$ (horizontal) and $d/3$ (vertical).

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification (Middle Third Rule):** Total horizontal extent of kern = $2 \times (b/6) = b/3$, which is the middle third of the total base width $b$. ‚úď

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Kern of a Solid Circular Section (Diameter $D$)

### Given
- Diameter = $D$, Radius = $R = D/2$
- Area: $A = \pi D^2 / 4$
- Moment of inertia about any centroidal diameter: $I = \pi D^4 / 64$
- Extreme fiber distance: $y_{max} = D/2$

### Step 1: Apply the limiting eccentricity formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{max} &= \frac{I}{A \cdot y_{max}} \\[6pt]
e_{max} &= \frac{\left(\dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)} \\[6pt]
e_{max} &= \frac{\dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}}{\dfrac{\pi D^3}{8}} \\[6pt]
e_{max} &= \frac{\pi D^4}{64} \cdot \frac{8}{\pi D^3} \\[6pt]
e_{max} &= \frac{8 D^4}{64 D^3} = \frac{D}{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Conclude the kern shape

Since the section is **symmetric about every axis through the centroid**, the kern is a **circle of radius $D/8$** centered at the centroid. The kern is **isotropic** ‚ÄĒ its extent is the same in every direction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Kern of a Hollow Circular Section (Outer $D$, Inner $d$)

### Given
- Outer diameter = $D$, Inner diameter = $d$
- Area: $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$
- Moment of inertia: $I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4)$
- Extreme fiber: $y_{max} = D/2$

### Step 1: Factor the moment of inertia

$$D^4 - d^4 = (D^2 - d^2)(D^2 + d^2)$$

Therefore:

$$I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(D^2 - d^2)(D^2 + d^2)$$

### Step 2: Substitute into the limiting eccentricity formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{max} &= \frac{I}{A \cdot y_{max}} \\[6pt]
e_{max} &= \frac{\dfrac{\pi}{64}(D^2 - d^2)(D^2 + d^2)}{\dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}} \\[6pt]
e_{max} &= \frac{\dfrac{\pi}{64}(D^2 + d^2)}{\dfrac{\pi D}{8}} \quad \text{(after cancelling } D^2 - d^2) \\[6pt]
e_{max} &= \frac{\pi (D^2 + d^2)}{64} \cdot \frac{8}{\pi D} \\[6pt]
e_{max} &= \frac{D^2 + d^2}{8D}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Conclude the kern shape

The kern is again a **circle** (due to symmetry), with radius:

$$r_{kern} = \dfrac{D^2 + d^2}{8D}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** As $d \to 0$ (solid section), $r_{kern} = D^2/(8D) = D/8$. ‚úď This matches the solid circular case.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Limiting Stress Conditions at the Kern Boundary

At the kern boundary, the load $P$ passes through a point such that the stress on **one extreme fiber is exactly zero**. Let us verify the stress at this fiber.

The combined stress at a fiber distance $y$ from the neutral axis:

$$\sigma_y = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

where $M = P \cdot e$ is the moment due to eccentricity. At the edge opposite to the load, $y = -y_{max}$ (sign convention: positive $y$ on the load side):

$$\sigma_{-y_{max}} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y_{max}}{I}$$

Setting this to zero:

$$\frac{P}{A} = \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y_{max}}{I}$$

$$e = \frac{I}{A \cdot y_{max}} = \frac{r^2}{y_{max}} \quad \blacksquare$$

The stress at the **near edge** (on the load side) becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{+y_{max}} &= \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y_{max}}{I} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot y_{max}}{A \cdot y_{max}} \quad \text{(substituting } e = r^2/y_{max}) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P}{A} = \frac{2P}{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

So at the **kern boundary**, the stress distribution is **triangular**, with **zero on one edge** and **twice the average stress** on the other edge.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Summary Table

| Section | Area $A$ | $I$ | $y_{max}$ | $r^2 = I/A$ | Kern Radius/Extent |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangular ($b \times d$) | $bd$ | $bd^3/12$ | $d/2$ | $d^2/12$ | $d/6$ |
| Solid Circle ($D$) | $\pi D^2/4$ | $\pi D^4/64$ | $D/2$ | $D^2/16$ | $D/8$ |
| Hollow Circle ($D, d$) | $\pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$ | $\pi(D^4 - d^4)/64$ | $D/2$ | $(D^2 + d^2)/16$ | $(D^2 + d^2)/(8D)$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Kern Construction Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Given Cross-Section] --> B{Identify Axis of Bending}
    B --> C[Compute Area A]
    B --> D[Compute Moment of Inertia I about that axis]
    B --> E[Identify extreme fiber distance y_max]
    C --> F[Apply e_max = I / A*y_max]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Mark kern boundary point on that axis]
    G --> H{Repeat for all symmetry axes?}
    H -->|No| B
    H -->|Yes| I[Connect all boundary points]
    I --> J[Final Kern Shape Obtained]
    J --> K{Rectangular?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Rhombus, diagonals d/3 and b/3]
    K -->|No| M{Circular?}
    M -->|Yes - Solid| N[Circle radius D/8]
    M -->|Yes - Hollow| O[Circle radius D^2 + d^2 / 8D]
    M -->|No| P[Compute via r^2 / y_max directly]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow ‚ÄĒ Stress Distribution with Load Position

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Load P at Center] --> S1[Uniform Compression: sigma = P/A everywhere]
    P2[Load P inside Kern] --> S2[Trapezoidal Compression: no tension anywhere]
    P3[Load P on Kern Boundary] --> S3[Triangular: zero on far edge, 2P/A on near edge]
    P4[Load P outside Kern] --> S4[Tension develops on far edge - UNSAFE for masonry]
    S4 --> S5[CRITICAL FAILURE for unreinforced materials]
    style S5 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style S4 fill:#ffa500,color:#fff
    style S1 fill:#90ee90,color:#000
    style S2 fill:#90ee90,color:#000
    style S3 fill:#ffff99,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block ‚ÄĒ Rectangular Section Kern Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SECTION[Rectangular Section b x d]
        R1[Top Edge: y = +d/2] --- R2[Centroid: 0,0] --- R3[Bottom Edge: y = -d/2]
        R4[Left Edge: x = -b/2] --- R2 --- R5[Right Edge: x = +b/2]
    end
    subgraph KERN[Kern Region: Rhombus]
        K1[Top: 0, +d/6] --- K2[Right: +b/6, 0] --- K3[Bottom: 0, -d/6] --- K4[Left: -b/6, 0] --- K1
    end
    SECTION ~~~ KERN
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block ‚ÄĒ Circular Section Kern Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SECTION2[Outer Circle: Diameter D]
        C1[Extreme fiber at +D/2] --- C2[Centroid 0,0] --- C3[Extreme fiber at -D/2]
    end
    subgraph KERN2[Kern: Concentric Circle radius D/8]
        CK1[Boundary point at +D/8] --- CK2[Centroid shared] --- CK3[Boundary point at -D/8]
    end
    SECTION2 ~~~ KERN2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** In all four diagrams, the **outer shape** represents the **physical cross-section**, while the **inner shape** represents the **kern boundary**. Any load applied within the inner shape produces purely compressive stress distribution.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (2 √ó 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2023 Model]**

**Q: Define the term "kern of a section." Why is the concept of kern important in the design of masonry dams?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Kern Definition (2 marks):** The kern of a cross-section is the central region within the section such that if a compressive load is applied at any point within this region, the **entire cross-section experiences only compressive stress**, with **no tensile stress** developing at any point in the section.
>
> **Importance in Masonry Dams (1 mark):** Masonry (and concrete) is **weak in tension** but strong in compression. The resultant of all forces acting on a dam (self-weight + water pressure) must fall **within the kern** of the base section. This ensures that the **base experiences only compression** and no tension develops at the heel or toe, preventing cracking of the masonry.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2022 Model]**

**Q: For a solid circular column of diameter $400$ mm, determine the radius of the kern. Also, state the shape of the kern.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply the formula for solid circular kern radius:

$$r_{kern} = \frac{D}{8} = \frac{400 \text{ mm}}{8} = 50 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Since the section is symmetric about every centroidal axis, the kern is a **circle**.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The kern is a circle of radius **$50$ mm** centered at the centroid of the column, with diameter $100$ mm. Any compressive load applied within this $100$ mm diameter central zone produces purely compressive stress.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Full 14-Mark Questions (University ESE Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B questions have internal choice. The student attempts **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both have sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the kern of a **rectangular section** of width $b$ and depth $d$. Show that the kern is a rhombus with diagonals of length $b/3$ and $d/3$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular masonry pillar of size $600$ mm $\times 400$ mm carries a vertical load of $500$ kN. Determine the **maximum eccentricity** of the load about both axes such that no tension develops in the section. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the condition for no tension:

The stress at the extreme fiber on the side opposite to the load must be $\geq 0$:

$$\sigma_{min} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y_{max}}{I} \geq 0$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Derive the limiting eccentricity:

For rectangular section with bending about the axis parallel to $b$:
- $A = b d$
- $I_{xx} = b d^3 / 12$
- $y_{max} = d/2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{max} &= \frac{I_{xx}}{A \cdot y_{max}} = \frac{b d^3/12}{b d \cdot d/2} = \frac{d}{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Repeat for the perpendicular axis:

For bending about the axis parallel to $d$:
- $I_{yy} = d b^3 / 12$
- $y_{max} = b/2$

$$e_{max} = \frac{d b^3/12}{b d \cdot b/2} = \frac{b}{6}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Construct the kern:

The kern extends $\pm d/6$ vertically and $\pm b/6$ horizontally from the centroid. Connecting these four points produces a **rhombus**. The diagonals of this rhombus measure:
- Vertical diagonal: $2 \times d/6 = d/3$
- Horizontal diagonal: $2 \times b/6 = b/3$

Hence the kern is a **rhombus with diagonals $b/3$ and $d/3$**. $\blacksquare$

### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Section: $b = 600$ mm, $d = 400$ mm
- Load: $P = 500$ kN
- Find: maximum eccentricities $e_x$ and $e_y$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute geometric properties:

$$A = b \times d = 600 \times 400 = 240{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Maximum eccentricity about X-X axis (parallel to $b$, depth direction):

The depth along this axis is $d = 400$ mm. So:

$$e_{y, max} = \frac{d}{6} = \frac{400}{6} = 66.67 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Maximum eccentricity about Y-Y axis (parallel to $d$, width direction):

The width along this axis is $b = 600$ mm. So:

$$e_{x, max} = \frac{b}{6} = \frac{600}{6} = 100 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Result:

- Maximum eccentricity about the **major axis** (X-X): $\mathbf{e_{y, max} = 66.67 \text{ mm}}$
- Maximum eccentricity about the **minor axis** (Y-Y): $\mathbf{e_{x, max} = 100 \text{ mm}}$

> **Final Answer:** The load can be applied anywhere within $66.67$ mm of the centroid along the depth direction and $100$ mm along the width direction, with no tension in the section.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the kern radius for a **hollow circular section** of outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$. Show that the result reduces to the solid circular case when $d = 0$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A short hollow circular column has outer diameter $500$ mm and inner diameter $300$ mm. It carries an axial load of $1000$ kN with eccentricity $40$ mm from the centroid. Check whether the load lies **within the kern**. If yes, calculate the **maximum and minimum stresses** in the section. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State properties of hollow circular section:

- Area: $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$
- Moment of inertia: $I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4)$
- Extreme fiber: $y_{max} = D/2$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Factor and substitute:

Using $D^4 - d^4 = (D^2 - d^2)(D^2 + d^2)$:

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(D^2 - d^2)(D^2 + d^2)$$

Substituting into the kern formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{max} &= \frac{I}{A \cdot y_{max}} = \frac{\dfrac{\pi}{64}(D^2 - d^2)(D^2 + d^2)}{\dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Simplify:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{max} &= \frac{\pi (D^2 + d^2)}{64} \cdot \frac{8}{\pi D} = \frac{D^2 + d^2}{8D}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Verify limit case:

When $d = 0$ (solid section): $e_{max} = D^2/(8D) = D/8$ ‚úď (matches the solid circular kern formula).

Hence: $r_{kern} = \dfrac{D^2 + d^2}{8D}$ $\blacksquare$

### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 500$ mm, $d = 300$ mm
- $P = 1000$ kN = $1000 \times 10^3$ N
- $e = 40$ mm

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Calculate the kern radius:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{kern} &= \frac{D^2 + d^2}{8D} = \frac{500^2 + 300^2}{8 \times 500} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{250000 + 90000}{4000} = \frac{340000}{4000} = 85 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compare eccentricity with kern radius:

Since $e = 40$ mm $< r_{kern} = 85$ mm, the load **lies within the kern**. ‚úď No tension will develop.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Calculate the section modulus:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4) = \frac{\pi}{64}(500^4 - 300^4) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\pi}{64}(6.25 \times 10^{10} - 8.1 \times 10^8) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\pi}{64}(6.169 \times 10^{10}) = \frac{\pi \times 6.169 \times 10^{10}}{64} \\[4pt]
&= 3.027 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Calculate stresses:

Section modulus: $Z = I/y_{max} = 3.027 \times 10^9 / 250 = 1.211 \times 10^7$ mm¬≥

Axial stress: $\sigma_a = P/A = 1000 \times 10^3 / (\pi/4)(500^2 - 300^2)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi}{4}(250000 - 90000) = \frac{\pi}{4}(160000) = 125664 \text{ mm}^2 \\[4pt]
\sigma_a &= \frac{10^6}{125664} = 7.958 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compressive)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Bending stress: $\sigma_b = M/Z = (P \cdot e)/Z = (10^6 \times 40)/(1.211 \times 10^7) = 3.304$ N/mm¬≤

**Maximum compressive stress** (on load side):
$$\sigma_{max} = \sigma_a + \sigma_b = 7.958 + 3.304 = 11.26 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compressive)}$$

**Minimum stress** (opposite side):
$$\sigma_{min} = \sigma_a - \sigma_b = 7.958 - 3.304 = 4.65 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compressive)} > 0 \checkmark$$

> **Final Answer:** The load lies within the kern. Maximum stress = **$11.26$ N/mm¬≤ (compressive)**, Minimum stress = **$4.65$ N/mm¬≤ (compressive)**. No tension develops.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Axis Mismatch (‚Äď2 marks):** Students often confuse $b$ and $d$ while computing kern for a rectangular section. The kern extent along the axis **parallel to the dimension $d$** is $d/6$, not $b/6$. Always match $y_{max}$ to the **same axis** of $I$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Verify (‚Äď1 mark):** In numerical problems, students compute the kern but forget to **explicitly state** "since $e < r_{kern}$, the load is within the kern." Examiners specifically look for this conclusion.
>
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency (‚Äď1 mark):** Mixing mm and m. If the section is in mm, load in N, the kern dimension comes in **mm** and stress in **N/mm¬≤ (= MPa)**.
>
> 4. **Sign Convention Error (‚Äď1 mark):** The minimum stress must be checked to be $\geq 0$ (compressive). Some students mistakenly check that it is $\leq 0$.
>
> 5. **Skipping the "Middle Third Rule" Statement (‚Äď1 mark):** For rectangular sections, explicitly mentioning the **Middle Third Rule** in your answer earns **bonus conceptual marks** in the KTU evaluation key.
>
> 6. **Wrong kern shape (‚Äď2 marks):** Stating "the kern is rectangular" instead of "rhombus/diamond" for a rectangular section. The kern is **always a rotated shape**, not aligned with the original section boundaries.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kern of a Section ‚ÄĒ High-Density Revision Notes**

- **Definition:** The kern is the central region of a cross-section where the load must be applied to ensure **no tensile stress** anywhere in the section.
- **Master Formula:** $e_{max} = \dfrac{I}{A \cdot y_{max}} = \dfrac{r^2}{y_{max}}$
- **Solid Circle:** $r_{kern} = D/8$
- **Hollow Circle:** $r_{kern} = \dfrac{D^2 + d^2}{8D}$
- **Rectangle ($b \times d$):** Rhombus with diagonals $b/3$ and $d/3$; extents are $b/6$ and $d/6$ from centroid.
- **Middle Third Rule:** For a rectangular base, the kern is the **middle third** of the base width ‚ÄĒ this is the foundation of **gravity dam design**.
- **Stress at Kern Boundary:** Triangular distribution ‚ÄĒ **zero** on far edge, **$2P/A$** on near edge.
- **Stress Inside Kern:** Trapezoidal distribution ‚ÄĒ purely compressive everywhere.
- **Stress Outside Kern:** Tension develops on the far side ‚ÄĒ **unsafe** for masonry/concrete.
- **Isotropy:** The kern of a circular section is a **perfect circle**; rectangular sections give **rhombus**.
- **Common KTU pitfall:** Always verify that $e \leq r_{kern}$ **before** computing stresses; otherwise, your "minimum stress" will be negative, signaling tension.
- **Real-world applications:** Masonry dams, chimneys, bridge piers, retaining walls, spread footings, unreinforced concrete columns.
- **KTU favourite topics for 14-mark questions:** (i) Derivation of kern for a given section, (ii) Numerical verification of whether a load lies within the kern, (iii) Stress computation with stress diagrams.
- **Memorization tip:** Remember the sequence ‚ÄĒ Area ‚Üí Moment of Inertia ‚Üí $y_{max}$ ‚Üí Apply $e = r^2/y_{max}$ ‚Üí Construct shape.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is a Slender Column?

A **slender column** is a structural member whose length is very large compared to its lateral dimensions, causing it to fail by **buckling** (lateral deflection) rather than by direct compression/crushing of the material. The cross-sectional dimensions are small relative to the unsupported length, making the column susceptible to sudden lateral instability under an axial compressive load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A column is classified as a **slender column** when its **slenderness ratio** $\lambda$ (ratio of effective length $L_e$ to the least radius of gyration $k$) exceeds a critical value beyond which failure occurs by **elastic buckling** as predicted by **Euler's theory**, rather than by yielding or crushing of the material.

## 1.2 Classification of Columns Based on Slenderness Ratio

| Type | Slenderness Ratio $\lambda = L_e / k$ | Mode of Failure |
|------|---------------------------------------|-----------------|
| **Short Column** | $\lambda < 30$ | Crushing / Yielding |
| **Medium Column** | $30 \le \lambda \le 100$ | Combined crushing & buckling |
| **Slender / Long Column** | $\lambda > 100$ | Pure Elastic Buckling (Euler) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slenderness Ratio ($\lambda$):** $\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{k}$, where $L_e$ is the effective length and $k$ is the least radius of gyration. The unit is **dimensionless**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Spaghetti Test"

Imagine holding a **thick wooden stick (dowel)** vertically and pressing down on it. You need a *huge* force to break it ‚ÄĒ it crushes eventually. Now take a **thin, long uncooked spaghetti strand** and press it from the top. It does **not** crush; instead, it suddenly **bows sideways with a snap** even under a tiny load. That sideways snap is **buckling**. The spaghetti is the "slender column." The wooden dowel is a "short column."

The key intuition: **Slender = Geometry dominates over Material strength.** The slenderness controls failure, not the material's compressive strength.

## 1.4 Effective Length ($L_e$)

The effective length is the length of an *equivalent pin-ended column* that has the same buckling load as the actual column with its given end conditions. It is expressed as:

$$L_e = \alpha \cdot L$$

where $\alpha$ is the **effective length constant** depending on end fixity, and $L$ is the actual length of the column.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effective length constant ($\alpha$)** values for different end conditions (these are *board-exam favorites*):
> - Both ends **hinged (pinned)**: $\alpha = 1.0$
> - Both ends **fixed**: $\alpha = 0.5$
> - One end **fixed, other free (cantilever)**: $\alpha = 2.0$
> - One end **fixed, other hinged**: $\alpha = 0.7$ (or $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$)

## 1.5 Core Terminology at a Glance

- **Buckling Load / Crippling Load ($P_{cr}$):** The axial compressive load at which the column suddenly deflects laterally.
- **Critical Stress ($\sigma_{cr}$):** Buckling load divided by cross-sectional area, $\sigma_{cr} = P_{cr} / A$.
- **Radius of Gyration ($k$):** $k = \sqrt{I/A}$, where $I$ is the moment of inertia and $A$ is the cross-sectional area.
- **Least Radius of Gyration ($k_{min}$):** Always used in column design (uses the *weaker* principal axis).
- **Euler's Crippling Load ($P_E$):** Buckling load predicted by Euler's elastic theory.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Euler's Theory of Buckling (1744)

Euler derived the classical formula for the elastic buckling load of a long, slender, initially straight column with a centric axial load, assuming:

> [!NOTE]
> **Euler's Assumptions (Must Memorize for KTU):**
> 1. The column is *initially perfectly straight* and the load is *perfectly axial (centric)*.
> 2. The column material is *homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic* (obeys Hooke's Law up to buckling).
> 3. The *self-weight of the column is neglected*.
> 4. The *end loads are purely axial*; there is no eccentricity.
> 5. The *cross-section is uniform* throughout the length.
> 6. Failure occurs by *pure bending* (no shear deformation, no crippling).
> 7. The column *buckles in a single curvature* in the plane of least resistance (i.e., about the axis with minimum moment of inertia).

## 2.2 Why Does a Slender Column Buckle? (The "Why" Behind the Theory)

When a perfectly straight, axially loaded column is in equilibrium under a small load $P$, it remains straight. However, this *straight* equilibrium becomes **unstable** above a critical load $P_{cr}$. Theoretically:

- **Mathematically:** The differential equation of the deflected shape $EI \cdot \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} + P \cdot y = 0$ has a *trivial solution* ($y = 0$ everywhere) for *all* $P$, but a *non-trivial buckled solution* exists **only when** $P$ equals a specific eigenvalue $P_{cr}$.
- **Physically:** Any infinitesimal lateral disturbance triggers runaway lateral deflection. The lateral deflection produces a bending moment $M = P \cdot y$ that creates additional deflection, which increases the moment, and so on ‚ÄĒ leading to catastrophic lateral instability.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **examination-critical**. Memorize units carefully. **Never use `|` for absolute value in this table** ‚ÄĒ use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Euler's Crippling Load** | $P_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 \, E \, I}{L_e^{\,2}}$ | $P$ in N, $E$ in N/m¬≤, $I$ in m‚Āī, $L_e$ in m |
| 2 | **Euler's Critical Stress** | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \, E}{\lambda^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \, E \, k^2}{L_e^{\,2}}$ | $\sigma_{cr}$ in N/m¬≤, $\lambda$ dimensionless |
| 3 | **Slenderness Ratio** | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{k}$ | $k = \sqrt{I/A}$ ‚ÄĒ least radius of gyration used |
| 4 | **Radius of Gyration** | $k = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | $I$ in m‚Āī, $A$ in m¬≤, $k$ in m |
| 5 | **Effective Length** | $L_e = \alpha \cdot L$ | $\alpha$ depends on end conditions |
| 6 | **Rankine's Critical Load** | $P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \cdot (\lambda^2)} = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \cdot (L_e/k)^2}$ | $\sigma_c$ = crushing stress, $a$ = Rankine's constant (material-dependent) |
| 7 | **Rankine's Constant** | $a = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\pi^2 \cdot E}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | **Equivalent / Safe Column** | $P_R = \dfrac{P_E \cdot P_C}{P_E + P_C}$ | $P_C = \sigma_c \cdot A$ (crushing load) |
| 9 | **Crippling Load (Secant Formula)** | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + (e \cdot c / k^2) \cdot \sec[(L_e / 2k)\sqrt{P/(AE)}]}$ | Eccentric loading |

## 2.4 Effective Length Constants ‚ÄĒ Master Diagram Reference

| End Condition | $\alpha = L_e / L$ | Effective Length $L_e$ |
|---------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Both ends hinged (pinned-pinned) | $1.0$ | $L$ |
| Both ends fixed (fixed-fixed) | $0.5$ | $L/2$ |
| One end fixed, one end free (cantilever) | $2.0$ | $2L$ |
| One end fixed, one end hinged | $0.7$ (or $1/\sqrt{2}$) | $L/\sqrt{2}$ |

## 2.5 End-Condition Reasoning ‚ÄĒ The "Why" Behind the Constants

- **Hinged-Hinged ($\alpha = 1$):** The column is free to rotate at both ends but cannot translate. The buckled shape is a *single half-sine wave*. This is the **reference case** for which $L_e = L$.
- **Fixed-Fixed ($\alpha = 0.5$):** Both ends resist rotation and translation. The buckled shape has *inflection points* at $L/4$ and $3L/4$. The portion between the two inflection points behaves like a pin-ended column of length $L/2$, hence $L_e = L/2$.
- **Fixed-Free ($\alpha = 2$):** The fixed end resists rotation; the free end can rotate and translate freely. The buckled shape is a *quarter sine wave* equivalent to a pin-ended column of length $2L$, hence $L_e = 2L$.
- **Fixed-Hinged ($\alpha = 0.7$):** Mixed conditions. The buckled shape has an inflection point at $0.3L$ from the hinged end. The effective length is $0.7L = L/\sqrt{2}$.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Euler's Theory

- **Design of steel columns** in multi-storey buildings, bridges, towers, and industrial structures.
- **Design of long struts** in aircraft fuselage and ship masts.
- **Sizing of scaffolding poles** and formwork supports.
- **Foundation piles** under lateral loads.
- **Pressure vessel skirt supports** in chemical engineering.

## 2.7 Limitations of Euler's Formula

> [!WARNING]
> Euler's formula is **valid only for slender columns** where $\lambda > \lambda_{critical}$ (a value dependent on the material's yield point). For $\lambda$ below this threshold, Euler's formula *over-predicts* the buckling load and **Rankine's formula must be used**.

The critical slenderness ratio at which Euler's theory becomes valid is given by:

$$\lambda_{critical} = \pi \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\sigma_y}}$$

where $\sigma_y$ is the yield stress of the material. For mild steel, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm¬≤ and $\sigma_y = 330$ N/mm¬≤ gives $\lambda_{critical} \approx 77.5$.

## 2.8 Rankine's Theory ‚ÄĒ Empirical Improvement over Euler

Rankine observed that:
- For **short columns** ($\lambda \to 0$), failure load should approach the crushing load $P_C = \sigma_c \cdot A$.
- For **long/slender columns** ($\lambda \to \infty$), failure load should approach Euler's load $P_E$.

Rankine proposed a single empirical formula that smoothly bridges these two limits:

$$\boxed{P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \cdot \lambda^2}}$$

This is the **most widely used formula in KTU board examinations** for medium and long columns.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Crippling Load (Pin-Ended Column)

### 3.1.1 Setup and Coordinate System

Consider a column of length $L$, hinged at both ends, with an axial compressive load $P$ applied at the centroid. Let the column deflect laterally by an amount $y$ at distance $x$ from the bottom support.

**Bending moment at section $x$:**

$$M = -P \cdot y$$

(The negative sign indicates the moment causes curvature opposing the deflection direction.)

**Euler-Bernoulli bending equation:**

$$EI \cdot \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M = -P \cdot y$$

### 3.1.2 Formulating the Differential Equation

Rearranging:

$$\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + \dfrac{P}{EI} \cdot y = 0$$

This is a **second-order linear homogeneous ODE** with constant coefficients. The general solution is:

$$y = A \cdot \cos(\beta x) + B \cdot \sin(\beta x)$$

where $\beta = \sqrt{P / (EI)}$.

### 3.1.3 Applying Boundary Conditions (Pin-Ended)

For a pin-ended column:

- **At $x = 0$:** $y = 0$ (deflection at the support is zero)

$$A \cdot \cos(0) + B \cdot \sin(0) = A = 0$$

- **At $x = L$:** $y = 0$ (deflection at the other support is zero)

$$0 = B \cdot \sin(\beta L) \Rightarrow \sin(\beta L) = 0$$

The condition $B = 0$ would give the *trivial* (straight) solution $y \equiv 0$. For a non-trivial buckled solution:

$$\beta \cdot L = n \pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

The **smallest** buckling load corresponds to $n = 1$ (fundamental mode):

$$\beta \cdot L = \pi \Rightarrow \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{EI}} \cdot L = \pi$$

### 3.1.4 Solving for the Critical Load

Squaring both sides:

$$\dfrac{P \cdot L^2}{EI} = \pi^2$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{L^2}}$$

This is **Euler's crippling load for a pin-ended column**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the smallest $n$?** Buckling is a *stability* problem. The column will buckle at the *lowest* possible load because any load above the first critical load triggers the *first mode* of lateral instability. The first mode is therefore the governing mode.

## 3.2 Effective Length Derivation ‚ÄĒ Fixed-Fixed Column

For a column with both ends fixed, the buckled shape has zero deflection AND zero slope at both ends.

- At $x = 0$: $y = 0$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$
- At $x = L$: $y = 0$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$

The general solution is $y = A \cos(\beta x) + B \sin(\beta x)$.

From $y(0) = 0$: $A = 0$, leaving $y = B \sin(\beta x)$.

From $\dfrac{dy}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = 0$: $B \cdot \beta \cdot \cos(0) = B \beta = 0$, which forces $B = 0$ (trivial). 

This is contradictory ‚ÄĒ meaning the **general solution form must be modified** using hyperbolic functions, or we use the fact that the buckled shape is symmetric with inflection points. The physical argument: the fixed-fixed column buckles into an *S-shape* with two nodes (inflection points) where $M = 0$. Between these two inflection points, the column behaves like a pin-ended column of length $L/2$.

Therefore:

$$L_e = \dfrac{L}{2}, \quad \alpha = 0.5$$

## 3.3 Effective Length Derivation ‚ÄĒ Cantilever (Fixed-Free)

For a column fixed at one end and free at the other:

- At fixed end: $y = 0$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$
- At free end: $M = 0$ (no moment) and shear = 0

The buckled shape is a *quarter sine wave*. By symmetry, this is equivalent to a pin-ended column of length $2L$ (mirror image of the half-sine wave about the midpoint).

Therefore:

$$L_e = 2L, \quad \alpha = 2.0$$

## 3.4 Effective Length Derivation ‚ÄĒ Fixed-Hinged

The general solution satisfies:
- At fixed end: $y = 0$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$
- At hinged end: $y = 0$ and $M = 0$ (so $\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = 0$)

The characteristic equation yields $\beta L = 4.493$, giving:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{(4.493)^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{L^2} = \dfrac{20.19 \, E \, I}{L^2}$$

Comparing with $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \, E \, I}{L_e^{\,2}} = \dfrac{9.87 \, E \, I}{L_e^{\,2}}$:

$$L_e^{\,2} = \dfrac{9.87 \cdot L^2}{20.19} = 0.489 \cdot L^2 \Rightarrow L_e = 0.7 L$$

## 3.5 Derivation of Rankine's Formula

### 3.5.1 Empirical Foundation

Rankine proposed that the actual load $P$ causing failure is related to the crushing load $P_C$ and Euler's load $P_E$ by:

$$\dfrac{1}{P_R} = \dfrac{1}{P_C} + \dfrac{1}{P_E}$$

This is the **reciprocal formula** ‚ÄĒ add reciprocally, sum inversely.

### 3.5.2 Mathematical Derivation from the Reciprocal Form

Starting point:

$$\dfrac{1}{P_R} = \dfrac{1}{P_C} + \dfrac{1}{P_E}$$

Substituting $P_C = \sigma_c \cdot A$ and $P_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^{\,2}}$:

$$\dfrac{1}{P_R} = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_c A} + \dfrac{L_e^{\,2}}{\pi^2 E I}$$

Multiply throughout by $\sigma_c A$:

$$\dfrac{\sigma_c A}{P_R} = 1 + \dfrac{\sigma_c A \cdot L_e^{\,2}}{\pi^2 E I}$$

Note that $k^2 = I / A$, so $L_e^{\,2} / I = (L_e / k)^2 \cdot (1/A)$, hence:

$$\dfrac{L_e^{\,2}}{I} = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{A}$$

Substituting:

$$\dfrac{\sigma_c A}{P_R} = 1 + \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot L_e^{\,2}}{\pi^2 E \cdot (I/A)} = 1 + \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\pi^2 E} \cdot \lambda^2$$

Defining Rankine's constant $a = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\pi^2 E}$:

$$\dfrac{\sigma_c A}{P_R} = 1 + a \cdot \lambda^2$$

Inverting:

$$\boxed{P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \cdot \lambda^2}}$$

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A hollow circular steel column has outer diameter $D = 100$ mm, inner diameter $d = 80$ mm, length $L = 5$ m, and is fixed at both ends. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm¬≤, $\sigma_c = 320$ N/mm¬≤. Calculate:
> (a) Euler's crippling load.
> (b) Rankine's crippling load.
> (c) The safe load using a factor of safety $FOS = 2$.

**Solution ‚ÄĒ Part (a): Euler's load**

For a fixed-fixed column, $\alpha = 0.5$, so $L_e = 0.5 \times 5000 = 2500$ mm.

Moment of inertia of hollow circular section:

$$I = \dfrac{\pi}{64} (D^4 - d^4) = \dfrac{\pi}{64} (100^4 - 80^4)$$

Computing $D^4 = 10^8$ and $d^4 = 4.096 \times 10^7$:

$$I = \dfrac{\pi}{64} (10^8 - 4.096 \times 10^7) = \dfrac{\pi}{64} \cdot 5.904 \times 10^7$$

$$I = \dfrac{3.1416 \times 5.904 \times 10^7}{64} = 2.898 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

Euler's crippling load:

$$P_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{L_e^{\,2}} = \dfrac{(9.8696) \cdot (2 \times 10^5) \cdot (2.898 \times 10^6)}{(2500)^2}$$

Numerator $= 9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 2.898 \times 10^6 = 5.721 \times 10^{12}$

Denominator $= 6.25 \times 10^6$

$$P_E = \dfrac{5.721 \times 10^{12}}{6.25 \times 10^6} = 9.15 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 915 \text{ kN}$$

**Solution ‚ÄĒ Part (b): Rankine's load**

Cross-sectional area:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (100^2 - 80^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (10000 - 6400) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 3600$$

$$A = 2827.4 \text{ mm}^2$$

Slenderness ratio:

$$k^2 = \dfrac{I}{A} = \dfrac{2.898 \times 10^6}{2827.4} = 1025 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$k = \sqrt{1025} = 32.02 \text{ mm}$$

$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{k} = \dfrac{2500}{32.02} = 78.08$$

Rankine's constant:

$$a = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\pi^2 E} = \dfrac{320}{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5} = \dfrac{320}{1.9739 \times 10^6} = 1.621 \times 10^{-4}$$

$a \cdot \lambda^2 = 1.621 \times 10^{-4} \times (78.08)^2 = 1.621 \times 10^{-4} \times 6096.5 = 0.9883$

$$P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \cdot \lambda^2} = \dfrac{320 \times 2827.4}{1 + 0.9883} = \dfrac{904768}{1.9883} = 455{,}050 \text{ N} \approx 455 \text{ kN}$$

**Solution ‚ÄĒ Part (c): Safe load**

$$\text{Safe load} = \dfrac{P_R}{FOS} = \dfrac{455}{2} = 227.5 \text{ kN}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification check:** Since $\lambda = 78.08 \approx \lambda_{critical} \approx 77.5$ for mild steel, this column is *borderline*. In practice, the engineer would use the **smaller** of the two loads (Rankine's) as the design value, which we did.

## 3.7 Summary of Solution Methodology (Board Exam Approach)

1. Identify end conditions and determine $\alpha$, then $L_e = \alpha L$.
2. Calculate $A$ and $I$ for the given cross-section.
3. Compute $k = \sqrt{I/A}$ (least $k$ if asymmetric sections).
4. Compute $\lambda = L_e / k$.
5. If $\lambda > \lambda_{critical}$ ‚Üí use **Euler's formula**.
6. If $\lambda \le \lambda_{critical}$ ‚Üí use **Rankine's formula** (or equivalent / Johnson's formula).
7. Apply factor of safety for safe load.
