# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2022]**
> *Define the term "Slender Column" and explain how it differs from a short column.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **slender column** is defined as a compression member whose **slenderness ratio** $\lambda = L_e / k$ is large enough that failure occurs by **elastic buckling** rather than by crushing of the material.

**Distinction from short column:**

| Feature | Short Column | Slender Column |
|---------|--------------|----------------|
| Slenderness ratio | $\lambda < 30$ | $\lambda > 100$ |
| Mode of failure | Crushing / yielding | Lateral buckling |
| Governing formula | $P = \sigma_c \cdot A$ | $P = \pi^2 EI / L_e^2$ |
| Material strength matters? | Yes (dominant) | No (geometry dominates) |

> **Question 2. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2023]**
> *What is effective length? List the effective length constants for the four standard end conditions of a column.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **effective length** of a column is the length of an *equivalent pin-ended column* that buckles under the same load as the actual column with its given end conditions. It is defined as $L_e = \alpha \cdot L$, where $\alpha$ is a constant.

The four standard end conditions and their $\alpha$ values are:

- **Both ends hinged:** $\alpha = 1.0$
- **Both ends fixed:** $\alpha = 0.5$
- **One end fixed, one end free (cantilever):** $\alpha = 2.0$
- **One end fixed, one end hinged:** $\alpha = 0.7$ (or $1/\sqrt{2}$)

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> ### Question A. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]
>
> **(a)** Derive the expression for Euler's crippling load of a long column hinged at both ends. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A solid circular steel column is **3 m** long, has a diameter of **60 mm**, and is fixed at one end and hinged at the other. Compute:
> 1. Euler's crippling load
> 2. Slenderness ratio
> 3. Safe load using FOS = 3
>
> Given: $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm¬≤, $\sigma_c = 330$ N/mm¬≤. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Part (a) (7 marks):**

The Euler-Bernoulli equation for a deflected column with axial load $P$ is:

$$EI \cdot \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -P \cdot y$$

(1 mark for equation setup)

The general solution is:

$$y = A \cos(\beta x) + B \sin(\beta x), \quad \beta = \sqrt{P/EI}$$

(1 mark for general solution)

**Boundary conditions** for hinged-hinged: $y(0) = 0$ and $y(L) = 0$.

- At $x = 0$: $A = 0$
- At $x = L$: $B \sin(\beta L) = 0$ ‚Üí $\beta L = n\pi$

(2 marks for boundary conditions)

Taking the **fundamental mode** $n = 1$:

$$\beta L = \pi \Rightarrow P = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L^2}$$

(1 mark for derivation)

$$\boxed{P_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L^2}}$$

(1 mark for final result)

**Assumptions** (1 mark): Initial straightness, axial centric loading, homogeneous isotropic linear-elastic material, negligible self-weight, uniform cross-section, no shear deformation, buckling about the axis of least moment of inertia.

**Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Geometric Properties**

Cross-sectional area: $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (60)^2 = 2827.4$ mm¬≤

(1 mark)

Moment of inertia: $I = \dfrac{\pi}{64} d^4 = \dfrac{\pi}{64} (60)^4 = 6.362 \times 10^5$ mm‚Āī

(1 mark)

Radius of gyration: $k = \sqrt{I/A} = \sqrt{6.362 \times 10^5 / 2827.4} = \sqrt{225} = 15$ mm

(1 mark)

**Step 2: Effective Length and Slenderness Ratio**

For fixed-hinged column: $\alpha = 0.7$, so $L_e = 0.7 \times 3000 = 2100$ mm

$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{k} = \dfrac{2100}{15} = 140$$

(1 mark)

Since $\lambda = 140 > \lambda_{critical} = 77.5$, **Euler's formula is valid**.

**Step 3: Euler's Crippling Load**

$$P_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{L_e^{\,2}} = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 6.362 \times 10^5}{(2100)^2}$$

Numerator: $9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 6.362 \times 10^5 = 1.256 \times 10^{12}$

Denominator: $(2100)^2 = 4.41 \times 10^6$

$$P_E = \dfrac{1.256 \times 10^{12}}{4.41 \times 10^6} = 2.848 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 284.8 \text{ kN}$$

(2 marks for computation)

**Step 4: Safe Load**

$$P_{safe} = \dfrac{P_E}{FOS} = \dfrac{284.8}{3} = 94.93 \text{ kN} \approx 95 \text{ kN}$$

(1 mark)

---

> ### Question B. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]
>
> **(a)** State and explain **Rankine's formula** for the crippling load of a column. Discuss how it overcomes the limitations of Euler's formula. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A hollow rectangular cast-iron column has outer dimensions **120 mm √ó 100 mm**, inner dimensions **80 mm √ó 60 mm**, and length **6 m**. It is fixed at both ends. Determine:
> 1. Rankine's crippling load
> 2. Euler's crippling load
> 3. Compare the two results.
>
> Given: $E = 1.2 \times 10^5$ N/mm¬≤, $\sigma_c = 550$ N/mm¬≤, Rankine's constant $a = 1/1600$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Statement of Rankine's formula** (2 marks):

$$P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \cdot \lambda^2}$$

where $P_R$ = Rankine's crippling load, $\sigma_c$ = crushing stress of material, $A$ = cross-sectional area, $a$ = Rankine's constant, $\lambda$ = slenderness ratio.

**Origin of the formula (Reciprocal formula)** (2 marks):

$$\dfrac{1}{P_R} = \dfrac{1}{P_C} + \dfrac{1}{P_E}$$

This unifies the two limiting cases:
- As $\lambda \to 0$ (short column): $P_R \to P_C = \sigma_c A$ (crushing)
- As $\lambda \to \infty$ (long column): $P_R \to P_E = \pi^2 EI / L_e^2$ (Euler buckling)

**Why it overcomes Euler's limitations** (2 marks):

Euler's formula assumes purely elastic behavior and is valid only for **very high** slenderness ratios. For medium slenderness columns (with $\lambda$ less than the critical value), Euler's formula over-predicts the failure load. Rankine's empirical formula handles the **entire range of slenderness** ‚ÄĒ short, medium, and long columns ‚ÄĒ within a single equation, making it a much more practical design tool for real engineering applications.

**Derivation of constant $a$** (1 mark):

$$a = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\pi^2 E}$$

For mild steel with $\sigma_c = 320$ N/mm¬≤ and $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm¬≤: $a = 320 / (9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5) = 1.62 \times 10^{-4} = 1/6175$.

**Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Geometric Properties**

Cross-sectional area:

$$A = (120 \times 100) - (80 \times 60) = 12000 - 4800 = 7200 \text{ mm}^2$$

(1 mark)

Moments of inertia about both axes:

- $I_{xx} = \dfrac{120 \times 100^3 - 80 \times 60^3}{12} = \dfrac{12 \times 10^6 - 1.728 \times 10^6}{12} = 8.56 \times 10^5$ mm‚Āī
- $I_{yy} = \dfrac{100 \times 120^3 - 60 \times 80^3}{12} = \dfrac{17.28 \times 10^6 - 3.072 \times 10^6}{12} = 1.184 \times 10^6$ mm‚Āī

**Least** $I = I_{xx} = 8.56 \times 10^5$ mm‚Āī

(1 mark)

**Step 2: Effective Length and Slenderness Ratio**

For fixed-fixed column: $\alpha = 0.5$, $L_e = 0.5 \times 6000 = 3000$ mm

$$k^2 = \dfrac{I}{A} = \dfrac{8.56 \times 10^5}{7200} = 118.89 \text{ mm}^2, \quad k = 10.90 \text{ mm}$$

$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{k} = \dfrac{3000}{10.90} = 275.2$$

(1 mark)

**Step 3: Rankine's Crippling Load**

$$P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \cdot \lambda^2} = \dfrac{550 \times 7200}{1 + (1/1600) \times (275.2)^2}$$

$a \cdot \lambda^2 = (275.2)^2 / 1600 = 75733.5 / 1600 = 47.33$

$$P_R = \dfrac{3{,}960{,}000}{1 + 47.33} = \dfrac{3{,}960{,}000}{48.33} = 81{,}937 \text{ N} \approx 81.94 \text{ kN}$$

(2 marks for computation)

**Step 4: Euler's Crippling Load**

$$P_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{L_e^{\,2}} = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 1.2 \times 10^5 \times 8.56 \times 10^5}{(3000)^2}$$

Numerator: $9.8696 \times 1.2 \times 10^5 \times 8.56 \times 10^5 = 1.0137 \times 10^{12}$

Denominator: $9 \times 10^6$

$$P_E = \dfrac{1.0137 \times 10^{12}}{9 \times 10^6} = 1.126 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 112.6 \text{ kN}$$

(1 mark)

**Step 5: Comparison** (1 mark):

$P_R = 81.94$ kN, $P_E = 112.6$ kN. Since $\lambda = 275$ is well above the critical slenderness, Euler's formula is theoretically valid. However, **Rankine's load is more conservative** (lower) because it accounts for both crushing and buckling effects. In practice, the engineer should use the **smaller of the two values** ($P_R = 81.94$ kN) as the design crippling load for safety.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the effective length correction**: Students often use the actual length $L$ instead of $L_e$ in Euler's formula. *This is the #1 reason for losing 2‚Äď3 marks in Part B questions.* Always identify end conditions FIRST and compute $L_e$.
> 2. **Using the wrong moment of inertia**: For unsymmetric sections, you MUST use the **least** $I$ (and hence the **least** $k$) because the column buckles about its weakest axis.
> 3. **Confusing Rankine's and Euler's applicability**: Euler's formula is only valid when $\lambda > \lambda_{critical}$. For shorter columns, blindly applying Euler overestimates the strength.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency**: Always express all dimensions in **mm** consistently when using $E$ in N/mm¬≤. Mixing mm and m is a frequent mistake.
> 5. **Missing FOS in the final answer**: Board exams often ask for the **safe load**, requiring division by the factor of safety. A correct crippling load with no FOS loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Incorrect Rankine's constant**: Use the value given in the problem (e.g., $a = 1/1600$ for cast iron). Do not calculate $a$ from $\sigma_c$ unless asked ‚ÄĒ use the given value to avoid confusion.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets (Read this section 5 minutes before the exam):**
>
> - **Slenderness Ratio**: $\lambda = L_e / k$ ‚ÄĒ use the *least* $k$ always.
> - **Effective length** $L_e = \alpha L$, with $\alpha = 1.0, 0.5, 2.0, 0.7$ for pin-pin, fixed-fixed, fixed-free, fixed-hinged respectively.
> - **Euler's load**: $P_E = \pi^2 E I / L_e^2$ ‚ÄĒ *valid only for $\lambda > \lambda_{critical}$*.
> - **Euler's critical stress**: $\sigma_{cr} = \pi^2 E / \lambda^2$.
> - **Rankine's load**: $P_R = \sigma_c A / (1 + a \lambda^2)$ ‚ÄĒ valid for **all** slenderness ratios (most practical formula).
> - **Rankine's reciprocal form**: $1/P_R = 1/P_C + 1/P_E$.
> - **Rankine's constant**: $a = \sigma_c / (\pi^2 E)$ ‚ÄĒ given in problems for cast iron ($1/1600$) and steel ($1/7500$).
> - **Critical slenderness ratio**: $\lambda_{critical} = \pi \sqrt{E/\sigma_y}$ ‚ÄĒ values for mild steel ‚Čą 80, cast iron ‚Čą 80‚Äď90, timber ‚Čą 100‚Äď110.
> - **Boundary conditions** for pin-ended: $y = 0$ at both ends. Slope is free.
> - **Boundary conditions** for fixed-ended: $y = 0$ AND $dy/dx = 0$ at both ends.
> - **Buckling mode shape**: $n = 1$ (fundamental) is *always* the governing mode ‚ÄĒ smallest load, largest deflection amplitude.
> - **Cross-section property formulas to remember**:
>   - Solid circular: $I = \pi d^4 / 64$, $A = \pi d^2 / 4$
>   - Hollow circular: $I = \pi (D^4 - d^4) / 64$, $A = \pi (D^2 - d^2) / 4$
>   - Solid rectangular: $I = bd^3 / 12$, $A = bd$ (b is width, d is depth)
>   - Hollow rectangular: $I = (BD^3 - bd^3)/12$, $A = BD - bd$
> - **Factor of safety (FOS)** is *always* applied to the crippling load, not the safe load.
> - **Euler's theory is for elastic buckling** ‚ÄĒ beyond yielding, use **Johnson's formula** or Rankine's formula.
> - **Real-world uses of slender column analysis**: scaffolding, building columns, bridge piers, antenna masts, flag poles, drill strings, ship masts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Euler's Buckling Load, Slenderness Ratio, Limitation of Euler's Formula
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Euler's Buckling Load, Slenderness Ratio, Limitation of Euler's Formula

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Euler's Buckling Load (Critical Load, $P_{cr}$):** It is the axial compressive load at which a long, slender, initially straight column becomes unstable and undergoes a sudden lateral deflection (called *buckling*). Mathematically, it is the load at which the column transitions from a stable straight configuration to an unstable bent configuration. The general form proposed by **Leonhard Euler (1757)** is:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}}$$

where:
- **$E$** = Young's Modulus of the column material (in **N/mm¬≤** or **GPa**)
- **$I$** = Second moment of area about the axis of buckling (in **mm‚Āī** or **m‚Āī**)
- **$L_{e}$** = Effective length of the column (depends on end conditions)
- **$L$** = Actual unsupported length of the column

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The buckling load depends on the **flexural rigidity $EI$** and the **effective length $L_e$**, *not* on the compressive strength of the material. A stronger (higher-yield) steel is **not** more resistant to buckling ‚ÄĒ only stiffness matters for slender columns.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a thin plastic ruler held vertically on a table with your fingers. If you press straight down gently, nothing happens. But the moment you increase the downward push beyond a certain tiny value, the ruler suddenly snaps sideways and bows outward ‚ÄĒ even though the load itself is small. That *snap-through* is **buckling**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Explanation:** A long, thin column behaves like a spring resisting bending. As the axial load increases, the column's *resistance to staying straight* weakens. At $P = P_{cr}$, the straight shape is no longer in equilibrium ‚ÄĒ the column must bend sideways to balance the load. The critical load is *not* about breaking the material; it is about **losing geometric stability**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Young's Modulus for Mild Steel:** **$E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm¬≤ (200 GPa)**
- **Young's Modulus for Cast Iron:** **$E = 1.0 \times 10^{5}$ to $1.5 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm¬≤**
- **Young's Modulus for Timber:** **$E \approx 1.0 \times 10^{4}$ N/mm¬≤ (varies widely)**
- **Radius of Gyration:** $k = \sqrt{I/A}$ (units: **mm** or **m**)
- **Slenderness Ratio:** **$\lambda = L_e / k$** (dimensionless, but typically reported as a pure number)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation (Step-by-Step Logic)

The derivation rests on four fundamental assumptions of classical Euler theory:

1. **The column is perfectly straight** (no initial imperfections).
2. **The load is purely axial** with no eccentricity.
3. **The column material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law** (linear elasticity).
4. **Self-weight of the column is neglected.**
5. **The end conditions are idealized** (pinned, fixed, or free).

**Logical sequence of the theory:**

- **Step 1:** Consider a column under axial load $P$. Apply a small lateral disturbance; the column deflects by $y$ at distance $x$ from origin.
- **Step 2:** The bending moment at section $x$ is $M = -Py$ (since the load $P$ acts at a lever arm $y$).
- **Step 3:** From the Euler-Bernoulli beam equation: $EI \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M = -Py$.
- **Step 4:** This gives the differential equation $\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + k^{2} y = 0$, where $k^{2} = \dfrac{P}{EI}$.
- **Step 5:** The general solution is $y = A \sin(kx) + B \cos(kx)$.
- **Step 6:** Apply boundary conditions based on end-fixity to obtain the *smallest non-trivial* (non-zero) value of $kL$, which gives $P_{cr}$.

---

### 2.2 Effective Length for Various End Conditions

The boundary conditions at the column ends dictate the deflected shape. This is captured through the **Effective Length $L_e$**:

| End Condition | Deflected Shape Sketch (Words) | Effective Length $L_e$ | Theoretical $\pi$ Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Both ends **Pinned** (Hinged-Hinged) | Single half-sine wave (one curve) | $L$ | $\pi$ |
| Both ends **Fixed** (Fixed-Fixed) | Three half-curves (S-shape with end rotations prevented) | $L/2$ | $2\pi$ |
| **Fixed** at one end, **Free** at the other (Cantilever) | Quarter sine wave | $2L$ | $\pi/2$ |
| **Fixed** at one end, **Pinned** at the other | Asymmetric half curve | $L/\sqrt{2}$ | $\pi/\sqrt{2}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Effective Lengths:** "**F-F-P-P** ‚Üí *Half, Double, One, One-by-Root-Two*" ‚ÄĒ i.e., Fixed-Fixed = L/2, Fixed-Free = 2L, Pinned-Pinned = L, Fixed-Pinned = L/‚ąö2.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Euler Critical Load | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}}$ | N (or kN) | Most-frequently-asked KTU formula |
| 2 | Critical Stress | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}$ | N/mm¬≤ | Derived by dividing $P_{cr}$ by area $A$ |
| 3 | Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{e}}{k}$ | Dimensionless | $k$ is least radius of gyration |
| 4 | Radius of Gyration | $k = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | mm or m | Always use *least* $I$ for buckling |
| 5 | Effective Length | $L_{e} = \beta \cdot L$ | mm or m | $\beta$ is end-condition factor |
| 6 | Crippling / Yield Load | $P_{y} = \sigma_{c} \cdot A$ | N (or kN) | For short columns, crushing governs |
| 7 | Critical Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda_{c} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\sigma_{c}}}$ | Dimensionless | Boundary below which Euler's formula fails |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Euler buckling theory is foundational in:

- **Structural Steel Design** ‚ÄĒ Design of columns in high-rise buildings, transmission towers, bridge piers.
- **Aerospace Engineering** ‚ÄĒ Slender struts in aircraft wings and helicopter rotors.
- **Offshore Structures** ‚ÄĒ Risers and subsea pipelines under hydrostatic pressure.
- **Civil Tall Structures** ‚ÄĒ Chimneys, flagpoles, scaffolding poles.
- **Mechatronics & Robotics** ‚ÄĒ Telescoping booms and telescopic cylinders where slender sections may fail by buckling well before yielding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The famous 1907 collapse of the **Quebec Bridge (Canada)** during construction was largely attributed to buckling of compression chords. This disaster made buckling analysis a compulsory part of structural design codes worldwide.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Euler's Buckling Load (Pinned-Pinned Column)

**Starting Setup:**
Consider a straight column of length $L$, pinned at both ends, subjected to an axial compressive load $P$ at its top. The column is assumed to be slightly disturbed so that it deflects laterally.

**Step 1: Free-Body Diagram and Bending Moment**

Let $y$ be the lateral deflection at a distance $x$ from the bottom (origin). The bending moment at section $x$ is the product of the axial load and the deflection (lever arm):

$$M = -P \cdot y$$

The negative sign indicates that the moment is *restoring* (opposes further deflection) once buckling initiates.

**Step 2: Apply Euler-Bernoulli Beam Equation**

The differential equation of the elastic curve is:

$$EI \frac{d^{2} y}{dx^{2}} = M$$

Substituting $M = -Py$:

$$EI \frac{d^{2} y}{dx^{2}} = -P y$$

**Step 3: Rearrange to Standard Form**

$$\frac{d^{2} y}{dx^{2}} + \frac{P}{EI} y = 0$$

Define a constant:

$$k^{2} = \frac{P}{EI}$$

The equation becomes:

$$\frac{d^{2} y}{dx^{2}} + k^{2} y = 0$$

**Step 4: General Solution**

The general solution of this second-order linear ODE is:

$$y(x) = A \sin(kx) + B \cos(kx)$$

The first derivative (slope) is:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = Ak \cos(kx) - Bk \sin(kx)$$

**Step 5: Apply Boundary Conditions (Pinned-Pinned)**

- **At $x = 0$** (bottom pin): Deflection $y(0) = 0$
  $$0 = A \sin(0) + B \cos(0) \implies B = 0$$
- **At $x = L$** (top pin): Deflection $y(L) = 0$
  $$0 = A \sin(kL) + 0 \cdot \cos(kL) \implies A \sin(kL) = 0$$

**Step 6: Trivial vs. Non-Trivial Solution**

For a non-trivial (non-zero) deflection, $A \neq 0$, hence:

$$\sin(kL) = 0$$

This is satisfied when:

$$kL = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 7: Smallest Value of kL**

The smallest non-zero value corresponds to $n = 1$:

$$kL = \pi \implies k = \frac{\pi}{L}$$

**Step 8: Substitute Back to Find $P_{cr}$**

Since $k^{2} = \dfrac{P}{EI}$:

$$\frac{P_{cr}}{EI} = \left(\frac{\pi}{L}\right)^{2}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Text-Only Logic Recap:** The column deflects as a sine curve. The boundary "zero deflection" at both pins forces the sine to complete exactly half a wavelength. The smallest $k$ that satisfies this is $\pi/L$, and the critical load is the corresponding axial force $P$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Critical Stress and Slenderness Ratio

**Step 1:** Divide $P_{cr}$ by the cross-sectional area $A$:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{A L_{e}^{2}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $I = A k^{2}$ (since $k^{2} = I/A$):

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E (A k^{2})}{A L_{e}^{2}} = \frac{\pi^{2} E k^{2}}{L_{e}^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Introduce slenderness ratio $\lambda = L_e / k$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Euler Crippling Stress Formula** in its most concise form. It clearly shows that as $\lambda$ increases, $\sigma_{cr}$ drops *quadratically* ‚ÄĒ very long columns are dramatically weaker in buckling.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Critical Load for All End Conditions

| End Condition | Boundary Conditions | Solution for $kL$ | Effective Length $L_e$ | $P_{cr}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pinned-Pinned** | $y(0)=0,\ y(L)=0$ | $kL = \pi$ | $L$ | $\pi^{2}EI/L^{2}$ |
| **Fixed-Fixed** | $y(0)=0,\ y'(0)=0,\ y(L)=0,\ y'(L)=0$ | $kL = 2\pi$ | $L/2$ | $4\pi^{2}EI/L^{2}$ |
| **Fixed-Free (Cantilever)** | $y(0)=0,\ y'(0)=0$ | $kL = \pi/2$ | $2L$ | $\pi^{2}EI/(4L^{2})$ |
| **Fixed-Pinned** | $y(0)=0,\ y'(0)=0,\ y(L)=0$ | $kL = 4.493 \approx \pi/\sqrt{2}$ | $L/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7L$ | $\approx 2.046\pi^{2}EI/L^{2}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** The *stronger* the end fixity, the *smaller* the effective length, and hence the *larger* the buckling load. Fixed-Fixed columns can carry **4 times** the load of an identical Pinned-Pinned column.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A hollow cast iron column of external diameter 200 mm and internal diameter 150 mm is fixed at both ends and has a length of 4 m. Find the Euler's crippling load. Take $E = 1.2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm¬≤. Also find the slenderness ratio. (Use $L_e = L/2$ for fixed-fixed.)

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the second moment of area $I$:

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right) = \frac{\pi}{64} \left(200^{4} - 150^{4}\right) \text{ mm}^{4}$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left(1.6 \times 10^{9} - 5.0625 \times 10^{8}\right) = \frac{\pi}{64} \left(1.09375 \times 10^{9}\right)$$

$$I \approx 5.367 \times 10^{7} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 2:** Effective length:

$$L_e = \frac{L}{2} = \frac{4000}{2} = 2000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Euler's crippling load:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_e^{2}} = \frac{\pi^{2} \times (1.2 \times 10^{5}) \times (5.367 \times 10^{7})}{(2000)^{2}}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \times 1.2 \times 10^{5} \times 5.367 \times 10^{7}}{4 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{6.355 \times 10^{13}}{4 \times 10^{6}} \approx 1.589 \times 10^{7} \text{ N} = 15887 \text{ kN} \approx 15887 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4:** Slenderness ratio:

Area $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(200^{2} - 150^{2}) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(40000 - 22500) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(17500) \approx 13744.5 \text{ mm}^{2}$

$$k = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{5.367 \times 10^{7}}{13744.5}} \approx \sqrt{3904.5} \approx 62.48 \text{ mm}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{k} = \frac{2000}{62.48} \approx 32.0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** Although the Euler's formula gives a valid number, this $\lambda$ is quite small (32). For cast iron, the *crushing strength* may govern instead of buckling. This naturally leads to the **Limitations of Euler's Formula** discussed next.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Deflected Shapes for Different End Conditions

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["COLUMN BUCKLING MODES"] --> B["Pinned Pinned"]
    A --> C["Fixed Fixed"]
    A --> D["Fixed Free Cantilever"]
    A --> E["Fixed Pinned"]

    B --> B1["Shape: Single Half Sine Wave"]
    B --> B2["Le equals L"]
    B --> B3["Pcr equals pi squared EI over L squared"]

    C --> C1["Shape: S curve with 2 inflection points"]
    C --> C2["Le equals L over 2"]
    C --> C3["Pcr equals 4 pi squared EI over L squared"]

    D --> D1["Shape: Quarter Sine Wave"]
    D --> D2["Le equals 2L"]
    D --> D3["Pcr equals pi squared EI over 4L squared"]

    E --> E1["Shape: Asymmetric Half Curve"]
    E --> E2["Le equals L over root 2"]
    E --> E3["Pcr equals 2.046 pi squared EI over L squared"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Column Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    START["AXIAL LOAD ON COLUMN"] --> Q1{"Slenderness Ratio Lambda?"}

    Q1 -->|"Lambda less than Lambda_c"| SC["SHORT COLUMN: Failure by CRUSHING"]
    Q1 -->|"Lambda between Lambda_c and Lambda_s"| IC["INTERMEDIATE COLUMN: Use EMPIRICAL FORMULA Rankine or IS code"]
    Q1 -->|"Lambda greater than Lambda_s"| LC["LONG COLUMN: Failure by BUCKLING"]

    SC --> SC1["Use Crushing Strength sigma_c"]
    IC --> IC1["Use Empirical Straight Line or Rankine Formula"]
    LC --> LC1["Use EULER FORMULA: Pcr equals pi squared E I over Le squared"]

    SC1 --> END["DESIGN CHECK PASS"]
    IC1 --> END
    LC1 --> END
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Effective Length and Boundary Conditions Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BC1["Pinned Pinned"]
        PP1["Bottom: y equals 0, M equals 0"]
        PP2["Top: y equals 0, M equals 0"]
    end
    subgraph BC2["Fixed Fixed"]
        FF1["Bottom: y equals 0, dy/dx equals 0"]
        FF2["Top: y equals 0, dy/dx equals 0"]
    end
    subgraph BC3["Fixed Free"]
        FC1["Fixed end: y equals 0, dy/dx equals 0"]
        FC2["Free end: M equals 0, V equals 0"]
    end
    subgraph BC4["Fixed Pinned"]
        FPi1["Fixed: y equals 0, dy/dx equals 0"]
        FPi2["Pinned: y equals 0, M equals 0"]
    end
    BC1 --> R1["Le equals L"]
    BC2 --> R2["Le equals L over 2"]
    BC3 --> R3["Le equals 2L"]
    BC4 --> R4["Le equals L over root 2"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block: Derivation Logic Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["Axial Load P applied to straight column"] --> S2["Small lateral disturbance: y at x"]
    S2 --> S3["Bending moment M equals negative P y"]
    S3 --> S4["Beam equation: EI d2y/dx2 equals M"]
    S4 --> S5["Rearrange to d2y/dx2 plus k squared y equals 0"]
    S5 --> S6["General solution: y equals A sin kx plus B cos kx"]
    S6 --> S7["Apply boundary conditions"]
    S7 --> S8["Smallest kL gives critical load"]
    S8 --> S9["Pcr equals pi squared E I over Le squared"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]**

**Define slenderness ratio. What is its significance in column design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The slenderness ratio is defined as the ratio of the effective length of a column to its least radius of gyration.

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{k} = \frac{L_e}{\sqrt{I/A}}$$

**Significance (1.5 marks):**
- It is a dimensionless parameter that classifies columns as **short, intermediate, or long**.
- For $\lambda > \lambda_{c}$, the column fails by **elastic buckling** (Euler's formula applies).
- For $\lambda < \lambda_{c}$, the column fails by **crushing** (Euler's formula is not valid).
- Higher $\lambda$ means lower critical load ‚ÄĒ *longer and thinner* columns buckle more easily.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď June 2023]**

**State the limitations of Euler's formula for buckling of columns.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Validity Range (1 mark):** Euler's formula is valid only for *long* columns with high slenderness ratio. For short columns, it gives unrealistically high values of $P_{cr}$ that exceed the crushing load.
2. **Idealizations (1 mark):** It assumes a *perfectly straight* column, *perfectly axial* loading, *homogeneous and isotropic* material, and neglects *self-weight* and *initial stresses*.
3. **Material Behaviour (1 mark):** It assumes the material remains within the *elastic limit* (Hooke's law valid). Hence it cannot be used when buckling stress exceeds the proportional limit or yield stress.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2022, Modified]**

**A solid circular steel column of diameter 80 mm and length 6 m is effectively pinned at both ends. Determine:**

**(a)** *Euler's crippling load* (7 marks)
**(b)** *The slenderness ratio and critical stress* (7 marks)

**Given:** $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm¬≤. Take crushing stress $\sigma_{c} = 330$ N/mm¬≤. State whether Euler's formula is applicable.

---

#### **Solution (a): Euler's Crippling Load (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Area and Moment of Inertia (2 marks)**

For a solid circular section of diameter $D = 80$ mm:

$$I = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi \times (80)^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 4.096 \times 10^{7}}{64}$$

$$I = 2.0106 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 2: Effective Length (1 mark)**

For both ends pinned:

$$L_e = L = 6 \text{ m} = 6000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Apply Euler's Formula (3 marks)**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_e^{2}} = \frac{\pi^{2} \times (2.0 \times 10^{5}) \times (2.0106 \times 10^{6})}{(6000)^{2}}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5} \times 2.0106 \times 10^{6}}{3.6 \times 10^{7}}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{3.969 \times 10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^{7}} = 1.1025 \times 10^{5} \text{ N} \approx 110.25 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Final Answer (1 mark)**

$$\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 110.25 \text{ kN}}$$

---

#### **Solution (b): Slenderness Ratio and Critical Stress (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Cross-sectional Area (1 mark)**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (80)^{2}}{4} = 5026.55 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2: Least Radius of Gyration (1 mark)**

For a solid circular section, $I_{min}$ occurs about any diameter and is the same value:

$$k = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.0106 \times 10^{6}}{5026.55}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Slenderness Ratio (2 marks)**

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{k} = \frac{6000}{20} = 300$$

**Step 4: Critical Stress (2 marks)**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{1.1025 \times 10^{5}}{5026.55} \approx 21.93 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

Or using the alternative formula:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}} = \frac{\pi^{2} \times 2.0 \times 10^{5}}{(300)^{2}} = \frac{1.974 \times 10^{6}}{9.0 \times 10^{4}} \approx 21.93 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 5: Applicability Check (1 mark)**

Compute critical slenderness ratio:

$$\lambda_{c} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2} E}{\sigma_{c}}} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2} \times 2.0 \times 10^{5}}{330}} = \sqrt{5984.4} \approx 77.36$$

Since $\lambda = 300 > \lambda_c = 77.36$, **Euler's formula is applicable** and the column will fail by elastic buckling.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using $L$ instead of $L_e$ for non-pinned-pinned columns ‚ÄĒ costs **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert metres to millimetres in the formula consistently ‚ÄĒ results in $P_{cr}$ being off by a factor of $10^6$.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not verifying applicability of Euler's formula using $\lambda_c$ ‚ÄĒ KTU examiners allocate at least 1 mark for this *check*.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Using $I$ about a wrong axis. Always use the **least** $I$ for buckling.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ [KTU University Exam ‚Äď June 2024, Modified]**

**A built-up column consists of two ISMC 250 channels placed back-to-back with their webs vertical, connected by lacing. The column has an effective length of 8 m and is fixed at the base and free at the top (cantilever). Take $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm¬≤. For each ISMC 250 channel: $A = 3126 \text{ mm}^{2}$, $I_{xx} = 4380 \times 10^{4} \text{ mm}^{4}$, $I_{yy} = 247 \times 10^{4} \text{ mm}^{4}$, $C_{yy} = 23$ mm (distance of centroid from back of web). Determine:**

**(a)** *Euler's buckling load for the built-up column about both axes* (7 marks)
**(b)** *The safe load using a factor of safety of 3, and identify the governing axis* (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution (a): Euler's Load About Both Axes (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Combined Section Properties (3 marks)**

For two channels placed back-to-back (webs vertical, toes outward), the section is symmetric about the vertical $y$-axis (since the channels are mirror images). The distance between the centroids of the two channels:

$$d = 2 C_{yy} = 2 \times 23 = 46 \text{ mm}$$

Total area: $A_{total} = 2 \times 3126 = 6252 \text{ mm}^{2}$

**About the $x$-axis (bending in plane of lacing):**

$$I_{x,total} = 2 \times I_{xx} = 2 \times (4380 \times 10^{4}) = 8760 \times 10^{4} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**About the $y$-axis (perpendicular to plane of lacing):**

Using parallel axis theorem (each channel's centroid is at distance $C_{yy} = 23$ mm from the combined centroidal $y$-axis):

$$I_{y,total} = 2 \times \left[ I_{yy} + A \cdot C_{yy}^{2} \right] = 2 \times \left[ 247 \times 10^{4} + 3126 \times (23)^{2} \right]$$

$$I_{y,total} = 2 \times \left[ 2.47 \times 10^{6} + 3126 \times 529 \right] = 2 \times \left[ 2.47 \times 10^{6} + 1.653 \times 10^{6} \right]$$

$$I_{y,total} = 2 \times 4.123 \times 10^{6} = 8.246 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4} = 824.6 \times 10^{4} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 2: Effective Length (1 mark)**

For fixed-free (cantilever):

$$L_e = 2L = 2 \times 8 = 16 \text{ m} = 16000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Euler's Load (3 marks)**

**About $x$-axis:**

$$P_{cr,x} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I_{x}}{L_e^{2}} = \frac{\pi^{2} \times (2.0 \times 10^{5}) \times (8760 \times 10^{4})}{(16000)^{2}}$$

$$P_{cr,x} = \frac{9.8696 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5} \times 8.76 \times 10^{7}}{2.56 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$P_{cr,x} = \frac{1.7291 \times 10^{14}}{2.56 \times 10^{8}} = 6.755 \times 10^{5} \text{ N} \approx 675.5 \text{ kN}$$

**About $y$-axis:**

$$P_{cr,y} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I_{y}}{L_e^{2}} = \frac{\pi^{2} \times (2.0 \times 10^{5}) \times (824.6 \times 10^{4})}{(16000)^{2}}$$

$$P_{cr,y} = \frac{9.8696 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5} \times 8.246 \times 10^{7}}{2.56 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$P_{cr,y} = \frac{1.6275 \times 10^{14}}{2.56 \times 10^{8}} = 6.357 \times 10^{5} \text{ N} \approx 635.7 \text{ kN}$$

---

#### **Solution (b): Safe Load and Governing Axis (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Identify Minimum Critical Load (3 marks)**

$$P_{cr,min} = \min(P_{cr,x}, P_{cr,y}) = \min(675.5, 635.7) = 635.7 \text{ kN}$$

The **$y$-axis governs** the buckling (smaller moment of inertia produces smaller critical load).

**Step 2: Apply Factor of Safety (2 marks)**

$$P_{safe} = \frac{P_{cr,min}}{\text{FoS}} = \frac{635.7}{3} = 211.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Final Interpretation (2 marks)**

$$\boxed{P_{safe} \approx 211.9 \text{ kN}, \text{ governed by buckling about the } y\text{-axis}}$$

The column is much weaker about the $y$-axis because the channels are placed *back-to-back* ‚ÄĒ the lacing plane offers much lower flexural stiffness than the plane through both webs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning for Built-up Columns:**
> - **Critical Mistake:** Forgetting the **parallel axis theorem** for the axis perpendicular to the lacing plane ‚ÄĒ this loses **3 marks** instantly.
> - **Common Mistake:** Using total $A$ vs. individual channel $A$ inconsistently. Always pair $A$ and $I$ correctly when using parallel axis theorem.
> - **Common Mistake:** Failing to identify the *governing axis* (least $I$ axis). The critical load is the **minimum** of $P_{cr}$ about both principal axes.
> - **Pitfall:** Using $L$ instead of $2L$ for cantilever columns.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Euler's Formula:** $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}}$. Always use the **least** $I$.
- **Critical Stress:** $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}$ ‚ÄĒ depends only on slenderness ratio and $E$.
- **Slenderness Ratio:** $\lambda = L_e / k = L_e / \sqrt{I/A}$. Dimensionless; for buckling use the **least** $I$ and corresponding $k$.
- **Effective Lengths:**
  - Pinned-Pinned ‚Üí $L_e = L$
  - Fixed-Fixed ‚Üí $L_e = L/2$
  - Fixed-Free ‚Üí $L_e = 2L$
  - Fixed-Pinned ‚Üí $L_e = L/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7L$
- **Column Classification by $\lambda$:**
  - **Long column** ($\lambda > \lambda_{c}$): Elastic buckling, Euler's formula valid.
  - **Short column** ($\lambda < \lambda_{c}$): Crushing failure, Euler's formula *not* valid.
  - **Intermediate column**: Empirical formulas (Rankine, IS 800 straight-line, etc.) are used.
- **Critical Slenderness Ratio:** $\lambda_{c} = \sqrt{\pi^{2} E / \sigma_{c}}$. Compute this *before* applying Euler.
- **Limitations of Euler's Formula:**
  1. Valid only for *long* columns (high $\lambda$).
  2. Assumes perfectly straight column (no initial crookedness).
  3. Assumes perfectly axial concentric load.
  4. Material must remain in *elastic* range.
  5. Self-weight of column is neglected.
  6. Material is assumed homogeneous and isotropic.
  7. Buckling and crushing loads must be compared for validity.
- **Unit Consistency Check:** For SI units, use $E$ in N/mm¬≤, $I$ in mm‚Āī, $L$ in mm ‚Üí $P_{cr}$ in **N**. Or use $E$ in kN/m¬≤, $I$ in m‚Āī, $L$ in m ‚Üí $P_{cr}$ in **kN**.
- **Built-up Columns:** Always use the **parallel axis theorem** for the axis perpendicular to the plane of lacing/battening.
- **Examiner's Hot Tip:** State and verify the **applicability condition** ($\lambda > \lambda_c$) at the end of every Euler's problem. KTU awards a separate mark for this verification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rankine's Formula
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rankine's Formula

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 ‚ÄĒ Canonical Definition**
> **Rankine's Formula** is an **empirical relationship** used to determine the **crippling (ultimate) load** of a column, taking into account the combined effects of **direct compressive crushing** and **buckling instability**. It is a unified, practical design equation that works for both short (stocky) and long (slender) columns, unlike Euler's formula which is valid only for long columns.

The mathematical statement of Rankine's formula is:

$$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \left(\dfrac{L_e}{k}\right)^2}$$

Where:
- $P_R$ = **Crippling load** predicted by Rankine (in N or kN)
- $\sigma_c$ = **Ultimate crushing stress** of the material (in N/mm¬≤)
- $A$ = **Cross-sectional area** of the column (in mm¬≤)
- $L_e$ = **Effective length** of the column (in mm)
- $k$ = **Least radius of gyration** of the cross-section (in mm)
- $a$ = **Rankine's empirical constant** (dimensionless), a property of the material

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ "The Squeezing Straw Test"

> [!NOTE] **Intuitive Analogy**
> Imagine a thin, hollow straw versus a thick, short pillar, both made of the same paper:
> - The **short thick pillar** fails by being **crushed flat** (material gives way under direct compression) ‚ÄĒ this is *crushing failure*.
> - The **long thin straw**, even though it can support some direct load, suddenly **bows sideways** and snaps in the middle before crushing ‚ÄĒ this is *buckling failure*.
> - In real engineering columns, **both** crushing and buckling coexist. A paper straw standing upright won't simply crush OR simply buckle ‚ÄĒ it does a bit of both.
>
> **Rankine's formula** is the engineer's way of saying: *"Let's add up the contribution of crushing failure AND buckling failure, and find a single combined failure load that works whether the column is short, medium, or long."* It is the **universal compromise equation**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP] **Standard KTU Design Values (Must be Memorized)**
> **Rankine's Constant $a$ for common materials:**
>
> | Material | $a$ | $\sigma_c$ (N/mm¬≤) | $E$ (N/mm¬≤) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Mild Steel** | $1/7500$ | 320 ‚Äď 330 | $2.0 \times 10^5$ |
> | **Cast Iron** | $1/1600$ | 550 ‚Äď 700 | $1.0 \times 10^5$ |
> | **Wrought Iron** | $1/9000$ | 250 ‚Äď 300 | $1.9 \times 10^5$ |
> | **Timber** | $1/750$ | 50 ‚Äď 80 | $1.2 \times 10^4$ |
>
> These values are **standard KTU reference values** expected to be used directly in university problems without being re-derived.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ‚ÄĒ Why Rankine Proposed This Formula

Euler's buckling formula:

$$P_E = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$$

is **mathematically rigorous** but suffers from two serious drawbacks:

1. **It is valid ONLY for long, slender columns** (where slenderness ratio $L_e/k$ is very high).
2. **It does not account for direct crushing** of the material ‚ÄĒ a short column does not buckle, it crushes.
3. **Euler's predicted load becomes extremely high** (theoretically infinite) as the column gets shorter, which contradicts the real crushing limit.

Rankine observed experimentally that the **actual failure load of a column** lies somewhere between the Euler buckling load and the crushing load. He proposed the elegant **reciprocal addition principle**:

$$\frac{1}{P_R} = \frac{1}{P_c} + \frac{1}{P_E}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Physical Meaning of the Reciprocal Relation**
> - If the column is **very short** (stocky): $P_E \to \infty$, so $\frac{1}{P_E} \to 0$, and $P_R \to P_c$ (crushing governs).
> - If the column is **very long** (slender): $P_c \to$ relatively small influence, $P_E$ dominates, and $P_R \to P_E$ (buckling governs).
> - For **intermediate lengths**: Both terms contribute, and $P_R$ is a smooth blend.
>
> This is precisely the behavior observed in real laboratory tests, making Rankine's formula an **excellent empirical fit**.

## 2.2 Conversion of the Reciprocal Relation into Standard Rankine Form

Starting from the reciprocal principle:

$$\frac{1}{P_R} = \frac{1}{P_c} + \frac{1}{P_E}$$

Taking LCM and simplifying:

$$P_R = \frac{P_c \cdot P_E}{P_c + P_E} = \frac{P_c}{1 + \dfrac{P_c}{P_E}}$$

Now substitute $P_c = \sigma_c \cdot A$ and $P_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E \cdot A k^2}{L_e^2}$:

$$\frac{P_c}{P_E} = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A \cdot L_e^2}{\pi^2 E A k^2} = \frac{\sigma_c}{\pi^2 E} \cdot \left(\frac{L_e}{k}\right)^2$$

Define Rankine's constant:

$$a = \frac{\sigma_c}{\pi^2 E}$$

We obtain the **canonical Rankine formula**:

$$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \left(\dfrac{L_e}{k}\right)^2}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **High-Yield Formula Bank ‚ÄĒ KTU Board Exam Essentials**
>
> | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Use Case |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | $P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c A}{1 + a(L_e/k)^2}$ | Main Rankine formula | Crippling load |
> | 2 | $P_c = \sigma_c \cdot A$ | Crushing load (N) | Short column limit |
> | 3 | $P_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$ | Euler's buckling load (N) | Long column limit |
> | 4 | $\dfrac{1}{P_R} = \dfrac{1}{P_c} + \dfrac{1}{P_E}$ | Reciprocal principle | Conceptual derivation |
> | 5 | $a = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\pi^2 E}$ | Definition of Rankine's constant | Material classification |
> | 6 | $k = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | Least radius of gyration (mm) | Slenderness input |
> | 7 | $\lambda = L_e / k$ | Slenderness ratio (dimensionless) | Column classification |
> | 8 | $\sigma_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{1 + a(L_e/k)^2}$ | Rankine stress (N/mm¬≤) | Direct stress version |
> | 9 | $A, I_{\min}$ | Area, min. moment of inertia | Cross-section property |
> | 10 | $L_e = L, 2L, L/2$ for fixed-hinged-fixed | Effective length | End condition factor |
>
> **CRITICAL NOTATION WARNING (no pipes `\|` in math):** All absolute values, fractions, and ratios are typeset using $\vert$ or $\left(\right)$ to maintain markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP] **Where Rankine's Formula is Used in Industry**
> - **Design of building columns** (steel, RCC, timber) when slenderness is moderate.
> - **Mast and tower design** (telecom, transmission lines).
> - **Pressure vessel skirt supports** in petrochemical plants.
> - **Crane jib columns and boom supports**.
> - **Bridge piers** of moderate height.
> - **Strut members in trusses** (roof trusses, railway bridges, transmission towers).
> - **Quick preliminary design** before detailed buckling analysis (FEA) is conducted.

**Advantage over Euler's formula**: Rankine's is valid across the **entire slenderness range** ‚ÄĒ short, medium, AND long columns ‚ÄĒ making it the **go-to formula for KTU board exams and practical office design**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Rankine's Formula

> [!IMPORTANT] **Derivation Roadmap**
> We start from the **reciprocal principle** (an experimentally observed truth) and algebraically convert it into the standard Rankine form, introducing the empirical constant $a$.

### Step 1: State the empirical reciprocal principle

$$\frac{1}{P_R} = \frac{1}{P_c} + \frac{1}{P_E}$$

*Where $P_c$ is the crushing load and $P_E$ is Euler's buckling load.*

### Step 2: Take the LCM of the right-hand side

$$\frac{1}{P_R} = \frac{P_E + P_c}{P_c \cdot P_E}$$

### Step 3: Invert both sides to isolate $P_R$

$$P_R = \frac{P_c \cdot P_E}{P_c + P_E}$$

### Step 4: Divide numerator and denominator by $P_c$

$$P_R = \frac{P_E}{1 + \dfrac{P_E}{P_c}} = \frac{P_c}{1 + \dfrac{P_c}{P_E}}$$

### Step 5: Substitute the explicit expressions for $P_c$ and $P_E$

$$P_c = \sigma_c \cdot A$$

$$P_E = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$$

Therefore:

$$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A \cdot L_e^2}{\pi^2 E I}}$$

### Step 6: Express moment of inertia in terms of radius of gyration

By definition:

$$I = A \cdot k^2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k^2 = \frac{I}{A}$$

Substitute $I = A k^2$ into the denominator term:

$$\frac{\sigma_c \cdot A \cdot L_e^2}{\pi^2 E \cdot A k^2} = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot L_e^2}{\pi^2 E \cdot k^2}$$

### Step 7: Define Rankine's empirical constant

Let:

$$a = \frac{\sigma_c}{\pi^2 E}$$

This is a **pure material constant** (no dependence on geometry), making it a true material property.

### Step 8: Substitute $a$ back to obtain the canonical Rankine form

$$\frac{\sigma_c \cdot L_e^2}{\pi^2 E \cdot k^2} = a \cdot \left(\frac{L_e}{k}\right)^2$$

Therefore, the final form is:

$$\boxed{\,P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \left(\dfrac{L_e}{k}\right)^2}\,}$$

**DERIVATION COMPLETE** ‚ÄĒ this is the exact form expected in KTU 14-mark derivations.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Standard KTU 7-Mark Sub-Question)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2024 Pattern]**
> A hollow circular cast iron column has an external diameter of 200 mm and internal diameter of 150 mm. The effective length of the column is 4.5 m. Determine the **crippling load** using Rankine's formula. Take $\sigma_c = 550$ N/mm¬≤ and $a = 1/1600$.

### Solution ‚ÄĒ Step by Step

**Step 1: Calculate the cross-sectional area $A$**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D^2 - d^2\right) = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(200^2 - 150^2\right)$$

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(40000 - 22500\right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 17500$$

$$A = 13744.47 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate the moment of inertia $I$ about the centroidal axis**

For a hollow circular section, the minimum (and only) moment of inertia is:

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(D^4 - d^4\right) = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(200^4 - 150^4\right)$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(1.6 \times 10^9 - 5.0625 \times 10^8\right)$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64} \times 1.09375 \times 10^9$$

$$I = 5.367 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3: Calculate the least radius of gyration $k$**

$$k = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{5.367 \times 10^7}{13744.47}}$$

$$k = \sqrt{3904.79} = 62.49 \text{ mm}$$

*Sanity check using hollow circle shortcut:* $k = \sqrt{\dfrac{D^2 + d^2}{16}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{200^2 + 150^2}{16}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{62500}{16}} = \sqrt{3906.25} = 62.50$ mm. ‚úď

**Step 4: Calculate the slenderness ratio $\lambda = L_e / k$**

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{k} = \frac{4500}{62.50} = 72$$

**Step 5: Calculate the slenderness ratio squared**

$$\lambda^2 = 72^2 = 5184$$

**Step 6: Calculate the denominator term $a \lambda^2$**

$$a \lambda^2 = \frac{1}{1600} \times 5184 = 3.24$$

**Step 7: Apply Rankine's formula**

$$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a\lambda^2} = \frac{550 \times 13744.47}{1 + 3.24}$$

$$P_R = \frac{7559458.5}{4.24}$$

$$\boxed{P_R = 1,782,420 \text{ N} \approx 1782.42 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 8: Compute the safe working load (using factor of safety, if asked)**

If the question asks for safe load with FOS = 4 (typical for cast iron columns):

$$P_{safe} = \frac{P_R}{\text{FOS}} = \frac{1782.42}{4} = 445.6 \text{ kN}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Answer-Writing Pattern (7-Mark Sub-Part)**
> 1. Write the formula symbolically first (1 mark).
> 2. List all given data and material constants (1 mark).
> 3. Compute $A$, $I$, $k$ step-by-step (2 marks).
> 4. Compute $\lambda$ and the denominator (1 mark).
> 5. Final substitution and answer with correct units (2 marks).

## 3.3 Special Case: Crank-Nicolson of Hollow Rectangular Sections

> [!NOTE] **KTU Frequently Tested Cross-Sections**
>
> | Section Type | Area $A$ | $I_{\min}$ | $k_{\min}$ |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Solid Rectangle $(B \times D)$ | $B \cdot D$ | $\dfrac{B D^3}{12}$ | $\dfrac{D}{\sqrt{12}}$ |
> | Hollow Rectangle $(B_o \times B_i, D_o \times D_i)$ | $B_o D_o - B_i D_i$ | $\dfrac{B_o D_o^3 - B_i D_i^3}{12}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{I_{\min}}{A}}$ |
> | Solid Circular $(D)$ | $\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | $\dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}$ | $\dfrac{D}{4}$ |
> | Hollow Circular $(D, d)$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4)$ | $\dfrac{\sqrt{D^2 + d^2}}{4}$ |
> | I-Section | $A_{\text{flange}} + A_{\text{web}}$ | (use parallel axis theorem) | $\sqrt{I_{\min}/A}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Derivation Logic Flow

> The following Mermaid flow diagram maps the **logical flow** of the Rankine formula derivation ‚ÄĒ from empirical observation to final canonical form.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Empirical Observation] --> B[Reciprocal Principle: 1/PR = 1/Pc + 1/PE]
    B --> C[Invert: PR = Pc √ó PE / Pc + PE]
    C --> D[Substitute Pc = ŌÉc √ó A]
    D --> E[Substitute PE = ŌÄ¬≤EI / Le¬≤]
    E --> F[Group terms: ŌÉc √ó A √ó Le¬≤ / ŌÄ¬≤ √ó E √ó I]
    F --> G[Use I = A √ó k¬≤ identity]
    G --> H[Cancel A: ŌÉc √ó Le¬≤ / ŌÄ¬≤ √ó E √ó k¬≤]
    H --> I[Define Rankine Constant a = ŌÉc / ŌÄ¬≤E]
    I --> J[Final Rankine Form: PR = ŌÉc √ó A / 1 + a √ó Le/k squared]
    J --> K[End: Apply to Numerical Problems]
    L[Material] --> I
    M[Cross-section Geometry] --> G
    N[End Conditions] --> E
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Column Failure Classification

> The following diagram maps **how Rankine's formula behaves across the slenderness spectrum** ‚ÄĒ a critical concept for KTU viva and conceptual questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Column Cross-section + Length] --> B{Compute L over k Ratio}
    B --> C[Slenderness Ratio Lambda]
    C --> D{Lambda Compared to Limits}
    D -->|Lambda < 20| E[SHORT Column: Crushing Dominates]
    D -->|20 ‚Č§ Lambda ‚Č§ 100| F[MEDIUM Column: Rankine Formula]
    D -->|Lambda > 100| G[LONG Column: Euler Buckling Dominates]
    E --> H[Failure Mode: Direct Crushing]
    F --> I[Failure Mode: Combined Buckling + Crushing]
    G --> J[Failure Mode: Elastic Buckling]
    H --> K[Safety Equation: ŌÉc / FOS]
    I --> L[Safety Equation: Rankine PR / FOS]
    J --> M[Safety Equation: Euler PE / FOS]
```

## 4.3 Effective Length Boundary Condition Map

> The following diagram shows the relationship between **end conditions**, **effective length factor**, and the resulting $L_e$ used in Rankine's formula.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[End Condition Type] --> B[Both Ends Hinged]
    A --> C[Both Ends Fixed]
    A --> D[One End Fixed, One Free]
    A --> E[One End Fixed, One Hinged]
    B --> F[Effective Length Le = L]
    C --> G[Effective Length Le = L divided by 2]
    D --> H[Effective Length Le = 2L]
    E --> I[Effective Length Le = L divided by sqrt 2]
    F --> J[Use in Rankine Denominator]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Final Slenderness Ratio Lambda = Le over k]
```

> [!TIP] **Visualization Aid (ASCII Schematic of Column Types)**
> ```
>  (a) BOTH ENDS HINGED        (b) BOTH ENDS FIXED
>     ‚Ė≥       ‚Ė≥                    |=====|
>     |   L   |                    |     |
>     |       |                    |  L  |
>     ‚Ė≥       ‚Ė≥                    |     |
>   Le = L                         |=====|
>                               Le = L/2
>
>  (c) ONE END FIXED, FREE     (d) ONE END FIXED, HINGED
>     |                            |=====
>     |  L                         |    \
>     |  ‚Ė≤ (free)                  |  L  ‚Ė≥
>     |=                           |    /
>                                Le = L/‚ąö2
>   Le = 2L
> ```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024]
**Q: State Rankine's formula for the strength of a column. Explain the significance of the empirical constant $a$ used in it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Rankine's formula gives the **crippling load** $P_R$ of a column as:
> $$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \left(\dfrac{L_e}{k}\right)^2}$$
>
> The constant $a$ is a **material-specific empirical constant** that captures the material's resistance to combined crushing and buckling. It is defined as:
> $$a = \frac{\sigma_c}{\pi^2 E}$$
>
> Higher $a$ implies lower Rankine load for the same slenderness. Typical values: $a = 1/7500$ for mild steel, $a = 1/1600$ for cast iron, $a = 1/750$ for timber. It has **no units** (dimensionless) and depends only on the material's elastic and crushing properties.

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]
**Q: Compare Euler's formula and Rankine's formula for column strength. When is Rankine's formula preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Euler's formula** $P_E = \pi^2 E I / L_e^2$ is theoretically exact but valid **only for long, slender columns** (high slenderness ratio) where failure is purely by elastic buckling.
>
> **Rankine's formula** combines both **crushing and buckling** failure modes using the reciprocal principle and is valid for **all slenderness ratios** ‚ÄĒ short, medium, and long columns.
>
> Rankine's formula is preferred for **medium-range columns** and when a single unified design equation is needed across varying slenderness. It is the **practical design choice** in KTU board examinations.

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> **Common student mistakes that cost marks in Rankine problems:**
> 1. ‚ĚĆ **Forgetting to use $L_e$ (effective length)** instead of actual $L$ ‚ÄĒ losing **2 marks** in 14-mark problems.
> 2. ‚ĚĆ **Using wrong $a$ value** ‚ÄĒ must match the material (steel: 1/7500, cast iron: 1/1600, timber: 1/750).
> 3. ‚ĚĆ **Mixing up $I_{\min}$ vs $I_{\max}$** ‚ÄĒ always use the **least** moment of inertia for $k_{\min}$.
> 4. ‚ĚĆ **Not squaring the slenderness ratio** $(L_e/k)^2$ ‚ÄĒ a very common algebraic error.
> 5. ‚ĚĆ **Unit inconsistency** ‚ÄĒ convert $L$ from m to mm BEFORE substitution. Mixing m and mm gives wild numerical errors.
> 6. ‚ĚĆ **Forgetting to convert $P_R$ to kN** for the final answer when the question expects kN.

---

### ūüĒĻ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Derive Rankine's formula for the crippling load of a column. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A solid circular mild steel column has a diameter of 120 mm and an effective length of 3.2 m. Using Rankine's formula, find the safe load on the column with a factor of safety of 4. Take $\sigma_c = 330$ N/mm¬≤ and $a = 1/7500$. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: State the empirical reciprocal relation.**

> Rankine proposed that the actual crippling load $P_R$ is related to the crushing load $P_c$ and Euler's buckling load $P_E$ by:
> $$\frac{1}{P_R} = \frac{1}{P_c} + \frac{1}{P_E}$$

**Step 2: Algebraic inversion.**

$$P_R = \frac{P_c \cdot P_E}{P_c + P_E} = \frac{P_c}{1 + \dfrac{P_c}{P_E}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute explicit expressions.**

$$P_c = \sigma_c \cdot A \quad \text{and} \quad P_E = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$$

$$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c A}{1 + \dfrac{\sigma_c A L_e^2}{\pi^2 E I}}$$

**Step 4: Introduce radius of gyration $I = A k^2$.**

$$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c A}{1 + \dfrac{\sigma_c L_e^2}{\pi^2 E k^2}}$$

**Step 5: Define Rankine's constant $a = \sigma_c / \pi^2 E$.**

$$\boxed{\,P_R = \frac{\sigma_c A}{1 + a \left(\dfrac{L_e}{k}\right)^2}\,}$$

This is the required Rankine formula. *(1 mark for statement, 2 marks for algebraic manipulation, 2 marks for introducing $k$, 2 marks for final form with constant definition.)*

##### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $D = 120$ mm, $L_e = 3.2$ m $= 3200$ mm, $\sigma_c = 330$ N/mm¬≤, $a = 1/7500$, FOS $= 4$.

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area.**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 120^2}{4} = 11309.73 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Least moment of inertia (only one for circular section).**

$$I = \frac{\pi D^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 120^4}{64} = 1.0179 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3: Least radius of gyration.**

$$k = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \frac{D}{4} = \frac{120}{4} = 30 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Slenderness ratio squared.**

$$\left(\frac{L_e}{k}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{3200}{30}\right)^2 = (106.67)^2 = 11377.78$$

**Step 5: Denominator term $a \lambda^2$.**

$$a \lambda^2 = \frac{1}{7500} \times 11377.78 = 1.5170$$

**Step 6: Apply Rankine's formula.**

$$P_R = \frac{330 \times 11309.73}{1 + 1.5170} = \frac{3732211.4}{2.5170} = 1,482,805 \text{ N}$$

$$P_R \approx 1482.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7: Apply factor of safety.**

$$P_{\text{safe}} = \frac{P_R}{\text{FOS}} = \frac{1482.8}{4} \approx 370.7 \text{ kN}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{\text{safe}} \approx 370.7 \text{ kN}\,}$$

---

### ūüĒĻ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** State and explain the assumptions made in deriving Rankine's formula. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A hollow rectangular cast iron column has outer dimensions $200 \text{ mm} \times 150 \text{ mm}$ and inner dimensions $160 \text{ mm} \times 110 \text{ mm}$. If the effective length is 4 m, find the **crippling load** using Rankine's formula. Take $\sigma_c = 600$ N/mm¬≤ and $a = 1/1600$. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Assumptions of Rankine's Formula (7 Marks)

1. **The column is initially perfectly straight** with no initial curvature or crookedness.

2. **The material is homogeneous and isotropic**, with uniform elastic properties throughout.

3. **The column fails by a combination of crushing and buckling** simultaneously ‚ÄĒ Rankine's empirical relation assumes both failure modes coexist.

4. **The principle of reciprocal addition** ($1/P_R = 1/P_c + 1/P_E$) holds true across the full range of slenderness ratios ‚ÄĒ this is an **empirical observation**, not a theoretical derivation.

5. **Self-weight of the column is neglected** (or is very small compared to the applied load).

6. **The end conditions determine the effective length** $L_e$ using standard factors (1, 1/2, 2, $1/\sqrt{2}$).

7. **The empirical constant $a$ is fixed for a given material** and does not vary with column geometry.

8. **The cross-section is uniform along the length** of the column (no steps or variations).

*(Allocate ~1 mark per key assumption, with diagrams if needed.)*

##### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** Outer: $200 \times 150$ mm, Inner: $160 \times 110$ mm, $L_e = 4$ m $= 4000$ mm, $\sigma_c = 600$ N/mm¬≤, $a = 1/1600$.

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area.**

$$A = B_o D_o - B_i D_i = 200 \times 150 - 160 \times 110 = 30000 - 17600 = 12400 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Least moment of inertia (about weaker axis).**

For a rectangular section, the minimum $I$ corresponds to the **smaller overall dimension**. Here, depth $D = 150$ mm, width $B = 200$ mm, so the **weaker axis is parallel to the 200 mm side** (bending about the smaller depth):

$$I_{\min} = \frac{B_o D_o^3 - B_i D_i^3}{12} = \frac{200 \times 150^3 - 160 \times 110^3}{12}$$

$$I_{\min} = \frac{200 \times 3,375,000 - 160 \times 1,331,000}{12}$$

$$I_{\min} = \frac{675,000,000 - 212,960,000}{12} = \frac{462,040,000}{12}$$

$$I_{\min} = 38,503,333.33 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3: Least radius of gyration.**

$$k_{\min} = \sqrt{\frac{I_{\min}}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{38,503,333.33}{12400}} = \sqrt{3105.11} \approx 55.72 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Slenderness ratio squared.**

$$\left(\frac{L_e}{k}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4000}{55.72}\right)^2 = (71.79)^2 = 5153.6$$

**Step 5: Denominator term $a \lambda^2$.**

$$a \lambda^2 = \frac{1}{1600} \times 5153.6 = 3.221$$

**Step 6: Apply Rankine's formula.**

$$P_R = \frac{600 \times 12400}{1 + 3.221} = \frac{7,440,000}{4.221}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_R \approx 1,762,615 \text{ N} \approx 1762.6 \text{ kN}\,}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep)**
>
> ‚úÖ **The Core Formula** (memorize verbatim):
> $$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c A}{1 + a(L_e/k)^2}$$
>
> ‚úÖ **The Reciprocal Principle** (essential for 7-mark derivations):
> $$\frac{1}{P_R} = \frac{1}{P_c} + \frac{1}{P_E}$$
>
> ‚úÖ **Rankine's Constant** (memorize these three ‚ÄĒ they are KTU standards):
> - Mild Steel: $a = 1/7500$
> - Cast Iron: $a = 1/1600$
> - Timber: $a = 1/750$
>
> ‚úÖ **Use $L_e$ (effective length), not $L$ (actual length)** ‚ÄĒ End conditions:
> - Both ends hinged: $L_e = L$
> - Both ends fixed: $L_e = L/2$
> - One fixed, one free: $L_e = 2L$
> - One fixed, one hinged: $L_e = L/\sqrt{2}$
>
> ‚úÖ **Always use the LEAST $I$ and LEAST $k$** (most students lose marks here by accidentally using $I_{\max}$).
>
> ‚úÖ **Rankine is valid for ALL slenderness ratios** ‚ÄĒ that is its biggest advantage over Euler's formula.
>
> ‚úÖ **Units**: Convert $L$ to mm; $\sigma_c$ in N/mm¬≤; $A$ in mm¬≤; result $P_R$ will be in N (divide by 1000 for kN).
>
> ‚úÖ **Crushing load shortcut**: $P_c = \sigma_c \cdot A$ ‚ÄĒ useful for sanity checks.
>
> ‚úÖ **Always check**: $(L_e/k)^2$ must be squared, NOT linear.
>
> ‚úÖ **Factor of Safety**: $P_{\text{safe}} = P_R / \text{FOS}$; standard FOS = 3 to 5 for cast iron, 4 to 5 for steel.
>
> ‚úÖ **Comparisons to remember**:
> - Euler valid only for long columns.
> - Rankine valid for all columns (short, medium, long).
> - Rankine reduces to $P_c$ for very short columns and to $P_E$ for very long columns.
>
> ‚úÖ **Common cross-section shortcuts**:
> - Solid circle: $k = D/4$
> - Hollow circle: $k = \sqrt{D^2 + d^2}/4$
> - Solid rectangle: $k_{\min} = d/\sqrt{12}$ where $d$ is the smaller dimension.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsion of Circular and Hollow Circular Shafts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET205 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS (CIVIL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Torsion of Circular and Hollow Circular Shafts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Torsion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torsion** is the twisting of a structural member (shaft) about its longitudinal axis when subjected to a couple (torque) acting in a plane perpendicular to the longitudinal axis. The internal reactive moment set up within the material to resist this twisting is termed the **Internal Torsional Moment** or **Torque of Resistance**.

In structural engineering and machine design, the most common application of torsional loading occurs in **transmission shafts** that transfer power from one point to another (e.g., drive shafts in automobiles, propeller shafts of ships, turbogenerator shafts in power plants).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Torsion of circular and hollow circular shafts* refers to the study of stresses, strains, and deformations produced in cylindrical shafts of solid and hollow cross-sections when subjected to torque about their longitudinal axis.

## 1.2 Key Assumptions in the Theory of Pure Torsion (Circular Shafts)

For a perfectly accurate theory of torsion in circular shafts, the following assumptions are adopted (these are **non-negotiable** for KTU board exam derivations):

> [!NOTE]
> 1. The material of the shaft is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law** (linearly elastic).
> 2. The cross-section of the shaft remains **plane and circular** even after twisting.
> 3. **Cross-sections rotate about the longitudinal axis** like a rigid disc (warping is neglected for circular sections).
> 4. **Radial lines remain radial and straight** after deformation; they only rotate through a small angle.
> 5. The distance between any two cross-sections remains **unchanged** (no axial strain).
> 6. The shaft is loaded by **pure torque only** (no bending, axial, or shear forces).

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy ‚ÄĒ "The Wet Towel Concept"

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: Twisting a wet towel (the classic engineering analogy):**
> - Imagine holding a wet towel with both hands and twisting it to wring out water. Your two hands apply equal and opposite turning forces (a **couple**) at each end.
> - Each fiber of the towel experiences **shear** as it is twisted relative to the next.
> - The maximum stress occurs on the **outermost fibers** (just like the surface of a shaft), and the center fibers experience little to no stress.
> - This is why **hollow circular shafts** are more efficient ‚ÄĒ they remove the low-stress core material and put material where the stress is highest!
> - The towel returns to its original shape once you let go (elastic recovery ‚ÄĒ Hooke's Law in action).

## 1.4 Torsion of Hollow Circular Shafts ‚ÄĒ Special Considerations

A hollow shaft is essentially a solid shaft with the central low-stress core removed. The same torsion equation applies, but the **polar moment of inertia** is calculated as the difference between the outer and inner polar moments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance of Hollow Shafts:**
> - **Weight reduction** for the same torque capacity.
> - **Material economy** ‚ÄĒ places material where shear stress is maximum.
> - Used in **bicycle frames, drive shafts of cars, helicopter rotor masts, and marine propeller shafts**.

**Physical Constants Referenced:**
- **Shear Modulus (G) or Modulus of Rigidity:** For mild steel, **G = 77 GPa (‚Čą 80 GPa)**; for brass, **G = 39 GPa**; for cast iron, **G = 41 GPa**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Torsion Equation ‚ÄĒ The Heart of Module 4

The torsion equation links the **applied torque**, **shear stress**, **geometry of the shaft**, and **angle of twist** into one elegant relationship:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{q}{r} = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L}$$

Where:
- **T** = Applied external torque (or internal torque of resistance), units: **N¬∑m**
- **J** = Polar moment of inertia of the cross-section, units: **m‚Āī** (or mm‚Āī)
- **q** = Shear stress at radius *r*, units: **N/m¬≤** (Pa) or **N/mm¬≤** (MPa)
- **r** = Radial distance from the center of the shaft, units: **m** or **mm**
- **G** = Modulus of rigidity of the material, units: **Pa** or **N/m¬≤**
- **őł** = Angle of twist, units: **radians (rad)**
- **L** = Length of the shaft, units: **m** or **mm**

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Torsion Equation

The equation emerges from **two fundamental relationships**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Relationship 1 ‚ÄĒ Strength of Materials (Stress Equation):**
> From the equilibrium of the torque on a small element on the cross-section:
> $$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{q}{r} \quad \Rightarrow \quad q = \frac{T \cdot r}{J}$$
> This shows that **shear stress is directly proportional to the radius** ‚ÄĒ zero at the center, maximum at the outer surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Relationship 2 ‚ÄĒ Rigidity of Materials (Stiffness Equation):**
> From the geometry of twist and Hooke's Law in shear:
> $$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$$

## 2.3 Polar Moment of Inertia ‚ÄĒ J

The polar moment of inertia is the **torsional equivalent of the area moment of inertia** used in bending. It measures the cross-section's resistance to twisting.

### 2.3.1 Solid Circular Shaft

For a solid circular shaft of diameter **D** (radius **R = D/2**):

$$J = \frac{\pi \cdot D^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \cdot R^4}{2}$$

### 2.3.2 Hollow Circular Shaft

For a hollow circular shaft of outer diameter **D** (radius **R**) and inner diameter **d** (radius **r**):

$$J = \frac{\pi \cdot (D^4 - d^4)}{32} = \frac{\pi \cdot (R^4 - r^4)}{2}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **S.No** | **Quantity** | **Solid Shaft Formula** | **Hollow Shaft Formula** | **Units** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Polar Moment of Inertia (J) | $\frac{\pi D^4}{32}$ | $\frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$ | m‚Āī or mm‚Āī |
| 2 | Maximum Shear Stress ($q_{max}$) | $\frac{16T}{\pi D^3}$ | $\frac{16T \cdot D}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}$ | Pa or MPa |
| 3 | Angle of Twist (őł) | $\frac{32TL}{\pi D^4 G}$ | $\frac{32TL}{\pi G (D^4 - d^4)}$ | radians |
| 4 | Angle of Twist per unit length ($\theta/L$) | $\frac{32T}{\pi D^4 G}$ | $\frac{32T}{\pi G (D^4 - d^4)}$ | rad/m |
| 5 | Power Transmitted (P) | $\frac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | $\frac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Watts (W) |
| 6 | Torque from Power (T) | $\frac{60 P}{2\pi N}$ | $\frac{60 P}{2\pi N}$ | N¬∑m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where:**
> - **N** = Speed of rotation in **revolutions per minute (rpm)**
> - **P** = Power transmitted in **Watts (W)**
> - **T** = Torque in **N¬∑m**
> - **D** = Outer diameter, **d** = Inner diameter (for hollow shaft)
> - **G** = Modulus of rigidity in **N/m¬≤** or **MPa**

## 2.5 Shear Stress Distribution ‚ÄĒ A Visual Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **For a Solid Shaft:**
> - At center (r = 0): **q = 0** (no stress)
> - At outer surface (r = R): **q = q_max = 16T/(ŌÄD¬≥)**
> - Stress varies **LINEARLY** from 0 at the center to maximum at the surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **For a Hollow Shaft:**
> - The material is concentrated between radius *r* and radius *R*.
> - Minimum shear stress occurs at the **inner surface**: $q_{min} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{16T \cdot d}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}$
> - Maximum shear stress occurs at the **outer surface**: $q_{max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} = \frac{16T \cdot D}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}$
> - The ratio of stresses: $\frac{q_{max}}{q_{min}} = \frac{D}{d}$

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!TIP]
> **Where Torsion Theory is Applied in Civil & Mechanical Engineering:**
> 1. **Reinforced Concrete Beams** ‚ÄĒ Torsion design in beams subjected to eccentric loading or curved bridges.
> 2. **Steel Buildings** ‚ÄĒ Members subjected to wind/earthquake torsional moments in asymmetric structures.
> 3. **Bridge Girders** ‚ÄĒ Box girders, curved bridges, and skew bridges.
> 4. **Helical Staircases** ‚ÄĒ Central column experiences torsional loading.
> 5. **Power Transmission Shafts** ‚ÄĒ In turbogenerators, motor shafts, and industrial machinery.
> 6. **Offshore Structures** ‚ÄĒ Drill strings, riser pipes, and platform legs under wave-induced torsion.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Torsion Equation for a Solid Circular Shaft

### Setup:
Consider a solid circular shaft of radius **R** and length **L**, fixed at one end and subjected to a torque **T** at the free end. After twisting, the free end rotates through an angle **őł** (in radians) relative to the fixed end.

### Step 1: Geometric Analysis of Twist

Consider a point **P** on the surface of the shaft (at radius R) at the free end. After twisting, this point moves to a new position **P'**. The arc length **PP'** represents the relative shear displacement on the outer surface.

$$\text{Arc length } PP' = R \cdot \theta$$

The corresponding shear strain **ő≥** on the surface is the arc length divided by the length of the shaft:

$$\gamma = \frac{R \cdot \theta}{L}$$

### Step 2: Apply Hooke's Law in Shear

Since the material is elastic and obeys Hooke's Law:

$$q = G \cdot \gamma$$

Substituting the value of ő≥:

$$q = G \cdot \frac{R \cdot \theta}{L}$$

$$\frac{q}{R} = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L}$$

### Step 3: Equilibrium of the Cross-Section

Now consider an elemental ring on the cross-section at radius *r* with thickness *dr*. The shear stress on this ring is *q*, and the area is *dA = 2ŌÄr¬∑dr*.

The elemental torque contributed by this ring is:

$$dT = q \cdot dA \cdot r = q \cdot (2\pi r \, dr) \cdot r = 2\pi r^2 q \, dr$$

The total internal torque of resistance is:

$$T = \int_0^R 2\pi r^2 q \, dr$$

### Step 4: Substitute the Stress Distribution

From the linear stress distribution (since q/r is constant on the cross-section):

$$q = \frac{q_{max}}{R} \cdot r = \frac{q}{R} \cdot r$$

Therefore:

$$T = \int_0^R 2\pi r^2 \cdot \left(\frac{q}{R} \cdot r\right) dr = \frac{2\pi q}{R} \int_0^R r^3 \, dr$$

$$T = \frac{2\pi q}{R} \cdot \left[\frac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^R = \frac{2\pi q}{R} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi q R^3}{2}$$

### Step 5: Solve for the Maximum Shear Stress

Substituting $R = D/2$:

$$T = \frac{\pi q (D/2)^3}{2} = \frac{\pi q D^3}{16}$$

Solving for q (which is the maximum shear stress on the surface):

$$q_{max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi D^3}$$

### Step 6: Combine the Two Relationships

We have two equations:

$$\text{(a)} \quad \frac{q_{max}}{R} = \frac{G \theta}{L} \quad \text{... (from Step 2)}$$

$$\text{(b)} \quad \frac{T}{J} = \frac{q_{max}}{R} \quad \text{... (where } J = \frac{\pi R^4}{2} = \frac{\pi D^4}{32}\text{)}$$

Combining (a) and (b):

$$\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{q}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L}}$$

This is the **Torsion Equation** ‚ÄĒ universally applicable to all circular shafts (solid and hollow).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Polar Moment of Inertia for a Hollow Circular Shaft

### Setup:
A hollow circular shaft has outer diameter **D** (outer radius **R**) and inner diameter **d** (inner radius **r**).

### Step 1: Visualize the Hollow Section

The hollow shaft is equivalent to a **solid shaft of radius R minus a solid shaft of radius r** (the removed core).

### Step 2: Apply the Subtraction Principle for Moments of Inertia

$$J_{hollow} = J_{outer} - J_{inner}$$

$$J_{hollow} = \frac{\pi R^4}{2} - \frac{\pi r^4}{2} = \frac{\pi (R^4 - r^4)}{2}$$

### Step 3: Convert to Diameters

Since R = D/2 and r = d/2:

$$J_{hollow} = \frac{\pi}{2} \left[\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^4 - \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^4\right] = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{D^4 - d^4}{16}$$

$$\boxed{J_{hollow} = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Angle of Twist for a Hollow Shaft

Starting from the torsion equation:

$$\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$$

Substituting J for a hollow shaft:

$$\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}}$$

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{32 \cdot T \cdot L}{\pi \cdot G \cdot (D^4 - d^4)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **angle of twist in radians**. To convert to degrees, multiply by **180/ŌÄ**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Maximum Shear Stress in a Hollow Shaft

From the torsion equation: $q_{max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J}$

Substituting R = D/2 and J for hollow shaft:

$$q_{max} = \frac{T \cdot (D/2)}{\dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}} = \frac{16 \cdot T \cdot D}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}$$

$$\boxed{q_{max} = \frac{16 T D}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Comparison: Solid vs. Hollow Shaft

| **Parameter** | **Solid Shaft (Diameter D)** | **Hollow Shaft (D, d)** | **Ratio (H/S)** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| Mass per unit length | $\rho \cdot A_s = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | $\rho \cdot A_h = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$ | $1 - (d/D)^2$ |
| Polar Moment (J) | $\frac{\pi D^4}{32}$ | $\frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$ | $1 - (d/D)^4$ |
| Stiffness (GJ) | $\frac{\pi G D^4}{32}$ | $\frac{\pi G (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$ | $1 - (d/D)^4$ |
| Shear Stress (q_max) | $\frac{16T}{\pi D^3}$ | $\frac{16T D}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}$ | $\frac{1}{1 - (d/D)^4}$ |
| Angle of Twist (őł) | $\frac{32 T L}{\pi G D^4}$ | $\frac{32 T L}{\pi G (D^4 - d^4)}$ | $\frac{1}{1 - (d/D)^4}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** For the **same weight per unit length**, a hollow shaft has a **much higher polar moment of inertia** and hence a **much lower angle of twist** and **lower maximum stress** compared to a solid shaft. This is the primary reason hollow shafts are preferred for high-power transmission applications (e.g., aircraft propeller shafts, marine shafts).

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example ‚ÄĒ Torsion of a Hollow Shaft

> [!TIP]
> **Example 1 (Type Question for KTU 2024):**
> A hollow steel shaft of outer diameter 80 mm and inner diameter 50 mm transmits 60 kW at 200 rpm. Take G = 80 GPa. Calculate:
> (a) The maximum shear stress in the shaft
> (b) The angle of twist per metre length
> (c) The minimum shear stress (at the inner surface)

### Given Data:
- D = 80 mm = 0.080 m
- d = 50 mm = 0.050 m
- P = 60 kW = 60,000 W
- N = 200 rpm
- G = 80 √ó 10‚ĀĻ Pa

### Step 1: Calculate the Torque

$$T = \frac{60 \cdot P}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 60{,}000}{2\pi \times 200}$$

$$T = \frac{3{,}600{,}000}{1256.64} = 2864.79 \, \text{N¬∑m}$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Polar Moment of Inertia

$$J = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32} = \frac{\pi [(0.080)^4 - (0.050)^4]}{32}$$

$$J = \frac{\pi [4.096 \times 10^{-5} - 6.25 \times 10^{-6}]}{32}$$

$$J = \frac{\pi \times 3.471 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 3.408 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^4$$

### Step 3: Maximum Shear Stress (at outer surface, r = R = 40 mm)

$$q_{max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} = \frac{2864.79 \times 0.040}{3.408 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$q_{max} = \frac{114.59}{3.408 \times 10^{-6}} = 33.62 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{q_{max} \approx 33.62 \, \text{MPa}}$$

### Step 4: Angle of Twist per metre length (L = 1 m)

$$\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{T}{G \cdot J} = \frac{2864.79}{80 \times 10^9 \times 3.408 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{2864.79}{272{,}640} = 0.01051 \, \text{rad/m}$$

$$\boxed{\theta/L \approx 0.01051 \, \text{rad/m} = 0.602¬į/\text{m}}$$

### Step 5: Minimum Shear Stress (at inner surface, r = 25 mm)

$$q_{min} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{2864.79 \times 0.025}{3.408 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$q_{min} = \frac{71.62}{3.408 \times 10^{-6}} = 21.01 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{q_{min} \approx 21.01 \, \text{MPa}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification using ratio formula:** $q_{max}/q_{min} = D/d = 80/50 = 1.6$
> Check: $33.62/21.01 = 1.6$ ‚úď
